text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y M with PMH significant for SVT, aflutter, prior aflutter ablation, pAF, and PVI on ___ for symptomatic AF, transferred to ___ service for asthma exacerbation and new hyponatremia. # A fib: paroxysmal afib since ___ prior to this, had a-flutter and prior ablation in ___. S/P PVI on ___ for increasing symptomatic a-fib. Tolerated procedure well. Continued on Digoxin 250 mcg PO/NG 4X/WEEK (___) and 125 mcg PO/NG 3X/WEEK (___), and also continued on metoprolol 12.5 mg PO/NG TID (although pt takes much greater quantities of this at home). Discharged with ___ of Hearts monitor, and with INR follow-up on ___. # Wheezing: likely asthma exacerbation as symptoms improve with nebs, and chest x-ray without PE or evidence of pneumonia. Received IV lasix, however currently appears euvolemic; no further diuresis at this time. Continued on home levalbuterol tartrate, budesonide *NF* 180 mcg/actuation inhalation BID, continue Montelukast 10 mg PO/NG HS (pt taking his own meds), and discharged on prednisode taper (25 mg on ___- pt controls his own prednisone tapers). Improved on discharge. # Hyponatremia: euvolemic hyponatremia. Most likely SIADH due to either stress of procedure, constipation, or asthma exacerbation. No new drugs, no intracranial process, not eating a low solute diet. On admission, Na of 138. On ___, 124. On ___, 134 with only fluid restriction. This increase is not dangerous because he dropped in less than 48 hrs. Consider rechecking Na at next PCP ___. # Constipation: resolved # Hypertension: normotensive. Continued on home lisinopril 5 mg. HCTZ discontinued. # HLD: continued on home simvastatin 10 mg # HSV: continued on home acyclovir TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ================= TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ===================== # HCTZ was discontinued upon discharge # discharged with ___ of Hearts Monitor # pt is taking very high doses of metoprolol at home (> 300 mg/day) # please recheck chem 7 at first PCP ___ # INR monitoring already in place at ___: they would like to see him on ___
summary: You were admitted to have a procedure to treat your atrial fibrillation called Pulmonary Vein Isolation. The procedure went well. We kept you for an extra day in the hospital because you had an asthma exacerbation and also because the level of sodium in your blood became low. Your low sodium was likely due to the stress of either the procedure or your asthma exacerbation, which commonly happens; it corrected itself and was normal at the time of your discharge. We stopped your hydrochlorathiazide, which you should not take once leaving the hospital. Please record your heart rhythm three times daily as well as any symptoms as instructed using the ___ ___ Monitor.

text: Brief Hospital Course: A/P: ___ p/w syncope and upper respiratory symptoms found to have influenza A. . # Influenza A: The patient tested positive for influenza A. Likely contributing to syncopal episode and explaining URI symptoms, decreased PO intake and left shift. She was started on Oseltamvir 75mg BID x5d given <72h symptoms in hospitalized pt with asthma and diabetes. She was given supportive care with fluids, and nebulizers, zofran prn nausea, tylenol prn fever, guaifenisen prn cough. . #Syncope: Likely secondary to influenza A and decreased PO intake. Patient had witnessed episode and did not have tonic-clonic movement, and was without post-ictal phase or loss of control of bladder or bowels. EKG was unchanged, cardiac enzymes were negative. CT head/spine in the ED were non-concerning for acute injury. U/a was negative. Ucx neg, bcx pending. Syncopal episode may have been exacerbated by polypharmacy with oxycodone and clonazepam. Pt was given IVFs and orthostatics were negative prior to dispo. PO intake was encouraged. Pt was continued on home doses vicodin, tylenol prn pain from fall. . #DM2: Controlled on metformin monotherapy with aspirin ppx for CVD. Normal alb/cre at baseline. mild neuropathy at baseline. Last A1c (___): 7.1, last urine alb/creatinine (___): 3.9, LDL ___. Metformin was held and pt was covered with ISS. Discharged on home metformin. . # Anxiety/Depression: continued home clonazepam, citalopram . # Pain: on home vicodin ___, gave hydrocodone 5mg q6h prn, tylenol ___ q6h prn pain/fever, celebrex ___ daily. . # GERD: continued ranitidine 150mg BID, omeprazole 20mg daily . # Asthma: Gave albuterol nebs prn wheezing, increased home flovent 110mcg to Flovent 220mcg to be continued x1mo and return to previous dosing. . # HTN: held home lisinopril, restarted on dispo. . # HL: continued home simvastatin. . # Allergies: continue loratadine 10mg daily . # FEN: diet, replete electrolytes prn, IVFs/encourage PO fluid intake . # PPX: PPI+H2 blocker, heparin sq/pneumoboots . # Code: full . # Social issues: pt stated that during admission she slept better than she has since her husband died because she has been sleeping on the couch because she cannot bear to sleep in the bed where they used to sleep together, but cannot afford to move to another apt. Should continue to follow-up with social work as an outpt.
summary: You were admitted for an episode of fainting and found to have the flu. The flu likely caused this episode as you were weak from infection and had not been eating and drinking enough. We did CT scans of your head and spine and found that there was no injury to your bones from falling. We did tests to show that your fall was not due to a heart problem. We have been treating you with intravenous fluids and antibiotics. You were also found to have some worsening of your asthma due to flu infection. We treated this with increased frequency of your home medications. You will be discharged on a higher dose of Flovent which you should use for a month and then return to your regular dose. You should expect to continue to feel sick, with muscle aches, and some weakness for the next several days. It is very important that you continue to drink a lot of fluids to prevent feeling lightheaded or passing out.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient was admitted with shortness of breath that was likely an acute exacerbation of chronic diastolic heart failure consistent with his increased edema and recently decreased lasix dose. The patient was treated with lasix, however his blood pressure dropped to the 80's systolic after 0.3L urine output. Therefore his Norvasc and Valsartan and lasix were held. Also during this episode it was noted that he was bradycardic with a heart rate into the 30's, with a baseline heart rate in the 60___s. The patient's drop in blood pressure was attributed to over diuresis and he was treated with a fluid bolus and transferred to the cardiology service for possible evaluation for a pacemaker. . He was also wheezing on admission and his shortness of breath was improved with the diuresis and with albuterol and ipratropium treatments. The symptoms were from a combination of heart failure, and worsened by underlying asthma. The patient required oxygen and at discharge was at 2 liters nasal cannula when walking. The patient may require home oxygen when discharged from rehab. . On the cariology service the patient was found to have bradycardia, and while on telemetry monitoring he had a heart rate into the low 20's transiently multiple times, with no symptoms. Pt was also not increasing his heart rate appropriately with activity, it would not exceed the mid-70's while walking, even while short of breath. His echocardiogram showed mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function, Mild aortic regurgitation, Moderate mitral regurgitation, Moderate pulmonary hypertension, and enlarged atria and an ejection fraction of >55%. Also his EKG's had very low voltage. . Due to this the patient was tested for amyloidosis- his SPEP, UPEP, and fat biopsy were negativef for signs of amyloid. Due to the continued bradycardia the patient was evaluated for a pacemaker, he received a single lead left sided pacemaker. He tolerated the procedure without problem. He also was slowly diuresised with lasix and put on a 2 liter fluid restriction to treat his heart failure. The patient was on telemtry monitoring. Lasix was given conservatively due the patients elevated creatinine with too much lasix. He was started back on his Diovan, but his Norvasc was held due to the bradycardia. If the patient becomes hypertensive after discharge, there should be consideration to restart his Norvasc. . Since the patient has received a pacemaker he was treated prophylactically with vancomycin, and he will be discharged with 1 days of treatment on Keflex BID. He has a follow up appointment with the device clinic and with his cardiologist to monitor his pacemaker function and symptoms of heart failure. . Of note, the patient was noted to have a very congested voice, which was new for the patient for the last month. He had a speech and swallow evaluation before discharge who recommended soft solids for his diet. This will need further evaluation at rehab, to see if the patient still requireds this restriction. Patient was tolerating this diet without problems at discharge, and stated that it was much easier for him to consume. . The patient was also noted to have a bullous pemphigoid rash, (has had it for ___ years). Pt had recently been decreased from Prednisone 15 every day to every other day. However, likely secondary to the stress of being in the hospital the patient's rash increased and had more bullae on his forearms. This was non-painful, not pruritic, and did not appear to be infectous. The prednisone was increased to everyday and clobetasol ointment was started BID, this improved the rash and the bullae were resolving at discharge. Pt is to follow up with his Dermatologist this week to evaluate for futher treatment and long term managment of this skin condition. . He was hemodynamically stable at discharge and able to ambulate with assitance.
summary: You were admitted to ___ with heart failure. Your heart rate was also decreased. You were recommended to have a pacemaker, therefore, you received the pacemaker and toleraed the procedure well. You will need to go to a rehab center once you leave the hospital since you are not strong enough to be home alone at this time. As you were instructed after your pacemaker was placed, do not get your bandages wet, or shower, or raise your left arm above your head until you are seen in the Device clinic in 1 week. During your stay you also had more blisters and rash related to you skin condition, your predinisone was increased to everyday and you were given steriod cream. You will need to see your dermatologist for further care of your skin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with history of DLBCL, CAD, CKD, and hypertension who presents with left foot pain concerning for gout vs. septic arthritis. # Left Foot Pain/Swelling: Exam with significant swelling and tenderness over right MCP joint and right ankle joint. Concern for possible inflammatory vs. septic arthritis. No prior history of gout. She did have trauma about 1 month ago that could have been a trigger however no more recent trauma or infectious symptoms. Plain films without fracture. Uric acid and CRP elevated. She was initially started on prednisone given concern for inflammatory arthritis (gout). She was also started on IV vancomycin for possible septic joint. Orthopedics was consulted for potential arthrocentesis of the left ankle which was attempted at beside but not successful. Blood cultures grew coagulase negative staph which is likely a contaminant. Vancomcyin was discontinued. She had significant improvement in exam and symptoms (likely from steroids) and she was able to bear weight on her left leg. She was discharged home on a short prednisone taper. She was evaluated by Physical Therapy who recommended discharge home. # Chronic Kidney Disease: Cr 1.8 on admission, currently at baseline. # Hypertension: Continued home metoprolol, lasix, and losartan. # CAD: Continued home aspirin, metoprolol, and statin. # Chronic Pain: The patient has chronic pain due to arthritis. Her pain is mostly in her lower back and in her knees. She is on a narcotics contract for Percocet, which she takes up to three times daily. Her medications were continued. # Asthma: Continued home advair. # Hyperlipidemia: Continued home crestor. # GERD: Continued home ranitidine. ==================== Transitional Issues: ==================== - Patient with likely gout which improved with steroids and patient was discharged home with on a prednisone taper. Joint tap was attempted at bedside however no joint fluid was obtained. Please note elevated uric acid and consider starting allopurinol. - Please follow-up pending blood cultures from ___, and ___. - Please ensure Oncology follow-up. - Please recheck renal function at follow-up visit on ___. - Code Status: Full Code - Contact: ___ (daughter/HCP) ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (100/50) 1 INH IH BID 2. Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO TID:PRN pain, fever 3. Albuterol Inhaler ___ PUFF IH Q6H:PRN wheezing 4. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 5. Benzonatate 200 mg PO TID:PRN cough 6. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID 7. FoLIC Acid 1 mg PO DAILY 8. Losartan Potassium 50 mg PO DAILY 9. Metoprolol Succinate XL 75 mg PO DAILY 10. Multivitamins W/minerals 1 TAB PO DAILY 11. Ranitidine 150 mg PO DAILY 12. Rosuvastatin Calcium 40 mg PO QPM 13. Senna 8.6 mg PO BID:PRN constipation 14. Clobetasol Propionate 0.05% Cream 1 Appl TP BID 15. Nitroglycerin SR 0.4 mg PO ONCE:PRN chest pain 16. Oxycodone-Acetaminophen (5mg-325mg) 1 TAB PO Q8H:PRN pain 17. Furosemide 20 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted with pain and swelling of your left foot. You had x-rays of your foot that showed no fracture or dislocation. There was a concern for an infection if your foot and you were started on antibiotics. Your blood cultures were negative and the antibiotics were stopped. You were started on a steroid called prednisone and your pain and swelling improved significantly. You likely have gout which is a type of inflammatory arthritis. Please talk to your primary care doctor about starting a medication to help prevent gout flares in the future. You were discharged on a steroid called prednisone to help with the pain and inflammation in your foot. You will taper (decrease the dose) of this medication over the next week as below: Take 5 tablets (50mg) on ___. Take 4 tablets (40mg) on ___ and ___. Take 3 tablets (30mg) on ___ and ___. Take 2 tablets (20mg) on ___ and ___. Take 1 tablet (10mg) on ___ and ___. Then stop this medication.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Diarrhea: suspect infectious etiology, possibly travelers diarrhea. IBS less likely given patient's normal IBS symptoms usually are shorter duration. Low suspicion for underlying chronic malabsorptive process. Apart from fatigue, appears well clincally, and exam/labs are reassuring. C. diff and O and P negative, remainder of stool studies pending. Received 2 liters of normal saline, orthostatic vital signs normal. Following admission, patient did not have any recurrent diarrhea for over 12 hours, and had one episode of loose stool, non-bloody, prior to discharge. Anti-motility agents were stopped. Patient diet was advanced to low residue/BRAT, tolerated without worsening abdominal pain. Patient returned home, and was instructed to continue to stay hydrated with pedialyte/gatorade, etc. continue a low BRAT diet, and resume her home medications. Cultures pending will be followed up. # ? Hematuria- patient reported that she had voided and noted small blood clots in urine. Repeat void was clear urine, which was sent for urinalysis, which showed blood on dipstick and and one RBC. A CK level was added on to previous days labs, although there is a low suspicion for rhabdomyolysis, as patient was ambulating the halls prior to discharge. The CK level will also be followed up. # IBS: continued probiotics # Hemorrhoids: improved with Anusol, ___ baths # Bipolar disorder: stable, continued quetiapine and lamotrigine # Asthma- chronic, stable, continued albuterol # GERD- PPI # Code- full # Communication- patient, wife ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Albuterol Inhaler ___ PUFF IH Q4H:PRN wheezing 2. ClonazePAM 1 mg PO BID:PRN anxiety hold for sedation, RR < 10 3. LaMOTrigine 150 mg PO DAILY 4. Mirena *NF* (levonorgestrel) 20 mcg/24 hour ___ years) Injection in place 5. Lithium Carbonate 150 mg PO BID 6. MetFORMIN (Glucophage) 500 mg PO BID 7. Omeprazole 40 mg PO BID 8. Align *NF* (bifidobacterium infantis) 4 mg Oral daily 9. Bismuth Subsalicylate Chewable 2 TAB PO QID 10. Citracal + D Maximum *NF* (calcium citrate-vitamin D3) 315-250 mg-unit Oral daily 2 tablets daily 11. Vitamin D 400 UNIT PO DAILY 12. LOPERamide 2 mg PO QID:PRN diarrhea 13. Ranitidine 150 mg PO HS 14. QUEtiapine extended-release 100 mg PO QAM 15. QUEtiapine extended-release 250 mg PO QPM
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with diarrhea. Stool cultures to date have revealed no bacterial or parasitic infection. You received 2 liters of IVF, and there was no recurrent orthostasis (low blood pressure upon standing) or evidence of abnormalities on labs, which is reassuring. Following admission, you had one episode of diarrhea, which is decreased from th ___ daily episodes prior to admission, which was also reassuring. Hydrocortisone cream and ___ baths helped relieve your hemorrhoidal pain. You likely have a resolving intestinal infection. Please continue to stay hydrated with Pedialyte, Gatorade, etc. if diarrhea persists. Please continue a BRAT/low residue diet for the coming days. There a few stool cultures and a urinalysis pending, I will follow up these results.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Right-sided low back pain: The patient was admitted with right sided low back pain and MRI findings concerning for an acute inflammatory process and possible osteomyelitis. On exam she had several tender and firm lumps on her right low back in the paralumbar area. The lumps became noticeable ___ year ago after she started to lose weight from her gastric bypass surgery. A MRI from ___ showed small fluid collections and inflammation in subcutaneous tissue extending deep through fascia. There was also enhancement in the L3-L5 area and spinous process of L5. We initially thought this was an infectious process, however the patient was afebrile and did not have leukocytosis. The Infectious Disease team was consulted and recommended obtaining a tissue biopsy to send for microbial cultures, including mycobacteria and other fungal organisms. Both ESR and CRP were not elevated (ESR 5, CRP 0.3), which made osteomyelitis less likely. Several surgical services were consulted to obtain samples from the fluid collection and a soft tissue biopsy. Neurosurgery and orthopedic surgery did not deem it necessary to obtain a deep tissue or bone biopsy. ___ surgery was therefore consulted. They took her to the OR on ___ and obtained culture swabs and a soft tissue biopsy. Per the operative note, the surgery service did not encounter any areas of purulence during their procedure. The wound was left open to drain and close by secondary intention. Her pain was controlled with MS contin, dilaudid, and acetaminophen. Pain medication was uptitrated to her comfort. Microbiology results of swabs showed no organisms on Gram stain and no growth on wound culture, anaerobic culture, and fungal culture. No fungal elements on ___ prep. She did not receive any antibiotics during her hospitalization. The pathology report noted fibroadipose tissue with bland reactive myofibroblastic proliferation and soft tissue edema, with a differential diagnosis that includes reactive conditions such as localized massive lymphedema. The differential also included inflammatory response to a prior biopsy and possible inflammation from previous cortisone injections. She was discharged home with ___ services for wet to dry dressing changes and was recommended to follow up with her primary care physician. The patient also has a scheduled elective surgery on ___ ___ (___) for excision of the back lumps. . # Esophageal spasm: This is a long-standing issue for her and she previously underwent esophageal procedures including a funduplication. Unclear etiology of spasm, and per OMR she has had several EGDs with normal appearing mucosa. The patient experienced one episode of esophageal spasm during this admission and received nitroglycerin with good affect. She was also continued on her home omeprazole. . # Asthma: Patient uses albuterol at home as needed for shortness of breath and wheezing. She continued to receive inhalers and nebulizer on a prn basis. She did not have any breathing or respiratory issues during her hospitalization.
summary: You were admitted to the ___ for evaluation and management of your chronic low back pain and back lumps. Prior to admission, you had a MRI of your back which showed progression of an inflammatory process, fluid collections, and possible bone involvement. This MRI was compared to previous ones. Due to concern for infection, it was recommended you be admitted for workup in the hospital. During this admission you were seen by multiple teams, including the infectious disease team and ___ surgery service. Your back pain and the inflammatory changes on MRI were initially thought to be related to an infectious process. You underwent a soft tissue biopsy by the ___ surgeons which was sent for multiple cultures and tests in an attempt to isolate and identify infectious organisms. All of these results returned as negative. The tissue biopsy was also reviewed by pathology, which revealed chronic inflammatory changes but no acute signs of infection. In addition, due to your stable clinical exam, lack of fever, and no evidence of infection based on labs, you were not given any antibiotics. The etiology of the lumps on your back and your back pain is not known, however we think it may be related to chronic inflammatory changes from previous cortisone injections and the biopsy you underwent in ___. Throughout your hospital stay and after the surgical biopsy, pain control has been a main problem and we continued to adjust pain medications to optimize your comfort. At the time of discharge, we increased morphine (MS ___ doses to achieve a steady basal rate of pain control, as well as short acting breakthrough morphine.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female with DM, adrenal insufficiency, asthma/COPD, fibromyalgia and OSA who presents with COPD exacerbation. . #) COPD/chronic bronchitis exacerbation: Upon arrival to the emergency room, the patient received SoluMedrol 125mg x 1, azithromycin 500mg IV x 1 and nebulizer treatments with some immediate relief of symptoms. She was admitted to the medicine service and was transitioned to a prednisone taper, starting at 40mg x 2 days, then 30mg x 2 days and discharged on 20mg x 2 days, 10mg x 2 days and return to baseline dose of 7.5mg daily. She was treated with doxycycline 100mg q12 hrs for a total of 7 day course. She received nebulizer treatments - albuterol and atrovent with improvement of her symptoms. She initially was placed on oxygen 2L but was weaned to room air with O2 sat 95% or higher both at rest and on ambulating. Her home dose of Advair was continued. Finally, her atenolol dose was reduced to 25mg daily with intent to taper off as an outpatient given good BP control and potential for this to contribute to bronchospasm. . #) Chest pain: She was monitored on telemetry, had serial EKGs and cardiac enzymes that were all unremarkable. It was felt that her chest tightness was pulmonary ___ origin as she had improvement of her discomfort with nebulizer treatments. . #) ?Blood per rectum: The patient reported 2 episodes of this prior to admission, noting a small amt of dried blood. She has no episodes while ___ the hospital and was guiaic negative on the day of admission. Given her COPD flare, it was decided to postpone her previously scheduled colonoscopy/endoscopy to a later date. Her aspirin was held with instructions to hold on discharge. . #) Diabetes mellitus: The patient did have some high ___ up to >300 when on higher doses of prednisone. She was continued on her home dose of metformin, Lantus and sliding scale with much better control on discharge. . #) Adrenal insufficiency: Prednisone at higher dose as above, then taper to baseline dose. . #) Depression/Anxiety: Continued on Cymbalta and clonazepam. She was tearful at times, but denied suicial ideation and felt that she would be safe at home. . #) Chronic pain/Fibromyalgia: Continued outpatient doses of oxycodone, methadone and gabapentin. . #) Diastolic heart failure, chronic w/o exacerbation: Last ECHO ___ ___ w/ LVH and likely diastolic dysfunction. Her Lasix dose was held for two days given a bump ___ creatinine to 1.1 and reduced on discharge to 80mg bid.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for evaluation of chest pain, cough, and shortness of breath. You were monitored on a heart monitor, had multiple EKGs, and blood tests that showed NO evidence of a heart attack. Your symptoms were felt to be a result of an acute flare up of bronchitis or COPD and you were started on antibiotics and a higher dose of prednisone. You will take a tapering dose of steroids as prescribed and then restart your home dose of 7.5mg daily. You will take an antibiotic called doxycycline for a total of a 7 day course. Your aspirin was stopped while you were ___ the hospital, do not restart this medication until you talk to Dr. ___. Finally, your dose of Lasix was changed to 80mg twice daily and atenolol dose reduced to 25mg daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ obese F with dCHF, asthma, HTN and atrial fibrillation (on coumadin and verapamil) who presents with worsening SOB and fluid retention. . #. SOB/Edema: Patient presented with symptoms consistent with CHF exacerbation. She was treated with multiple prn doses of 40mg IV furosemide and had excellent response with fluid output. Her lower extremity edema improved. We continued to check her electrolyte status during diuresis. Her creatinine levels remained within normal ranges during diuresis. On admission she was very SOB and required 4L of 02 satting at 95%. She was maintained on oxygen for several days. She was also treated with ipratroprium nebulizers PRN for SOB. By discharge, she was satting 93% on 1 L and 86% on RA, so was d/c on ___ L home O2. The patient also had an Echocardiogram performed to evaluate her heart function and establish a possible cause of this acute exacerbation of CHF. Patient had a previous echo but results were limited due to body habitus. New echo showed global hypokinesis and an ejection fraction of ___. Due to her CHF, patient was started on lisinopril 5mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, increased to metoprolol succinate 150mg daily, and started on torsemide 40mg daily. She should continue with these medications as an outpatient. Ms. ___ has an appointment to follow up with her PCP, ___, on ___ ___ at 8:30am. Dr. ___ was informed of all of the changes made to Ms. ___ regimen during her stay. Patient should also follow up with her cardiologist, Dr. ___, on ___ at 2:40. Dr. ___ has also been notified about her hospital course. She was discharged on ___ on home O2, with stable VS. Metoprolol 75mg TID was changed to Toprol XL 200mg daily for ease of dosing and she was also d/c on torsemide 20mg daily. . #. Asthma: Patient had minimal wheezing throughout her stay but was maintained on prn Albuterol nebs for intermittent SOB. Patient should continue on home albuterol inhaler after dishcarge. . #. Atrial fibrillation: Patient was found to be in afib per EKG. Not symptomatic. Patient was maintained on coumadin with therepeutic INR. Home dose of coumadin was changed from 5mg 5x/week and 2.5mg 2x/week to 5mg daily. Her home verapimil was DCed and metoprolol was substituted due to better protection from CHF. Patient should continue these medications at home. . #. EtOH: Patient drinks ___ drinks per night. CAGE (-). No signs of withdrawal during her stay. CIWA scale was calculated to be less than 10. No ativan was given. She was counseled on the contraindications of drinking to her extent, especially related to her heart health. Patient was informed that alcohol use might be contributing to her worsening heart failure, and should discontinue alcohol in the future. She should follow up with her PCP to discuss this in more detail. . #. Anxiety/depression: Patient was maintained on home dose of sertraline and had no symptoms. Patient should continue on sertraline at home.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for increasing shortness of breath and fluid retention that was not being alleviated with no resolution at home with PO lasix. You were treated in the hospital with multiple doses of IV furosemide. You had excellent output of urine in response to the IV furosemide. Your leg edema was much improved. You also received an Echocardiogram of your heart to try to interpret why you had this acute exacerbation of water retention. It looks like you have worsening heart failure since your last echo was performed in ___. We added some new medications which will help to protect your heart in the setting of congestive heart failure. You should also stop your intake of alcohol to help improve your heart failure, as this is likely contributing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PRESENTATION: ___ w h/o moderate intermittent asthma p/w likely asthma exacerbation and PNA. Infection likely the trigger for this asthma exacerbation. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Asthma The pt received PO prednisone 60mg QD, Albuterol nebs q6h standing and q2h PRN, ipratropium nebs q6h standing, and supplemental O2 (2L N/C). He also received levofloxacin 750mg to empirically treat for community-acquired pneumonia given new opacities on CXR. He noted improvement in his shortness of breath on this regimen. His lung exam also improved, with less expiratory wheezing by day of discharge. He was discharged to complete a 5 day prednisone burst and with prescriptions for albuterol and advair. # Pneumonia As noted above, the pt received levofloxacin 750mg to empirically treat for community-acquired pneumonia. His white blood cell count was 11.8 on admission (___) and 11.0 on day of discharge (___). At the time of discharge, blood cultures were pending. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Rhinitis The pt was without complaint and did not require treatment for rhinitis this admission. # Reflux The pt did not complain of reflux and did not require treatment for reflux this admission. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Pt will need to continue taking prednisone 60mg x 3 days - Pt will need to continue taking levofloxacin 750mg x 5 days - ___ blood final blood cultures
summary: You were admitted to ___ on ___ with shortness of breath likely due to an asthma exacerbation, possibly triggered by a concurrent pneumonia. A chest xray showed a possible opacity in your right lung. You were treated with prednisone 60 mg, albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers, and levofloxacin 750mg. You were also given supplemental oxygen. Following discharge, please continue to take prednisone and levofloxacin as prescribed. Please also continue to use Advair and an albuterol inhaler for your asthma. During your admission, you complained of some abdominal bloating that is likely gas pain. Please take simethicone (Gas-X) to treat this.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with a complicated past medical history including asthma and OSA who presents with acute onset shortness of breath since approximately 2 am on the morning of admission. She was briefly admitted to the ICU on a BiPap then NRB. She swiftly transitioned to nasal cannula on asthma exacerbation therapy. . ACTIVE ISSUES: #Chronic Asthma with Acute Exacerbation: Given her history of asthma, wheezing on exam, and improvement over several hours with nebs, this was likely another asthma exacerbation. Despite her decreased mobility, we doubted PE, as she had a low D-dimer of 271, and she has no calf tenderness. Given her elevated white count with slight left shift, she could have early pna, but she was afebrile and had no other sx and CXR and her exam was not consistent with infection. Given her risk factors of DM2, HTN, HLD she is also at risk of ACS. She denied any CP upon development of her SOB, her first trop was negative, and her recent cath was unremarkable. The inspiratory nature of her wheezing is also concerning for vocal cord dysfunction. Also, her recent URI is likely a contributing factor to the exacerbation. COPD, Pickwikian, bronchitis, and vocal cord dysfunction are also possible and her acute decompensation is likely multifactorial. She was treated with IV steroids (125 mg solumedrol), albuterol nebs, and a 100% non-rebreather and was brought to the ICU. By the time she reached the ICU, her sats had improved and she was stable on 4L by nasal cannula. She was brought up to the floor where she was continued on Q4 albuterol nebs and 30 mg PO prednisone and her O2 was weaned. She noted significant improvement in her breathing and felt that she was near her baseline by the morning following her admission. She was discharge on albuterol nebs, which she has at baseline, as well as a 3 day prednisone taper and flovent inhaler . INACTIVE ISSUES: #Type 2 DM Uncontrolled with complications- We held her hold home metformin and glipizide and kept her on ISS. . #Benign Hypertension- Home meds were continued: valsartan, furosemide. . #Hyperlipidemia- continued home statin . #GERD- continued omeprazole . #Osteoarthritis- continued home pain treatment regimen with vicodin and motrin . #Panic disorder- continued home quetiapine QHS . #depression- continued mirtazapine and citalopram . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: 1. She will go back to the ___ for continued care. 2. She has a follow-up appointment with a pulmonologist to evaluate her respiratory issues. An ___ appointment was being discussed at discharge and may be arranged pending appointment availability. 3. She has a follow-up appointment with ENT to evaluate for possible vocal cord dysfunction.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had an acute episode of shortness of breath at night that made your oxygen saturation very low. You were given oxygen therapy and IV steroids in the emergency department, which helped your breathing a great deal. It is likely that this episode was the result of an asthma exacerbation and may have been triggered by your recent cold and the congestion and sputum you mentioned. There may also be some involvement from a dysfunction of your vocal cords. You were continued on oxygen by nasal cannula, with a higher rate than at your nursing home and you were given a low dose of oral steroids on your second day in the hospital. You will go back to your nursing home with continued albuterol nebs and oxygen therapy as you were on before admission.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with a history of bronchopulmonary dysplasia and tracheomalacia s/p multiple reconstructive surgeries, chronic tracheostomy, asthma, and GERD, who presented with several weeks of ongoing production of thick green sputum, increased secretions, general weakness, dizziness, and new sputum cultures positive for MRSA, consistent with acute on chronic tracheitis. #Tracheitis The patient's presenting symptoms of green sputum production, SOB, chills, subjective fatigue and poor PO intake, no leukocytosis and clear CXR resembled previous admissions for acute exacerbations of tracheobronchitis in the setting of chronic tracheostomy and poor baseline secretion control. New on this admission was a sputum culture positive for sparse growth of MRSA. In the ED, she received ceftazadine, aztreonam, and vancomycin, and on the floor was descalated to Vancomycin. Her symptoms quickly improved and was clinically ___ throughout her admission. Nebulizer treatments were continued. She was discharged home to complete 10 day course of IV vancomycin with Pulm, ID, ENT, and PCP follow up. - vancomycin q12h to be completed on ___ - vanc trough to be drawn ___ and faxed to PCP - follow up with Primary care provider, pulmonologist, and ENT. # Asthma: Preadmission nebulizer treatments were continued. # GERD: She was continued on home regimen of Loratadine 10mg QD, omperazole 40mg QD, ranitidine 150mg QD. # OCP: She received her oral contraceptive of levonorgestrel 1.5mg and ___ 1mg/20mcg as per her home medications. Transitional Issues: - vancomycin q12h to be completed on ___ - vanc trough to be drawn ___ and faxed to PCP ___ on ___: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Albuterol 0.083% Neb Soln 1 NEB IH Q6H:PRN sob, wheezing 2. budesonide 0.5 mg/2 mL inhalation BID 3. ___ Phosphate 5 mL PO Q6H:PRN cough 4. Ipratropium Bromide Neb 1 NEB IH Q8H:PRN SOB 5. Microgestin ___ (21) (norethindrone ___ estradiol) ___ ___ oral Qday 6. omeprazole 40 mg oral Qday 7. Ranitidine 150 mg PO DAILY 8. Sodium Chloride 3% Inhalation Soln 15 mL NEB DAILY 9. Acetaminophen 650 mg PO Q6H:PRN pain/fever 10. Loratadine 10 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with increased sputum production, shortness of breath, and chest pain. Your sputum cultures that were obtained in the emergency room grew a bacteria called MRSA. You were found to have an acute worsening of your tracheitis. You were given IV antibiotics to treat your infection. We discharged you on IV Vancomycin which you will administer to yourself through your port to complete a 14 day course, stopping on ___. It is important that you finish your course of antibiotics as instructed, and that you continue your home nebulizer treatments. In addition, make sure to perform daily tracheostomy cleaning to prevent future infections.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ yo ___ male with h/o ESRD on HD, HTN, HL, and mild CAD who presented with asymptomatic bradycardia and underwent pacemaker placement. # Bradycardia - Patient presented with bradycardia consistent with 2nd degree AV block. His heart rates were in the 30's-40's at this time. He received a ___ single-lead pacemaker. He had difficult access for the pacemaker, and required multiple sticks on the right side. He ended up having it placed via axillary access on the right side, with some bleeding during the procedure. His pacemaker was in the correct place via follow-up CXR and an interrogation the morning after the procedure showed a normally functioning pacemaker. He had one episode of chest pain after an attempt at pacemaker insertion that was associated with no ECG changes and no elevation in cardiac enzymes. This pain was not thought to be cardiac-related. # Hypertension - Patient with persistent hypertension while in the CCU. His BP was highest post-dialysis, as he does not take his medications the morning before dialysis. We increased his dose of clonidine 0.2mg po daily to 0.2mg po bid. We continued his lisinopril, diovan, amlodipine, and HCTZ. His BP stabilized by the time of discharge on these medications, although he would likely benefit from outpatient BP monitoring. # CAD - Patient with history of coronary artery disease (last cath ___ showed 50% lesion of ramus intermedius)who presented with asymptomatic bradycardia. It was felt that his bradycardia was unlikely to be related to current ischemia, and patient's ECG was not suggestive of ischemia or infarct. He was continued on aspirin 81mg po daily and he was started on simvastatin 40mg po daily. # Pump - Last echo in ___ showed mildly depressed systolic function, with LVEF = 55% and inferior/inferolateral hypokinesis. It was felt that his pump would benefit most from optimal blood pressure control. # Chronic Kidney Disease Stage V on HD - patient dialyzed MWF while in house, and has follow-up with ___ clinic. His electrolytes were monitored and repleted, and calcium acetate and nephrocaps were continued. Aspirin 81mg po daily was also continued. # Peripheral Vascular Disease - patient with significant peripheral vascular disease and recently seen by vascular here at ___. He missed an appointment for arterial studies (noninvasive) which he was an inpatient, and it was attempted to get these studies as an inpatient but they were not completed before discharge. The studies were ordered again as an outpatient. Asthma/COPD - Patient with long-standing asthma and COPD with wheezing at baseline. He presented with diffuse wheezing that improved with his home medications and albuterol.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital due to a slow heart rate (bradycardia). You were given a pacemaker ___ VDD) in order to speed up your heart rate. You will need to keep the dressing over the pacer clean and dry for one week until you go to the device clinic. You should wear a sling for 24 hours after your pacemaker placement. You cannot shower but may take a bath as long as the dressing stays dry. You cannot lift your right arm over your head for 6 weeks, no swimming or tennis. No carrying more than 5 pounds for 6 weeks with your right arm. Please see the discharge instructions regarding pacemakers that was given to you at discharge. Please call Dr. ___ you notice any increasing swelling, bruising, bleeding or increasing pain at the pacer site. If the pacer site is sore, you can take Tylenol. Also call Dr. ___ ___ fevers, chills, dizziness, chest pain or trouble breathing. You were started on simvastatin 40mg by mouth daily to help lower your cholesterol and prevent your risk of heart attacks in the future. In addition, your dose of clonidine was doubled because your blood pressure was high during your hospital stay.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old female with a history of pre-diabetes and anxiety presenting with wheeze, as well as cough and back discomfort. # Community-acquired pneumonia, post-nasal drip, and reactive airway disease: ___ year-old female with a history of pre-diabetes and anxiety, who was diagnosed with community acquired pneumonia on ___, and took a 5-day course of azithromycin 250 mg daily from ___. She presented to the ___ emergency department with continued wheeze, cough, post-nasal drip, and back discomfort. Chest x-ray showed a right middle lobe focal opacity in the right middle lobe compatible with pneumonia. Patient's presentation consistent with community-acquired pneumonia and post-nasal drip/reactive airway disease. However, azithromycin treatment failure was also a possibility. During this hospitalization, the patient was treated with Levofloxacin 750 mg PO Daily (last ___, Guaifenesin and Tessalon Perles for cough, Flonase for post-nasal drip, and albuterol and ipratropium nebs. Given concern for reactive airway disease, the patient was treated with a Prednisone burst course (Prednisone 40 mg PO Daily, last ___. At the time of discharge, the patient symptoms were markedly improving. # Back Pain: Likely due to muscle strain from vigorous coughing, or possibly due to pleural irritation from her pneumonia, possibly with an element of referred pain. Pain remained lateral to the spinous or paraspinous region, and patient had no signs or symptoms of cauda equina syndrome or disc herniation. CHRONIC # Anxiety Continued home Fluoxetine 20 mg PO/NG QAM and Lorazepam 0.5-1 mg PO/NG ONCE:PRN Anxiety. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: []CODE STATUS: FULL []Levofloxacin 750 mg orally once daily for 5 days (___) []Prednisone 40 mg orally once daily for 5 days (___) []Patient discharged with Albuterol inhaler 2 puffs every 6 hours as needed for wheezing. []Patient discharged with Benzonatate 100 mg orally three times per day,guaifenesin ___ mL orally every six hours for cough. []Patient discharged with Flonase 2 sprays daily to each nostril []Patient instructed not to use home calcium carbonate until ___, given that it can decrease absorption of oral levofloxacin []Patient will have PCP followup
summary: You were admitted with wheezing, as well as coughing, nasal drip, and back pain. We found that pneumonia was probably causing your wheeze, cough, and nasal drip. Your back pain is likely related to muscle strain from coughing or irritation of your lungs from pneumonia. We gave you levofloxacin to treat your pneumonia. We also gave you DuoNebs to help with your wheezing, benzonatate and guaifenesin to help with your cough, and Flonase nasal spray to help with your nasal congestion. We felt your symptoms are related to your pneumonia and a process called reactive airway disease, similar to asthma, which is secondary to the pneumonia. Your wheeze, cough, and nasal drip improved while you were in the hospital. Please take levofloxacin once daily through ___, and prednisone through ___. As need be, you can also take benzonatate and guaifenesin for cough, Flonase for nasal congestion, and an albuterol inhaler for wheeze.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old male with history of DM, recent neck surgery, history of heroin abuse; now admit after found altered and cyanotic in car likely due to heroin overdose. # Overdose (OD): Admits to heroin, vicodin, and valium use without other abuses. No EtOH, sedatives, OTC meds, other illicits. This is supported by toxicology screen. Some concern initially of APAP OD if possible Vicodin use, however this was negative on tox and not supported by history. Mental status significantly improved on Narcan gtt which was weaned and discontinued on the morning of ___. Patient was seen by social work and was given the contact information for substance abuse rehabilitation programs. # Hyperglycemia. History of DM with poor medicine compliance. The hyperglycemia was likely related to stress response from hypoxia/respiratory acidosis plus poor baseline control. No evidence of ketones, metabolic acidosis. He was managed with insulin sliding scale, with Metformin held on admission. # Leukocytosis. Also likely stress response from acute hypoxia and acidosis. Other possibilities include infection (aspiration pneumonia or CAP but CXR negative; UA clean; no evidence of surgical site infection; denies h/o IVDU), alcoholic hepatitis. This improved dramatically the morning following admission. His vlood cultures turned positive for peptostreptococcus and we felt this was likley due to tooth surgery. HIs cultures cleared on antibiotics. # Respiratory acidosis/hypercarbic respiratory failure. Likely overmedication effect from heroin. Improved on Narcan gtt. # Tachycardia. Sinus with normal ECG otherwise, and improved with minimal IV fluids. This was likely related to above episode. # Wheezes. History of asthma; poor air entry and mild wheezing on exam. He was started on flovent MDI (patient unsure of name of inhaled steroid) and albuterol nebulizers. # Heroin abuse. The patient denied any suicidal ideation and expressed clearly that this was an accidental overdose. He stated that he was amenable to a discussion with social work/addiction specialists.
summary: You were admitted with heroin overdose. You were treated with a medication, narcan, in the intensive care unit to counteract the effects of the overdose. You were found to have bacteria growing in your bloodstream and you were treated with intravenous antibiotics. An MRI of your neck was done to look for a source of infection. A small amount of fluid was seen around the C-7 neck bone, the site of your surgery and this was sampled. This fluid did not show signs of infection. A syphillis test done was negative. A chest xray was done to look for infection in your lungs and this was also negative. We also did an MRI of the rest of your spine which showed no sign of infection. You have degenerative disk disease in your lumbar spine An echocardiogram of your heart was done. It showed no infection on your heart valves but you do have a small hole in your heart called a "Patent Foramen Ovale". It is important for you to let your primary care doctor about this as it places you at higher risk for having a stroke.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old man w/ tobacco dependence who presents with multifocal pneumonia. No known history of pulmonary disease. History of GI symptoms and sick contacts raise the question of legionella, so sent urinare for antigen testing. No history of prior infections, incarceration, or IVDU, no chronic constitutional symptoms, although history of unprotected sex with multiple partners. Sent HIV although suspicion for HIV is low. Extremely low suspicion for TB based on lack of risk factors or consistent clinical presentation. Recent binge drinking may have led to aspiration event w/ GNR PNA. # CAP: Septic on presentation to ED w/ tachycardia and leukocytosis. Both resolved quickly after IVFs and abx. His HRs normalized prior to discharge despite no IVFs x 24h. His constitutional symptoms and dyspnea improved somewhat during the admission, although he continued to have a severe cough and wheezing on exam. Given the multifocal nature of his PNA, he was admitted for observation and close monitoring of his respiratory status. At rest he maintained sats in mid-high ___ on room air. With ambulation his sats were low-mid ___ on room air. He has a very small effusion on CXR but felt to be too small to warrant diagnostic thoracentesis at this time. Given the impressive radiographic picture and mild hypoxia the patient was strongly encouraged to seek close ___ with his primary care physician. Would recheck O2 sats and also plan for repeat imaging in 4 weeks, or sooner if any worsening. While inpatient the patient completed 3 days of ceftriaxone/azithromycin and will complete 5 days of levofloxacin as an outpatient. He was also prescribed PRN benzonotate, codeine-guaifenesin, and albuterol. He was also provided with an incentive spirometer and smoking cessation recommended. ===================================== ___ issues: (1) pending labs at discharge: GC/CT, HIV, legionella urinary Ag (2) recommended PCP ___ within 1 week for ___ check and O2 sat check (3) would repeat CXR in 1 month or sooner if any clinical worsening; could consider diagnostic thoracentesis if effusion any larger (4) consider further asthma work-up and continue to counsel for smoking cessation =====================================
summary: You were admitted to the hospital due to pneumonia. Your chest xray showed that the pneumonia was fairly extensive, which explains the severity of your symptoms. Your symptoms improved somewhat while in the hospital, although you continued to have a severe cough. We are prescribing you an antibiotic course to complete at home - The antibiotic is levofloxacin (Levaquin) and should be taken daily for 5 more days. We are also prescribing two cough medications, codeine-guaifenesin, and benzonotate. Because codeine is a narcotic it is important to exercise safety precautions, such as avoiding driving or other dangerous activities while taking it, and keeping it safely stored. We will also prescribe an albuterol inhaler, which you may find helpful for wheezing or shortness of breath. It will be very important to ___ closely, ideally this week, with your primary care doctor. Because of the extent of your pneumonia you will need a repeat xray checked in about a month. It will also be important to have your primary care office check your oxygen levels to make sure that they are improving.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ year old primarily ___ (also ___ speaking woman with a history of asthma, atrial fibrillation on coumadin, DM, HTN and HLD who presented ___ with dyspnea x 3 days . # Respiratory distress ___ asthma exacerbation: Ms. ___ ___ presented with respiratory distress. She was found to be tachypneic with increased work of breathing and diffuse wheezing and thus was placed on BiPAP temporarily in the ED and admitted to the MICU. Labs were notable for a BNP of 2800. She was given nebulizers and was diuresed with 20mg IV lasix x1. She was transferred to the floor on ___, where she was found to be still tachypneic to ~36 breaths/min with diffuse wheezing. Her presentation was felt to be most consistent with an asthma exacerbation. She was started on prednisone 40mg and continued on azithromycin (ceftriaxone was d/c'ed) and standing ipratropium/albuterol nebulizers. She improved over the next few days and on discharge was breathing comfortably without wheezing. Dr. ___ pulmonologist, via email communication indicated that she may have trouble using her inhalers, so she was set up with home albuterol nebulizers. She will continue to take them standing every 6 hours for 2 days and then PRN afterwards. She completed a 5d course of azithro during her admission and has one dose of a 5d burst of prednisone (last dose on ___. Of note, pt had a TTE done during her ICU stay which revealed at least moderate pulmonary HTN and RV hypertrophy and dilatation. One might consider an outpatient V/Q scan given pt h/o PE and the possibility of small recurrent VTE as a cause of these TTE findings. . # Atrial fibrillation: Patient has a history of atrial fibrillation. Coumadin was continued. However, she was found to be subtherapeutic on presentation, so received an extra 2.5mg on ___ and ___ for a total of 5mg. Her most recent INR was 1.9 on ___. This should continue to be monitored as an outpatient with appropriate warfarin titration. She had occasional tachycardia to 170s with ambulation. Rate control can be considered at outpatient follow-up. Either a Beta-1 selective beta blocker (e.g. metoprolol) or diazepam would be appropriate in the context of her asthma. . # Deconditioning: She was found to be quite deconditioned on evaluation by physical therapy. She will receive home physical therapy on discharge. . >>Chronic problems # DMII: Metformin was held in the context of her kidney disease and acute respiratory illness. Insulin sliding scale was used to control her sugars. Metformin was restarted on discharge as Cr close to baseline. . # HTN: Valsartan and HCTZ were held during the hospitalization. Valsartan was restarted on discharge. HCTZ was held at discharge due to occasional low SBPs to 100s. HCTZ can be restarted at follow-up if appropriate. . = = = = = = = = ================================================================ TRANSTIONAL ISSUES SUMMARY # Home med changes: - Given home albuterol nebulizers - Prednisone 40mg (last dose ___ - Holding HCTZ for now. Can restart at follow-up if needed given some recorded SBPs in 100s off HCTZ and ___ during admission. - Held Valsartan during hospitalization. Restarted on discharge. # Studies: Can consider V/Q scan after resolution of her acute respiratory distress given pulmonary HTN and RV changes noted on her echo # INR: Please have her INR checked at her ___ appointment and adjust warfarin dose as needed # Afib: please consider rate control with a beta-1 specific beta blocker such as metoprolol vs. diltiazem if needed # Emergency contact: ___ h ___ c ___ # Code: Full = = = = = = = = ================================================================
summary: You were admitted for difficulty breathing due to exacerbation of your asthma. You initially required monitoring in the intensive care unit. You were given medications to help decrease the inflammation in your lungs and help your breathe. You will continue some of these breathing medications at home. It is very important for you to take your FLOVENT inhaler twice a day, everday. We have also set you up with nebulizers at home. These are breathing treatments that you can use when you feel short of breath. You should use a nebulizer every 6 hours for the first 2 days. You can use additional nebs every 2 hours if needed. After 2 days if your breathing feels good, you only need to use the nebulizer if you feel short of breath or are wheezing. You should take 1 dose (2 pills) of prednisone tomorrow (___) to finish your course for your asthma treatment.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old female with past medical history alcoholic and HCV cirrhosis complicated by pancytopenia, encephalopathy, portal hypertension with ascites, and varices, OSA not on CPAP, atrial fibrillation not on anticoagulation, asthma and tracheobronchomalacia admitted ___ with asthma exacerbation, bright red blood per rectum thought to be hemorrhoidal bleeding, course complicated by alcohol withdrawal, alcoholic hepatitis, positive blood culture, leaving against medical advice. # Discharge against medical advice As below, patient left against medical advice, despite counseling otherwise. She was able to Patient verbalized her understanding of the risks including worsening infection, liver failure and death. Discussed with patient's HCP/sister and patient's PCP, neither of whom were able to convince patient to stay in the hospital. Discharged home with prescriptions for prednisone, azithromycin, thiamine, folate. # GI Bleed suspected to be secondary to hemorrhoids Patient presented with several days of bright red blood per rectum. History became more clear as hospitalization progressed--patient reported initial onset of watery diarrhea, followed by bright red blood per rectum. Hgb similar to prior baseline. Given history of varices and cirrhosis (as below) patient was seen by hepatology consult service who agreed history consistent with hemorrhoidal bleed. During hospital stay, patient noted to have small amount of blood most noticeably on wiping. Hgb remained stable, was 8.8 at discharge. # Moderate Persistent Asthma with Acute Exacerbation Presented with several days of rhinorrhea, sore throat and cough. CXR without signs of consolidation. Flu negative. Trigger thought to be recent viral URI. Initially with wheezing, rapidly improving with bronchodilators, steroids and azitrhomycin. Continued home Flonase, loratadine, symbicort, tiotropium. Peak flow prior to discharge was 310. # Diarrhea Patient reported watery stool ___ times per day x several days. Cdiff PCR positive, toxin negative, not felt to represent infection. In setting of above symptoms, thought to be viral in etiology. Treated supportively and symptoms improved. # Alcoholic Hepatitis # Alcoholic Cirrhosis # Alcohol abuse Patient with history of cirrhosis complicated by pancytopenia, encephalopathy, portal hypertension with ascites, and varices, who presented in the setting of a recent alcohol binge with elevated LFTs. Hepatology saw patient, recommended against additional treatment given DF 28. On hospital day 3, DF increased to 32.5. Patient left against medical advice before any additional management could be pursued. Continued rifAXIMin. Provided prescription for thiamine and folate. # Alcohol Withdrawal Patient presented with alcohol level 200s. On hospital day 2 developed increasing tremor and anxiety, with CIWA scores > 10. She was started on PO Ativan. She left against medical advice before withdrawal symptoms resolved. # GPC Sepsis At 36 hours, admission blood culture grew GPCs. Started empiric IV vancomycin. Patient left against medical advice before additional information was available. As above, she was able to verbalize understanding of her diagnosis and its potential life threatening complications. # Chronic Iron deficiency anemia Continued iron # Lower back pain Continued Gabapentin # Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation Continued ASA #OSA Not adherent to CPAP, so was monitored with nocturnal O2 sat monitoring > 30 minutes spent on discharge
summary: You were admitted with an exacerbation of your asthma and blood in your stool. You were seen by GI specialists who thought the bleeding was likely from hemorrhoids. We also found that you had worsening liver failure--we think this is due to alcohol. You also had signs of alcohol withdrawal. You also had a blood test that was concerning that you might have an infection in your blood stream. Before we could further treat you, you opted to leave against medical advice. You were able to verbalize your understanding of your life threatening condition, and that you might die without further treatment You are now leaving the hospital against medical advice.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ was admitted to the hospital with a non-purulent right hand cellulitis that developed while the patient was on doxycycline (for a community acquired pneumonia). X-rays ruled out a fracture. Hand surgery evaluated him and did not feel that any of his joints were involved. He had no signs of systemic illness. Blood cultures never grew any bacteria. He received 48 hrs of IV vancomycin with improvement in his hand swelling and erythema. He was transitioned to oral keflex (low suspicion for community acquired MRSA given evolution of infection on doxycycline) to complete a 10 day course (through ___. SUBACUTE ISSUES: #Cough, bronchospasm: history of seasonal asthma and treated for CAP as outpatient with doxy. had 2 days left in his course. CXR here without evidence of PNA. Dc'd his doxy and gave him albuterol inhaler prn. #SI: Initially endorsed in ___ office. None after ED. Briefly required 1:1 sitter supevision. # Substance abuse: Continued home suboxone and clonidine. #GERD: Continued omeprazole. # Chronic pain: gabapentin continued. #HCV: genotype 1b. Untreated as of yet. Outpatient providers aware. Transitional Issues: -PCP ___ on ___ -Keflex through ___ -___ at ___ ___ for ongoing suboxone prescription -discharging with presciption for clonazepam, only dispensing enough pills to get to PCP appointment on ___ -If hand cellulitis does not continue to improve, may follow-up in hand clinic, to be coordinated by PCP ___ on ___: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. BuPROPion (Sustained Release) 150 mg PO BID 2. CloniDINE 0.3 mg PO BID 3. Omeprazole 40 mg PO DAILY 4. Gabapentin 800 mg PO QID 5. Buprenorphine-Naloxone (8mg-2mg) 1 TAB SL BID 6. ClonazePAM 1 mg PO TID
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with an infection of your R hand. X rays ruled out a fracture. The hand surgeons evaluated you and felt that none of the joints in your hand needed to be drained. You were treated with 48 hours of IV antibiotics, after which time you were transitioned to oral antibiotics. You will continue taking this for a total of 10 days of antibiotics. Our psychiatrists evaluated you due to concern that you were feeling suicidal on admission. The next day, they felt that you were not a danger to yourself and would be able to go home. We also re-established care with you at your ___ clinic, so you can continue to receive treatment to help you stop using heroin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ yo obese woman with h/o asthma now admitted for LLE cellulitis. . Active Issues: . # Cellulitis: Patient was failing outpatient treatment on oral Levaquin. Patient is MRSA +. During admission patient's cellulitis was treated with Vancomycin 1gm initially and then increased to 1.5gm given patient's morbid obesity. Cellulitis improved slowly over admission. Lower extremity neurovascular exam was intact bilaterally. She never exhibited signs of systemic infection (no fevers, blood cultures NGTD). WBC on admission was 11.2, but was WNL thereafter. Pain was controlled with tylenol. Patient can be transitioned to an oral antibiotics that covers MRSA (Clindamycin). Was on Vancomycin for 3 days in house, give a dose of clindamycin prior to discharge, and discharged with 7 day course of clindamycin, for total antibiotic course of 10 days. . # Mild CHF per CXR: Patient experiencing episodes of SOB, likely related to her asthma and relieved by her albuterol inhaler, however CXR during admission showed cardiomegaly and engorged pulmonary vasculature. Patient's home lasix of 40mg PO was administered as 40mg IV during admission and creatinine and electrolytes were monitored. Recommend outpatient echocardiogram to assess heart function. . Chronic Issues: . # Venous stasis ulcers on RLE: These ulcers are a chronic issue for the patient. On admission, patient had a wound care consult and ulcers were kept clean and dressed regularly. . # Asthma: Patient with occasional episodes of wheezing, relieved with home flovent and albuterol inhalers. . # Elevated bicarbonate: Patient has morbid obesity and an elevated bicarb (33) on labs. Given her morbid obesity, she likely has some obstructive sleep apnea and CO2 retention at baseline. Patient should be worked up for OSA as an outpatient with her PCP. . # Depression: Continued home Fluoxetine 60mg daily. . # Overactive bladder: Continued home oxybutynin 5mg daily. . Transitional Issues: Patient is reliable, regularly sees PCP, and has a follow up appointment soon after discharge (___). Patient was informed of warning signs to watch out for, which would require her to seek medical attention. No foreseable issues.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for a cellulitis of your left lower leg that had not been improving on outpatient oral antibiotics (Levaquin). Throughout your admission, you were treated with intravenous Vancomycin, an antibiotic that treats cellulitis very well. Your pain decreased, your symptoms improved, and the area of cellulitis decreased. You never exhibited any systemic signs of infection (no fevers and no bacteria ever grew in your blood) and your physical exam reassured us that your infection was superficial. You also had some shortness of breath during your admission which was treated with your home asthma medications and an occasional albuterol nebulizer. On chest xray, your lungs seems to have some fluid in them. We increased your home lasix dose. We recommend that you have an outpatient echocardiogram to further assess your heart function. You should speak to your primary care doctor about this.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ man with h/o asthma, depression who presented with one-day history of increasing left-sided neck pain, urinary incontinence, left-sided upper and lower extremity weakness, and left-sided facial, upper and lower extremity numbness after a motor vehicle accident. ACUTE ISSUES # Left sided weakness/Numbness # Left sided pain/muscle spasm: Patient had left sided weakness/numbness after motor vehicle accident on ___, was evaluated at ___ with normal ___ and shoulder imaging and discharged home. He then presented to the ___ ED with pain, weakness and numbness in his left arm and leg, as well as left sided neck spasm. Given involvement of upper extremity and face, upper cervical/brainstem/cortical lesion was most likely. However, MRI/MRA of the brain and MR of the cervical spine in the ED did not show any acute neurologic injury. Pt was seen by neurology who reviewed imaging and agreed that there was ___ clear cause for his sxs and presentation could be consistent with worsening radiculopathy following MVA. Numbness, pain and weakness improved throughout the course of the hospitalization with pain medication and anti-spasmotics and he was discharged to rehab. At time of discharge, he had ___ strength in all his extremities. # Urinary incontinence: Patient reported urinary incontinence at home, as well as an episode of incontinence in the ED and another while inpatient. Bladder scan showed accumulation of urine to 625 cc. Patient was straight-cathed x2. He had ___ MRI evidence to suggest central neurologic process for cause of symptoms. Patient's urinary retention may be due to opioids he received while in the hospital. By the end of his hospital stay patient was able to void independently. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Depression: Patient was continued on home Zoloft 100mg qdaily. # Psychosocial stressors: Patient is currently homeless. Furthermore, his wife and children suffered multiple injuries in MVA. A social worker met with the patient to offer resources and contacts regarding disability and housing. ***EXPECTED LOS AT REHAB IS <30 DAYS*** CODE: Full confirmed CONTACT: ___ (mother, ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Sertraline 100 mg PO DAILY 2. Albuterol Inhaler ___ PUFF IH Q6H:PRN SOB/wheezing
summary: You were admitted with weakness, numbness and pain on the left side of your body, as well as urinary incontinence, after being in a motor vehicle crash on ___. A full clinical neurological exam and imaging of your brain and spine, did not show any evidence of acute trauma. You had painful muscle spasms that we treated with several medications to decrease pain and relax the muscles. You had urinary incontinence so a catheter was inserted into your bladder to drain the urine. The catheter was removed on ___. You had one further episode of incontinence and a catheter had to be inserted twice to drain urine from your bladder. Your urinary incontinence did not appear to be due to neurologic damage. It may have been a side-effect of the opioids you received for pain. You were able to urinate on your own by the end of your hospitalization.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with HTN, hypothyroidism, pre-eclampsia, maternal diabetes presenting with chest pain, diffuse ST elevations suggestive of myopericarditis vs acute coronary syndrome. # STEMI. On presentation to the ___, patient was complaining of chest pain that sounded pleuritic in nature. An EKG showed NSR 103 bpm, normal axis, normal intervals, ST elevations 1mm II and 2mm III aVF and 1mm V2-V4, <1mm ST depressions I aVL, and trops were elevated to 0.65. Cardiology was consulted in the ___, and felt that pain was most likely secondary to myopericarditis, rather than a STEMI given that the ST elevations appeared to be diffuse and the quality of the chest pain was described as pleuritic. However, a heparin drip was started overnight. The following day there was increasing concern for STEMI, and the patient underwent a cardiac catheterization on ___, which showed a 90% blockage of mid-RCA. Two stents placed, with resolution of chest pain. The patient was started on aspirin for life, Plavix for 6 months, metoprolol, atorvastatin 80mg daily, and her lisinopril was increased to 10mg daily. She also had a social work consult for a new diagnosis and to discuss resources available to patient. At time of discharge, patient was chest pain free and trop 0.6. #Diabetes - On admission, the patient was noted to have blood glucose of 378 and an A1c of 10.5%. The patient was started on an insulin sliding scale. ___ was consulted for further recommendations, and ___ educator worked with the patient on initial diabetes teaching. The patient was discharged with a regimen of standing lantus 15u at bedtime, and a Humalog sliding scale. #HTN. The patient's lisinopril was increased from 5mg to 10mg daily. #Hypothyroidism. The patient was found to have an elevated TSH of 36. She was continued on her home levothyroxine in the hospital, with plans for endocrinology follow up as an outpatient. #Hypertriglyceridemia - Patient noted to have triglyceride level of 796 on admission. Patient started on atorvastatin 80mg daily, and will followup as an outpatient for her elevated tryglycerides. # Asthma. The patient's albuterol inhaler was stopped to avoid beta agonism in the setting of a STEMI. She was discharged with a prescription for ipratropium inhaler. ====================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ====================== - The patient was started on a daily aspirin, which she will need to take for life. - The patient was started on clopidogrel, which she will need to take for ___ year. - The patient was started on metoprolol and atorvastatin 80mg for cardiac protection. - The patient was found to be persistently hypertensive during this hospitalization, and her lisinopril was increased to 10mg daily. - The patient's metoprolol and lisinopril should be uptitrated as tolerated by her heart rate and blood pressure. - The patient was found to have a HgA1c of 10.5%, which represents a new diagnosis of diabetes. She was seen by the diabetes specialists in the hospital, and discharged on an insulin regimen and metformin. - Albuterol was stopped to avoid beta-agonistic effects following an MI. She was discharged with an ipratropium inhaler, and this issue should be addressed by her cardiologist and PCP. - The patient is a smoker, and has been advised to quit to protect her heart. - TSH elevated (36), T4 pending at time of discharge; will need to be followed up by PCP ___ ___ weeks to evaluate for under treated hypothyroidism. - Due to high dose of levothyroxine patient reports to be taking there is some concern for celiac disease (which can disrupt levothyroxine absorption). PCP can evaluate for celiac disease. - The patient will need close cardiology follow up. #Code: FULL #Contact: mother ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Lisinopril 5 mg PO DAILY 2. Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q4H:PRN wheezing 3. Levothyroxine Sodium 300 mcg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted because of your chest pain, and we were concerned that you may be having a heart attack. We did a procedure to look at the blood vessels that bring blood to your heart, and found that there was a large blockage of one of them, and that you were having a heart attack. We placed a stent to open up this blockage, and you felt better. There are some new medications that you will need to take when you leave the hospital. It is very important that you take them, or you can have another heart attack. First, you should take aspirin and plavix (clopidogrel) every day to help stop your blood from clotting in the stents. To help protect your heart you will also need to take metoprolol and atorvastatin. Your lisinopril dose was increased because you had high blood pressure while in the hospital. It is really important that you stop smoking in order to lower the risk of having another heart attack.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx of alzheimer's dementia, prior MIs, CVA ___, HTN, HL presenting with SOB. . #SOB: Pt was sent to ED due to concerns from nurses at ___ for labored breathing and wheezing. Pt did not have known hx of asthma, COPD or smoking. Also did not have hx of DVT or PE. Per son, episodes of SOB/wheezing have been occurring occasionally for ~1 month. At ED, she had received 1 dose of levofloxacin and cefepime for possible health-care associated PNA but as she did not have cough, fever, or leukocytosis, these were not continued. CXR was of poor quality but did not show clear consolidation or edema. On exam, pt had audible wheezing and RR in ___, worse when approached by medical staff. She reported feeling "nervous" and was given 0.25mg po haldol x 2 for possible anxiety-induced tachypnea. She was initially kept on 4L oxygen by NC with O2 consistently in high ___. She was weaned off and remained in the high ___ on room air. Wheezing improved with ipratropium/albuterol nebs, short course of prednisone, and z-pack. She was discharged to nursing home with instructions to complete a course of prednisone 40mg and azithromycin 250mg (5 days total). Wheezing was likely due to self-limited reactive airway disease triggered by a viral infection. Of note, blood cultures were still pending on discharge. . #CAD: Pt with hx of multiple MIs. EKG on admission showed old inferior infarct and 1st degree AV block. She complained about chest pain on ___. She reported pain relief with 1 SL nitroglycerin tab. EKG was unchanged from admission. Troponin was less than 0.01. She was continued on her statin during hospital admission and monitored on telemetry without any significant events. Aspirin was discontinued b/c she was already on aggrenox. . #Hx of CVA: Pt with hx of CVA ___. She was continued on aggrenox at home dose. On day of originally expected discharge, pt was noted to have difficulty getting up into wheelchair. She seemed to have weakness more pronounced on the left side in upper and lower extremities. She was also less active in general, having to be fed rather than eating herself. CT head w/o contrast was obtained which showed right frontal encephalomalacia and multiple hypodensities likely related to chronic ischemia. Her physical therapist was contacted who reported that pt has always had more weakness on the left side and that she was likely deconditioned from her hospital stay. She was evaluated by physical therapy at the hospital who recommended discharge to rehab given impaired strength and balance. . #HTN: No acute issues. She was continued on triamterene/hctz at home dose . #Alzheimers: No acute issues. On discussion with her family, her mental status was at baseline. She was alert to self and knew names of family members. Was not oriented to place or time, which is her baseline. She was continued on namenda and aricept at home doses . # Chronic renal insufficiency: Cr was elevated to 1.6 on admission. Records were obtained from OSHs where she had received care in the past; most recent Cr recorded was 1.4. By admission, Cr was 1.5.
summary: You were admitted with labored breathing and wheezing. Your chest xray did not reveal a pneumonia. Your symptoms improved with nebulizers, steroids, and antibiotics. You were discharged with two more days of the steroids and antibiotics. Also during your hospital stay you complained of chest pain which self-resolved. Your EKG and blood tests did not indicate that you were having a heart attack and you did not complain of any further pain. Additionally, you were noted to have left sided weakness during your hospital course. We called your physical therapist who confirmed that this was not new. A CT scan of your head showed old changes but nothing acute. You were seen by our physical therapist who agreed that it would be best for you to go to a rehab facility where you would have more medical attention.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with T2DM, CAD s/p ___ MIs and 5 stents, on aspirin and plasugrel, who presented with chest pain for a week. No EKG changes and negative trops, but typical chest pain similar in character to unstable angina from 4 months prior when we was cathed and stented. Underwent LHC showing distal LAD restenosis, underwent balloon angioplasty of the apical LAD after discussion with the patient with intent to consider bypass surgery thereafter. Successful angioplasty with resolution of chest pain, patient seen by surgery who scheduled a CABG for ___, patient receeived pre-surgery w/u (CXR, echo, LFTs) during admission. Holding prasugrel starting 5 days before surgery (___) and starting mupirocin intranasal cream BID for 5 days starting ___. Of note, pt's metoprolol succinate was decreased to 100mg PO QDay given that his HR was largely in the ___'s while pt was on metoprolol tartrate 25mg QID. # Stent restenosis: Underwent LHC on ___ showing critical apical LAD restenosis, successful POBA employed. High likelihood of stent restenosis in near future, patient strongly recommended to have CABG; seen by CT surgery in house who scheduled CABG for ___ patient received full workup in house including echo, CXR, type&cross, LFTs, MRSA nasal swab, and 5 day course of mupirocin started. Patient stable on discharge with no chest pain, with close PCP and cardiology followup. # CAD: s/p ___ MIs and 5 stents, most recent 4 months ago at ___, and now s/p POBA of LAD restenosis on ___. Continued prasugrel (hold 5 days before CABG). Continued ASA 81, atorvastatin 80mg, Ranexa 500mg BID, isosorbide dinitrate PRN chest pain. # Hypertension: Held home losartan initially, continued on d/c. Fractionating home metoprolol 200mg QD to metoprolol 25mg QID, on discharge metoprolol succinate was decreased to 100mg PO QDay given that his HR was largely in the ___ while pt was on metoprolol tartrate 25mg QID. #T2DM: Hold metformin during admission, SSI. #Asthma: Continued albuterol inhaler PRN wheezing. #GERD: Continued pantoprazole.
