Transitional issues =================== [] Metoprolol succinate to 100mg PO QDay at discharge. [] CABG scheduled on ___, will be NPO the night before, managed by CT surgery who will give chlorhexidine scrubs, etc. [] Holding prasugrel 5 days prior to CABG (starting ___ [] Starting mupirocin intranasal cream BID for 5 days starting ___. [] Patient to follow up with PCP on ___ at 9:15AM [] f/u with cardiology on ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of chest pain for a week. The cardiologists did a cardiac catheterization and found that the stent you had placed four months ago was blocked off. They used a balloon to open up the stent and improve blood flow, relieving your chest pain. However, the cardiologists feel sure that the stent will block of again, so you will need a surgery to bypass the blocked area. You were seen by the cardiothoracic surgeons in the hospital, who determined that you needed a bypass surgery on ___. Please STOP taking your prasugrel in preparation for the surgery. Please continue using your mupirocin ointment through ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with DM1, HTN, asthma, nonrelated living renal transplant in ___ on cellcept and sirolimus presenting with influenza-like illness. 1. Influenza- Found to have Influenza A on nasopharyngeal swab, likely H1N1 although final results still pending at the time of discharge. As the patient presented less than 48 hours after the onset of symptoms and was at high risk for complications, started on os___ 75mg BID x 5 days. Also continued supportive care with tylenol prn fever. Given that the patient was immunocompromised with a chronic respiratory illness, a high index of suspicion was maintained for complications such as an asthma exacerbation or bacterial superinfection (see below). By the time of discharge, the patient had been afebrile for > 24 hours and his symptoms had markedly improved. 2. Community acquired pneumonia: On ___, the patient was still intermittently febrile to 101, complained of new left sided pleuritic pain and worsening cough. CXR revealed a new left lower lung infiltrate, consistent with a diagnosis of pneumonia. Patient started on a seven day course of levofloxacin. 3. Asthma: Presented with symptoms of mild dyspnea and wheezing on exam, consistent with diagnosis of asthma exacerbation. Throughout hospital stay, the patient was maintained on his home medications of advair with albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers prn wheezing. Peak flow was monitored daily and always remained above 400, so patient was not felt to benefit from systemic corticosteriods. At the time of discharge, the patient was breathing comfortably on room air and only requiring nebs twice every ___ hours. 4. DM type 1: stable, continued on outpatient regimen of lantus and preparandial insulin with sliding scale inulin to correct for any hyperglycemia. Blood glucose remained at/ near goal throughout hospitalization with preparandial glucose ranging from 80 - 150 mg/dL. 5. s/p renal transplant in ___: Patient continued on regimen of cellcept and sirolimus (level measured within therapeutic range). Also continued on bactrim for primary prophylaxis of PCP in setting of immunosuppression. Renal function was trended daily and remained at baseline of 1.1. creatinine increased to 1.3 from baseline of 1.1. 6. HTN: Throughout hospital stay, patient's blood pressure remained slightly elevated with SBP in 130- 160s. Continued on home medications of amlodipine +/ - ramipril (enrolled in sirolimus/ ramapril study). Further optimization of blood pressure was deferred to the outpatient setting, once the patient was not acutely ill.
summary: You came to the hospital for fevers, body aches, headache, and cough. You were given fluids and Tylenol/Oxycodone for your pain. Blood cultures and labs were sent, which came back normal. You were swabbed for a flu test, which came back positive for influenza A. You were treated with Tamiflu while you were in the hospital and you will need to continue for a total duration of 5 days. While you were in the hospital you also developed evidence of a pneumonia as evidenced by a repeat chest X-ray and increased cough with sputum. We started you on levofloxacin, an antibiotic that you will need to take for a total of 7 days. Make sure to drink plenty of fluids, get plenty of rest, eat healthy foods, wash your hands frequently and stay home to avoid spreading the flu to other people (for at least 48 hours after the resolution of your fever).

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with diffuse scleroderma (on MMF) and interstitial lung disease (TLC 85% pred, DLCO 81% pred) with ___ years of chronic cough who presents with 9 days of increased cough, occasional dyspnea, subjective fevers, sore throat and myalgias. Chest x-ray with stable interstitial changes and no new consolidation. Nasopharyngeal swab showed parainfluenza. She also had significant wheezing on exam, consistent with virus-induced asthma. She was initially treated with duonebs and oral prednisone. She did not tolerate the nebulizers due to throat irritation, but she responded well to prednisone with complete resolution of wheezing and significant symptomatic improvement. She completed a 5-day course of azithromycin started at a previous ED visit for atypical pneumonia. Finally, we reduced her atenolol to 25mg once a day (from BID) due to concern for asymptomatic bradycardia to low ___ and possible contribution to reactive airway disease. We recommend considering replacing atenolol with an alternative non-beta blocker antihypertensive. #Parainfluenza Lower Respiratory Infection: Parainfluenza positive by nasopharyngeal swab. Cough treated symptomatically with guaifenesin with codeine and benzonatate. Completed azithromycin course prior to discharge. #Reactive airway disease: Although she has not had a methylcholine challenge or bronchodilator test, she has some coving on her PFTs and air trapping on CT that her pulmonologist has suggested is likely due to asthma physiology. Significant wheezing on initial evaluation suggestive of an acute exacerbation likely triggered by viral bronchitis. Initially trialed with ipratropium/albuterol nebulizers but they were discontinued due to upper airway irritation. Complete resolution of wheezing with prednisone. Discharged with prednisone 40mg daily x5 days (ending ___. CHRONIC #Anxiety: Treated with clonazepam for anxiety-related insomnia as home med. Worse in hospital setting. Controlled with home clonazepam QHS. #Nausea: has some mild nausea at baseline of unclear etiology, although possibly related to scleroderma-related esophageal disease that is being follow by outpatient GI. Controlled with home ondansetron PRN. #Diffuse Scleroderma w/ interstitial lung disease: Diagnosis 9 months ago. On CellCept (brand name) for past 6 months. No prior serious infections on CellCept. Stable, continued home medication. #HFpEF: mild diastolic dysfunction per cardiology notes; no history of acute decompensated HFpEF. No signs of volume overload on physical exam or imaging. Continue home furosemide. #Hypertension: Blood pressures were stable. Continued home losartan but reduced atenolol dose to 25mg daily given reactive airway disease and asymptomatic bradycardia to ___. #CV Risk/Hyperlipidemia: continued home rosuvastatin #Normocytic Anemia: Hgb 10.6. Baseline range 10.7 to 12.0. Like anemia of chronic disease. #GERD: presumed esophageal dysmotility secondary to scleroderma, although not confirmed by motility study. Stable, continued home esomeprazole #Hypothryoidism: last TSH: 1.5 ___, continued home levothyroxine #Hypoparathyroidism: Ca: 9.2. continued home calcium, vitamin D #CKD Stage ___: Cr 0.9. eGFR 61. Within baseline range. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - sent with 5 days of prednisone 40mg to treat inflammation - consider formal methalcholine challenge or testing for reactive airway disease - Needs: Tdap, influenza vaccine, PCV 13 (PPSV-23 in ___ - continued home atenolol, but reduced dose to 25mg daily given reactive airway disease and bradycardia
summary: You came to the hospital because of cough, shortness of breath and fever. You were found to have a respiratory infection with the parainfluenza virus. This is a virus that your immune system will eliminate by itself without specific treatment. This virus also caused an asthma-like syndrome which made you wheeze and have trouble breathing. We treated this asthma-like syndrome with nebulizers and prednisone. We also gave you medication to control your cough, including guaifenesin with codeine and bezonatate. While you were here, you also completed your course of the antibiotic, azithromycin, which you were given in the emergency department just in case you had a bacterial pneumonia (which you did not have). Finally, we believe that your hypertension medication, atenolol, was causing your heart rate to be low and may have been worsening your asthma-like syndrome. We have reduced your dose to once-a-day and we have suggested that your primary care physician stop this medication and replace it with a different medication to treat your blood pressure. Please continue taking prednisone 40mg daily until ___. You may stop sooner if your symptoms resolve before then. Please take Mytussin (guaifenesin/codeine) and Tessalon Perles (benzonatate) only when you have a bad cough. These will not affect your recovery. Please take atenolol 25mg only once a day until you see Dr. ___ discuss changing to a different antihypertensive.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is a ___ ___ w PMH of coronary artery disease s/p DES to distal RCA and proximal ___, PVD, DM2, HLD, HTN, chronic back pain, and anxiety disorders now admitted for diagnostic cath after exeriencing severe chest and L arm pain and ST depressions during her exercise stress test today. ACTIVE ISSUES: #atypical chest pain vs angina: Pt reports having had symptoms for ~ 9 months. During her cardiac stress test today, she experienced her typical ___ chest pain and had ECG changes showing 1.5-2 mm horizontal ST segment depression in leads V3-6, and at 3 minutes of recovery, there was 1.5-2 mm horizontal/downsloping ST segment depression in the inferolateral leads which resolved by 24 minutes of recovery. This is consistent with the ___ prior cardiac cath in ___ during which she was noted to have 80% proximal OM1 stenosis and RCA with a focal 70% stenotic region into which drug eluting stents were placed. She states that the caths never fully alleviated her chest pain. Her troponins are currently negative at < 0.01, no significant ECG changes. We optimized medically by continuing her home meds including amlodipine, clopidogrel, hydrochlorothiazide, losartan, and metoprolol, and increased aspirin to 325mg. We trended her troponins, which were all negative. Pt also remained asymptomatic. Her cardiac cath did not show any stenoses - her RCA and OM1 stents were widely patent. Currently, the cause of her chest pain and positive stress test remains unclear. Coronary vasospasm is a possibility, although there was no evidence of this during her cardiac cath. Also, the ___ chest pain is rather atypical and may represent cervical radiculopathy, or brachial plexus or other primary neurological source. . # CORONARIES: known 80% proximal OM1 stenosis and RCA with a focal 70% stenotic region s/p drug eluting stents placed in ___. We optimized medically with rosuvastatin 40 mg po daily, aspirin 325 mg po daily, clopidogrel 75 mg po daily, and metoprolol 50 mg po bid. Since she did not have any signs of coronary stenoses, she was discharged on her prior dose of aspirin 81 mg po daily. . # PUMP: ___ Per ___ echo, LVEF > 55%. No clinical signs or symptoms of CHF. Per PCP records, ___ SBP recently have been 140s. Per her new stress Echo, Pt has normal global and regional LV systolic function w/ LVEF > 55%. . #-Hypertension: Pt hypertensive to 170/80s on admission due to holding meds prior to stress test today. After restarting her meds including amlodipine 10 mg po daily, hydrochlorothiazide 50 mg po daily, losartan 100 mg po daily, and metoprolol 50 mg po bid, her SBPs were stably in 110-140s. . CHRONIC ISSUES: # Asthma: stable on outpatient meds including albuterol inhaler prn wheezing, and fluticasone-salmeterol 1 puff bid. . #Hyperlipidemia: continuing home rosuvastatin 40 mg po daily . #Anxiety- stable. continuing home med diazepam 5 mg po bid PRN back pain and anxiety . #Peripheral vascular disease (status post bilateral femoropopliteal bypass). Stable. . #Chronic pain: Pain management with home regimen including oxycodone-acetaminophen, trazodone, diazepam #GERD. stable, continuing ranitidine 150mg po bid. . #Diabetes mellitus, insulin dependent: continued on home scheduled and sliding scale insulin dosing.
summary: You were sent to the hospital because your cardiac stress test was concerning for a blockaged in your heart. Once you were in the hospital, blood tests indicated that you were not having a heart attack. You were then treated with your normal medications, and you were taken to the cardiac catheterization lab. The blood vessels of your heart were examined, but there was no evidence of any signficiant blockage or narrowing of any of your heart blood vessels. The two stents that were previously placed in your heart in ___ are functioning well. Your heart appears to be working well and coronary artery disease is probably not the cause of your repeated chest pain. We have not made any changes to your medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH HTN, HLD, DM presenting with transient positional vertigo, nausea, and vomiting, resolved with ondansetron and meclizine. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Labrynthitis: The patient initially presented to the ED on ___ with one day of vertigo. On presentation a code stroke was called for vertigo with associated nausea/vomiting. CT head was negative for acute intracranial process. She was seen by neurology who noted nystagmus to left gaze. No evidence of basilar insufficiency/occlusion on CTA head/neck and positive ___ test. Neurology felt her symptoms to be most consistent with BPPV. She was given meclizine and discharged home. On ___ she re-presented with nausea and persistent dizziness. She has continued to vomit multiple times at home and found it difficult to stand, so she was admitted to medicine for symptomatic treatment. Labs were stable with Cre: 0.9. The patient was continued on meclizine and her nausea was controlled with ondansetron. The patient was given 1L of IVF and her symptoms improved. Orthostatics were negative. Her presentation was notable for sore throat, ear fullness, submandibular lymphadenopathy and mild expiratory wheezing. The patient was thought to have viral URI, which would make labrynthitis more likely her cause for vertigo. She was taught different manuevers by physical therapy and discharged home with ondansetron and meclizine. # Asthma: On exam noted to have decreased air movement with mild expiratory wheezes. She denied shortness of breath, but reported that she was using her inhalers more frequently at home. Likely triggered by viral URI as above. Her symptoms were controlled by home albuterol and her sats remained stable on room air. CHRONIC ISSUES: # DM2: At home on metformin 500mg po BID. Metformin was held while inpatient, and her fingerstick sugars were well controlled on insulin sliding scale. Metformin was resumed on discharge. # Hypertension: Pt with history of hypertension on HCTZ and lisinopril. Blood pressures stable on home regimen ranging from 100-130s systolic. # Degenerative disc disease of cervical spine: Stable during admission and given acetaminophen as needed for pain. ***TRANSITIONAL ISSUES*** #Pt may benefit from vestibular therapist or ENT evaluation for persistent ear fullness #Discharged with meclizine and ondansetron with PCP ___ #FYI: CTA H/N demonstrated two pulmonary nodules within the right lung apex which are unchanged from ___. #Code: FULL #Contact: ___ ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ for dizziness called vertigo. This is likely caused from a viral infection causing inflammation of your inner ear which can cause dizziness. A CT scan of the vessels in your brain showed that it was not related to a stroke. You were started on a medication called meclizine and given IV fluids with improvement of your symptoms. Your nausea was treated with medications and you were able to tolerate a regular diet prior to discharge. Physical therapy worked with you on different manuevers that can help with dizziness. Please continue to take meclizine as prescribed for dizziness. You should also take ondansetron as needed for nausea.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Shortness of Breath/Pneumonia, Comm Acquired: Afebrile, no cough, no leukocytosis, but asymmetric RLL opacity on CXR concerning for possible PNA. We continued levofloxacin 750mg PO Q48hrs x 5 days (last day ___ and continued duonebs PRN. Patient's breathing improved and she was able to be weaned from 2L NC to RA. We got an echo which showed critical AS. We informed patient and her son who did not wish to pursue surgical intervention at this time, but we set up a f/u appointment for pt with cardiology to discuss this issue in the future. We started lisinopril 2.5mg QD to help with afterload reduction. Patient was sent to rehab with significantly improved respiratory status. . # Thrombocytopenia: Major concern was HIT, given recent hospitalization with heparin exposure. Per 4Ts, + 2 points for > 50% drop in platelets, + 2 points for 9 days since initially exposed to heparin, 2 points given non-occlusive RUE thrombus (found on screening U/S on this admission), and 1 point for other possible cause (infection). We got a heme consult and because of high suspicion for HIT, and started argatroban. We checked heparin dependent antibodies, however, which returned negative, so pt was taken off argatroban and started on heparin drip. Her platelets improved to 136 the day of dispo. She was then started on lovenox for a bridge to coumadin (goal INR 2.0-3.0). She will remain on coumadin for at least 1 month, likely 3 months, but this will be determined by pt's PCP. . # s/p Percutaneous thranshepatic cholecystostomy tube - had no abdominal pain throughout admission, was afebrile, and had no leukocytosis. We followed abdominal exam, alk phos, bili, all of which remained stable. Pt to see ACS in clinic on ___ for follow-up. . # Atrial Fibrillation: Rate controlled with diltiazem, not on coumadin on admission, but was as an outpatient prior to ___. Per heme consult, we did not restart coumadin while pt on argatroban, but did restart it once her HIT Abs returned negative. She was determined to be a severe fall risk from ___, and we considered not starting her on any anticoagulation for fear of ICH, but she had a documented RUE thrombus and needed anticoagulation for at least 1 month for that issue, so she was restarted on warfarin. She may need to be on warfarin for longer than 1 month, but we recommended that she follow-up with her PCP for this issue. . # Asthma: stable, with some very mild wheezing on exam, much improved by dispo. We continued neb tx and daily advair. . # Critical AS: Suggestive of AS. We got an echo which showed critical AS. We presented the surgical options to the patient and her son (the HCP) and they did not wish to pursue either aortic valve replacement surgery, percutaneous aortic valve replacement or percutaneous valvuloplasty at this time. Low dose ACE-I was started . # DVT, acute: RUE thrombus. Anticoagulation as above. Recommend ___ months Coumadin, goal INR ___ . # Code: DNR/DNI - confirmed with patient and son ___ . # Communication: Patient, son ___ ___
summary: You were seen in the hospital because your percutaneous drainage tube had fallen out. However, once you were here we determined that you have pneumonia and a what was likely a reaction to a medication called heparin. We treated you with antibiotics and a medication called argatroban to prevent complications like blood clots with your heparin allergy. It was found that you did have a blood clot in your right arm. However, our lab results returned that you did not have a heparin allergy, and we were able to transition you back to coumadin as a blood-thinner for a treatment of your right arm blood clot. You will need to be on coumadin for at least 1 month to treat your blood clot, and likely for 3 months, but this will be determined by your fall risk once you leave rehab.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old gentleman with distant 20 pack year smoking, asthma/COPD recently diagnosed with metastatic non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma c/b malignant pleural effusion who underwent thoracoscopy, pleural biopsies, talc pleurodesis, and chest tube and pleurodesis catheter placement ___ admitted for post-procedure monitoring. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= #PLEURAL EFFUSION #NON-SMALL CELL LUNG ADENOCARCINOMA Pleural effusion is due to underlying malignancy, and pleural fluid studies consistent with exudative effusion by 3-test rule. Patient had planned thoracoscopy and pleurodesis, with left-sided chest tube in place to suction initially, subsequently removed on ___. Also had a pleurodesis catheter placed, which remained in place at time of discharge. The patient had ongoing chest wall pain at tube site and at left upper sternal border, which was managed with PO oxycodone, Tylenol, and lidocaine patches. Other respiratory symptoms, including cough and feelings of chest congestion, were managed symptomatically with guaifenesin and benzonatate. Patient had O2 requirement on post-op day 1, felt to be due largely to splinting from his pain. He was given an incentive spirometer to use. He had an episode of respiratory distress on ___ in the afternoon, possibly due to mucous plugging; repeat CXR was unchanged, and his symptoms resolved with a nebulizer treatment. He was ultimately discharged on ___ after being weaned to RA, with pleurodesis catheter in place, to be drained daily at home (___ services arranged to assist patient and family in this process). Patient did desat to 86% with activity/ambulation, however he was asymptomatic and is not on supplemental O2 at home. Decision was made to discharge without home O2, but he should be re-ambulated as an outpatient and have O2 set up for him. #ANXIETY #INSOMNIA Patient reports feeling very anxious about his symptoms, as well as his lack of sleep in the hospital. His anxiety was managed with lorazepam, and his insomnia was managed with ramelteon and trazodone. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #ASTHMA/COPD: Continued home advair (only uses daily), albuterol inhaler. Added standing Duonebs as above, as well as PRN albuterol inhaler, for wheezing, SOB. #GERD: Continued home omeprazole. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ================== [ ] Being discharged with a tunneled pleural catheter in place, and sent home with a Pleurx Catheter Kit. Instructions and information are in the instruction folder of the kit. Patient can call the IP office with any questions, at ___. [ ] Pleurex needs to be drained daily, he has had training here, but is requesting additional assistance [ ] Patient desatted to 86% with activity/ambulation, asymptomatic, not on home O2. ___ benefit from home O2 in the future. [ ] ___ also benefit from pulmonary follow-up or PFTs outpatient. [ ] Patient discharged over weekend and will need PCP ___ #CODE: Full presumed #CONTACT: Name of health care proxy: ___ Relationship: Wife Phone number: ___ This patient was prescribed, or continued on, an opioid pain medication at the time of discharge (please see the attached medication list for details). As part of our safe opioid prescribing process, all patients are provided with an opioid risks and treatment resource education sheet and encouraged to discuss this therapy with their outpatient providers to determine if opioid pain medication is still indicated.
summary: You came in for a planned thoracoscopy/biopsy, and had 2 catheters placed (a chest tube and pleurodesis catheter). You had a procedure called a tunneled pleural catheter placement. A small tube (or catheter) was threaded into your chest to drain fluid. This catheter remained in place at time of discharge. A chest tube was also placed to drain some of the fluid off your lung, but was removed (as planned) by the IP team on ___. After the procedure, you had trouble breathing and needed to be on oxygen. We treated your catheter-related chest pain, as well as your respiratory symptoms, with various medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with h/o HTN and DM who presented with chest pain and was admitted for dizziness. #. Chest Pain: Her chest pain had resolved by the time of arrival to the floor. She was ruled out for MI with cardiac biomarkers. It was felt that her chest pain may be related to her significantly elevated blood pressures. She underwent exercise MIBI stress test which showed no ischemic ECG changes during exercise but borderline changes during recovery; perfusion study was normal. She did not have any symptoms, but did have any episode of pain radiating down the back of her neck which she has had multiple times in the past. She had one further episode of chest pain prior to discharge that resolved spontaneously and was not associated with ECG changes. She was discharged with instructions for PCP ___. #. Dizziness: She endorsed dizziness in the ED and numerous times when she was walking or sitting during this admission. She was not orthostatic and was monitored on telemetry which showed no events. Her dizziness did not appear to be related to her blood pressure and has been worked up extensively in the past. She was ambulating well independently prior to discharge. #. Hypertensive Urgency: She had significantly high blood pressures in the ED and during her stress test. It appears she has an exaggerated blood pressure response to exercise, as her stress test was limited due to her blood pressure response. She was started on low-dose clonidine, which she tolerated well. She was also continued on her home regimen of valsartan, nadolol, and HCTZ. #. Asthma: She stated on admission that she uses inhalers at times to help with her breathing. It was felt that she had some wheezing on exam and she had a decreased peak flow (approximately 250). She was instructed to use standing albuterol and fluticasone on discharge for a possible asthma component to her shortness of breath and chest pain. #. DM: She was continued on her home glipizide, NPH and insulin sliding scale. #. GERD: Continued ranitidine #. Leukopenia and thrombocytopenia: Were noted as an outpatient prior to admission and are stable. #. Prophylaxis: She was given SC heparin for DVT prophylaxis #. Code Status: Full code, confirmed on admission
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain in the setting of high blood pressures. Your blood pressure decreased and your chest pain went away. Your lab tests showed that you did not have any injury to your heart and did not have a heart attack. You had a stress test that showed that heart disease is unlikely to be causing your chest pain. You are being started on a medication called clonidine for your blood pressure. You also have some wheezing and your lung flow was decreased. You are being sent home with a prescription for two inhalers that may help as you may have asthma. You should check your blood pressures at home and keep a diary to bring to your primary care doctor's office.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old right handed woman with history of ___ (AChR -, MUSK unknown) not on treatment, recently diagnosed PE on Eliquis, and asthma who presents with a 4 day history of worsening dizziness and 1 day history of diplopia. History is notable for a recently diagnosed PE just 4 days ago of unclear etiology and is currently pending hypercoagulable workup. She also has a history of possible myasthenia for which she has been off treatment for several years and generally stable. CT head / CTA head and neck was done in the ER and was negative for any acute pathology. She was admitted to the stroke team for an MRI brain to rule out posterior circulation stroke. #Vertigo: MRI brain was done once admitted and was negative for any stroke, brainstem pathology, bleed, mass etc. The patient's symptoms of headache and vertigo improved drastically with IV fluids , anti-emetics, and Tylenol. Given her migraine, vertigo, and negative MRI, the most likely exaplanation of her symptoms is migraine vs. peripheral vestibulopathy. She was discharged with anti-emetics. #Diplopia: Patient was noted to have R eyelid ptosis and fluctuating horizontal diplopia that was worse with near vision and would dissipate when closing either eye. The diplopia would come and go. Her ptosis worsened with sustained upgaze supporting possible myasthenia ___. She will be connected to the ___ clinic for further management of these symptoms #PE: -Patient was diagnosed with PE a few days ago in the ER when she presented on ___ with shortness of breath. Etiology of PE unknown however she was initiated on eliquis. Hypercoaguable work up was not yet sent, and will need to be done by PCP this week. 1. PE: Hypercoaguable work up such as anticardiolipin, antiphospholipid, beta2microglobulin, protein c/s, antithrombin have not yet been sent. Patient to have these labs drawn by PCP during the week of ___. She will continue eliquis for the appropriate duration. 2. Neurology follow-up to be scheduled, coordinator has been emailed to set this up in a few weeks to months with Dr. ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Apixaban 5 mg PO BID 2. Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q4H:PRN wheezing
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after you experienced a headache, double vision, and room-spinning/vertigo. You were admitted to the stroke neurology service where you had extensive evaluation with an MRI brain and CT of your blood vessels. All of the imaging was negative for any pathology such as strokes, bleeds, tumors, or abnormal blood vessels which is reassuring. We feel that your vertigo/dizziness was due to a migraine and or an inner ear problem that should continue to improve over the next few days. In addition, your double vision was felt to be due to myasthenia ___. As you know, you have not been on medication for a few years. We would like you to follow-up with us in the neurology clinic (with Dr. ___. We have emailed our coordinators to set up an appointment and will be in touch with you about the specific date.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with a history of G6PD deficiency, hep C treated, BPPV, asthma, HTN, who presented with acute substernal chest pain radiating to the left scapula with negative troponins and no ischemic changes on EKG, initially concerning for unstable angina, but subsequently improving with Maalox and TUMS, most consistent with a diagnosis of GERD. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ===================== [ ] Patient has not been seen by a PCP in ___ few months, we have arranged an appointment for her with a PCP at the requesting location. [ ] PCP: please assess ASCVD risk factors, including lipids and A1C [ ] Patient requesting GI consultation for her bothersome symptoms of GERD [ ] Patient would benefit from nutrition counseling as an outpatient [ ] Patient would benefit from ongoing management of her constipation, IBS. ACTIVE ISSUES ============== # atypical chest pain # concern for GERD When the patient initially presented, there was concern for ACS. Her EKG was not suggestive of acute ischemia and her troponins were not elevated. A D-dimer drawn in the ED was negative as well. Initially, the patient was going to undergo an exercise stress, however over the course of her time here, it became clear that she her pain was secondary to gastrointestinal symptoms, as it improved with Maalox, TUMS, and simethicone. The patient also provided additional history of the presenting symptomatology occurring after eating spicy food or eating a large, high fat meal. Non-exertional. No associated dyspnea. Non radiating, no associated nausea, diaphoresis, non pleuritic. The patient ultimately requesting evaluation by GI, which would be done as an outpatient. She did continue on her home PPI, omeprazole 20mg BID. # HTN She was continued on her home Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO DAILY and home Lisinopril 5 mg PO DAILY # ASCVD risk Her lipids were checked and showed a total cholesterol of 172. She should have a full panel checked as an outpatient. She is not on a statin. CHRONIC/STABLE ISSUES ===================== #BPPV Patient with recent diagnosis of BPPV. She has been taking meclizine with good effect. This was continued during this hospitalization. #Depression She continued her home BuPROPion (Sustained Release) 150 mg PO BID #Chronic Constipation On intensive regimen for many years. Without medications, she has 1 BM every 2 weeks. She was continued on her home bowel regimen, Polyethylene Glycol 17 g PO DAILY and Lubiprostone 24 mcg PO BID #Asthma Does not use daily medication for asthma, however has the following at home which she uses as needed: Albuterol Inhaler, Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg, Fluticasone Propionate NASAL 1 SPRY, Ipratropium-Albuterol Neb. These were offered to her during this hospitalization but she declined as she was asymptomatic. #G6PD Deficiency Diagnosed ___ years ago during pregnancy. #Hep C s/p tx Undetectable viral loads since treatment with Pegasys. # CODE: Full Code, confirmed # CONTACT: HCP: ___ Relationship to patient: Husband Contact: ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Omeprazole 20 mg PO Q12H 2. Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 1 PUFF IH BID 3. Albuterol Inhaler 1 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN wheezing 4. Polyethylene Glycol 17 g PO DAILY 5. Fluticasone Propionate NASAL 1 SPRY NU DAILY 6. Ipratropium-Albuterol Neb 1 NEB NEB Q6H 7. Lubiprostone 24 mcg PO BID 8. Meclizine 25 mg PO Q6H:PRN dizziness 9. Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO DAILY 10. Lisinopril 5 mg PO DAILY 11. BuPROPion (Sustained Release) 150 mg PO BID
summary: You came into the hospital with pain and pressure in the chest. What happened to me while I was hospitalized? You were evaluated with a careful history and physical exam. Your heart rhythm was looked at carefully and it did not suggest that you were having a heart attack. Your blood tests were carefully checked and they did not suggest you were having a heart attack either. Your blood tests also did not suggest that you were having a Pulmonary Embolus. We believe that your pain was most likely caused by GERD: reflux disease. Your symptoms improved with Maalox and with other Over-the-Counter therapies.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ with hx dementia, CVA c/b by aphasia (wheelchair-bound at baseline), s/p PPM, HTN, HLD who presented to the ___ ED on ___ with 3 days of cough and increased work of breathing, found to have hypercarbic respiratory failure d/t exacerbation of tracheobronchomalacia i/s/o aspiration. ===================== # Hypercarbic respiratory failure/Dyspnea: Ms. ___ presented with wheezing and significant WOB, found to have elevated pCO2 to 61. Admitted to the MICU, where she was on BiPAP overnight. Had significant improvement and was transferred to the floor the next day on room air. CXR was negative for PNA. CT scan was ordered given unclear etiology of obstructive lung disease (no history of smoking or COPD, no history of asthma) and it showed tracheobronchomalacia, especially severe below the carina. Pt was treated with nebulizers, albuterol PRN, and a course of prednisone and azithromycin for presumed reactive airway disease/aspiration pneumonia in the setting of severe TBM. Flu swab, RSV negative. Her breathing improved but she continued to have periodic episodes of choking and coughing wheezing after eating pureed diet. Speech and swallow evaluated and noted risks for aspiration and recommended pureed diet vs. video swallow evaluation, although this would only be useful if family would be interested in pursuing parenteral access. Family was not interested in this. Family meeting was held on ___ to discuss TBM diagnosis, tx options, and code status. Discussed other treatment options such as stent placement, although we stressed that this would be an invasive procedure and given her current comorbidities likely not a first-line approach for her. Daughter will continue pureed diet, give nebulizers, and calm the patient to encourage passive exhalation in setting of TBM flare. Pt will followup with pulmonology as an outpatient. Family asked for more time to discuss code status among themselves. # Dementia: Continued home donepezil and quetiapine. # HTN: BPs 120s-130s/70s-80s throughout admission. Continued home HCTZ. # Nutrition: Pt was continued on pureed, nectar thick liquid diet. Even with this diet, she has trouble with coughing while eating. Speech and swallow evaluated and recommended continuing with this diet, cautioning that she will continue to aspirate. #TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -NEW MEDICATIONS: Prednisone 40 mg (end date ___, Azithromycin 250 mg (end date ___ -Pt will followup with pulmonology at ___ in order to optimize home nebulizer/inhaler regimen. -Please consider more detailed conversation about code status -CT of the chest revealed moderately large goiter. Thyroid ultrasound would be required to detect thyroid nodules.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after you had difficulty breathing and cough for 3 days. While you were in the hospital, you were treated with nebulizers. We took a picture of your chest and it shows that you have "tracheobronchomalacia." This means that airways in your lungs are softer and can collapse easily. This may also cause you to feel wheezy or short of breath. You have nebulizers at home that you should use if you are feeling short of breath. We have set you up with a pulmonologist (lung doctor) here at ___. This appointment is on ___ at 2:30 pm. We have also set you up with your primary care doctor. You will have two new medicines to take at home, prednisone and azithromycin. Please take each of these medication through ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ year old F with PMH of chronic obstructive asthma, COPD (never smoker) FEV1 45% predicted, FEV1/FVC 37%, and multiple myeloma, currently on Pomalidomide/Carfilzomib/Dex therapy who presented to ___ clinic for chemo where she was found to be SOB, wheezing, and complaining of back pain. She was found to have a COPD exacerbation with sputum culture positive for pseudomonas, treated with steroids and antibiotics, as well as degenerative changes of her spine. # COPD exacerbation: The patient was found to have a COPD exacerbation. Her presentation was not suggestive of volume overload, given baseline BNP, negative troponin, and recent TTE without changes. COPD exacerbation was thought to be precipitated by chemo administration (pt has had worsening COPD with carfilzomib ___ the past) vs. pseudomonas bronchitis as sputum culture showed pan-sensitive pseudomonas. The patient was treated with her home albuterol nebs, fluticasone-salmeterol BID, fluticasone propionate 110mcg 4puff inh BID, tiotropium 1cap inhaled qday, azithromycin 250mg PO qday as well as a prednisone taper: 80mg x 4 days (___), 60mg x 4 (___) days, 40mg x 4 days (___), 30mg x 4 ___, 20mg x 4 days (___), 10mg x 4 days (___), then off, per her outpatient pulmonologist recommendations. Given pt's allergies (to cefepime and levofloxacin), the patient was started on meropenem 500mg IV q8hrs per ID recommendations to treat pseudomonas. The patient was discharged with a PICC and will continue abx through ___. After discharge, the patient should have labs on ___ including CBC, Chem7, LFTs for monitoring while on meropenem. The patient should follow up with her pulmonologist and oncologist for further management of her COPD ___ the setting of treatment for multiple myeloma. # Multiple myeloma: The patient has a history of MM, currently treated with Pomalidomide/Carfilzomib/Dex therapy as outpatient. Her most recent administration had been held due to her respiratory status. The patient was evaluated with labs while hospitalized which showed decreasing protein counts. The patient should follow up with her oncologist and pulmonologist for further management of myeloma given her COPD and history of exacerbations with treatment. The patient was continued on acyclovir and Bactrim prophylaxis ___ addition to a multivitamin. The patient should follow up with her oncologist for monitoring of medication compliance as she has been taking acyclovir BID rather than q8hrs as prescribed. #Back Pain: The patient presented with back pain. She was evaluated with an MRI which showed stenosis and cord compression ___ the cervical spine without cord edema/inflammation as well as other degenerative changes. There was no report of lesions suggestive of multiple myeloma involvement. The patient's back pain was thought to be musculoskeletal ___ origin. The patient was treated with oxycodone 2.5mg PO q8hrs PRN pain. # Anxiety: The patient was continued on her home lorazepam 0.5-1mg PO qHS PRN # History of constipation: The patient was continued on her home bowel regimen. She did not have constipation during this admission. Transitional Issues: - Continue meropenem 500mg IV q8hrs though ___ - Pt should have monitoring labs whie on meropeneum: CBC, Chem 7, LFTs to be drawn on or before ___. - Continue prednisone taper: 80mg x 4 days (___), 60mg x 4 (___) days, 40mg x 4 days (___), 30mg x 4 ___, 20mg x 4 days (___), 10mg x 4 days (___), then off - Pt given small amount of oxycodone PRN for back pain, the patient should f/u with PCP for further evaluation, consideration of physical therapy ___ the future - Pt should follow up with oncology re: acyclovir dosing, pt reportedly takes medication BID rather than q8hrs as prescribed - Pt should follow up with oncology and pulmonology for further management of COPD and therapy for multiple myeloma - f/u right upper lobe lung nodule visualized on CTA ___, pt has appointment scheduled for repeat CT Chest
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of difficulty breathing and back pain. You were found to have a COPD exacerbation. This may have been caused by pseudomonas bronchitis ___ your lungs. We treated you with steroids and with antibiotics and your breathing improved. We evaluated your back with an MRI of your spine which showed some degenerative changes. This is likely contributing to your back pain. While ___ the hospital, you were also found to have some swelling ___ your legs. We evaluated you with an ultrasound which showed no clot. After discharge, you should continue to take meropenem through your PICC through ___. You should continue your prednisone taper as prescribed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Key Information for Outpatient ___ woman with PMHx notable for uncontrolled hypertension, lacunar infarcts, asthma, and hyperlipidemia who presents admitted for hypertensive urgency and recent episodes of weakness and expressive aphasia. # HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY She initially presented to her PCP with BP 259/100 prompting ED referral. Although neurologically intact on presentation, she did endorse history of transient neurologic deficits prior to presentation (see below), otherwise on presentation she did not have any evidence of acute end-organ damage. Her Cr was elevated and EKG showed diffuse TWI, but these were more likely suggestive of chronic hypertensive disease than acute changes. The patient admitted to not being compliant with her outpatient antihypertensive regimen, so this was felt to more likely be essential hypertension than secondary hypertension. She did have a renal artery ultrasound previously that showed some stenosis, but this study was not repeated, again because of reported non-compliance as an outpatient and because blood pressure control was achieved with PO medications. She was discharged on amlodipine 10mg daily, lisinopril 40mg daily, and chlorthalidone 25mg daily. She was also started on ASA and Atorvastatin 80mg daily. # EXPRESSIVE APHASIA / MOTOR WEAKNESS # Hx LACUNAR INFARCTS # HYPERTENSIVE ENCEPHALOPATHY Although neurologically intact on presentation, she reported episodes of motor weakness and expressive aphasia for several weeks leading up to presentation. As part of the workup for these episodes she had an MRI/MRA head and neck, carotid ultrasounds, and a TTE. MRI/MRA showed diffuse areas of stenosis suggestive of atherosclerotic disease and acute/subacute areas consistent with hypertensive microangiopathy. Carotid ultrasound showed bilateral carotid stenosis but not less than 40%. Neurology was consulted and agreed with hypertensive management and no acute need for neurology given she did not have recurrence of symptoms while hospitalized as her BP was controlled. She was started on ASA 81mg daily and Atorvastatin 80mg daily. # LEFT VENTRICLE HYPERTROPHY As part of her TIA work she had a TTE which showed "Severe symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with apical predominance suggestive of the apical predominant variant of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in the absence of significant hypertension". This was discussed with the cardiologist who read the TTE. Although the findings may just be from poorly controlled hypertension, she should have a non-urgent cardiac MRI done as an outpatient to further evaluate if this is hypertrophic cardiomyopathy vs changes from hypertension. # RENAL DYSFUNCTION Unclear baseline though suspicious that she may be chronically elevated given prior recorded Cr 1.4 and proteinuria. Overall high likelihood of hypertensive nephropathy. # ASTHMA She was continued on her home Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q4H:PRN wheezing/SOB # HYPERLIPIDEMIA Started Atorvastatin 80mg daily Transitional Issues: ====================== [ ] Should have a cardiac MRI done as an outpatient to eval for apical hypertrophic cardiomyopathy [ ] Please check chemistry panel, specifically to look at K, at follow up PCP ___ [ ] Continue to titrate BP meds as an outpatient for goal SBP < 140
summary: You had neurologic events in which you had trouble speaking and had weakness. In the ER you were found to have very high blood pressure You were started on amlodipine, lisinopril, and chlorthalidone to treat your high blood pressure. You had imaging of your head and neck done to evaluate for strokes. The images showed that you have some acute and chronic changes. These changes are narrowing of the blood vessels in your neck and brain from cholesterol build up. Neurology saw you and felt that your symptoms were due to the narrowing of your blood vessels as well as severe high blood pressure. You had an ECHO done, which is an ultrasound that looks at your heart. It showed enlargement of one of the ventricles, which is something we often see in people with high blood pressure.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is an ___ year old lady with asthma, COPD (not on oxygen at home), HTN, systolic CHF (EF 30%, ___ who presented with progressive fatigue and dyspnea, found to be clinically volume overloaded, as well as with a profound peripheral eosinophilia and pyuria. Active Issues. # Acute on chronic systolic CHF: last EF 30%, weight has increased from 135 lbs. on ___ to 145 lbs. on admission. Appeared volume overloaded on exam. Likely precipitant holding of outpatient diuresis in the setting of hypotension. Diuresed to weight on discharge: 63.9kg (141 pounds). She will stop taking furosemide and start torsemide, as below. Continued lisinopril and metoprolol. # Severe eosinophilia: Differential is broad, from infectious, endocrine, rheumatologic, medication-related and neoplastic. However, for such high eosinophilia (70% of WBC 18k) in an elderly woman with almost no travel history, malignancy, (eosinophilic malignancy or lymphoid malignancy, NHL, T-cell lymph/leuk, Sezary syndrome, ALL, or other solid malignancy) was of high concern. Genetic testing was sent but returned negative. IgE level was elevated, concernign for reactive processes, secondary to IL-5 release. She was also at risk for adrenal insufficiency, given h/o long term prednisone use, however AM cortisol was normal. ___, given long standing h/o asthma and chronic slight elevation of eosinophils, or other connective tissue diseases also possible, but less likely. No new drugs or exposures to invoke allergic reaction or DRESS -- also had no rash or other systemic symptoms aside from fatigue. Repeat CBC with differential shows sustained eosinophilia, with AEC 14k. Abdominal imaging showed known RCC, which could be culprit. Possible sigmoid lesion also seen, but colonscopy performed showed no e/o malignancy. Treated with dexamethasone 1 mg daily. Will continue DMZ until sees ___ MD in follow up. # Pyuria: without urinary symptoms. More likely that her fatigue is related to her underlying eosinophilic process. Will not treat asymptomatic bacteruria. UCX negative. No ABX given. # Pulmonary emboli: patient found to have multiple segmental PEs as well as few subsegmentals. Inciting factor uncertain, patient had known history of DVT in past. Son reported that patient spends >20 hours/day in bed. Treated with heparin gtt, then bridged to apixaban on discharge. # Fatigue: possibly related to CHF and/or eosinophilic process, but DDX remains broad especially given her age and comorbidities. AM cortisol normal. TSH normal. Question possible depression as well. Suggest follow up on possible depression with PCP on discharge. Patient denied suicidality. CHRONIC ISSUES # Asthma/COPD: patient with considerable wheezing on exam. Currently requiring 2L NC (off O2 at home). Standing DuoNebs for increased wheeze while in house. Continued Flovent and albuterol. Discharged on home Spiriva, DuoNebs stopped. # Hyperlipidemia: stable. Continued statin. # Primary prevention: Continued vitamin D supplementation and aspirin 81 mg daily. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Repeat chest CT in 3 months to ensure stability or resolution of solid and ground glass nodules - Indeterminate 1.6 cm lesion arising from the upper pole of the right kidney, slowly enlarging since the prior study in ___ which may represent a second renal cell carcinoma or complicated cyst. Evaluation with ultrasound is recommended if clinically relevant. - If within goals of care, repeat colonoscopy in 6 months when off anticoagulation to perform polypectomy - New Rx for 1mg PO dexamethasone daily to treat eosinophilia. Follow up with Dr. ___ in ___ clinic. - New Rx for apixaban (review cost with patient in future) - Discharged home with home oxygen because of ambulatory sats 81% (related to PEs and COPD/Asthma). Advised O2 use with activity. No oxygen needed at rest. - Follow up ascending colonic biopsy path result
summary: You were admitted because of shortness of breath and found to have blood clots in your lungs. In addition, you had a buildup of fluid in your lungs from your decreased heart function. We treated you with medication to help your kidneys pee out the extra fluid. We also put you on a blood thinning medication to prevent further clot formation in your lungs. It will take time for your body to naturally dissolve the blood clots currently in your lungs. It is extremely important to continue the blood thinning medication. The blood clots caused low oxygen levels, especially while you were walking around. We are discharging you home with oxygen which you should use every time you walk to prevent dangerously low levels of oxygen. We also found abnormally high levels of a special kind of white blood cell called "eosinophils." We consulted the specialized blood doctors who recommended testing to evaluate for cancer. The blood tests we ran to check for lymphoma were negative. A CT scan showed an area of thickening of your colon concerning for colon cancer. We had the gastroenterologists perform a colonoscopy and that test did NOT show a cancer. In summary, we believe your high eosinophil levels are related to inflammation, likely from your COPD and asthma. We treated you with a steroid medication called dexamethasone.

text: Brief Hospital Course: A ___ with PMH significant for CAD s/p stent RCA in ___, 2 Cypher stents to LAD in ___ and NSTEMI in ___, diastolic CHF, HTN, paroxysmal afib s/p cardioversion, O2-dependent COPD/asthma and CKD, who presents with increasing DOE, PND, orthopnea and 7lb weight gain in ~1wk, suggestive of acute exacerbation of CHF. 1) Acute exacerbation of chronic Diastolic CHF: Last Echo (___) showed EF 50-55%. She was last discharged from hospital on ___ with acute blood loss anemia due to GI Bleeding. She uses 3L of nasal canula at home. In the ED, she was afebrile, HR ___, BP 110/40, RR 16, satting at 98%suppl oxygen. CXR showed moderate cardiomegaly with increased lower lung opacities with no evidence of pneumonia. Given her allergy to furosemide, she was given 2mg IV bumex. On the floor, patient looked uncomfortable and became short of breath even with talking. She was kept on continuous telemetry. Her lung exam was remarkable for bibasilar crackles and sporadic wheezing. We initially increased her dose of bumex to 2.5mg and started her on metolazone 5mg BID x 1day, then decreased bumex to 2.0mg IV with metolazone 5mg the next day, and switched her to torsemide 100mg BID and metolazone for the remainder of this hospitalization. Patient was net negative 2.5L/day on average. Her weight dropped from 144.4kg on admission to 140.6kg by time of discharge. We also continued metoprolol 25mg BID for HR and BP control. Patient breathing improved and she was at her baseline by time of discharge. Her lung exam improved; no bibasilar crackles or wheezes were heard. Patient was discharged to rehab on her home level of supplemental 02, 3L. She should take 10meq potassium daily and have potassium level checked in ___ days. The likely cause of this acute CHF exacerbation is not completely clear, although medication and dietary noncompliance are the most likely causes. During this admission, patient was ruled out for MI with negative cardiac enzymes x3. Her thyroid function was checked--she had TSH 29 and free T4 1.3, indicating subclinical hypothyroidism. Her levothyroxine dose was increased to 125mcg from 100mcg. She will need repeat TSH in ___ weeks. It is likely that hypothyroidism could be contributing to the recent acute CHF exacerbation. 2) paroxysmal atrial fibrillation: Patient has a h/o paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, but was in sinus rhythm throughout her hospitalilzation. Presumably, pt is on coumadin 7.5mg daily, with goal INR ___ for stroke prevention. On admission, she had subtherapeutic INR 1.1; however, rate was well-controlled with metoprolol and EKG showed sinus rhythm with HR ___. It is likely that patient had not been taking coumadin regularly. Coumadin 7.5mg daily was restarted during this hospitalization and she was maintained on home dose amiodarone. Her INR was slowly increasing as expected, but not yet therapeutic. She will need INR checked as outpatient and appropriate titration of her coumadin. PCP is ___. 3) Type II diabetes: Pt has a h/o uncontrolled with complications. We continued her home dose of lantus 48units at night and ISS. 4)Obstructive Sleep Apnea: Continued home CPAP at 14 cm H2O with 4L oxygen at night and home 02 3L during the day. 5) CAD Native Vessel, Peripheral Vascular Disease: continued aspirin 6) CKD Stage III: Her BUN/creatinine was 53/2.3 on arrival, with baseline creatinine is 2.3-2.6. We obtained strict I/Os and followed daily lytes. Potassium was repleted PRN. No acute issues during this hospitalization. 7)Dyslipidemia: continued ezetimibe 10mg qhs 8)Asthma/COPD: Patient is O2-dependent at home. We continued home albuterol 2 puffs PRN and O2 3L nasal cannula during the day. 9)Depression: continued home paroxetine 40mg daily 10) FEN:low-salt diet. Repleted lytes PRN
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with increasing shortness of breath and weight gain, suggestive of worsening congestive heart failure. We are not sure what could have precipitated this; medication and salt in your diet could be precipating factors. Your chest X ray was consistent with heart failure and did not show any evidence for pneumonia. We treated you with a diuretic to remove significant amount of fluid from your body. This improved your breathing. We recommend that you weigh yourself every morning and call Dr. ___ your weight increases by > 3 lbs. Also, please continue to adhere to a 2 gm daily sodium diet and restrict your fluids to 1L per day. Please continue to take torsemide 100mg twice a day. It is very important that you take this medication as advised. Please note that we increased your levothyroxine to 125mcg daily, based on your lab test of thyroid function. Your primary care physician ___ need to re-check your thyroid function in ___ weeks. Please continue taking warfarin (coumadin) 7.5mg daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Summary: ___ year old woman with a history of bulimia and iron deficiency anemia presenting with recurrent UTI and recent BRBPR. Active Issues: #UTI Failed bactrim, possible resistance to cipro since she started and stopped it. She was growing pansensitive klebsiella on ___, urine cx from ___ contaminated. Treated with CTX and switched to cefpodoxime once she could take PO. She will need to finish her course as an outpatient and if she has nausea or inability to take the cefpoxime she should call her PCP right away. #Anemia Low MCV and low ferritin consistent with iron deficiency. Per chart she also has alpha thal trait and B12 deficiency anemia for which she is receiving injections. B12 and folate were WNL ___. She reports that her dysmenorrhea has improved since her uterine polypectomy about a year ago, raising the question of a GI bleed as the cause as she reports blood in the toilet, has an acute crit drop, and guaic positive stools. She was given ferrous sulfate, H&H was monitored with no acute crit drop, and a transfusion was not necessary. #GI bleed - This may be from hemmorhoids or from another lower GI source, no continued bleeding while in house. She should follow with GI outpatient for colonoscopy #Depression/Anxiety/Bulemia It would be difficult for her to go without laxatives while in the hospital, and I felt that if she left AMA because of this it was not worth the risk of her developing resistant pyelo. She has been on laxatives chronically for many years, and they are not directly related to her current presentation as her chem7 was normal. She was allowed to continue 8 pills of dulcolax at night, however she refused them the first night due to nausea. She denies SI and says her depression is better since she has her son to live for. She was continued on effexor and a SW consult was obtained. She should continue to follow up with her PCP and therapist for this problem. Inactive Issues: #Asthma Albuterol PRN wheezing #GERD -continue home nexium Transitional Issues: #UTI - it is very important they she finish her 7 day course of cefpodoxime so she does not develop resistance. She was instructed to call her PCP if for any reason she feels she cannot take her antibiotics, and to follow up promptly to ensure resolution of her symptoms #GI bleed - she should see GI for a colonoscopy. #Depression/ Anxiety /Bulimia - She should follow up with her PCP and with her therapist for continued treatment, and continue taking her Effexor.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for treatment of a urinary tract infection which spread to your kidney. Because you were nauseous and not able to take oral medications you were initially treated with IV antibiotics. Eventually you were switched to oral antibiotics and it is extremely important that you finish taking them until they are gone. If you do not, your infection may come back but be resistant to the antibiotics that we gave you which would mean you would have to be hospitalized for a long time with IV antibiotics and this could be life threatening. If you feel nauseous taking this medication at home you should not stop taking it; instead you should see your primary care doctor right away.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY STATEMENT: ================== ___ female with Down syndrome, prolonged QT, complete AV canal defect s/p surgical repair ___ infancy with residual mild MR, ALL s/p treatment (at age ___ c/b CVA with resulting R sided hemiparesis, childhood leukemia, and hypothyroidism, who presented with acute respiratory failure and septic shock secondary to pneumonia, as well as new complete heart block and severe mitral regurgitation. ACUTE ISSUES: ================== #Acute Hypoxic Respiratory Failure #Multifocal PNA #Septic Shock Patient presented with fevers, cough, and hypoxic respiratory failure. Patient was intubated ___ for hypoxic respiratory failure, requiring levophed and vasopressin for blood pressure support. CXR on admission concerning for multifocal pneumonia and pulmonary edema. EKG also with new complete heart block. Concern initially for distributive shock ___ PNA) vs. cardiogenic shock ___ the setting of new heart block). She subsequently underwent right heart catheterization which showed normal to high cardiac index, and normal to borderline filling pressures/SVR ___ the setting of being on 2 vasopressors, most consistent hemodynamically with septic shock likely ___. Sputum cultures were positive for growing GPC ___ pairs/clusters. CT head, CT chest, and CTAP negative for alternate infectious source. Completed 8 day course of Vancomycin/Cefepime (D1: ___ for pneumonia, and was successfully extubated on ___. After extubation, patient continued to have wheezing and O2 requirement on nasal cannula. Pulmonary was consulted. She was treated supportively with inhalers, chest ___, and steroid burst x 3 days given concern for asthma exacerbation. On transfer, she is saturating well on RA. Patient was noted to have multiple de-sat events ___ evening. This finding was discussed with ENT over the phone who advised that patients with Down's syndrome often have OSA and that she should have this worked up as an outpatient if it continues to be an issue. #Complete heart block #Congenital cardiac disease, AV canal defect s/p repair Etiology of complete heart block likely ___ AV canal defect and repair. TSH, lyme serologies, and endomyocardial biopsies otherwise negative for alternate etiology. Temporary transvenous pacing wires were placed on admission and patient underwent successful permanent pacemaker placement on ___ without complications. #Severe Mitral Regurgitation Severe MR was noted on ___, likely ___ AV canal defect and repair. Would likely benefit from mitral valve repair as an outpatient. Spoke with patient and mother about surgical options. Will follow up with outpatient pediatric cardiologist, ___, with plan for surgery ___ the next few weeks. Otherwise, continue afterload reduction with low-dose captopril 3.125 po tid. #Femoral artery dissection Right heart catheterization complicated by posterior right femoral artery dissection. Concern for limb ischemia initially, but pulses still dopplerable and duplex with only partial obstruction. Vascular surgery consulted, with ___ indication for acute intervention. #THRUSH: on fluconazole for 21-day course, started ___ and plan to end ___. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # Hypothyroidism: Continue home levothyroxine. # Prolonged QTc: QTc 508 on admission. Avoid QTc prolonging medications. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [ ]Follow up with cardiology on ___ [ ]Please continue fluconazole po through ___ for thrush [ ]New med: captopril 3.125 tid for afterload reduction due to severe MR [ ]Discharge weight 49.2kg [ ]Please follow-up symptoms of OSA and order sleep study if necessary #CODE: Full code, confirmed with mother #CONTACT/HCP: Sister: ___ ___ Mother: ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list may be inaccurate and requires futher investigation. 1. Levothyroxine Sodium 50 mcg PO DAILY 2. Albuterol Inhaler ___ PUFF IH Q4H:PRN wheezing 3. Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID 4. Cetirizine 10 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted because you had trouble breathing and low oxygen levels and were found to have pneumonia. You were also found to have an abnormal heart rhythm. You were treated with antibiotics for your pneumonia. You also underwent a procedure with our electrophysiology (heart) doctors to ___ a pacemaker. This should prevent your heart from going into an abnormal rhythm. On our heart imaging, we also found that you have a heart valve which is leaky. This valve will need to be repaired. You should see your cardiologist, Dr. ___ you go home to schedule this surgery. You developed an infection ___ your mouth that made it difficult for you to eat.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ F with a PMHx of Crohn's disease on Remicade every 6 weeks, prednisone, and recently started on ___, and asthma presenting with c/o fever and URI symptoms, with cough, improved with steroids and antibiotic therapy. ACUTE ISSUES: ================== # Sepsis due to URI: Patient met multiple SIRS criteria on admission, unclear source at this time although given cough/congestion suspect viral versus bacterial URI, with sinus tenderness, ear fullness, and clear CXR, however lower resp infection also on ddx given immunosuppressed state. Given immunosuppressed status and recent hospitalization initially covered broadly for HCAP/CAP with vanc/cefepime/azithro, as well as tamsulosin for possible influenza, and supported with IVF. She was also started on stress dose steroids with hydrocortisone given chronic steroid use (for Crohns Disease, see below). Patient's tachycardia/hypotension has responded to IVF as well as stress steroids. By HD2, symptoms had largely resolved. Micro data ultimately growing aspergillus from sputum, negative rapid screen for influenza. ID was consulted, and felt aspergillus was a contaminant, and felt comfortable sending home to complete short 5 day course of antibiotics, so was transitioned to monotherapy with PO azithromycin (vanc/cefepime/tamsulosin stopped) and patient was discharged with one further dose to complete the 5 day course. Her steroids were successfully tapered back to her home dose of 6cc prednisolone (15mg/5cc solution) by the time of her discharge, and her pressures remained stable. # Hypotension: Initially presented with hypotension to SBP ___ the ___, likely due to an infection, though given chronic steroid use can't rule out element of adrenal insufficiency contributing. Resolved with stress dose steroids (hydrocortisone) and fluids, as well as treatment on antibiotics for presumed respiratory source. Ultimately tapered back to her home dose of prednisolone, and her blood pressures continued stable. CHRONIC ISSUES: ================== # Asthma: Patient with intermittent wheezing on exam on admission, did not appear ___ distress and not hypoxic, breathing comfortably on RA throughout her hospitalization. Following treatment of presumed respiratory infection as well as albuterol treatments, her wheezing resolved. # Crohn's: Presentation not consistent with an acute flare of her Crohns Disease. ___ discussion with her outpatient GI physician, her ___ was stopped given concern for active infection. She was given stress dose steroids with hydrocortisone as discussed above, and ultimately tapered back down to her home dose of prednisolone. She was instructed to continue her home dose of 6cc prednisolone (15mg/5cc solution) without tapering, and to continued off the ___ until her next outpatient appointment with Dr. ___. She was continued on PCP prophylaxis with ___. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ====================== #Patient will take one more dose of azithromycin 500mg on ___ ___ was discontinued this admission per outpatient GI physician ___ remained off medication on discharge; will remain off ___ until follow up with Dr. ___ to determine when to restart. #Will continue 18mg prednisolone without further tapering while off ___ until follow up with Dr. ___ #Emergency Contact: ___ Relationship: Husband Phone number: ___ #Code status: FULL
summary: You came ___ with a fever and cough, with sinus pressure and congestion, ___ the setting of chronic immunosuppressive therapy for your Crohns disease. You were treated with some fluids and antibiotics, and we got an x-ray of your chest to look for deeper lung infection which was clear. Your symptoms improved with the fluids and antibiotics, and we transitioned you over to oral antibiotics. The infectious disesaes team came to see you while you were here because your sputum culture showed a fungus called aspergillus, however they agreed that this was only colonization ___ the respiratory tract, and not an active infectious process causing your symptoms.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with PMHx of fibromyalgia, chronic pain syndrome, myofascial pain syndrome, peripheral nueropathy, hypertension and anxiety who presents with diffuse body pain over the past three months in the setting of discontinuation of her prior narcotic medications for her pain syndromes. # Back Pain: Ms. ___ presented to ___ with diffuse body pains over the past three months in the setting of her previous prescribing not being able to prescribe narcotics. On presentation she did mention that she had back pain which caused her to fall and led to urinary incontinence. On further questioning it was determined that the urinary incontinence was not true urinary incontinence, but was rather an inability to get to the bathroom in time to urinate. Neurology was consulted in the Emergency Department and did not believe there was a neurologic cause of her symptoms, as there were no neurologic deficits noted on examination. She had good strength and sensation in her extremities and DTRs were normal. To rule out an epidural abscess an MRI was obtained which did not show any evidence of cord compression. Ortho-spine saw patient and did not believe any intervention was required. Etiology was likely flare in her fibromyalgia/myofascial/chronic pain syndromes. On physical examination, she did not display pain when distracted. To control her pain she was continued on her home dose of her baclofen, gabapentin, and lamotrigine. During her hospitalization she also received oxycodone for pain relief. The oxycodone was discontinued at the time of discharge. At the time of discharge she was ambulating with a walker without difficulty. Physical therapy was consulted but given the fact that she was walking to and from her bathroom without a walker, a physical therapy evaluation was not necessary. She deferred home physical therapy. Occupational therapy was also consulted. They recommended home safety evaluation and home occupational therapy upon discharge. # Fibromyalgia/Myofascial Pain Syndrome: Her diffuse body pain over the past three months is likely related to her fibromyalgia/myofascial/chronic pain syndrome. Continued with pain management with her home medications of baclofen, gabapentin, lamotrigine. Oxycodone was given during hospitalization, although she does not have a prescription of this at home. Thus, oxycodone was discontinued at the time of discharge. Ms. ___ able to ambulate with a walker without pain at the time of discharge. # Anxiety: Ms. ___ has history of anxiety. She was previously on clonazepam but this prescription expired prior to coming to the hospital. Her anxiety remained stable during hospitalization without the use of clonazepam. We recommended she contact Psychiatry services at ___ at ___ for further evaluation and management. # Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease: Ms. ___ has a history of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease. She was continued on her daily dose of omeprazole 20 mg PO daily. # NEUROPATHY: Ms. ___ has a history of peripheral neuropathy. We continued her home medications including gabapentin and lamotrigine. # ASTHMA: Ms. ___ has a history of asthma. She did not have wheezing during hospitalization but did have albuterol prn for wheezing/sob. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== # PSYCHIATRY: Ms. ___ sought Psychiatry referral service. We provided the number to ___ Psychiatry at ___ with her discharge paperwork. # CONTACT: ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ with worsening diffuse body pain over the past three months. This was likely in the setting of worsening of your fibromyalgia/chronic pain syndrome/myofascial pain. Since you noticed back pain, we obtained an image of your spine (Magnetic Resonance Imaging-MRI). The results of this image was reassuring with no evidence of any infection present and no compression of your spinal cord. You were treated with your home medications (baclofen, gabapentin, lamotrigine) as well as a short dose of oxycodone for your pain. With the improvement of your pain, your strength increased and you were able to walk the halls with a walker. We attempted to get a physical therapy evaluation but because you were able to walk on your own, no physical therapy evaluation was needed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with history of Atrial Fibrillation on Apixiban, Sick Sinus Syndrome status post Permanent Pacemaker, Chronic Kidney Disease and type 2 diabetes mellitus recently started on insulin admitted with cough, wheezing, sore throat, recent leg swelling and weight gain, and was found to have significant hypoxemia. Her presentation was felt to be most consistent with viral bronchitis +/- reactive airway disease. She was treated with prednisone and her symptoms improved. She was also re-started on torsemide 10 mg PO QD, which she tolerated well. Overall picture seems consistent with a viral bronchitis overlaid upon a chronic HFpEF with possible chronic/subacute worsening of volume status. ___ evaluated her and recommended home with outpatient ___, and when she was symptomatically improved, she was discharged home. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [ ] Patient to perform daily standing weights at home [ ] Needs outpatient Cardiology f/u in ___ weeks for further eval/mgmt of HFpEF and titration of torsemide dose. [ ] We stopped her home HCTZ on admission given hyponatremia and normotension, this was not resumed at discharge. [ ] Patient should have a repeat electrolyte panel done at her next PCP ___ visit to ensure she is tolerating resumption of torsemide without electrolyte abnormalities or renal dysfunction. [ ] Patient prescribed an albuterol-ipratropium inhaler PRN at discharge in case she develops wheezing in the future.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath, wheezing and cough that we think were most likely caused by bronchitis, possibly from a viral infection. You improved with conservative treatment including nebulizers, cough medicines, and a short course of steroids. Your blood sugars were elevated because of the steroids, but they will go back to normal once you complete the course of steroids (prednisone). We also found that you have evidence of increased fluid in your lungs and ankles from your heart failure, so we re-started a medication you had been on previously (called Torsemide) at a lower dose than you had previously been on, and we are stopping your hydrochlorothiazide. You should weigh yourself every day at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT/PLAN: ___ yo male history of hyperlipidemia, gastritis, hypertension, anxiety, alcohol abuse, presenting with acute shortness of breath with hypoxemia evidence on ABG now improved on supplemental O2 and heparin gtt. #Hypoxia: Patient presented with new hypoxia with repeat V/Q scan showing v/q mismatch in RLL c/f PE. He underwent non-con CT which is not protocoled for PE, but given the findings, radiology was highly suspicious for PE. Heme/onc was consulted as pt reported that he had genetic susceptibility testing the reports that he had abnormal metabolism of warfarin. Per heme/onc he was started on lovenox with anti-Xa levels to be monitored as an outpatient. He also had expiratory wheezing which improved with nebs c/f obstructive airway disease. He underwent PFTs which showed obstructive pattern that is likely more chronic that patient realized per Pulm. He was discharged on albuterol and advair with pulmonary follow-up as an outpatient. #Troponin Leak: Likely cardiac strain. CK-MB remains flat suggesting against NSTEMI, could be ___ to RHS in setting of PE. In addition, given chronic ___ disease, troponin elevated will persist longer. His troponins have trended down even with his CKD. His PE was managed as above. He will follow-up with pulmonary and cardiology as an outpatient. #CKD: Chronic stage III. US of kidneys not suggestive of obstruction or parenchymal disease. Recent UA bland. SPEP/UPEP were unremarkable. All meds were renally dosed and creatinine was monitored daily. #Alcohol Abuse: Reportedly drinks heavily per family members. No history of acute withdrawal or seizures, although did get defensive when discussing alcohol use. His recent epidoses of diaphoresis, tachypnea and hypertension may be related to alcohol withdrawal given that his last drink was now 48-72 hours ago. He was started on a CIWA and treated with diazepam for withdrawal symptoms. After 72 hours he was transitioned back to his home clonazepam. SW consultation was offered given the patient's need to be on systemic AC for PE as above, but patient declined. He prefers to speak with his personal acquantainces regarding alcohol cessation. #HTN: continued chlorthalidone, atenolol was changed to metoprolol given CKD. His reactive airway disease remained stable on Beta blockade while in the hospital. #GERD: continue omeprazole #Anxiety: continued home citalopram and clonazepam after cessation of CIWA scale as outlined above # Code: Full (discussed with patient) Wife ___ ___ Son ___ MD ___ TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Patient should f/u with heme/onc and pulm as outpt for further management of PE and Obstructive lung disease. - Patient should be sure to have age-appropriate cancer screening, as unclear etiology for his PE
summary: you were admitted to ___ with complaints of chest pain. You were found to have a blood clot in one of the arteries in your lungs and will need to be on blood thinners for at least 3 months. You were also diagnosed with obstructive lung disease (asthma) and will be discharged on two new inhalers. It is imperative that you not drink alcohol while on blood thinners as falls can increase your risk of stroke or life threatening bleeding. Additionally, you mentioned you take Klonipin/aka Clonazepam for anxiety. These medications can make you sedated and greatly increase your risk of falls and should be used with extreme caution. Please note, we changed your medication ATENOLOL to METOPROLOL as METOPROLOL is better metabolized in patients with ___ diseas rather than ATENOLOL.

text: Brief Hospital Course: In the ED, patient was given nebulizers and prednisone. She got a GI cocktail which also improved her pain. Her peak flow was 150--> 250 after neb. She was Observed for stress test, but was wheezy and unable to do stress on ___ so was admitted for inpt stress. # Chest pain - Given pt's history, it seems most likely to be MSK chest pain precipitated by cough, and she was breathing shallowly in order to instigate cycle. ___ also represent angina, although pt is very healthy at baseline and does not have exertional angina making unstable angina less likely. Pt has no known cariac risk factors except age. CE negative x2, EKG normal. Records from ___ ___ indicate normal ___ ETT for chest pain or EKG changes of ischemia at satisfactory heart rate (87%) workload.
