Also notable for hepatic steatosis #ANTIBIOTIC REGIMEN: She was discharged on Augmentin 875 mg PO Q12 for total antibiotic course of 10 days with end date on ___. #FOLLOW UP TESTS: ___: Fluid, other gram stain, respiratory culture; acid fast smear; acid fast culture; fungal culture; potassium hydroxide preparation, ___: blood cultures x2 # CODE: Full Code (confirmed) # CONTACT: HCP ___ (father): ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ after experiencing shortness of breath in the setting of mucus production. This was concerning for mucus plugging of your tracheal stent. You underwent a CT scan of the neck which showed mild mucus plugging of the stent. You received antibiotics (vancomycin, piperacillin tazobactam) for one day. You also received a medication called mucinex to help clear up the congestion. These helped improve your symptoms and decreased the mucus production. You also underwent a procedure called a bronchoscopy to assess the stent. The bronchoscopy did reveal mucus. Your tracheal stent was replaced. Due to the mucus that was noted, the lung doctors would ___ to take an antibiotic called augmentin. Please take this medication (875 milligrams, 125 milligrams) EVERY 12 HOURS with end date on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ y.o. Male with CLL, COPD, Asthma, DM II who was apparently referred to the ED for a mass in his lungs found on CT scan and recent right sided neck pain. . #)Incidentaloma: Patient denied any neck pain in the hospital and had a full ROM with no eivdence of LAD or trauma. Per the patient, he was sent here because his PCP told him to come in due to a lung mass seen on a CT chest. Patient's record from at___ were retrieved. The chest CT report noted a new RUL 10-12mm lesion. His case was discussed with his oncologist and PCP and it was agreed that this case was better followed up as an outpatient. He is scheduled for an MRI on ___ and will follow up wth both his PCP and oncologist within ___ days. . #) COPD: While in the hospital, patient had audible weezing with no episodes of word dyspnea or tripoding. He denied any change in his breathing pattern/effort. His CXR portable shows reticular interstitial pattern. There was no evidence of consolidation. Patient reported no worsening of his cough or SOB. He has noted no recent change in his sputum frequency or quality. WBC count was 25.8 (50% lymphocytes) which was attributed to a combination of CLL and his steroid use. Patient's BNP was 337. Due to patient's wheezing and history of COPD, he was treated for a COPD exacerbation. He was started on prednisone and albuterol/ipratroprium nebs and azithromycin in the ED. Due to an absence of sputum production his Azithromycin was stopped. He did have an O2 requirement in the high 80's off of O2 which dropped further on ambulation. He was discharged home with instructions to ___ his O2 at all times especially with ambulation. He was dichared home on advair, spiriva and fluticasone inhalers and advised. He is scheduled to follow up with his PCP in the next ___ days. . #) Hyperkalemia. Patient's potassium was noted to be elevated up to 5.7 on admission with no EKG changes. He was treated with 30 g Kayexalate. His potassium was 5.0 at discharge. . #) DMII: Oral meds were held on admission. His sugars were well controlled on SSI. He was dischargde home on his home meds. . #) Leukocytosis: Noted to be 31.5 on admission and believed to be secondary to CLL and steroids. He dropped to 26 by the morning following admissionand this was noted to be baseline compared to atrius records.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with a mild exacerbation of COPD. It is unlikely that you have bacterial pneumonia. We reviewed the report from your CT scan which showed a 1.5 cm density that could be inflammation or malignancy. It was recommended that you have a repeat CT scan in months, in addition to any other evaluation recommended by your outpatient providers. You were started on a new inhaler called Spiriva (tiotropium). Your prednisone taper was changed slightly. Please continue using oxygen as previously prescribed. Please talk with your primary care doctor and with a pulmonology specialist, if you so desire, about increasing your use of supplemental oxygen.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, hypertension, myocardial bridging, diastolic congestive heart failure, asthma who presented with worsening chest pain. Anihypertensive home medications were initially held but a NTG drip was started. Chest pain alleviated with the NTG. EKG stable with prior studies. Trop negative. Patient became hypotensive on the NTG gtt and Cr bumped to 2.3 from 1.0. NTG stopped immediately and patient was given fluids with good response. Given preload dependence of HCM, decision was made to hold all home medications including Diltiazem, HCTZ, Imdur, Lisinopril. Metoprolol was started at a low dose and patient tolerated it well. Echo showed: 1) Severe asymmetric left ventricular hypertrophy suggestive of non-obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. No resting LVOT gradient. 2) Normal biventricular regional/global systolic function. 3) Grade III diastolic dysfunction with mild to moderate type II pulmonary hypertension in setting of normal RV size/function and RA size. 4) Moderate mitral regurgitation in the absence of valvular/chordal ___. Given findings, disopyramide was deferred after discussion with patients cardiologist. On discharge, pertinent cardiac medications include Metoprolol tartate 6.25 q6H. Of note, her course was complicated by right flank pain. UA with negative nitrites and small leukocyte esterase, small blood. Patient remained afebrile. Renal ultrasound was done to evaluate for stones (given her long standing history of nephrolithiasis)which showed no hydropnephorsis, stones or hydroureter. Patient had resolve of CVAT <24 hours. Decision for further imaging or antibiotics deferred as symptoms resolved. # Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: Likely etiology of CP. Discontinued on all home medications and cautiously added metoprolol. Patient tolerated it well and went back to her baseline chest pain. Repeat TTE on ___ with no evidence of resting LVOT gradient. Remains CP with activity. Chronic issues: # HTN: Discontinued diltiazem and lisinopril. Continued metoprolol. as above # Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine # Asthma: Continued Advair, albuterol nebs prn # Depression: Continued sertraline # Non-insulin dependent DM: Discharged on home medication of metformin. Transitional issues: 1. Patient requires a CPAP machine at home 2. Follow up with cardiology for further management of on going chest pain at rest 3. Follow up with primary care for flank pain, consider urological evaluation as patient has had several stones in the past 4. AVOID diuretics and nitrates as much as possible given pre-load dependence. Code: Full Contact:HCP: ___ (husband) ___, ___ (daughter/alternative HCP) ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Diltiazem 30 mg PO QHS 2. Diltiazem Extended-Release 240 mg PO BID 3. Isosorbide Mononitrate (Extended Release) 30 mg PO DAILY 4. Levothyroxine Sodium 50 mcg PO DAILY 5. Pravastatin 40 mg PO QPM 6. Ipratropium-Albuterol Neb 1 NEB NEB Q6H:PRN shortness of breath 7. Nitroglycerin SL 0.3 mg SL Q5MIN:PRN chest pain 8. Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q4H:PRN SOB 9. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID:PRN Constipation 10. Albuterol 0.083% Neb Soln 1 NEB IH Q2H:PRN SOB, wheezing 11. Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (500/50) 1 INH IH BID 12. Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO DAILY 13. MetFORMIN (Glucophage) 500 mg PO BID 14. Sertraline 50 mg PO DAILY 15. Lisinopril 20 mg PO BID
summary: You were admitted because of chest pain and shortness of breath. We did an EKG to evaluate your heart function. The EKG was similar to your prior EKGs which was reassuring. We also measured a troponin level, this is something that is found in the blood when you heart is in danger. Your troponin level was normal which was also reassuring. We gave you a medication called nitroglycerin to decrease your chest pain. Although this did alleviate your pain, it caused your blood pressures to become low which caused some injury to your kidneys. We immediately discontinued that medication and gave you fluids. Your kidneys recovered nicely. As you know, you have a condition called hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Your heart muscle is very thick, so much so that it cannot hold as much blood as it normally should. You were on several medications prior to your admission that can cause the amount of blood in your heart to decrease even more.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with history of difficult to control asthma with historical intubation for asthma who presents with a few days of worsening DOE and wheezing in the setting of cold exposure but no evidence of URI or respiratory infection. Active Diagnoses ================ # Moderate Persistent Asthma with exacerbation: Based on her history of asthma and her symptoms of dyspnea, wheezing, cough, and chest tightness this was initially felt to be consistent with an asthma flare. She was on medium dose fluticasone, albuterol, and montelukast prior to admission. She states that she had palpitations on Advair previously, presumably from the salmeterol. She had no significant improvement on standing albuterol/ipratropium nebs and steroids in the ER for 24 hours so she was admitted. She again failed to improve after 48 hours of inpatient continuation of steroids/nebs. CXR was not concerning for PNA and she had no leukocytosis. Pulmonology was consulted who felt the time course for her symptoms was not classic for an asthma exacerbation. They recommended CT trache to evaluate for tracheobronchomalacia. However, before this was completed the patient's symptoms rapidly improved so that further testing was deferred to the outpatient setting. Of note, her peak flows were 430-450 at the peak of her symptoms and she was not hypoxemic, though she was tachypneic to the high ___. She was treated with a steroid burst for 6 days. She was discharged on Advair (fluticasone-salmeterol) instead of fluticasone inhaler so that she would have a ___. If she does not tolerate the salmeterol because of palpitations/tachycardia, she may benefit from higher dose fluticasone (440mcg BID). #Lactatemia: Patient had a lactate on ABG of 5.8 on ___. This was thought to be due to a combination of metformin (continued on admission) and repeated albuterol nebulizers, which may cause elevated lactate in the absence of tissue hypoperfusion. Chronic Diagnoses ================= #Diabetes mellitus type 2 Documented history of diabetes mellitus treated with metformin and insulin at home. While an inpatient, her metformin was initially continued. She had poorly controlled glucose while on steroids and was told to take 40 units of Lantus for the 2 days post-discharge while on steroids and then to resume her home regimen. #Hypertension Documented history of hypertension treated with losartan, hydrochlorothiazide, clonidine, and nifedipine. Her home regime was increased slightly while an inpatient, but she was discharged on her home medications. Transitional Issues =================== -consider outpatient evaluation of her symptoms to exclude tracheobronchomalacia or subglottal stenosis -consider increasing steroid dose, especially if the patient does not tolerate salmeterol -consider outpatient PFTs
summary: you were admitted after your shortness of breath and wheezing did not improve with nebulizers and steroids in the ER. While you were an inpatient you were treated with steroids and nebulizer treatments for your wheezing. We initially thought that your symptoms were from an asthma flare because you did not have signs of an infection. Typically asthma gets better more rapidly, but you were having symptoms after 3 days of treatment. We had the lung specialists see you and they recommended more advanced imaging of your chest. However, your symptoms resolved suddenly on the day of discharge so we thought that you could have this done as an outpatient. While you were here your blood pressure and blood sugars were a little higher because of the steroids. This is temporary.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: ======== This is a ___ female patient ___ s/p C-section on ___ (breech} who was readmitted with 3 days of progressive exertional dyspnea, productive cough, and pleuritic chest pain, as well as progressive ___ edema that began a few days after C-section. On arrival, found to be hypertensive (171/81) and SpO2 88% on RA - escalated to BiPAP in the ED with lower extremity edema on exam, CXR w/ pulmonary edema, and bedside echo showing EF ~40% concerning for pre-eclampsia w/ severe features (SBP > 160, headache, pulm edema). She was given multiple 20mg IV Lasix boluses, a nitro gtt, ibuprofen/acetaminophen, and magnesium infusion and her symptoms improved greatly. Her oxygen requirement was weaned to room air over two days as we continued to get fluid off, and her nitro gtt was weaned with captopril being introduced and titrated as appropriate for afterload reduction. Formal echo showing an EF of 35-40%. Patient transferred to the Ob-Gyn service on ___ ___ on ___. She remained stable and cardiology was consulted on ___ for further medical optimization. She was transitioned from captopril to lisinopril 20mg qday and started on metoprolol 12.5mg qday. Further diuresis was deferred given her euvolemic state. She did remarkably well and was meeting all milestones by ___. She was ambulatory on room air without SOB or desaturations. She was clinically euvolemic and her blood pressures were under control on her current regimen. She was discharged home with close cardiology and Obstetric follow-up in place. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= #Acute HFrEF exacerbation #Peripartum cardiomyopathy She has no significant hx of cardiac disease including cardiomyopathy. The most likely trigger for her dyspnea and volume overload is peripartum cardiomyopathy given her pregnancy w/ C-section done on ___. Her BP was also elevated on admission concerning for pre-eclampsia, as below. In regards to other etiologies, she has no significant hx of tachyarrhythmias, and given her previous lipid profile and A1c in the setting of her young age, CAD is less likely. Other considerations would include vial prodrome [reports recent sputum production but no sick contacts], coronary dissection [less likely], PE [less likely] and HIV induced cardiomyopathy although she has been negative for HIV in the past. Responded well to intermittent IV Lasix boluses and a nitro gtt for afterload reduction that was then weaned to captopril being titrated as needed. #Delayed onset postpartum pre-eclampsia w/ severe features She presented 17 days after C-section with dyspnea; she meets the criteria based on a systolic blood pressure ___ mmHg, headache, and pulmonary edema. Her urine protein was <4 making her SPOT protein/cr ratio <0.4. She denies new-onset cerebral or visual disturbances, or severe persistent RUQ pain. She does not have thrombocytopenia or elevated liver enzymes (initial AST elevated, but specimen hemolyzed) so there is a low concern for HELP syndrome. Started on magnesium infusion and maintained a goal range of 4.8-8.4. Afterload reduction as above. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #Asthma She present with dyspnea and has a previous hx of asthma with exam significant for wheezing. The ___ component of her acute dyspnea is related to her acute HF, but Albuterol available as needed.
summary: You were admitted because of shortness of breath You were admitted to the hospital because you had been feeling short of breath and you were found to have an elevated blood pressure concerning for a condition called pre-eclampsia. You were also found to have fluid on your lungs. This was felt to be due to a condition called heart failure, where your heart does not pump hard enough and fluid backs up into your lungs. You were given a diuretic medication through the IV to help get the fluid out. You were given medications to lower your blood pressure to a normal range and medications to help protect your heart. You improved considerably and were ready to leave the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ CAD s/p CABG, T2DM, HTN, presenting with SOB, found to have NSTEMI, and heart failure exacerbation. # NSTEMI: Patient with known CAD s/p CABG, now with SOB and found to have NSTEMI. Cath ___ with patent grafts except for occluded vein graft on R with good collaterals. Treated with ASA 81 (received 325 at ___), Atorvastatin 80 mg, home labetalol as below for blood pressure, and home lisinopril 40mg daily. # Acute on chronic diastolic CHF exacerbation: Patient presents with SOB, BNP elevation and crackles on lung exam. Initially reportedly received IVF at ___, then given diuresis with 40 mg IV lasix ___. She also received IV solumedrol and nebs for what was initially felt to be asthma/COPD. Dry on RHC on ___ after diuresis. Continued home lisinopril, labetalol (see below) and discharged on lasix 20 mg 3x/week on discharge to prevent volume overload. # Cough, rhonchi: Patient with cough and wheezes/rhonchi consistent with bronchitis without fevers or focal finding on exam to suggest pneumonia. Dry on cardiac cath so low suspicion for ongoing CHF. Started albuterol inhaler prn and scheduled for outpatient PCP ___. # HTN: Carvedilol started on admission for afterload reduction however in setting of hypertension overnight, restarted labetalol 200mg BID (home dose). Continued home lisinopril, hydral and amlodipine. # T2DM: Held glimepiride and metformin. HISS. # HL: Stopped simvastatin, started atorvastatin 80 mg daily. Transitional issues: - Medication changes - added aspirin 81 mg and changed simvastatin to atorvastatin 80 mg daily. Added 20mg lasix every ___ and ___. -Started albuterol for bronchospasm related to likely bronchitis. -Will need repeat labs (Cr, K) in 1 week
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath and found to have changes on your EKG showing possible damage to your heart. We did a catherization to look at the vessels of your heart but there were no new areas to stent so we recommend continuing with your medical treatment. We also gave you medication to remove excess fluid in your lungs to help your breathing. You were started on lasix (furosemide) to help prevent fluid in your lungs. We have also started you on aspirin and changed you to atorvastatin from simvastatin. Due to your ongoing cough and lung symptoms, we have started a medication called albuterol (an inhaler) which you can take if you are short of breath. Please return to the hospital if you feel short of breath or have chest pain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old gentleman with ESRD on peritoneal dialysis (PD), who presented with diffuse abdominal pain, with Staph aureus peritonitis. . . ACTIVE ISSUES: # Peritonitis: He presented with intense, diffuse abdominal pain, subjective fevers/chills, and cloudy peritoneal fluid concerning for peritonitis. Peritoneal fluid was sampled returning 6400 WBC with 96%PMN and culture from the broth eventually grew MRSA. He was initially treated with broad spectrum antibiotics, which were then narrowed to only intraperitoneal vancomycin 1gram dosed by vancomycin level (goal vanc level ___. Typical dosing was every other day. Please administer 1gm vancomycin with ___ morning exchange and let dwell for 6 hrs before draining. Then please check vancomycin level on ___ morning BEFORE vancomycin administration in order to re-dose for ___ administration. He will need IP vancomycin for 3 week course (last day ___. Renal and transplant surgery consults advised primary team to keep current PD catheter with plan to remove if he develops recurrent infection. His pain was initially controlled with tylenol and PRN oxycodone but he has not required narcotics for >24 hours at discharge. At discharge he continued w/ mild abdominal pain (___), which was a significant improvement compared to prior, but was afebrile and without peritoneal signs on exam. . . CHRONIC ISSUES: # ESRD on PD: Stable for past ___ years on PD. Renal follwed the patient and assisted with PD orders. He continued calcitriol and Nephrocaps. . # Asthma: Not using inhalers at baseline, but has been intermittently wheezy on exam during hospitalization. We was treated with albuterol/ipratropium nebs q6 prn SOB/wheezing. . # Hypertension: Continued metoprolol tartrate 25 mg PO BID. . @ Hyperlipidemia: Continued simvastatin 20 mg PO daily. . . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: # Please administer 1gm vancomycin with ___ morning exchange and let dwell for 6 hrs before draining. Then please check vancomycin level on ___ morning BEFORE vancomycin administration in order to re-dose for ___ administration. # Will need IP antibiotics for 3 weeks (last day of antibiotics will be ___. Goal vancomycin trough level ~15. # CODE: full, confirmed # EMERGENCY CONTACT: ___ (aunt) ___
summary: You were admitted for abdominal pain and found to have an infection in your peritoneum (a portion of the abdomen). You were treated with antibiotics and your pain improved. You will need to continue intraperitoneal antibiotics for three weeks, ending ___, and this will be administered with your peritoneal dialysis. The plan is to administer 1 gram of vancomycin with your ___ morning exchange and let dwell for 6 hrs before draining. Then you will have a vancomycin level on ___ morning BEFORE vancomycin administration in order to re-dose for ___ administration. This information will be included in the discharge summary to the nephrologist taking care of you.

text: Brief Hospital Course: BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female ESRD on HD, PVD, HTN, chronic abdominal pain, and asthma who smokes who was admitted for dyspnea and improved after treatment for a COPD exacerbation. BRIEF COURSE ACTIVE ISSUES: # DYSPNEA: Likely multifactorial in etiology, due to an exacerbation of her chronic systolic congestive heart failure and a COPD exacerbation. Pt initially presented to ___ with 4 days worsening dysnpea in the setting of cough and rhinorrhea. Upon presentation, she was hypoxic to 87% on RA requiring up to 4L by NC to achieve saturations between 95-100%. CXR demonstrated pulmonary vasculature fullness. BNP was 30360. She was placed on standing nebulizers, and she was dialyzed to her dry weight. On HD2, she was started on prednisone 60mg and a Z-pack for a 5 day course. Her breathing improved steadily, and on the day of discharge, her wheezing and dyspnea had improved. Pt needs formal PFTs. Follow up with pulmonary per appointment below. # NONSPECIFIC EKG CHANGES: Pt's EKG demonstrated new diffuse ST-T wave abnormalities with borderline prolonged QTc interval. Her troponins were at her baseline and remained stable x 3. CK was WNL. She was monitored on telemetry without incident. Repeat EKG on HD2 demonstrated less prominent ST-T wave abnormalitis, and she denied any chest pain throughout her stay. These changes were thought likely secondary to demand ischemia in the setting of slight volume overload. # UTI: Pt remained asymptomatic throughout her hospitalization. Her urine culture was growing pansenitive Klebsiella pneumoniae. She was started on cefpodoxime 200mg x 3 doses on the day of discharge. # ESRD: Pt received hemodialysis on her normal schedule (T, Th, ___ without complication. # DM TYPE 2: Pt's blood sugars remained on the low side of normal (80-100) but increased to 230s-250s after initiating prednisone. She was started on an insulin sliding scale on HD3 to control her blood sugars while in house. She will check her blood sugars at home. # CHRONIC ABDOMINAL PAIN: Stable. Can continue with home pain regimen and bowel regimen as above (fentanyl patch, percocet, colace, senna). EUS biopsy results demonstrated the following: gastric bx - changes consistent with chemical injury; duodenal bx - chronic active duodenitis, indefinite for dysplasia. Pt should follow up with her gastrointerologist. BRIEF COURSE CHRONIC ISSUES: # ANEMIA: Darbapoetin w/ HD. # HTN: Home amlodipine, metoprolol, and valsartan were continued with good control. # Schizoaffective disorder: Stable. Continued home amitriptyline, aripiprazole, citalopram, risperdal. # CODE: FULL. RECOMMENDATIONS AND TRANSITION OF CARE: 1. ADD Prednisone 60mg daily for 2 more days (last dose on ___. 2. ADD azithromycin 250mg daily for 2 more days (last dose on ___. 3. ADD Cefpodoxime 200mg. Take this ___ and ___ (___). 4. Advair 2 puffs twice per day. Albuterol Q4H PRN shortness of breath. 5. DM2 Monitoring: ___ with meals. Call doctor if your sugars > 300 or less than 75. 6. Weights QAM, call MD if weight goes up more than 3 lbs. 7. Follow up with GI specialist regarding EUS biopsy. 8. Follow up with Pulmonary for dyspnea and for formal PFTs. 9. Follow up with PCP after discharge at appointment listed.
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath, wheezing, and cough. Your chext x-ray suggested that there was fluid on your lungs, and a blood test confirmed this. Some extra fluid was taken off in dialysis, which helped reduce the fluid in your lungs. Your EKG also showed some nonspecific changes, so we did some blood tests to monitor your heart. These blood tests were normal. Given your history of smoking and your wheezing, we also treated you for an exacerbation of COPD. You were started on a 5 day course of azithromycin and prednisone. Your wheezing and shortness of breath improved. You will continue the azithromycin and prednisone at home as directed below. Please also use your nebulizers as needed for wheezing or shortness of breath. You were also found to have some bacteria growing in your urine. You were treated with an antibiotic called cefpodoxime.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is a ___ yo F with history of asthma and a recent hospitalization for angioedema of unknown etiology (discharged ___ who came to the ED after she developed a new itchy, red raised rash over her arms, legs and trunk, consistent with urticaria. Ms ___ was admitted for observation. She did not have any difficulty breathing with this episode nor did she developed worsening of facial swelling. LFTs were normal. CBC notable for leukocytosis consistent with prednisone use. Rash remained stable through hospitalization, and itching responded moderately well to Benadryl, Hydroxyzine and Sarna lotion. Allergy was consulted and set up outpatient ___ this week for further workup of etiology. Given non-progression of symptoms and stable vital signs, patient was deemed safe for discharge with close ___. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== [ ] Please schedule PCP ___ for the next week [ ] Allergy appointment with Dr. ___ on ___ at 10:30 am. [ ] Continue to take Benadryl, cetirizine and hydroxyzine for itching. Apply Sarna lotion as needed for itching as well. Space cetirizine and Benadryl by at least 3 hours because they act similarly. [ ] Continue prednisone taper as previously prescribed: 40 mg on ___, 20 mg on ___, 20 mg on ___ [ ] Should symptoms recur or worsen, go to ED immediately # CODE: FULL # CONTACT: mother, ___, ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Cetirizine 10 mg PO DAILY 2. Nexplanon (etonogestrel) 68 mg Other ___ 3. PredniSONE 60 mg PO DAILY Start: ___, First Dose: Next Routine Administration Time This is dose # 1 of 3 tapered doses 4. PredniSONE 40 mg PO DAILY Start: After 60 mg DAILY tapered dose This is dose # 2 of 3 tapered doses 5. PredniSONE 20 mg PO DAILY Start: After 40 mg DAILY tapered dose This is dose # 3 of 3 tapered doses 6. EPINEPHrine (EpiPEN) 0.3 mg IM ONCE MR1 7. Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN wheezing
summary: because you had developed a new whole body red itchy rash after recently being hospitalized for your throat closing up We checked some labs and found that the rest of your body is not affected. We gave you some medications to help with the rash and the itching. We monitored your breathing and vital signs closely to make sure that you are safe. Continue taking the prednisone as previously prescribed (40 mg on ___, 20 mg on ___, 20 mg on ___. Please have the epi pen with you at all times and use it as directed if you were to notice swelling in your throat. Take Benadryl, Cetirizine, hydroxyzine and use the sarna lotion as needed to help manage the itching. Please note that you should allow for at least 3 hours between cetirizine and Benadryl. Follow up with Dr. ___ in Allergy clinic on ___ at 10:30 am.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o female with CAD, asthma, COPD/emphysema, HFpEF, DM type II, and hx of who presents with approximately 1 hour of presyncope described as fairly acute onset of lightheadedness and generalized weakness in the setting of moderate exertion (for her) that was associated with dyspnea on exertion and mild/moderate wheezing, found to be volume overloaded likely ___ acute decompensated heart failure # Presyncope # Acute decompensated HFpEF Acute decompensated heart failure as evidenced by volume overload on exam and pulmonary vascular congestion on CXR. Other ddx included exercise-induced arrhythmias, orthostatic hypertension, and acute exacerbation of reactive airways disease in the setting of recent URI, however there were no events on telemetry, EKG unchanged, orthostatics negative. Normal BNP is difficult to interpret in the setting of obesity. Her dyspnea and generalized weakness improved with diuresis. Repeat CXR also showed improvement in extent of pulmonary edema. It is possible that her presyncope was secondary to overexertion in the setting of acute decompensated heart failure. TTE from earlier this year (___) showed a normal LVEF, with no significant valvular dysfunction, but did show LVH and a significant enlarged LA. . She was diuresed to a dry weight of 222 lbs and discharged on Lasix 20 mg daily. She was advised to increase her lasix if her weight went up by more than 3 lbs. She was discharged with ___. # Iatrogenic hyperthyroidism As above, levothyroxine dose was increased by ~30% in ___ after TSH was found to be 20. This admission, TSH is 0.24 and FT4 1.9. She was discharged on levothyroxine dose of 175 mcg but this should be 150 mcg and I emailed this on ___ to the PCP. # Asthma/Emphysema - continued home Advair; she has significant wheezing with ambulation for which I encouraged albuterol MDI or nebulizer use prior to activity. Chronic Problems # CAD - continued on home aspirin, ezetimibe, carvedilol, (not on home statin due to elevated CK and body aches per review of PCP and ___ notes) # HTN - continued home losartan, carvedilol, amiloride, and amlodipine # Hx of CVA w/ residual deficits & limited mobility - She received ___ evaluation and treatment during her hospital stay. # DM type II - she was continued on home lantus & Humalog insulin regimen w/ sliding scale Humalog TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Please follow up thyroid function tests on lower dose of levothyroxine.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you almost fainted while moderately exerting yourself. You have not been lightheaded during this hospitalization. You also had some increased shortness of breath, for which we gave you Lasix (a diuretic) to remove fluid in your lungs as well as treatments for your asthma. Please be sure to use albuterol (either inhaler or nebulizer) prior to walking. A nebulizer machine is being delivered to your house. Please check your weight daily. Your weight on discharge is 222 pounds. If your weight goes up by more than 3 pounds, please discuss increasing your Lasix dose with Dr ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with CHF, asthma, OSA, pericarditis on prednisone, and a prior PE on warfarin presenting with dyspnea and a nonproductive cough, likely due to an asthma exacerbation. She also had ___ with a Cr 1.5 and lactate up to 4 that improved with IV fluids. ============= ACTIVE ISSUES ============= # ASTHMA EXACERBATION: Presented to the ED with dyspnea. Found to have wheezing and rhonchi on pulmonary exam with no focal consolidation seen on CXR. Patient received duonebs, magnesium sulfate, and methylpred. patient started on 60 mg prednisone thereafter. O2 sats remained in the high ___, and dyspnea subsided on arrival to the floor. Of note on last discharge she had been on continuous oxygen. WHen she presented here she was off oxygen. She remained off of oxygen. On ambulation, her O2 sats were in the mid ___. Pt was discharged with salmeterol + fluticasone and predNasonex taper for further asthma management. She has an outpatient pulmonary followup on ___. # SUPRATHERAPEUTIC INR: INR 7.0 on admission. Given Vitamin K and was 3.7 the following morning. Held her home warfarin on admission. Discussed w/ ___ clinic, instructed Pt to take 5mg warfarin at discharge, followed by return to home dose before short followup on ___ with her PCP. Recommended discussion re: NoAC's. # PRERENAL ___: BUN/Cr at ___ (baseline 0.7) on admission, as well as lactate 4. Appeared dry on exam. Received 4L IVF, to improvement of her BUN/Cr (___) and lactate (1) on discharge. Lasix was held throughout hospitalization. Encouraged Pt to drink to satisfy her thirst at home over the next two days, as well as to monitor daily weights. She was instructed not to take her Lasix at discharge until she discussed further with PCP, or if she gained > 3 lbs in a day. ============== CHRONIC ISSUES ============== # CHF: Held Lasix given signs ___ improved with fluids suggesting prerenal azotemia. # PERICARDITIS: Continued prednisone # PE: Held warfarin during admission. Restarted at 5mg, and then home dose, on discharge. =================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== - Please consider whether this Pt should continue Lasix and stringent (< 1.5L) fluid restriction as patient presented with lactate 4.0 and Cr 1.5 - Dry weight reportedly around 175 lbs; her discharge weight was 182.5 lbs (82.8kg) but patient appearing dry on exam. - Please consider starting rivaroxaban when INR <3.0 in place of warfarin. - Witholding Lasix on discharge bc of admission with increased Cr and lactate as above - Started on Advair INH BID for better asthma control # CODE: FULL # CONTACT/HCP: ___ (son) ___
summary: You were seen in our hospital because ___ were having trouble breathing, which we believe was due to an asthma exacerbation. We treated your asthma with nebulizers and a higher dose of steroids while ___ were in the Emergency Department. We will send ___ home with an inhaler for your asthma (Advair), as well as a prednisone taper to gradually bring ___ down to the previous dose of prednisone that ___ had been taking. ___ also had some mild kidney damage when ___ came to us, possibly because ___ were very dehydrated. We gave ___ fluids through the IV, which fixed the damage. Going home, ___ can drink as much fluid as ___ want.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with a history of prior allergic reaction characterized by facial swelling who presents with one week of headache and fever and five days of rash, oral sores and lip/tongue/palate swelling. # Facial Swelling: She was initially transferred to the MICU for monitoring given her substantial facial swelling. She was evaluated by ENT who felt that her airway was not compromised and her swelling was mostly in the anterior mouth. Her facial and lip swelling was felt to represent an allergic reaction, possibly to demerol given the timing. She was placed on famotidine and solumedrol initially and her symptoms improved. She was then transitioned to prednisone for planned 5 day course. She was initially unable to eat but her diet was advanced without difficulty and she was tolerating a regular diet # HSV with Erythema Multiforme Major: She presented with a diffuse papular rash as well as some erythematous nodules, in addition to ulcerations in her mouth. She also had a 5 day history of intermittent fevers. This constellation of findings was felt to represent HSV infection with erythema multiforme major rash. HSV viral culture was sent and is pending at the time of discharge. She was treated with acyclovir and followed by dermatology who recommended bactroban to her EM rash as needed. She had a negative test for HIV, negative ___, and non-reactive RPR. She will complete her course of acyclovir after discharge, and she was given a refill in case she has further outbreaks. # Headache: She reported a headache on presentation which had been previously been evaluated with head CT and LP without concerning findings. It was felt to be related to her HSV infection and it improved during this hospitalization. # Asthma: She had mild wheezing at time during this admission and she was continued on her home flovent and albuterol. Transitional Issues: - Should see dermatology in follow-up - Pending tests: Mycoplasma Ab, HSV Ab, HSV/VZV culture
summary: You were admitted with facial swelling, rash, and fevers. It was felt that your facial swelling is due to an allergic reaction, possibly to the pain medication demerol. Please do NOT take this medication again. You should continue to take prednisone and famotidine for 3 more days for this reaction. In addition, you were found to have an infection in your mouth felt to be likely due to the herpes simplex virus. This virus can cause very painful ulcers in your mouth. You also had a rash on your body and feet that is a reaction to this virus. You are being given a prescription for a cream that you can use on your rash on your body and feet if it is painful. You are also being given a prescription for acyclovir to take for 8 more days to treat the infection. You are written for a refill on this prescription if you have another breakout at a later date.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr ___ is a ___ male with history of asthma on Advair presenting with 1 day of increasing wheezing, shortness of breath. . #. Asthma exacerbation: Presentation likely secondary to asthma flare triggered by URI/allergic rxn, further bolstered by mild eosinophila of 4% and recent exposure to dust allergens. Other contributoring factors/triggers could include PNA, however no fever, nl WBC, no infiltrate on CXR. Volume status, difficult to assess however patient without h/o of CHF, no vascular congestion on CXR making cardiac etiology to wheeze/SOB unlikely. Patient with habitus c/w OSA which likely contributed to overalld shortness of breath but not causative factor of presentation. Patient receive solumedral in the ED. On the floor patient received 2gm IV Mg started on a prednisone with plan to complete a ten day taper ___ - ___, nebulizer treatments (Q6hr standing, Q2 as needed), flonase as well as home Advair. Daily peak flow was measured, at time of discharge: 350. Discharged on 14day prednisone taper. salmetrol 50 1 puff BID, fluticasone 220 1 puff BID, singular, flonase, albuterol nebulizer and inhalers. . # Depression. Patient recently hospitalizated for depression/anxiety. Overall feeling that depressive syx improving. No SI. Appropriate affect on exam. SW consulted. Continued outpatient regimen of Celexa, trazadone. Restarted clonidine for treatment of anxiety as well as hypertension. SW consult . # EtOH abuse. Per patient sober for 1 month. No h/o DTs. Social work consulted. . # HTN. Normotensive in house. Restarte home clonidine 0.1mg TID. . # Diabetes, type 2 uncontrolled. Previously on metformin. HgA1c: 6.3. Currently diet-controlled. Sugars were monitored and patient placed on insulin sliding scale while on prednisone. ___ ranged from 180-320. Discussed need for insulin treamtment during taper. Decision to forego sending patient out in insulin. Will plan to follow-up with PCP regarding diabetic regimen and need for re-initiation of oral therapy. . # Facial Rash/Seborrheic dermatitis. Likely seborrhoeic dermatitis. Started on ketoconazole 2% with improvement. Discharged with bottle ketoconazole prn. . # Tobacco use. Started on nicotine patch and educated on the importance of smoking cessation especially in an individual with asthma. Patient interested in smoking cessation. Discharged with month's supply of patches. Instructed not to smoking when using patch. Will follow-up with PCP for further smoking cessation assistance.
summary: you were admitted to the ___ for treatment of an asthma flare that was likely triggered by a combination of both allergies an an upper respiratory infection. While hospitalized you received systemic steroids and nebulizer treatments and your breathing improved. It will be important to finish your prednisone taper as well as nebulizer treatments. In addition, it is utmost importance that you stop smoking. You were discharged with one months worth of nicotine patchs. Regarding your high blood pressure we restarted you on your home clonidine and your pressures were well controlled while hospitalized.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx of asthma, OSA, obesity p/w increasing dyspnea, found to have asthma exacerbation ___ influenza. # Acute Asthma Exacerbation ___ Influenza: The pt presented with complaints of worsening dyspnea, especially on exertion, wheezing, myalgias, cough, sneezing and rhinorrhea x5 days, with close family sick contacts. She attempted treatment with nebs of increasing frequency without relief. She presented to the ED and was found to have cxr without pna. Symptoms improved with methylpred/azithro/nebs however not to her baseline. Influenza A returned positive however the pt was out of the window for oseltamivir. The pt was treated with increased nebs, azithromycin x5 days, and home singulair, (flovent/foradil switched to advair while inpatient). She was also treated with a pulse dose of prednisone 60mg daily, and started on a rapid taper (50mg on the day of discharge, with plans for a daily decrease of 10mg). Admission was prolonged due to persistent fatigue and desaturation with ambulation. At time of discharge, pt was 89-90% on RA with ambulation, but 97-98% with 2L nc. Given pt has O2 at home, she was discharged with instructions to use O2 with ambulation, and for ___ to check daily ambulatory sats. # DM: Pt with well controlled diabetes (last hga1c 6.7%) one metformin 500mg daily. During admission, blood sugars were elevated due to prednisone. The pt was started on lantus for better glycemic control. Given her plan to continue steroid usage post-discharge, the pt was discharged with a ___ to administer 10u lantus daily while on prednisone. She was also instructed to check her FSG TID and notify her PCP of glucose readings >350. # HTN: continued amlodipine, diovan, metoprolol, hctz # CKD: Cr 1.1, improved from baseline 1.2-1.3 # Anemia: at 32, around baseline. Pt is a Jehovah's witness. Continued home ferrous sulfate. # Anxiety/depression: Continued home fluoxetine, xanax # OSA/CPAP: continued cpap at night # Fungal infection in back skin folds: pt was treated with miconazole powder # cns aneurysm with ___ s/p coil ___: stable # Morbid obesity: Pt has been trying to find bariatric programs that will take her given she is a Je___s witness. # Non resolving bandlike opacity in the right upper lobe may represent scarring from prior pneumonia but should be evaluated by non-urgent CT as neoplasm cannot be excluded. Transitional Issues - ___ to follow and tx with lantus 10u daily while on predisone - Pt to use home O2 with ambulation until O2 sats >92% - f/u Non resolving bandlike opacity in the right upper lobe may represent scarring from prior pneumonia but should be evaluated by non-urgent CT as neoplasm cannot be excluded.
summary: You were admitted for worsening shortness of breath and found to have the flu. You were treated with steroids, nebulizers and antibiotics in addition to your home medications with improvement in your symptoms. Your oxygenation has been good while at rest, however you continue to require 2 liters of oxygen while walking around. Please use your home oxygen when moving around the house until your visiting nurse tells you your oxygen saturations have improved. While on steroids, your blood sugars have been very high, so you have been treated with insulin. A visiting nurse ___ come to your house to give you a dose of long acting insulin every day while you are on steroids. In addition, you should check your blood sugars three times a day.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old female with past medical history of CAD (discovered of cath for work up of AVR), and AVR presenting with palpitations/dizziness and found to be in atrial flutter vs AVNRT. 1) Supraventricular Tachycardia: Unclear exact etiology. Regular SVT with irregular baseline that was somewhat regular appearing but very difficult to appreciate Aflutter vs AVNRT. Seen by ___ cardiology attending who thought this was most likely flutter though impossible to tell at this point. The patient received diltiazem 15 mg IV followed by 30 mg PO and remained in sinus rhythm thereafter. Her metoprolol succinate dose was increased from 200 mg to 300 mg daily. She was also started on coumadin with a goal INR of ___. She will follow up with the ___ clinic. Plan is for cardiology follow up in about a month at which time her primary cardiologist may consider an ablation. TFT's were checked and were not suggestive of hyperthyroidism. Most likely precipitating factor for SVT was thought to be viral illness and possible dehydration. 2)Persistent cough/ ?Asthma flare/ Dyspnea on exertion: The patient has had somewhat chronic respiratory symptoms since prior to her AVR but reports over the past weeks she has had much more irritating cough. No purulent sputum or high grade fevers to suggest pneumonia and the patient had wheezing suggesting post-viral asthma flare. She was continued on her home fluticasone-salmeterol and montleukast as well as given albuterol with reasonable control but still she complained of cough and shortness of breath. Therefore, as she still had wheezing she was given prednisone 40 mg PO daily with plan for a five day burst. 3) Fever: She was afebrile in house and work up not suggestive of concerning bacterial infection. Most likely etiology of fevers was thought to be a viral URI. Blood cultures were taken given new bioprosthetic valve and should these turn positive she will be instructed to return. 4) Hypertension: This was well controlled on her home ACEi, diuretic, and BB. 5) CAD: Nonobstructing disease found on cath in preparation for valvular surgery, she was continued on her ASA, BB, and ACEi. 6)Hypothyroidism: She was continued on her home levothyroxine. 7)Chronic chest pain: She was continued on hydrococodone-APAP for her likely chest wall pain s/p procedure She received subcutaneous heparin for DVT prophylaxis. She tolerated a full diet. She will follow up with ___ ___ clinic for an INR check on ___.
summary: You were admitted because of shortness of breath and palpitations you were having and were found to be in a fast heart rate called AVRNT. You were given a medicine and your heart rate returned to normal. We watched you overnight and though your heart had occasional irregular beats throughout that night these are not usually significant. You are being discharged on a larger amount of your usual heart rate controller medication as well as a blood thinner to help prevent stroke in the context of the irregular heart rate. You also were continuing to have intermittent shortness of breath and cough after having a likely cold last week. This is probably due to a flare of your asthma in the context of a viral infection.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # SOB/cough: SOB and wheezing consistent with rebound asthma exacerbation after discontinuation of PO steroids, but pt also had productive cough so was initially treated for both asthma and PNA with steroids, azithromycin, and albuterol and atrovent nebs. On morning after admission, patient lung exam had diffuse, moderate to severe wheezing, so frequency of nebs was increased. Her respiratory status improved quickly. She weaned from 2L NC to RA overnight. Her cough also improved significantly and was no longer productive. She was given 60 PO prednisone in ED and on HD#2, with plan for prolonged, 14-day taper after discharge. At time of discharge, patient was satting well on RA and was without respiratory distress. # Chest pain: Pt had episode of brief, L-sided CP associated with worsening SOB. The pain resolved without intervention and the SOB resolved w/ duonebs. EKG without signs of ischemia and trops negative x 2. Likely MSK in origin given recent respiratory distress and significant cough. CP did not recur. # RA: stable, chronic. Received Percocet for pain # Psoriasis: stable, chronic. Hydroxyzine and doxepin for pruritis # HTN: stable, chronic. Continued dyazide # Diabetes: well-controlled, non-insulin dependent, no complications. Continued metformin # Anxiety/depression: stable, chronic. Continued home clonazepam, doxepin, lamotrigine. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: # Please discuss w/ PCP when to restart Humira
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath and cough, which we believe was due to a worsening of you asthma. We treated your asthma with oxygen, nebulized treatments, steroids (prednisone), and antibiotics (azithromycin). Your breathing improved greatly with these treatments, and by the time you were discharged you were breathing comfortably without oxygen. We believe that your asthma exacerbation was caused by a viral illness. Because antibiotics due not treat viruses, you no longer need antibiotics. We added prednisone to your medications. You will take this medication for twelve more days. You will slowly reduce the amount you take each day. You will get a bottle of 10mg tablets. You will take between one and six tablets each day. If you are taking more than one tablet per day, you should take all the tablets for that day at once in the morning. Here are instructions on how many tablets to take each day: ___: take six tablets (60mg) ___: take four tablets (40mg) ___: take two tablets (20mg) ___: take one tablet (10mg) You should not take Humira again until you see your primary care doctor.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is a ___ y/o woman w/ PMH of HTH, diverticulosis, asthma, and hiatal hernia s/p repair in ___ presents w/ acute abdominal pain, N/V and BRBPR suddenly at 9PM on ___. She underwent CT scan concerning for distal transverse and descending colitis. Given sudden onset, potentially concerning for ischemic colitis vs possible infection or IBD. ACUTE ISSUES: #Abdominal Pain #Nausea/vomiting #Transverse and descending colitis (splenic flexure region) Given sudden onset, concerning for ischemic colitis vs possible infection or IBD. She does have documented hx of PVD, but the pain was not associated with food and she lacks further co-morbidities or pro-embolic disorders. WBC elevated may point to infection, but may be reactive to the colitis as is the original lactate elevation. Family hx of colitis uncertain, but may be UC if chronic, and IBD may be seen in elderly ages as a bimodal distribution. Most likely ___ ischemic colitis following asymptomatic BP drops undetected at home given sudden hypotensive response to Lisinopril. She was aggressively resuscitated with IVF, and pain/nausea controlled with morphine transitioned to oxycodone and Zofran. She was started on a 7 day abx course w/ ciprofloxacin and Flagyl PO. She was encouraged to maintain strong PO intake and diet advancement to keep up with continued diarrhea output and to restore to pre-hospital status, which she attained prior to d/c. CHRONIC ISSUES: #Hypertension: Held home lisinopril due to episode of hypotension crash following administration. Please do not restart until demonstration of stabilizing pressures and perhaps consider lower dose given potentially may have contributed to ischemic colitis. #Hyperlipidemia: Continued home simvastatin #Peripheral vascular disease: Continued home ASA TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: #New medications: -Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO q12h (to complete 7 day course on ___ -Metronidazole 500mg PO TID (to complete 7 day course on ___ #Stopped medications: -Lisinopril 20mg PO daily [] Do not restart Lisinopril until confirming her BP is higher consistently and she is able to tolerate lowering. We attempted to restart this dose with a subsequent fall in BP and triggered likely repeat bout of ischemic colitis, which may be occurring at home without signs (contributing to ischemic colitis) [] Expect lingering pulmonary edema for a temporary period following d/c, which she is expected to self-resolve. Please follow up CXR as an outpatient to ensure resolution. Diuresis deferred during hospitalization given recent likely ischemic colitis. [] Please get a colonoscopy as an outpatient to evaluate colitis. [] Please consider GI referral for management of likely ischemic colitis. [] Full code (presumed) [] Contact: ___ (Husband, HCP): ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN wheezing/ SOB 2. Famotidine 20 mg PO BID 3. Lisinopril 20 mg PO DAILY 4. Pravastatin 10 mg PO MWF 5. Flovent Diskus (fluticasone) 250 mcg/actuation inhalation ASDIR 6. Polyethylene Glycol 17 g PO QOD 7. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were hospitalized because of a sudden episode of sudden abdominal pain out of proportion to something you've experienced before in addition to excessive vomiting and heaves which did not improve immediately. Pictures were taken that showed that you had inflammation in a part of your intestines called colitis. Blood pressure readings proved to initially be low, suggestive of excessive lowering than anticipated due to your blood pressure medications. This can predispose you to develop a lack of adequate blood to your bowel as a cause of your colitis called ischemic colitis. You were started on aggressive fluids to replenish your blood pressure and volume, and on medications called anitibotics to help cover potential bacteria which may have played a role in the inflammation.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with h/o asthma and allergic rhinitis p/w SOB, worsening cough, chest tightness, intermittent wheeze, and pleuritic CP. # Asthma exacerbation: She presented with shortness of breath, cough, yellow sputum production, and chills without fevers. Her shortness of breath was not responsive to outpatient therapy with steroids and Z-Pak. She did not have fevers or evidence of pneumonia. The trigger for exacerbation is unclear but may be ___ viral illness, enviromental irritants, anxiety. She was treated with oral prednisone (40mg to complete 7 day course on ___, and was continued on her azithromycin for planned 5-day course until ___, as well as her home ipratropium and albuterol nebulizers. She received benzonatate and codeine-guiafenesin as needed for cough. # GERD: stable, continued ranitidine 300mg daily # Constipation: stable. continued senna bid prn. # Supplements: continued ascorbic acid, multivitamin, vitamin D # Primary prevention: continued ASA 81mg daily TRANSITIONAL ISSUES # Ms. ___ was admitted for asthma exacerbation, and was treated with a total 7-day course of steroid therapy (prednisone 40mg daily ending ___, 5-day course of azithromycin (ending ___, and her nebulizers, and advair. Please follow up on her shortness of breath and alter her asthma regimen as needed. # Her CXR showed a small new nodular focus projecting over the right upper lung, possibly a lung nodule. We recommend obtaining repeat chest x-ray in ___ weeks, or chest CT as appropriate in the outpatient setting. # Communication: Patient # Emergency Contact: ___ (son): ___. Husband ___ ___. ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Albuterol 0.083% Neb Soln 1 NEB IH Q4H:PRN dyspnea 2. Albuterol Inhaler ___ PUFF IH Q4H:PRN dyspnea 3. Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID 4. Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (250/50) 1 INH IH BID 5. Ipratropium Bromide Neb 1 NEB IH Q6H:PRN wheezing 6. Ranitidine 300 mg PO DAILY 7. Ascorbic Acid ___ mg PO DAILY 8. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 9. Vitamin D 400 UNIT PO DAILY 10. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY 11. Senna 1 TAB PO BID:PRN constipation 12. Guaifenesin-CODEINE Phosphate ___ mL PO Q6H:PRN cough
summary: You were admitted for asthma exacerbation, and were treated with nebulizer treatments, steroids (oral prednisone), antibiotics (azithromycin), and cough medications (guaifenisin, benzonatate). Your breathing improved, and at the time of discharge you had some cough and mild wheezing but no shortness of breath. Please take your albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers three times a day, up to every 4 hours as needed for shortness of breath. Please continue to take prednisone 40mg daily until ___. Please continue to take azithromycin 250mg daily until ___. Your chest x-ray showed a small round nodule in the right upper lung.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ ___ speaking female w/ pacer for slow rvr, CHF, remote h/o colon CA presents with cough found to have RUL mass concerning for invasive mucinuous adenocarcinoma and reactive airway disease. She was treated for acute bronchitis with steriods, albuterol/ipratropium, and azithromycin and improved over her 3 day hospital course. She opted for outpatient management of the lung mass with her family. Active Problems: #Cough- patient's chief complaint, appears to have a component of bronchospasm on exam which may represent new infection, a pareneoplastic or mass effect of the tumor in RUL, or an environmental process, may be actue bronchitis following URI though pt does not have classic symtoms of this, may be influenza as well though pt has no fevers or chills or muscle aches. Also consider cardic asthma if patient does not improve on reactive airway disease treatment, chf less likely with bnp <6. Start and continue prednisone 20mg for 5 days. Continue albuterol/ipratorpium nebs that were weaned to inhalers. She received dextromethrophan/guani for cough. Tested for influenza dfa- can't be interpreted and negative viral culture. Increased diskus to 250/50 and we continued her furosemide 20mg daily, although she was apparently on 40mg at night and 20 in the day at home. #Dypsnea: She says she is at baseline, pulmonary embolism was ruled out, no pneumonia on ct chest, no effusions, does not appear to be chf exacerbation since low bnp therefore it is likely multifactorial will continue to monitor and treat as above for cough. Outpatient pfts and consult with pulmonary. #Increased INR- 3.8 on discharge so coumadin 2.5 mg was held throughout hospitalization and will be rechecked on ___ for coumadin readjustment. #Lung mass- Discussed with patient and she will opt for outpatient management at this time. They understand that this is very likely cancer although it existed in the past and appears to be slow growing. #Bowel: Patient on docusate and gave script for senna since patient requested something for her bowels for "privacy of her own home." #Falls- lives in ___ home alone- but elderly people and she has help at home, doesn't want to move in with family. Patient refused tetanus. Inactive Problems: #Afib - holding coumadin as above #HTN - cont amlodpine - cont valsartan #HLD - cont atorvastatin Transitional issues: -Coumadin- elevated INR 3.8 on ___- holding till recheck on ___ -Lasix dose only 20 in hospital switched back to home dose on d/c, monitor volume status and titrate lasix dose - PFTS and pulm f/u outpt- inc diskus to 250/50 for interim and added albuterol - lung mass consistent w/ invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma to be decided by outpt management discussed with patient, son and daughter in law CODE: DNR/DNI confirmed with patient COMMUNICATION Patient and Daughter in law - ___ ___
summary: We evaluated you for your cough and shortness of breath. We found you to have wheezing. We treated you with antibiotics, medicines to help your breathing, and steriods. You have improved while you are in the hospital. The ct scan that was done in the ER showed a mass in your right upper lobe of the lung that is very concerning for invasive lung cancer. We discussed this with you and your family and believe that it can be mananged as an outpatient. We checked your INR and it was 4.9 so we stopped your coumadin. We need you to have it checked on ___ by ___ as you normally would so your dosage can be adjusted by the doctor there.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with asthma, bronchiectasis, ectodermal dysplasia, humoral immunity deficiency NOS on IVIG trial presenting with worsening dyspnea and productive cough. # Bronchiectasis exacerbation: Her presentation was more consistent with subacute to worsening chronic infectious process ___ setting of bronchiestasis and humoral deficiency NOS. CT Chest significant for interval worsening of bronchiectatic process, sputum cultures growing MSSA. Initially started on empiric broad coverage with Vancomycin, Cefepime and Doxycycline then based on IDs recommendations narrowed to ceftriaxone and doxycycline. Of note had allergic reaction to vancomycin, see details below. Was continued on ceftriaxone and doxycycline until discharge with clinical improvement, she was discharged on cefpodoxime and doxycycline to complete a 10-day course of antibiotics. Negative broad work-up including PJP smear, Cryptococcal serum Ag, Histoplasma Urine Ag, Aspergillus GM and Ab. Beta-D-glucan was positive which was thought to be related to her receiving IVIG. Had 3x induced sputum for AFB smears which were negative and culture which were pending at the time of discharge. #Iron deficiency anemia: MCV 73, ferritin 22, TRF 333, serum iron 25, TIBC 433, %Sat 5.4%. Started Ferrous sulfate 325mg PO BID w/ondansetron 4mg PO q8hr:PRN for nausea with iron. Nutrition saw the patient as an inpatient as well. #Humoral immunodeficiency: previously followed as an outpatient by Dr. ___ at ___. Received IVIG on ___, will have outpatient f/u. # Asthma exacerbation: Given significant wheezing and dyspnea she received prednisone 50mg ___ the hospital with a taper (decrease by 10mg each week) as well as albuterol/ipratropium nebs q4-6h and prn. Tiotropium, montelukast and ceterizine were continued. As symbicort was NF, she was bridged with Advair while ___ house. She was discharged on albuterol nebulizers and with a new nebulizer machine. # GERD: Continued on home omeprazole TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ================== - patient has switched insurance and is no longer eligible for ___ outpatient primary care - Her new PCP is ___ at ___ -> she needs a referral to see a new pulmonologist within ___ weeks of discharge from ___ - she received her dose of IVIG ___ house on ___ - she received pneumococcal vaccine on ___ - she received home nebulizer machine and was discharged w/nebs - pending infectious studies should be followed up by PCP and pulmonologist FULL CODE Fiance, ___ ___ HCP is sister, ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Tiotropium Bromide 1 CAP IH DAILY 2. LORazepam 0.5 mg PO DAILY:PRN anxiety 3. Orsythia (levonorgestrel-ethinyl estrad) 0.1-20 mg-mcg oral DAILY 4. triamcinolone acetonide 55 mcg nasal DAILY 5. Montelukast 10 mg PO DAILY 6. budesonide-formoterol 160-4.5 mcg/actuation inhalation BID 7. Omeprazole 20 mg PO BID 8. Cetirizine 10 mg PO DAILY 9. albuterol sulfate 90 mcg/actuation inhalation Q4H:PRN wheezing/dyspnea 10. Sodium Chloride Nasal ___ SPRY NU BID:PRN nasal congestion 11. levonorgestrel-ethinyl estrad 0.1-20 mg-mcg oral DAILY
summary: You came to ___ because of increasing shortness of breath, wheezing and cough producing yellow sputum with increasing need for inhalers as well as a fever that "broke" prior to admission. When you came to the hospital, we took a chest X-Ray that was concerning for a pneumonia ___ several spots ___ both lungs. While you were ___ the hospital, we treated you for both a bronchiectasis and an asthma flare and started you on antibiotics and 60mg of prednisone. Cultures of your sputum identified a bacteria called Staph aureus and you were continued on the appropriate antibiotics for a total of 8 days (___). We also contacted our colleagues ___ pulmonology and infectious disease and they suggested additional tests for less common infections that were all negative.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with history of noninsulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, hypertension/hyperlipidemia, and extensive psychiatric history who presented from ___ with chest pain. Active Issues: (1)Chest pain: Despite low suspicion for primary cardiac etiology in the setting of young age, female gender, multiple reassuring EKGs, and negative cardiac enzymes, pharmacologic stress test was performed for more definitive exclusion of coronary artery disease, resulting in no anginal-type symptoms or ischemic EKG changes; 2-day cardiac perfusion study was similarly negative (though extensive attenuation and motion artifact was present), with increased left ventricular cavity size with normal systolic function. CXR on admission was negative for focal infiltrate while CTA in the setting of concurrent back pain was negative for dissection and proximal pulmonary embolus. There were no events on telemetry. Ultimately, given reproducibility on palpation, musculokeletal etiology in the setting of recent fall was suspected, as was gastrointestinal etiology in the setting of concurrent postprandial right upper quadrant pain as below. Organic pain likely was augmented by significant anxiety. She received pain control and anxiolytics as needed. (2)Right upper quadrant abdominal pain: She experienced intermittent right upper quadrant abdominal pain, typically preceded by meals. Although CT abdomen/pelvis on the day prior to the current admission was negative for intraabdominal, including gallbladder pathology, concern remained for gallbladder cholic. Right upper quadrant ultrasound was negative for gallbladder pathology, and frequency of painful episodes decreased with restriction to low-fat diet. Outpatient gastrointestinal and/or surgical follow-up is advised in the event that she continues to experience symptoms, with consideration of HIDA scan if indicated. (3)Mental health: She has an extensive psychiatric history, including bipolar disorder, major depressive disorder, suicidal ideation with wrist cutting, posttraumatic stress disorder, and borderline personality disorder, and is presently admitted to ___ under ___, having presented reportedly voluntarily for suicidal ideation. She denied suicidal/homicidal ideation throughout admission and did not require a 1:1 sitter. Home psychiatric regimen, including Abilify, Cogentin, Luvox, lamotrigine, and Topamax, was continued without modification. She was evaluated by the psychiatry service and was not felt to require further inpatient psychiatric care, with discharge home advised. (4)Status post fall: She experienced a mechanical fall, striking the right side of her head without loss of consciousness, on ambulation to the bathroom on hospital day 4. Detailed neurologic exam was nonfocal and head exam atraumatic/normocephalic at that time, hence noncontrast head CT was deferred. She remained neurologically intact throughout admission. (5)Gastroesophageal reflux: Home omeprazole was increased from 20mg to 40mg daily, without significant relief of chest or right upper quadrant abdominal pain, hence she was discharged on home dose of 20mg daily. Inactive Issues: (1)Asthma: Home Symbicort and albuterol were continued. (2)Hypertension: Although she carries a history of hypertension, she remained normotensive on the floor off antihypertensives; she does not appear to be on a home antihypertensive regimen at present. (3)Hyperlipidemia: Home Zocor was continued. (4)Hypothyroidism: Home Synthroid was continued. (5)Noninsulin-dependent diabetes mellitus: Home Actos was held in favor of gentle Humalog insulin sliding scale, with Actos resumed at discharge. Transitional Issues: - Outpatient gastrointestinal and/or surgical follow-up is advised for right upper quadrant pain, with consideration of HIDA scan if clinically indicated. - Pending studies: None. - Code status: Full.
summary: As you know, you were admitted for chest/upper abdominal pain, back pain, shortness of breath, and lightheadedness. Multiple studies of your heart, lungs, abdomen, and major blood vessels showed no evidence of injury/dysfunction. Your stress test of the heart showed that your heart is okay. It is likely that your symptoms related to residual pain from your recent fall, exacerbated by anxiety. Given that your chest and abdominal pain seemed to relate to meals, further evaluation of your gastrointestinal tract may be needed in the outpatient setting. However, you did have a CT scan and an ultrasound of your abdomen, both of which showed NO gallbladder problems, NO acute abdominal problems. In the meantime, you are advised to eat a low-fat diet to minimize recurrence of pain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with a history of DM, asthma 2 weeks s/p recent prednisone taper, on home oxygen chronically, who presented with 1 week DOE and wheezing. Presented to ___ clinic and found to be in mild/mod distress so she was sent to ___ ED. Symptoms seemed triggered by preceding URI and allergy flare. Given continued low peak flow obtained in the ED and smoking history, there was high suspicion for COPD component to symptoms. Patient was treated empirically with duonebs, initially at q4h and spaced as tolerated. She received a prednisone burst along with Mg, with the intention of continuing a regimen for 5 days of prednisone 40 mg qd. She was also empirically given azithromycin 250 mg qd with the intention of completing a 4 day course. Her peak flows at discharge was 300 mL (from 125) and ambulatory sats were measured at time of discharge was 90% on room air. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - recommend outpatient follow-up with orthopedics for knee pain thought to be osteoarthritis - complete prednisone and azithromycin courses - patient discharged on new medication cetirizine and ketotifen eye drops for allergy symptom control # Code: FULL (confirmed w/ pt) # Emergency Contact: ___ (daughter) ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were found to have significant difficulty breathing and wheezing in the setting of a recent viral illness and possible triggers by allergies. You were evaluated with bloodwork and imaging and it was discovered that you most likely did not have a pneumonia or significant infection, but rather an exacerbation of your baseline asthma. You were determined not to have an acute infection that was linked to your knee pain. You were treated with nebulizers and steroid medication, after which your breathing continued to improve. Because of the renewed wheezing symptoms you are experiencing, we will have you complete a short 5-day course of steroids following discharge from the hospital. To further better control of your baseline allergies, we have also provided an allergy medication as well as eye drops for you to take daily along with your Flonase.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with ___ notable for PUD s/p Bilroth II, CAD s/p quadruple bypass, asthma sent from PCP with melena and found to be orthostatic. # Melena/BRBPR: Initial ddx included erosions/ulcers vs post-op ischemia - if this represents an upper GI bleed. On the other hand, he gives a history of sucralfate induced constipation that led to straining and large volume bright red blood once a few days ago (hemorrhoidal v diverticular). Patient underwent serial Hcts which showed a drop on admission from prior outpatient Hct but was stable throughout the hospital admission. He had no recurrence of BRBPR or melena though did have a guaiac positive stool. Patient maintained on BID IV PPI and his carafate was held. Upper endoscopy and colonoscopy were performed. Colonoscopy showed a prominent vein which may have bleed (BRBPR) with straining. Patient instructed/Rx provided for improved bowel reg to prevent straining. Upper endoscopy showed severe gastritis likely ___ bile reflux from previous Bilroth II. Patient was discharged with Rx for bowel reg, continue Carafate, BID PPI. While initial report recommended reglan and to consider ursodial, per conversation with GI, preference was to hold Ursodial until trial as outpatient if needed. Additionally, as reglan interacts with sertraline, pt was started on erythromycin instead at discharge per GI verbal instructions. # Orthostasis: Patient noted to be orthostatic at ___ office prior to admission. Likely ___ to acute blood loss (see above). Was not orthostatic prior to discharge. Chronic issues: # IBS: Held home Librax as NF. Restarted at d/c. # Asthma: Continued home albuterol PRN, flovent, ipratropium # HLD: Continued home zetia, rosuvastatin # CAD: Continued metoprolol, ASA # Depression: Continued home sertraline # Chronic pain: Continued home gabapentin # Health maintance: Continued MVI, Omega 3; Held phytosterol as NF - restarted at discharge. Transitional issues: -CODE: DNR/DNI (confirmed with pt and reports that wife is aware) -EMERGENCY CONTACT: ___ (wife) ___ ___ -Pathology pending at discharge but now finalized: Gastric mucosal biopsies: Oxyntic mucosa with changes of marked reactive (chemical-type) gastropathy -Will f/u with GI and will likely need repeat EGD in 8 weeks.
summary: You were admitted with hypotension when changing position (called orthostasis) and black stools and bleeding with a bowel movement. The black stools also represent bleeding in your GI tract. The hypotension was also due to the bleeding that you had. We monitored your blood counts closely and they remained stable, though they were lower than is normal for you. We consulted the gastroenterology service who performed a colonoscopy and upper endoscopy. They found severe gastritis. Your colonscopy showed no evidence of bleeding but you did have prominent veins, which could bleed if you strain to have a bowel movement.

text: Brief Hospital Course: HOSPITAL COURSE This is a ___ woman with a pmhx significant for ETOH abuse, HTN, and asthma who was admitted for hematemesis. EGD demonstrated ___ tear which was injected with epinephrine. . # UPPER GI BLEED: EGD demonstrates ___ tear with new clot, likely from repeated vomiting and wretching. Patient was scoped urgently in the MICU, and tear was eventually injected with epi. Two clips were placed initially but dislodged. Patient received 1 unit and remained hemodynamically stable with resolution of tachycardia. She was converted to a PPI BID from a PPI gtt. Hct stable on discharge. . # HYPERTENSION: Patient treated for hypertension as outpatient, likely a combination of essential hypertension and ETOH abuse. Anti-hypertensives were held in the setting of GI bleed, but restarted once patient was stabilized. . # ASTHMA: Advair and albuterol were continued. Zafirlukast was changed to forumulary montelukast. . # ETOH ABUSE: It was recommended that patient see substance abuse counselor in the hospital. Social work discussed alcohol cessation with the patient. She was given information regarding AA meetings and other services in her home town. She states she was committed to cutting down on her alcohol use. She was discharged on thiamine and folic acid supplementation. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:] # Code: Full # Follow-up: PCP and AA ___ on Admission: 1. albuterol sulfate 0.63 mg/3 mL Solution neb 1 q6hr for SOB 2. albuterol sulfate HFA ___ puffs(s) q4hrs 3. cephalexin [Keflex] 500 mg Capsule 4 Capsule(s) by mouth ONCE Please 30 minutes prior to any dental procedure for the next ___ years 4. diltiazem HCl 240 mg Capsule, ER qdaily 5. ergocalciferol (vitamin D2) 50,000 unit qweekly x 8 wks 6. fluticasone-salmeterol [Advair Diskus] 250 mcg-50 mcg/Dose BID 7. hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg Tablet qdaily 8. nitroglycerin 0.4 mg Tablet, SL for CP 9. omeprazole 20 mg Capsule,1 qday 10. prochlorperazine maleate 5 mg ___ Tablet by mouth nausea 11. tramadol 50 mg Tablet ___ Tablet(s) by mouth q6 hours pain 12. trazodone 50 mg Tablet ___ Tablet by mouth for insomnia 13. zafirlukast 20 mg Tablet 1 twice a day 14. docusate sodium 100 mg bid 15. loratadine 10 mg Tablet qdaily
summary: You were admitted after several episodes of vomiting blood. An upper endoscopy demonstrated a small tear in your esophagous most likely secondary to frequent vomitting. This is a common complication of alcohol abuse. You were hospitalized in the medical intensive care unit due to the severity of your initial presentation. We applaud your decision to cut down on your drinking. This will be a difficult process and we encourage you to completely stop drinking in order to further prevent medical complications of alcohol abuse. One of our social workers worked with you while you were admitted to assist you with continuing your efforts to cut down on drinking once you are home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response: Patient noted to be tachycardic with an irregularly irregular rhythm in the ED. EKG confirmed atrial flutter. ACS was felt to be unlikely given negative troponins x 2. She was evaluated for a PE, and had an indeterminate CTA and declined LENIs. She was started for rate control initially with diltiazem. An echo was performed. Echo demonstrated mild atrial dilation, a small ASD, and normal LV function with an LVEF of >55%. Given the finding of hyperthyroidism, she was changed to Propanolol, initially 40 mg TID, for rate control and also for treatment of hyperthyroidism. Anticoagulation was discussed given her CHADS score of 2, however, it was felt that the necessary family discussion of risks/benefits of this treatment, as well as likely compliance and monitoring, should be postponed for outpatient consideration. She was started on ASA 325 mg. Rate improved somewhat with medication, by HD #4, under 90 consistently. # Hyperthyroidism: TSH found to be less than 0.02 in the ED as part of work up for her new Afib. On history the patient/family endorsed weight loss, neck pain, palpitations, and a feeling of "dancing." The differential included thyroiditis, Grave's disease, and a toxic thyroid nodule. Endocrinology was consulted and thyroid studies were performed, demonstrating normal T3 and T4 but increased T4 index and free T4. Anti-TPO antibodies were negative, as were Anti-Thyroglobulin Antibodies, making autoimmune thyroiditis less likely. A thyroid ultrasound demonstrated no evidence of thyroiditis, an enlarged R lower retrothyroid LN vs enlarged R lower parathyroid gland, as well as a L upper thyroid lobe nodule. PTH was found to be normal. Given that she had received a contrasted CT scan to rule out PE in the ED, a thyroid uptake study could not be performed during this admission and will need to be done as an outpatient. She was started on Propanolol given its effects on peripheral T3/T4 conversion. Methimazole was also started on ___. She was discharged on both of these medications with endocrine follow-up as an outpatient. . # Dementia/ Delirium: The patient was noted to have dementia at baseline. On ___, she began pulling at lines and became very agitated. She was given one dose of lorazepam which calmed her down. Psychiatry was consulted. It was felt that she had an element of delirium, likely due to unfamiliar setting as well as communication difficulties with her providers. It was felt that her hyperthyroidism could also be contributing. There was a question of a history of psychotic disorder as well, although this could not be verified. She was continued on her home mirtazapine and trazadone both at bedtime and Q6 prn.
