Psychiatry recommended discontinuation of ativan given the possibility of systemic build-up; instead they recommended Seroquel 12.5 mg prn for severe agitation. By day of discharge, her agitation seemed much improved; she was much more cooperative with providers and appeared more calm. . # Asthma: Home medications of advair and atrovent were continued with prn albuterol. . # Dysuria: Pt c/o burning with urination on day of discharge. A clean catch urine specimen was obtained, and UA was negative. Culture was sent and results to be followed up as an outpatient.
summary: You were admitted to ___ because of an irregular heart beat and chest discomfort. You had an ECG which showed atrial fibrillation and a fast heart rate. We checked your labs and found that your body is producing too much thyroid hormone. This is most likely one reason why your heart rate is so fast. We had the endocrinology team see you and give recommendations for treatment. You were started on two medicines - Propranolol, which you have to take 3 times per day, and Methimazole which you have to take once daily. It is very important that you take these medications. It is also very important that you continue to take your vitamin D and Calcium for overall bone health. You continued to take your Trazadone and Mirtazipine for anxiety and agitation. You were given Ativan and a new medication called Seroquel on an as needed basis for severe anxiety. The psychiatrists saw you while you were here and thought it was best to stop the Ativan as they are concerned that this could build up in your system and cause adverse side effects. You also had a complaint of burning with urination on your day of discharge. We took a sample of your urine; this test has two parts. The first part looks like you are low risk for having a urinary tract infection, however the second part will take 24 hours to come back.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman, with past history of GERD s/p fundoplication, hypertension, multinodular goiter s/p left thyroidectomy, anemia, C. diff on fidaxomicin, recent treatment for community acquired pneumonia and pleural effusion, presented with Pleuritic chest pain, admitted for worsening left pleural effusion and chest tube placement. #Pleuritic pain #Pleural effusion Patient presented with loculated left pleural effusion and worsening pleuritic pain over past 4 months. Chest tube was placed with approximately ___ mL serous fluid drainage and pleural studies were sent. Pleural fluid was exudative based on Lyte's criteria. The etiology of the patient's exudative pleural effusion is infectious versus malignancy. In terms of possible infection, the patient was treated with empiric ceftriaxone and azithromycin. Gram stain did not show microorganisms. The patient remained afebrile while in the hospital. In terms of possible malignancy, cytology of the pleural effusion showed atypical cells that are still under evaluation by pathology. Of note, the patient had a CT-Chest in ___ that showed a 3mm mass in the left-lower lobe. The chest tube was removed without complication. The patient remained on room air throughout her hospital stay. She was discharged home with interventional pulmonology follow-up. The cytology of her pleural fluid was pending at time of discharge and will need to be followed-up as an outpatient. The patient may need further diagnostic CT imaging based on pleural fluid cytology. She will complete a course of levofloxacin for pneumonia. For her pain she will continue her home oxycodone. #C Diff: The patient has a history of recurrent C Diff infections. She recently tested positive on ___ during her previous visit to ___ ED. She was being treated as an outpatient with fidaxomycin. Patient did not experience diarrhea while in the hospital. She was continued on her home fidaxomycin. ============== CHRONIC ISSUES ============== # Asthma: - continued albuterol - continued duonebs # GERD s/p fundoplication: - continued famotidine 40 mg - continued pantoprazole 40 mg daily # Lower Extremity Edema: did have Lasix empirically in the past - would consider TTE as outpatient if persists # Hypertension: - continued HCTZ - continued valsartan # Anxiety: - continued lorazepam # Hyperlipidemia: - Continued lovastatin (on hold given non formulary) # Suspected CAD: Family history of CAD, and did have have MIBI without perfusion defects, and normal wall motion - continued metoprolol 25 mg BID ==================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== NEW MEDICATIONS: [ ] Levofloxacin 500mg Q24H ITEMS FOR FOLLOW-UP: [ ] Please follow-up pleural fluid cytology report (pending in OMR) [ ] If cytology is concerning for malignancy, will need to pursue further diagnostic imaging. [ ] Patient to follow-up in interventional pulmonology clinic in 4 weeks (Interventional pulmonology to make appointment) [ ] Please repeat chest X-Ray in 1 week to evaluate for pleural effusion re-accumulation [ ] TSH mildly elevated (4.8), please follow-up with free T4 as outpatient and treat if indicated Name of health care proxy: ___ Relationship: Husband Phone number: ___ Code: Full Code
summary: You came to the hospital because you were having trouble with breathing. You were found to have fluid around your left lung. A tube was placed to drain the fluid and your breathing significantly improved. It is important to find out what is causing the fluid around your lungs so the fluid taken off was sent for analysis. Not all of the studies have been completed. It is most likely due to either and infection, inflammation, or cancer. For the possible infection we started you on antibiotics. You will continue to take the antibiotics after you leave the hospital. We are still awaiting the final results from the lung fluid studies. These studies will be communicated to you as soon as they are finalized.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is an ___ woman with CAD s/p CABG, CHF with EF 55%, lung cancer s/p lobectomy, with ESRD from hypertensive diabetic nephrosclerosis who was recently intiated on hemodialysis who presented with worsening dyspnea on exertion, cough, rhinorrhea, and a sore throat. . Dyspnea/COPD Exacerbation - There is evidence of reactive airways on exam. Patient has no formal diagnosis of COPD in the past, but symptoms are consistent with reactive airway disease or COPD flare precipitated by URI. She does have a long smoking history, increased AP diameter on CXR, and previous PFTs consistent with COPD stage IIA done back in ___. The differential diagosis for SOB included: Cardiac ischemia (s/p ___, PE (neg at OSH), PNA neg on CXR, bronchitis, acute CHF was not evident on exam or CXR. While inpatient, she was started on Prednisone 60mg with quick taper over one week and a 5-day course of levofloxacin. She received a CT chest/trachea, which showed no evidence of focal tumor recurence. However, it did show small airway infection/inflammation, a new right upper paratracheal lymph node, tracheomalacia, centrilobular emphysema (mild/moderate), and some lower lobe atelectasis and mild bronchiectasis. The patient had O2-sat in mid/high-90s on RA at discharge and mid/low 90's with exertion. Home O2 was considered, but patient says she is close to baseline. At home, she often has to stop and sit down with exertion. She expressed desire not to go home with oxygen. She will go home with Advair (250/50), Spiriva, albuterol and a short prednisone taper. . CHF, chronic, systolic - Patient appears euvolemic on exam. There was no evidence of acute exacerbation at this time. An echocardiogram was performed, which showed an EF of 50-55% and no changes from a previous echocardiogram from ___. She was started on ACE inhibitor. . Hypertension - Remained normotensive to mildly hypertensive <160/90 while inpatient. Patient was continued on metoprolol and amlodipine. Lisinopril was added. She is to follow-up with her PCP. . ESRD - Patient received hemodialysis x2 while inpatient. She has experienced some sweats and hot flushes post-dialysis. She says this occurs at home as well. Patient gets dialysis at ___ in ___. . DM, type II - Patient had a an episodes of hyperglycemia and one episode of hypoglycemia during her stay. ___ consult was obtained, and her NPH/Humalog (75/25) regimen was modified to better control her blood sugar. She is to follow-up with her PCP regarding ___ control of her blood glucose. . Abnormal findings on Chest CT - There is a focal upper abdominal aortic dissection and questionable thrombus in medial portion of right brachiocephalic artery. The patient was evaluated by vascular surgery. The patient has been asymptomatic, and we recommend no treatment unless patient develops symptoms. The patient has a new right paratracheal lymph node, which will require follow up with CT scan of the chest in 3 months. Also found was diffuse thyroid enlargement, right more than left, that has progressed since ___. We recommend thyroid studies and possible thyroid ultrasound. These findings were discussed with the patient's PCP prior to discharge.
summary: You were admitted with shortness of breath. You were found to have chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) most likely from your previous ___ use. We believe your symptoms were worsened by a viral upper respiratory tract infection. You were seen by the Pulmonologist for further evaluation of your lung disease and difficulty breathing. They recommned that you follow up with them. During this admission, you received dialysis by the renal team. You should continue going to your regular dialysis center after discharge. Your next dialysis date should be ___. They recommend that you follow up in the ___ clinic. We had the endocrinologist from the ___ see you during this admission. They recommended that you add a humalog sliding scale to your regimen of humalog 75/25.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with a history of asthma requiring intubation in the past who presented with an asthma exacerbation. ACUTE ISSUES #) Asthma Exacerbation: Though the trigger for this exacerbation is unclear, sore throat and cough on the day prior to admission suggest a possible viral trigger although no other symptoms. Leukocytosis present, but no fevers, and no localizing signs for a bacterial infection. Patient also reports allergies to dogs and cats, both of which live in the house with her. In addition, she smokes ___ cigarettes per day. Her asthma is poorly controlled at baseline, as she requires her inhaler daily and she has not been on a daily controller medication due to lack of coverage by Mass Health. In the ICU, she was started on albuterol/ipratropium nebulizers q4h standing plus q2h PRN. Her peak flows continued to improve and she was continued on prednisone 60mg daily. She was started on fluticasone here as a long term controller medication. The Pulmonary team will set up a follow-up appointment for outpatient management. Given her splenectomy and asthma history, pneumovax was given on the floor. Once transferred to the floor, she continued to improve with albuterol/ipratropium nebulizers q6h standing as well as her fluticasone inhaler and oral steroids. On the day of discharge the patient was anxious to get home to take care of her son at home. She had improved wheeze, and good oxygen saturations while on room air. She was instructed that she needs close follow up in our discharge clinic to ensure that she continues to improve as an outpatient. She was instructed in how to use her fluticasone inhaler and the importance of using it daily as a control medication and she understands. She will be given a steroid taper to complete as an outpatient. # Hypertension: Systolic blood pressures to the 170s-180s persistently while in the ICU. She is not on any anti-hypertensives at home. She was started on a low dose of captopril for better control, which was increased when she was transferred to the floor. Initially it was felt hr hypertension was due to around the clock albuterol administration as well as a high steroid load administered in the emergency department exerting mineralocorticoid activity. However, at the time of discharge she remained hypertensive and the decision was made to initiate daily HCTZ for blood pressure control. This should be followed up as an outpatient in our discharge clinic as she may need this medication discontinued should her blood pressure return to baseline when not on albuterol so frequently. # ___ abuse, marijuana abuse: The patient was counseled on the importance of smoking cessation to her overall health and to decreasing her asthma symptoms. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES The patient is to follow up in our discharge clinic for further evaluation if her HCTZ should be continued as an anti-hypertensive medication. If plan to continue - should have BMP in ___ weeks. The patient is to follow up with Pulmonology for further input into managing her asthma symptoms, but she should begin using her fluticasone inhaler daily for baseline asthma control and continue her steroid taper
summary: As you know, you were admitted for an asthma exacerbation. Given how wheezy you were in the emergency department and your history of severe exacerbations in the past requiring a breathing tube, you were initially admitted to our ICU for around the clock breathing treatments, steroids, and monitoring. You never required supplemental oxygen. You improved quickly, and were moved to a medical floor. There we continued your breathing treatments and oral steroids. During your hospital stay your blood pressure was elevated. Because of this, we will start a medication to treat high blood pressure. It is unclear if your high blood pressure is a temporary issue that may resolved with time once your acute illness has subsided, or if you will need ongoing treatment.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old ___ with a history of asthma, DVT on Lovenox, and likely ___ transferred from ___ with shortness of breath. On arrival, she was hemodynamically stable with oxygen saturation >96% on room air, tachycardia to 110s and tachypnea to ___. Her exam was otherwise notable for nasal congestion and tenderness over the sinuses, clear lungs to auscultation, and no evidence of DVT. Overnight, she was noted by the nursing team to have periods of apnea associated with dips in her oxygen saturation to 77-88% that always resolved with deep inspiration. Her WBC was elevated to 14.7 with a left shift of 80.1%. A respiratory viral panel was positive for RSV. An ABG was consistent with respiratory alkalosis. An EKG was sinus tachycardia without ischemia; CK-MB and troponin were both negative. Lower extremity ultrasound were negative for DVT bilaterally. CXR was normal. A surface echocardiogram was notable for normal ventricular function and moderate pulmonary hypertension. She was evaluated by the Pulmonary team who confirmed the diagnosis of sinusitis and RSV URI. She received a five day course of augmentin, albuterol nebulizers, and degongestants for symptomatic relief. There was low concern for PE given her stable and improving respiratory status, anticoagulated state on Lovenox, and negative lower extremity ultrasounds for DVT. A CTA was later obtained that confirmed the absence of PE. Her nocturnal desaturations were more consistent with obstructive sleep apnea. Nightly CPAP was attempted but not tolerable due to the patient's significant nasal congestion. Instead, she used a 35% O2 misted air face mask with good results. Regarding her moderate pulmonary hypertension, the etiology is most likely secondary to previously untreated OSA. In order to avoid excessive testing and radiation exposure during her pregnancy, the plan set by the primary Obstetric team in conjunction with the Pulmonary and Hematology teams was to empirically treat the OSA for six weeks; then repeat the echocardiogram to assess for interval improvement. At that time, the need for further testing will be determined. For her history of DVT, she was evaluated by the Hematology team, who decided to maintain her present intermediate anticoagulation regimen (lovenox 80 mg daily). Antiphospholipid antibody testing was negative. For her asthma, she continued on her home flovent and albuterol. During her admission, she was diagnosed with gestational diabetes by an elevated 3h GTT. She did not require insulin, and will continue to monitor finger stick blood glucoses. The CTA identified ___ pulmonary nodules measuring 5-6 mm, which will need to be followed up by repeat CT scan in ___ year. She was discharged on hospital day ___ in stable condition with the following: misted air mask/supplemental O2 and CPAP to be delivered, social work assistance with transportation and housing needs, Pulmonary follow-up, Sleep study and sleep medicine follow-up, obstetric/prenatal care follow-up.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with trouble breathing. You were diagnosed with a sinus infection and given antibiotics, as well as a viral upper respiratory infection (respiratory syncytial virus, RSV). This was probably not an asthma flare. While you were here, we discovered that your oxygen level drops to dangerously low levels while you sleep, a sign of obstructive sleep apnea. The ultrasound of your heart showed elevated pressures in the arteries of your lungs. This is probably a result of your sleep apnea. It is very important to treat this with the CPAP machine when you are able to tolerate it. Also, while you were here you had a CT of your lungs to evaluate for a possible pulmonary embolism, however, this was negative. Incidentally, a 0.5 cm pulmonary nodule within the right upper lobe, and a possible 0.6 cm nodule in the right middle lobe, was found.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Diarrhea - Likely secondary to viral syndrome in the setting of weeklong cough. There was no report of bleeding and guaiac was negative. She did not eat on the day of presentation and has had no sick contacts, though food-borne illness was also a possible cause. Her emotional distress with hearing she lost her long, emotional case likely contributed. She had not been taking lubiprostone for appx. ___ days before her episodes of diarrhea. CXR was unrevealing for acute thoracic process. She was admitted and had been asymptomatic throughout her stay, taking adequate pos. She was given IV fluids for hypotension on presentation (4L) and once again in the setting of hypotension to SBP 90, with good response. Serum electrolytes, LFTs were normal with the exception of elevated creatinine (see below). Social work saw her and offered support. . # Hypotension - in the setting of explosive diarrhea. IV fluids per above. Her SBPs ranged from 90-121 in the 24 hours to discharge. Her irbesartan/HCTZ were held. She will follow up with Dr. ___ if/when to restart these medications. . # ___ - Cr 1.4 on presentation, resolved to 0.8 following hydration. U/A revealed ___ hyaline casts. Likely represented prerenal etiology given diarrhea, low pos on day of presentation and hypotension. We held her irbesartan/HCTZ while hospitalized and restarted at discharge per above. . # Asthma - Nonsmoking patient with possible family history of COPD. We continued her advair, albuterol, zafirlukast and zyrtec. A1AT testing as an outpatient may be considered. . # Hyperlipidemia - Continued ASA 81, rosuvastatin. . # Glaucoma - Continued brimonidine and latanoprost drops. . # Osteoporosis - Continued Ca/D supplementation.
summary: You were admitted with abdominal pain and diarrhea, in addition to lightheadedness. This was most likely due to a self-limited virus. You received intravenous fluids and were asymptomatic when you arrived at the hospital, though were found to have low blood pressure and abnormal kidney function (acute kidney injury). Both improved with intravenous fluids. You had intermittent low blood pressures, which also improved with intravenous fluids. By the time you were discharged you were asymptomatic (no more diarrhea), had normal blood pressures and were tolerating oral medications. You also have high blood pressure (hypertension) and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease which were managed to good effect while you were hospitalized. Your red blood count was also found to be low (anemia) due likely due to low iron stores. Since you have had problems with constipation while taking iron supplements in the past and are averse to taking them now, you may take a diet rich in iron including fortified cereals.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y.o. male with COPD, CAD, Hypertension, and Hyperlipidemia presenting with increased shortness of breath at home and found to be hypoxic. #) Respiratory Distress/ COPD Exacerbation: Unclear precipitant, but he does report that his granddaughter who lives with himself and his wife had a cold last week and does have a dry cough. Likely viral illness precipitating exacerbation of RAD/asthma component. No pneumonia on CXR. No swelling, JVD, or signs of heart failure. Given dramatic improvement on measures implemented so far will continue to treat for COPD exacerbation and monitor. Given lack of headache/fever/myalgias or any active viral prodrome did not feel inclined to test for flu at this time as minimal presentation in MA so far and very unlikely to be consistent with this. Continued on azithromycin 500 mg daily and home inhalers. Patient switched from methylpred to 40 mg PO prednisone daily starting on ___. Continued on albuterol and albuterol/ipratroprium short acting nebs PRN, as well as montleukast, fluticasone-salmeterol, and tiotroprium. After transfer out of the MICU, his steroid taper was increased to prednisone 60mg daily, with planes for a slow taper down to 20mg daily, which he will continue. Additionally, he was continued on azithromycin to complete a ___fter two days on the floor of continued nebulizer treatments, steroids and antibiotics he felt that his breathing had returned to his baseline and he felt that he was ready for discharge. #) Hypotension: Patient had brisk response to fluids and appeared dry on presentation. After fluid resuscitation his blood pressure stabilized and after transfer to the floor he had no further episodes of hypotension. At the time of discharge, his blood pressure had increased and he was instructed to restart his lisinopril when he returned home. #)Acute renal failure: Patient's Cr on admission was 2 from baseline of 0.6-0.7. Given dehydration and hypotension, it was thought that there was a component of pre-renal but also concern for hypoperfusion due to the hypotension. Urine studies and a renal ultrasound were performed but his renal function rapidly returned to his baseline with fluid resuscitation. #) History of CAD: No signs of active ischemia on admission, but did have slight increase in troponins with normal CK's, likely due to strain in the setting of hypotension and decreased clearance with his acute renal failure. Continued his outpatient CAD medications, while in the hospital. #) Chronic Pain: Remained stable, continued home regimen of oxycodone/APAP and fentanyl patch. #) Code Status: DNR/DNI
summary: You were admitted to ___ with shortness of breath, due to an exacerbation (worsening of your COPD). When you were admitted you we also found that your kidneys were not working as well as they had before and your blood pressure was low. You were given IV fluids that helped your blood pressure and kidney function, because we thought you were dehydrated. You were initially admitted to the MICU because we were concerned about your breathing. We treated your shortness of breath with steriods, nebulizer treatments and azithromycin. After one night in the ICU, your breathing and blood pressure had improved and you were transferred to a medicine floor. We think that your breathing worsened because you likely got a mild viral infection, which caused you not to eat and drink much which made your blood pressure lower and kidney function worsen. The viral infection seems to have resolved. After transfer out of ICU, your kidney function returned to normal and you felt like your breathing was back to baseline. We continued your nebulizer treatments, prednisone taper, and azithromycin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ hx of afib, PPM, hypothyroid, CKD, PE presents after fall x 2 at home. Active Issues: #s/p mechanical fall on coumadin: Pt has long hx of mechanical falls. Fall does not appear to be syncopal or cardiac in nature: denies any dizziness/lightheadedness/CP/palpitations prior to fall. Imaging all negative. Neuro exam remained unchanged throughout hospital stay. Pt kept on coumadin due to her hx of recurrent PEs. Pt evaluated by ___ and deemed to need rehab. #recurrant UTIs: has extensive hx of multi-drug resistant UTIs, possible assymptomatic bactiuria vs true UTI. Pt currently assymptomatic--no dysuria, no hematuria, no foul smelling urine. No systematic leukocytosis or fevers. Does have frequency and incontinence but this seems to be baseline for her. Urine cx did show >100,000 e.coli. Pt received 1 dose of ceftriaxone in ED and 1 dose of zosyn on the floor but abx stopped as pt thought to be assymptomatic. A foley was placed to monitor her I/O after lasix started as pt is incontinent. She has follow up with Dr. ___ urology. She has outpt cystoscopy scheduled. # Hypothyroid: Pt was lethargic, although denies cold intolerance or constipation. TSH elevated to 12 with low T3 (38), T4 (3.9). Synthroid increased to 150 mcg daily. Repeat TSH needs to be checked in 4 weeks. # dCHF, acute on chronic: Pt's lasst exacerbation in ___ (>60%) with worsening mitral regurg. Pt noncompliant with lasix--states it makes her urinate too frequently. On admission, pt fluid overloaded on exam and admits 2 pillow orthopnea plus DOE Her weight was about 6 lb up from admission in ___. Pt received 40 IV lasix BID for 2 days and 40 IV lasix daily for 2 days. Pt d/c home on 40 mg lasix PO daily. #generalized fatigue: Pt states she has been more tired in past weeks to months. Most likely multifactoral--untreated CHF with volume overload vs hypothyroid vs deconditioning with poor po intake vs depression vs infection. Pt seems much improved with diuresis and it seems untreated CHF playing large part. Hypothyroid could have also been also contributing: TSH 12, T3 38, T4 2.9--> pt's synthroid increased to 150 mcg daily. Infection was ruled out as pt was afebrile, ___ ct wnl, has cough but is chronic, CXR wnl, denies urinary symptoms. Statin myopathy was also ruled out as CK was in normal range. Although pt endorsed decrease PO intake, her albumin was 3.8 Chronic Medical issues: # afib: Pt in rate controlled afib. Continued coumadin: 5 mg ___, 7.5 mg all other days. -Pt subtherapeutic on ___ with INR of 1.7 -coumadin increased on ___ to 10 mg -f/u INR on ___ was 2.0 -home regimen resumed #tachy/___ syndrome s/p PPM: fall appears mechanical and not syncopal. Thus pacemaker interrogation was not deemed to be necessary. #Hypertensive: Metoprolol 50 daily was continued. #asthma: Continued albuterol PRN #GERD: Continued home omeprazole 40 mg daily #HLD: Continued simvastatin 20 mg daily #chronic knee pain s/p bilateral knee replacements: Continue tramadol 50 mg Q4 PRN and acetaminphen 500 mg Q4 PRN #depression: Continue home sertaline 100 mg daily Transitional Issues: -Pt should have TSH rechecked in ___ weeks (mid ___ due to recent synthroid adjustment. -Pt should follow up with urology about her persistant UTIs vs assymptomatic bactiuria, has outpt cystoscopy. -Dry weight unclear at the time of discharge. Discharged on 40mg po furosemide. Would continue to monitor daily weights and uptitrate furosmide if weight increasing. -Please check INR on ___ and adjust coumadin dose accordingly -Pt lives alone with home services. She is looking for an assisted living facility. -Repeat CXR or CT Chest in 4 weeks to assess for resolution of RUL infiltrate / opacity seen on CT C-spine on ___ (see above in PERTINENT RESULTS or in OMR for report). -PENDING STUDIES AT TIME OF DISCHARGE: NONE
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after you fell twice. We did multiple X-rays which showed you did not break any bones after your falls. You were also found to have bacteria in your urine. Since you did not have any symptoms of a urinary tract infection (UTI) such as burning when you urinate, smelly urine, or blood in your urine, we decided to not give you antibiotics. You were also very tired when you came to the hospital and had some trouble breathing. This was because you had fluid in your lungs because you have heart failure. We gave you lasix which helped get the fluid off your lungs and made you breath better. Please make sure you continue to take the lasix when you leave the hospital. This will help keep fluid off your lungs and help you to breath better and be less tired.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT/PLAN: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with a history of severe asthma, diabetes ___ type II, and hypertension who presents with worsening shortness of breath and cough x 1 day. . # Dyspnea: The patient presents with 1 week of cold-like symptoms, followed by 1 day of worsening dyspnea, cough productive of clear sputum, and non-radiating left-sided chest tightness. Her symptoms temporarily improved after taking albuterol at home. Her exam was notable for end-expiratory wheezes. She reports that her dyspnea improved after receiving albuterol nebs and prednisone in the ED. Her clinical picture was most consistent with an asthma exacerbation. While a early pneumonia was also possible, she has had no fevers, a normal WBC with no left shift, a negative CXR, and her clinical picture not consistent with a pneumonia. Blood cultures and expectorated sputum cultures were sent. She had been started on levofloxacin in the ED, which was stopped on the floor given the low suspicion for pneumonia. Pt has a history of distant adverse reaction to the flu vaccine and has refused yearly to receive it. In addition her last hospitalization for asthma exacerbation was during and influenza infection. Influenza testing was performed on nasal aspirate and was negative. Given that the patient is an elderly female with diabetes, her dyspnea and chest tightness could also be an atypical presentation of angina. Sshe had a normal EKG and normal Tropn T and CK-MB x 3. Her CXR showed prominence of interstital markings consistent with mild pulmonary edema, suggesting that the patient's dyspnea may be secondary to CHF. An ECHO in ___ showed mild LVH, EF>75%, and normal LV diastolic function. On this admission her BNP is within normal limits, suggesting that new or worsening CHF is unlikely to account for her symptoms. Her home medications were continued and in addition she was treated with Prednisone 60mg daily and Ipratropium q6h. Her symptoms improved and after 2 days she was breathing at her baseline with O2 sats > 96% on room air. Pt was comfortable and ambulating well on day of discharge. She was prescribed a Prednisone taper over 7 days per her PCP. The had an appointment scheduled with her PCP ___ 1 week and was set up with ___ services to perform cardiopulmonary evaluations at home. . # Elevated CK: The patient has an elevated CK of 853. CK prior to admission was 584 in ___. She has had a chronically elevated CK, since her earliest records on OMR dating back to ___. CK-MB and TropnT are within normal limits, suggesting that MI is not likely. Elevated CK occurred during her last admission as well at which time it was associated with an influenza infection, her influenza tests were negative during this admission. There was not evidence of muscle trauma, hypothyroidism, seizure activity, or alcohol use. She was not taking statins. Her PCP ___ follow up these laboratory results. . # Anemia: The patient's Hct on admission was 34.3, MCV 83. Her baseline Hct is in the high ___. She is hemodynamically stable with no signs of blood loss and remained so during her hospitalization. . # Diabetes ___ Type II: The patient's most recent HbA1c was 8.0 in ___. She is currently on a regimen of NPH 70 units in AM and 27 units in ___. Her home insulin was initially continued, but with initiation of prednisone, her sugars were difficult to control and she was temporarily increased back to 90 and 30 which she had been on previously. Per discussion with her primary care physicina she was discharged on 80 units in the AM and 27 in the ___, and will follow up within 1 week with her PCP. .
summary: You came to the hospital because you were experiencing shortness of breath. We felt that you were having an asthma exacerbation, likely caused by a viral respiratory infection. We treated you with nebulizers (albuterol and ipratropium) and steroids (prednisone). Please continue to take prednisone for 7 more days: Day 1 (50mg), Day 2 (40mg), Day 3 (30mg), Day 4 (20mg), Day 5 (20mg), Day 6 (10mg), Day 7 (10mg). We were also concerned that the shortness of breath may be due to damage to your heart. We measured enzymes in the blood that showed that you did not suffer a heart attack. We were also concerned that the shortness of breath may be due to pneumonia, so we gave you 1 dose of an antibiotic called levofloxacin. We took images of your chest that showed that you likely did not have a pneumonia, so we discontinued the antibiotic. You also tested negative for influenza. You chose not to receive the influenza vaccine. You had elevated blood sugar levels, so we increased your insulin to 80 units in the morning and 27 units in the evening. Please continue to take insulin at these doses.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F w/ hx of CHF, HTN, DM, CVA, cortical blindness, dementia, and ESRD on HD (MWF) via tunneled line who presents with asymptomatic bacteremia found on routine HD labs now found to have AMS. # CoNS and Klebsiella septicemia/Line Infection: Patient initially found to have GPCs and GNRs on blood cx at HD which eventually speciated to pansensitive staph epi and klebsiella. On admission patient met ___ SIRS criteria. Pt exhibited a mild cough concerning for respiratory source as well as a abdominal tenderness concerning for UTI vs intra abdominal infectious source, however the AMS after line flush at HD concern for HD line as the bacterial source was thought most likely. Given GPCs and GNRs in blood, HD line was replaced by ___. Patient was started on two week course of Vancomycin and ceftazidime (switched from initial cefepime for ease of administration) on HD protocol. Follow up blood cx showed no growth to date. Infectious disease was consulted and recommended TTE and US of new HD line site which was unremarkable. ID recommended 2 week course of vancomycin ceftazidime dosed at HD (day 1 ___ for line infection. A TTE was performed which was remarkable for no evidence of valvular vegetations. A TEE was not performed as pt had no clear e/o endocarditis and HD line tip was likely cause of bacteremia. # Acute encephalopathy: Patient newly unresponsive to questioning after HD ___. Electrolytes were wnl, VBG showed no acid-base abnormality, FSG was in 200s with no hypoglycemia, no ICH per head CT, CVA thought unlikely with no new neuro findings. DDx included new toxic/metabolic encephalopathy from bacteremia from HD line flush vs seizure. AN EEG was positive for seizure like activity. Neuro was consulted who recommended starting Keppra. Keppra was dosed per Neuro recs and patient was continued on abx for bacteremia and the HD line was replaced. She continued to be somnolent and minimally responsive to verbal stimuli and refused PO medications from ___. On ___ pt aware of surrounding alert and oriented and answered questions appropriately. She was discharged on Keppra with instructions to follow up with Neurology (epilepsy specialist) in 2 weeks. # HTN Pts blood pressure was labile during this admission ___ to not tolerating her home po medications especially from ___. Briefly her BP was controlled with IV hydralazine. On discharge she was tolerating POs and her BP remained in the 140-170 range. She was stable on her home amlodipine, captopril, and labetalol. Her home PO labetalol may need to be up titrated as and outpatient to further control her BP. # ESRD - Patient admitted with mild anion gap acidosis which resolved. Initial concern for DKA with elevated blood glucose but this rapidly resolved and acetone was negative. Lactate was wnl and VBG was normal. Likely ___ ESRD. Stable on sevelamer and HD MWF. # DM2 controlled with complications -Patient's glipizide was discontinued on admission, fsbg was elevated and Insulin sliding scale was started and adjusted as needed. Patient was continued on ISS on discharge. # Hyperlipidemia - Stable on simvastatin # GERD - Stable on omeprazole # Hypothyroidism - Stable on levothyroxine # CAD - Stable on ASA # Anemia - Stable on iron and B12 # Pain - Stable on hydromorphone-acetaminophen # Asthma - Stable on albuterol nebs prn Transitional Issues: -Requires vancomycin and ceftazidime dosed at HD until ___. -Requires Neuro f/u at ___ for epileptiform activity on EEG, appt made for 2 weeks -Continue HD per usual ___ schedule -Monitor BP and adjust labetalol if still elevated -Pt placed on insulin sliding scale during this hospitalization, depending on her dietary intake this may need to be adjusted at her facility -Code: DNR/DNI -Contact: ___ (sister) ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ because bacteria was found in your blood at dialysis. You had no symptoms on admission though after your dialysis session you became less responsive. You had a CT scan of your head which was normal as well as an EEG which showed epileptiform activity. Our Neurologists evaluated you and recommended you take a medication to control your epiletiform activity which is Keppra. You should take this medication daily and extra after dialysis. Your labs and vital signs were normal so the change in your mental status was probably due to the infection and the epileptiform activity. You dialysis catheter was replaced and you were treated with IV antibiotics. You were started on the antiepilesy medication and your mental status improved and you were transferred back to your nursing facility. You should continue taking antibiotics with your dialysis session for 2 weeks (___).

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year old male with auto-immune cirrhosis, history of HCV (s/p treatment) complicated by esophageal/rectal varices, hypersplenism and pancytopenia, a history of SBP (___) on diuretics, hepatic encephalopathy on lactulose, who presents with constipation, nausea, and increased confusion. # Hepatic Encephalopathy/constipation: The patient presented with mild confusion and asterixis in the setting of not taking his lactulose. A full infectious work up was negative. A formal liver ultrasound did not show ascites to tap for SBP. The ultrasound was inconclusive regarding a portal vein thrombosis due the large amount of bowel gas. The patient was placed on lactulose q2hr until his encephalopathy cleared. Then, he was maintained on his home dose of lactulose 15mL TID, titrated to ___ BMs/day. He was continued on rifaximin 550 BID. Blood and urine cultures negative to date at discharge. # Autoimmune hepatitis + HCV (treated) Complicated by Cirrhosis: Meld-NA on admission was 24. He is listed for liver transplant. He has a history of hepatic encephalopathy on rifaximin and lactulose, grade 2 varices on nadolol, and trace ascites on spironolactone. He also takes ciprofloxacin for SBP prophylaxis. He states that he does not receive regular paracentesis. He was continued on all of his home medications. # Pleural effusion, suspected hepatic hydrothorax: Imaging showed an improving pleural effusion. Patient states that he has required drainage in the past. However, he denied any respiratory symptoms, so his pleural effusion was monitored during this hospitalization. # Coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia: The patient has a chronic thrombocytopenia and coagulopathy from splenic sequestration. His normal platelet range is between ___. #Questionable history of colitis: His home mesalamine was held during the hospitalization as it is non-formulary #GERD: He was continued on his home omeprazole. #Asthma: Continue albuterol PRN and hold budesonide inhaler since patient has been taking it inconsistently, and it is non-formulary #Anemia: Patient with stably low hemoglobin. Likely secondary to cirrhosis. He did not require any transfusions during this hospitalization. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== - Consider a repeat RUQ ultrasound to better evaluate for PVT as the initial was obstructed with bowel gases. - Patient was using his budesonide as a rescue inhaler. It was explained to him that he should be taking it every day, and using albuterol for acute exacerbations. Please re-enforce this topic. Weight on ___: 174 lbs #Contact HCP: ___, Father, ___, cell ___ #CODE: Full (confirmed)
summary: You were admitted to ___. You were admitted because of some confusion and nausea in the setting of constipation. You were given lactulose every 2 hours until you became less confused and were not constipated anymore. We switched you to lactulose three times a day so that you had ___ bowel movements a day. We also monitored your labs and you had an ultrasound done which showed an open portal vein. The ultrasound was not optimal to assess all of your veins so we recommend that you get another ultrasound after talking with your outpatient liver doctor. You should continue to take your lactulose (15mL three times a day) and titrate taking it so that you have ___ bowel movements a day. This will ensure that you do not get confused. You can add Miralax if you are not having bowel movements which will also help you go. You should talk with your outpatient liver doctor about scheduling an ultrasound to re-assess the veins around your liver.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with morbid obesity (BMI >70), chronic lymphedema and cellulitis, HTN, depression w h/o suicide attempts, schizoaffective disorder, and asthma presents with chest pain with elevated d-dimer, admitted for V/Q scan. . ACTIVE ISSUES BY PROBLEM # Chest pain: The etiology of the patient's chest pain is unclear. Acute coronary syndrome was felt to be unlikely given EKG without ischemic changes and 2 sets of negative troponins. Pain was not reproducible to palpation to suggest MSK etiology. CXR without signs of PNA. Also without fevers or elevated WBC. Pt did have elevated D=dimer in 800s, however ___ Dopplers were negative. He is mimimally mobile which could put him at risk but states he has been ambulating regularly. Given his body habitus, a CTA was not possible. He had a V/Q scan which was low probability, so pulmonary embolism was essentially ruled out. It is possible that his symptoms could be GI in origin, such as from intermittent esophageal spasm or stricture. He does also endorse easily choking on foods at times and spitting up, especially with steak. He was started on therapeutic trial of calcium channel blocker with nifedipine, which will also help control his blood pressure. Should be monitored at rehab for BP and see if this makes any difference in his chest pain. Recommend outpatient EGD for further evaluation. . # Bradycardia: Patient was noted to have sinus bradycardia throught admission with HR dipping to the mid ___ while sleeping. The patient was asymptomatic with stable blood pressure. He was monitored on telemetry as above. . STABLE ISSUES # Asthma: Uses advair and albuterol at home (says he uses a neb nightly). He was continued prn albuterol nebulizer treatments and his home advair. . # HTN: Patient was continued on his home lisinopril and started on nifedipine CR 30 mg for therapeutic trial for esophageal spasm. . # Psychiatric disorders: Patient has a history of schizoaffective and depression. He was continued on his outpatient regimen of abilify and wellbutrin. . # Anemia: Microcytic, at baseline. Has known hemoglobin C trait. Hct was at baseline throughout admission. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Chest pain: started therapeutic trial of nifedipine for possible esophageal spasm, should assess affect on chest pain. - Recommend EGD to rule out esophageal stricture, possible esophageal manometry to futher assess for esophageal spasm. - BP: should have BP monitored at least twice daily for the first week after starting nifedipine. - FULL CODE this admission
summary: As you know you were admitted because you were having chest pain. Reassuringly both your EKG (test of the electrical activity) of your heart and blood test demonstrated that you did not have a heart attack. There was some concern that you may have had a blood clot in your lungs, so a special scan was done and showed no clot. The cause for your chest pain is still not clear, but it is unlikely to be anything serious. Sometimes problems with your esophagus can cause pain like this, so we are starting you on a new medicine called nifedipine to see if this might help the pain. This will also help control your blood pressure.

text: Brief Hospital Course: HEADACHE: Presented to OSH with severe headache (please see HPI for details). CSF labs were initially concerning for possible bacterial meningitis. Even though CSF sample was consistent with traumatic tap given elevated RBC's, corrected WBC count in light of amount of RBC's was still elevated greater than 100. Glucose in the 50's and total protein of 59 was not consistent with a bacterial meningits. Additionally, patient was afebrile. No opening pressures or presence of xanthochromia was reported in OSH laboratory data. Started empirically on vancomycin, ceftriaxone, and dexamethasone. Was given IV Dilaudid for pain control at OSH, and became hypotensive with SBP's in the 80's (baseline in the 100's). Given hypotension, transferred to ___ due to concern for possible worsening bacterial meningitis with hypotension. On presentation, no signs of systemic rash, nuchal rigidity, mild photophobia, not positive for Kernig's or Brydzynski signs. Able to ambulate. Complaining of mild HA. Vitals were stable with SBP's in the 90's to 100 range. Given pending bacterial cultures at OSH, continued to treat empiricially with ceftriaxone/vancomycin/acyclovir. Discontinued dexamethasone given GCS greater than 15 and low concern for Streptococcus Pneumoniae meningitis. Was given pain control with PO percocet. Neurology consulted as also concern for pseudotumor cerebrii, subarachnoid hemorrhage, or intracranial vasospasm. Pt. had CTA/CTV as wella s transcranial dopplers to assess for bleed/vasospasm respectively, and both studies were normal. Neuro evaluation felt still most likely vasospastic in etiology, and recommended instituting calcium channel blocker (verapamil 40 mg BID). Given baseline blood pressures around 100, concern for iatrogenic hypotension, and avoided use. Started patient on naproxen 500 mg TID prn for headaches, and she reported relief of symptoms. Discontinued antibiotics & antivirals after confirming 3 days of no growth on culture with ___ laboratory (___). Discharged home with instructions to use naproxen and acetaminophen PRN. Also given contact information of neurology clinic for follow up PRN. *Follow up symptomatology. Cannot explain aberrant CSF findings, but increased WBC may have been secondary to traumatic tap and recent steroid taper causing neutrophilic extravasation and falsely elevated peripheral WBC count. *Pending labs at time of discharge: HSV PCR/ Lyme antibody titers CHRONIC ISSUES: ASTHMA:no evidence of exacerbation in house. Completed recent prednisone taper. Has mderate intermittent asthma symptoms. ___ benefit from increasing Advair dosing. PENDING LABS: HSV PCR/ Lyme Ab's/ Final CSF culture data (No growth in 4 days) from ___ ___ TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: None
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with a severe headache. You had a lumbar puncture performed that was indeterminate for meningitis. You were treated for several days with antibacterials and antivirals until your lumbar puncutre data came back negative for an infection. You had a CT scan of your head that was negative for any type of bleed or growth that may have caused your headache. Neurologists evaluated you and felt your headaches may have been due to "Vasospasms", tightening of the blood vessels in the brain that can cause acute pain. The treatment for vasospasms however can cause low blood pressure, and your base line blood pressure was too low to begin this medication. Thus, you were given headache medication with naproxen (AKA ALEVE) as well as acetaminophen (AKA TYELENOL), which controlled your pain. We are not certain what caused your headache, but it may be related to your recent exposure to people with viral illnesses.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ yo female with diabetes, HTN, DM, extensive PVD requiring bypass, admitted for cardiac catheterization for unstable angina and positive stress test. She received single DES to RPDA, and balloon rescue of the jailed branch of RPL through radial artery. # Coronary Artery Disease: patient has multiple risk factors for coronary artery disease including HTN, obesity, HLD, tobacco use, PVD, DM, and family history. Her troponins and EKG results were negative for MI at ___. However, her stress test was positive and she was found to have flow limiting lesion of RPDA. She underwent DES to RPDA and PTCA of jailed RPL. She was given integrillin for 12 hours post procedure. She was started on aspirin 81 mg po and clopidigrel 75 mg po daily. She will follow up with Dr. ___ in one month. # Rhythm: Patient was thought to be in atrial fibrillation on an EKG post-procedure. No known hx. No other episodes of atrial fibrillation and she was on normal sinus rhythm for the rest of her hospitalization. Repeat EKG showed normal sinus rhythm. She was started on metoprolol PO 25 mg daily for rate control. Her CHADS2 score=3 but will continue to be on aspirin/plavix for now. She does not wish to start coumadin. Patient has a h/o rheumatic fever when she was ___ years ago. An ECHO was obtained, which showed LVEF>55%, no valves abnormalities, and no change in ventricular function. # Chronic problems: remained stable and she was continued with her home medications -Hypertension: amlodipine, chlorthalidone, losartan -Hyperlipidemia: continue with pravastatin -Diabetes Mellitus Type II: NPH, FSS, sliding scale -peripheral neuropathy: Gabapentin -COPD/asthma: albuterol, fluticasone, ipratropium bromide, adviar -GERD: ranitidine -Depression: bupropion, citalopram -Lumbar back pain: codeine PRN -Neck dermatitis: desonide cream
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain. You underwent several tests that showed that you were not having a heart attack. However you chest pain was most likely from unstable angina. You had an abnormal stress test and you were referred to ___. We found that you had blockages in your coronary arteries. One stent was placed to open a blockage and one was opened with angioplasty. You will need to take aspirin and plavix for at least one year to keep the stents open. Please do not stop taking these medications without talking to your cardiologist first. You were also started on metoprolol, which is a beta blocker to slow your heart rate.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ yo F with PMH of asthma, p/w acute SOB, chest tightness, and wheeze, c/w asthma flare, likely triggered by URI vs chemical irritants. . # Asthma, acute - Pt was admitted to the medical floor and continued on systemic steroids, standing MDI doses of albuterol and ipratropium. She was also given a peak flow meter and instructed in its use. We also reviewed proper technique in using MDI, and she was very adept and competent, so nebulizers were not used. She remained on room air and had no hypoxia. There was no evidence of infection on her CXR, so she was not treated for pneumonia. Of note, she did develop symptoms of sinusitis, with nasal discharge / drainage, facial pain / tenderness, so she was placed on a course of antibiotics (see below). Her symptoms improved with the above treatment. In reviewing her history, the most likely trigger for her asthma flare were most likely a combination of cold weather, URI/sinusitis, and chemical irritants (cleaning products at home, hair products at cosmetology school). We advised her to remain at home for the next few days after discharge and not return to school until her symptoms had improved significantly. . Additionally, in reviewing her history with her, per her report, she has consistent required more than 2 courses of oral steroids annually for asthma flare. As such, she classifies into "mild persistent" category of asthma, and based on guidelines, she is being initiated on inhaled corticosteroid (ICS). Given that she is being discharged on systemic steroids, ICS can be started after she finishes the prednisone burst. We also recommend that she continue standing albuterol until she is seen in f/u with her PCP. . # Sinusitis, acute: the pt was noted to have significant sinus drainage and facial tenderness. Although this is most likely viral sinusitis, in the setting of acute asthma flare, we will empirically treat for bacterial sinsusitis with a course of Augmentin x 5 days total and also use inhaled Flonase spray. . Transitional Issues: 1. f/u with PCP to assess for return to baseline respiratory status, resume using albuterol MDI PRN 2. complete course of antibiotics for acute bacterial sinusitis .
summary: You presented to the hospital with worsening shortness of breath and wheezing, despite using your inhaler at home. This is likely due to an exacerbation of your asthma. You also have a sinusitis, and although likely viral in origin, given your asthma flare, we will treat for bacterial sinusitis. Your asthma flare is likely due to exposure to both the URI and chemical irritants at home (cleaning supplies) and cosmetology school. Given that you have frequent exacerbations requiring PO Prednisone, your asthma classifies into the mild persistent category and you would benefit from an inhaled corticosteroid.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ y/o M w/PMHx EtOH abuse, gallstone pancreatitis s/p stent placement/removal and sphincterotomy, recurrent episodes of cholecystitis who was transferred from ___ ___ for evaluation of abdominal pain. # Abdominal pain: Patient has a history of gallstone pancreatitis and several episodes of acute cholecystitis. Ultrasound here shows no evidence of acute cholecystitis, but does show thickened gallbladder which could be secondary to chronic liver disease. Etiologies of abdominal pain include acute on chronic pancreatitis (without lipase elevation); alcoholic hepatitis ___ discriminant function 8.8 on admission with INR of 1.2 and T bili of 1.1). Patient received 2L IVF on admission and has been able to tolerate a diet. He underwent CT abdomen which showed gallbladder wall thickening (no acute cholecystitis) as well as hepatic and pancreatic inflammation. Patient underwent laparoscopic converted to open cholecystectomy on the ___. Patient tolerated the procedure well, and after a stay in PACU, was transferred to the floor for further care. # Transaminitis: Possibly secondary to alcoholic hepatitis given AST > ALT, mildly abnormal INR and T bili. DF on admission 8.8, thus no need for steroids. LFts remained stable. # Polysubstance abuse: Patient with benzos noted in urine; also hx alcohol abuse. Reports had AA set up in ___ is amenable to social work support. Last drink was ___. He was maintained on CIWA (diazepam 5 mg q6h:PRN CIWA > 8 and was continued on thiamine, folate, MVI. Social work was involved. # Asthma: Pt on albuterol nebulizer PRN. On ___, he was tolerating a regular diet, with JP draining serosanguinous fluid, and was ambulating and voiding wihtout assistance. His pain was controlled on PO medications. During this hospitalization, the patient ambulated early and frequently, was adherent with respiratory toilet and incentive spirometry, and actively participated in the plan of care. The patient received subcutaneous heparin and venodyne boots were used during this stay. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient left AMA before ___ services and discharge instructions and scripts could be given.
summary: Please note your hospital course below: You were admitted to the ___ because you were having abdominal pain. You had a CT scan of your abdomen which showed inflammation of your liver and of your pancreas. This inflammation is likely the cause of your pain moreso than your gallbladder, but because of problems with gallstones in the past you had your gallbladder removed. The likely cause of your pain is because of the inflammation of your liver and pancreas, which is caused by alcohol. It is VERY important that you stop drinking alcohol completely. We have set you up with a PCP at ___, which accepts your insurance. Please see below for your appointments.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ w/ Hx of EtOH abuse (prior withdrawal, DTs), Hx of pancreatitis, GERD, depression, polysubstance abuse, presenting with an unknown substance ingestion and an presentation concerning for pyelonephritis with GNR growing from her ___ urine culture. # Pyelonephritis: Presented w/fevers, tachycardia, flank tenderness, dysuria and a positive U/A. Ucx growing >100K GNR. ED started her on Levoflox, but she was transitioned to Cipro ___, D14: ___ for a 14 day course. Her Lactate was originally 2.6 but downtrended with aggressive IVF. # ___: Cr. 1.7 on admission, now 0.5 after 5L IVF. Most likely ___ in the setting of poor PO intake and EtOH use. # ?Syncope: Unclear story. Patient presented with +serum tox for EtOH. She did not require diazepam and was HD stable during her admission. # EtOH withdrawal. She has a long history of EtOH abuse. Although she presented with signs of withdrawal, she never scored >10 on the CIWA protocol. She was treated with thiamine, folate and MVI. # Mild hepatitis/Pancreatitis: Likely secondary to EtOH per AST/ALT ratio. Her amylase was mildy elevated, which is ___. # EtOH / cocaine / heroin abuse She was encouraged to seek out rehab and abstain from any further drug abuse. She was seen by social work who recommended outpatient detox. # HbS Trait: Normocytic anemia, chronic. Likely a combination of marrow suppression due to chronic EtOH, given RDW ~20%. She was treated with folic acid. # Asthma: Her CXR was clear but she was continued on her home albuterol inhaler:PRN. # Depression Patient does not take meds regularly so will hold citalopram and mirtazepine Transitional Issues: - Follow up with PCP - ___ detox - Patient has seen Social Work and is amenable to outpatient detox. - Follow up pending blood cultures - Repeat Chem 10 at next PCP ___ ======================= # Code: Full (discussed with patient) # Emergency Contact: ___, friend (___)
summary: You were admitted with painful urination and left flank pain. You were found to have an infection in your bladder, called a urinary tract infection (UTI). You were treated with antibiotics and your pain improved. After starting antibiotics, you developed diarrhea. You were tested for c.diff and were negative. You were treated with loperamide and your symptoms improved. Your diarrhea was most likely a ___ of your antibiotic. You were also managed for symptoms of alcohol withdrawal. You did not any valium but were monitored closely. You spoke with our social worker who encouraged you to participate in outpatient detox.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo woman with a history of gastric bypass on ___ ___ in ___ who presented to ___ ___ complaining of nausea and a pre-syncopal event, now found to have portal vein thrombosis currently on heparin gtt and transferred for further care. #Portal vein thrombosis: Pt presented from home after presyncopal episode at home to ___, and found to have left portal vein thrombosis on CT and U/S. Cause for PVT is broad, although most likely triggered by recent surgery on ___. No family history of clots or previous history of clots. 25% of PVT also without known cause. She is not on OCP's, had a negative HCG at ___, and has no evidence of cirrhosis. Lactate negative with no abdominal pain to suggest ischemic gut. She was initially started on heparin drip for anticoagulation. The patient was provided the option of either lovenox bridge to warfarin, or starting ___. It was discussed that ___ has been used for portal vein thrombosis in case series/reports, although no large studies available considering relatively new medication. She elected to take ___ as she did not feel she would be able to give herself lovenox injections. The patient was prescribed ___ 15mg po BID x 21 days, and will then need to be transitioned to 20mg po daily to complete ___ months of anticoagulation. #Pre-syncope: Likely in the setting of dehydration, as the patient has been unable to keep up with her fluid intake recently after her recent surgery and was orthostatic on presentation. She was bolused with IVF and started on maintenance fluids until her nausea improved. EKG from OSH with NSR, trops flat. She was continued on Bari-3 diet, and was tolerated po prior to discharge. Pt not orthostatic, and was discharged home with the instructions to continue fluid intake at home and follow-up with surgery. #s/p Gastric Bypass surgery: Bariatric surgery was consulted in the ED and recommended no surgical management. Pt initially started on clear diet, and was advanced to her home Bari-3 diet on the floor. Continued on home omeprazole. #Asthma: Pt with hx of asthma on albuterol inhaler at home as needed. Albuterol was given during admission for some mild shortness of breath, which subsequently resolved. ***TRANSITIONAL ISSUES*** -Pt should continue ___ 15mg po BID x 21 days (until ___, and then should continue on 20mg po daily to complete three-six month course. Prior authorization has been approved. -CODE: Full -Contact: Mother ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Omeprazole 20 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted to ___ from ___ for light-headedness, where you were found to have a portal vein thrombosis. This is when a clot forms in one of the vessels that leads to your liver. It is unclear what exactly caused the clot to form, but it is likely related to your recent surgery. You were started on IV heparin to thin your blood, and given IV fluids for dehydration. You were started on a medication called ___. You should take 15mg by mouth twice a day until ___, and then you should take 20mg daily for a total length of three months. A risk of this medication is bleeding so you should monitor your bowel movements for any blood or any black stools.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o ___ speaking only female with a remote history of sigmoid colon cancer s/p resection in ___ who presents with 1 month of productive cough and SOB most likely due to viral infection. She was also found to have a chronic LUL granulomatous nodule on imaging, active TB ruled out with induced sputum x 3. # Viral bronchitis/SOB: Pt described worsening cough and SOB over the last month. Given associated sore throat, nasal congestion and evidence of bronchitis on CT from OSH clinical picture was most consistent with viral respiratory infection. During this hospitalization SOB was associated with coughing fits and sputum production, likely with mucous plugging. There was also likely a component of inflammation and reactive airway disease secondary to viral infection given intermittent wheeze and decreased air movement. These symptoms improved with staggered albuterol/ipratropium nebs and mucolytics. She initially was on supplemental oxygen however this was weaned off and at the time of discharge she was satting >90% on room air at rest as well as with ambulation. She was transitioned from nebs to albuterol inhalers, with instruction on use prior to discharge. She was also discharged with guaifenesin-dextromethorphan and benzonatate for cough suppression. Will need follow up with her PCP. # LUL calcified granulomatous lesion: Given chronic LUL granulomatous lesion on imaging (not previously treated for active or latent TB per patient report), duration of symptoms and recent travel to ___ she was initially placed in negative pressure room on precautions while she was ruled out for TB with induced sputums x 3. Preliminary results from all 3 samples returned negative prior to discharge. PPD was also performed which returned negative (read on ___, 0mm), ruling out latent TB. # Abdominal/anterior chest pain: Pt complained of abdominal and chest discomfort with coughing thought to be muscular in nature. There was also thought to be a component of reflux given description of symptoms. Exam remained benign. She was treated with acetaminophen as needed for pain as well as GI cocktail with good effect. # Acute urticaria: She developed a new urticarial rash on ___. This was most likely a contact allergy. Offending allergen remained unclear, possibly oxygen tubing from nasal canula or hospital gown. Bedding was changed. She was treated with PRN benedryl and sarna with improvement. # Eosinophilia: Absolute eosinophil count of 1538 (moderate category) on admission. Unclear significance given normalization on repeat labs ___ although has been noted to have eosinophilia in the past. This may be related to environmental allergies. If this persists in the outpatient setting consider further workup. # Microscopic Hematuria: RBCs noted on UA. This appears to be chronic based on records. Etiology remains unclear. Consider further workup as an outpatient. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - Started albuterol inhaler, cough suppressants for supportive care - Assess for resolution of rash, prescribed benedryl and sarna PRN - Consider further workup for chronic eosinophilia - Consider further workup for chronic microscopic hematuria - Consider further workup of normocytic anemia - Incidental LUL pulmonary nodule vs granuloma measuring 8mm, non-calcified in superior lingular segment noted on OSH CT. Needs 6 month follow up CT - CODE STATUS: Full, confirmed - CONTACT: ___, ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ because of worsening cough and difficulty breathing. A chest XRAY was performed that showed a lesion in the left upper lobe of your lung that had been present previously. Given your symptoms and recent travel to ___ we placed you in respiratory isolation and got sputum samples to rule out active tuberculosis that returned negative. We placed a PPD (skin test for tuberculosis) that was also negative. Since this result was negative you do not need any further treatment with antibiotics. While you were here you were treated with nebulizers to help with your breathing along with medication to bring up mucus and help with cough. This cough will likely take several weeks to resolve completely. You can use the albuterol inhaler at home if you feel wheezy or short of breath with coughing. You had some abdominal pain from muscle strain due to coughing. You can continue taking tylenol at home for this after discharge (up to total of 3grams per day maximum). You also developed a rash that looked like hives from an allergy to something that came in contact with your skin. You can take benedryl (diphendyramine) and apply sarna lotion as needed for itch.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Primary Reason for Hospitalization: =================================== ___ year old female with Hx of Depression and anxiety who presented with atypical chest pain/palpitations. . ACTIVE ISSUES ============= # Chest Pain/Palpitations: Most likely this is non-cardiac given her recent normal stress test and echo. EKG here not concerning and BNP/Trop normal. EMS report of 7 second run of VT could not be confirmed as no recording available. There was nothing concerning on telemetry overnight but can't exclude paroxysmal tachyarrythmia as explanation for her symptoms. Therefore will d/c home with ___ ___ monitor. ___ was instructed in how to trigger the device. Long term cardiovascular risk reduction should involve weight loss and nutrition/exercise. ___ interested in seeing nutritionist as outpatient. . CHRONIC ISSUES =============== # Anxiety/Depression: Not having anxiety or depressive symptoms currently per her report. She will continue existing home meds as prescribed . # Asthma: Well controlled. She will continue fluticasone and albuterol . TRANSITION OF CARE ================== - ___ Monitor to be reviewed by Dr. ___ - ___ interested in seeing nutritionist as an outpatient for weight lost strategies, PCP can refer if indicated. ___ also informed about HCA lifestyle change group which she may be a good candidate for.
summary: As you know, you were admitted to the ___ ___ with chest pain and palpatations. We performed an EKG and blood tests and did not find anything concerning for a heart attack. We monitored your heart overnight and did not see anything concerning or which would explain the palpatations that you were having. We recommend that you wear the ___ ___ Hearts monitor to evaluate the palpatations that you have been having. When you feel palpatations, please trigger the device, this will allow us to examine the heart rhythm when you are experiencing the symptoms to understand what may be the cause of your symptoms.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ with PMH significant for bilateral PE on xarelto, HTN, HLD, and GERD who presents with fever and increased urinary frequency along with urine culture growing E. Coli and BCx growing GNR. Lactate elevated to 2.3. Improved with IVF and treatment with Zosyn. Switched from zosyn to cipro based on C+S. Continued to spike fevers, which was potentially c/f inadequate source control, but defervesced on ___ in the AM. Patient was treated with PEP for HIV exposure on ___, which he will continue for 28 days. HIV ab was negative, HIV viral load pending. He will need repeat HIV ab in ___ weeks. # Urosepsis # GNR Bacteremia: Patient with leukocytosis and UCx positive for E. Coli along with GNR bacteremia. Started on ciprofloxacin 500mg BID on ___, but noted to have worsening leukocytosis and bacteremia, so he was sent into the hospital. Improved with treatment with Zosyn. He was transitioned to cipro based on C+S on on ___. He will be continued on this regimen for 14 day treatment course to end ___. Continued to spike fevers on ___ c/f inadequate source control, although devervesced on ___ in the AM, so prostatic imaging was deferred. CT/NG negative. - zosyn ___ - cipro 500mg BID for 14 day treatment course [___] # ___ # Hyponatremia: Patient with Cr elevation to 1.4 from baseline of 1.0-1.1. Likely I/s/o volume depletion from sepsis and ongoing fevers with increased drive for ADH from hypovolemia contributing to hyponatremia. Improved with fluids. Will need to be rechecked at post-discharge f/u. # Potential HIV exposure: Patient notes he had sexual encounter with 2 HIV positive partners and would like PEP. Encounter was on ___. GC/NT negative. RPR nonreactive. HIV viral load pending, he will need repeat HIV ab in ___ weeks. Truvada/Dolutegravir daily for 28 days to end on ___. Consider PREP in consultation with PCP/ID doctor. Patient should be counseled on safe sexual practices post discharge. # Bilateral PE: Patient with history of bilateral PE in ___ with negative hypercoagulability workup. He was continued on rivaroxaban. # HTN: Patient presented initially to the ED and was normotensive. Held patient's losartan-hctz in setting of urosepsis, which he can restart after discharge. # HLD: Continued on home dose of atorva 20mg. # Asthma Continued on albuterol and fluticasone # GERD Continued on omeprazole ============================= TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ============================= - Will be continued on ciprofloxacin 500mg BID for 14 day treatment course to end on ___. - Repeat Chem 7 at follow up appointment on ___ to assess for resolution ___ and hyponatremia (discharge Na 128). - f/u HIV viral load, which was pending at time of discharge. - Will need repeat HIV1/2 Ab testing in ___ weeks given that exposure was recent and testing in hospital may be too early for acute infection - Patient will be continued on PEP for HIV exposure with Truvada/Dolutegravir for 4 weeks - Patient should be vaccinated against Hep A if not yet done (recommended in MSM populations) - Could consider counseling patient on PrEP for HIV prevention - Patient should be screened regularly for STDs # Contact: Name of health care proxy: ___ ___: cousin Phone number: ___ # Code - full
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after you were found to have bacteria in your urine which had spread to your blood. We gave you antibiotics, and you started to feel better. We initially treated you with an antibiotic called zosyn and switched you to a different antibiotic called ciprofloxacin based on your urine culture that showed E. Coli that was sensitive to this antibiotic. Your fever improved. You will need to continue taking this antibiotic twice daily until ___. It is important that you finish this antibiotic, even if you feel completely better. We also did a test for HIV, which was negative. However, this test is not always positive right away, and you should get tested again after ___ weeks. We are also prescribing you medication that can prevent an HIV infection. You should continue to take both of these every day for four weeks. You had slight worsening in your kidney function, which was likely due to the infection and fever.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with recent diagnosis of met. pancreatic ca. who received ___ cycle of gemcitabine on ___ admitted with h.influenza PNA and transient bacterimia. Also found to have bilateral subsegmental PE. . # Bacteremia / Pneumonia, healthcare-associated, bacterial: blood and sputum cultures with Haemophilus influenzae (negative for beta lactamase) on admission. Blood cultures subsequently cleared and pending cultures from ___ have had no growth to date. He defervesced soon after initiation of antibiotics. - Switched from vancomycin/cefepime/levofloxacin to ampicillin iv on ___, plan for 14d course (started abx ___, so would be through ___ would not swtich to PO given transient bacterimia and suboptimal bioavailability of amoxicillin - f/u blood cultures - urine legionella antigen negative - should have surveillance labs done on ___ . # Acute pulmonary embolic - Treating with LMWH, no plan to switch to coumadin as maligancy associated - Would treat for at least 6 months, but defer duration to primary oncologist. . # Chest wall & abdominal pain / Dyspnea: Attributed to the above. Seen by palliative care during this admission and started on: Oxycontin, oxycodone, dextromethorphan-guaifenasen, and gabapentin. Had previously been on percocet. Symptoms currently well controlled on this regimen. . # Conjunctivitis: concerning for viral etiology given appearance and sick contact with wife; ___ showed no leukocytes, and culture with 2 colonies of coag-neg Staph (likely contaminant), so likely viral. Was initially on contact precautions, but no longer necessary as conjuntivitis has resolved. . # Hypertension, benign: Irbesartan and HCTZ held during admission and patient maintain blood pressures in the 110/50 - 140/80 range. . # Diabetes mellitus type 2, controlled: - Home lantus decreased from 30 to 20 given decrease in food intake while inpt. Has had good control on humalog sliding scale. - Continued metformin; held glyburide . # Asthma, chronic: Possible exacerbation precipitated by pneumonia; wheezing on initial exam. Home regimen continued and MDI switched to nebulizers. . # Anemia, acute: Hct down a bit from baseline. ___ be fluid shifts vs bleeding. Hemodynamically stable with no obvious sign of bleeding. Stool guiaic negative. # Thrombocytopenia: Initially thrombocytopenic. This resolved prior to d/c and was attributed to gemcitabine . # Constipation: Home bowel regimen intensifed while inpatient. Given fleet's enema with good effect. .
summary: You were treated for pneumonia (infection in your lungs) and pulmonary embolism (blood clots in your lungs). The infection spread to your blood. You will continue taking antibiotics to treat the infection. A PICC line has been placed for you to continue receiving antibiotics for a total course of 14 days (including the days you have been in the hospital. For the blood clots, you will continue taking Lovenox (enoxaparin) injections, a blood thinning medicine that will help prevent more clots from forming. You should discuss the duration of anticoagulation with your oncologist. Some medications were held during this admission (percocet, HCTZ, irbesartan, glyburide).

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ CAD s/p CABG ___, T2DM, DL, exercise induced asthma, who has metastatic pancreatic neuroendocrine cancer diagnosed in ___, with liver, pulmonary, omental, and pelvic metastasis, s/p 20 cycles Carboplatin/Etoposide, c/b multiple biliary obstructions, cholangitis w/ pseudomonas bacteremia, and PCN sensitive enterococcus faecalis bacteremia requiring multiple ERCP procedures and metal stent in CHD ___, w/ cholecystocolic fistula who p/w recurrent fevers. ACTIVE ISSUES: #Recurrent Fever: Patient has had previous admits similarly, and defervesced with abx on zosyn. Bili/alk phos did not show evidence for a cholestatic or obstructive etiology for his symptoms but underwent ERCP which showed biliary sludge which is thought to be the source of his fevers. CT scan of his A&P showed mildly enlarged liver lesions however no abscess was seen. He was narrowed to Augmentin to complete a 7-day course on ___. #Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Ca: Previously scheduled for Irinotecan C1D1 ___ & ___ which was held off due to fevers and concern for infection. CT A&P showed enlarging liver lesions concerning for evolving mets. After discussion with Dr. ___ Dr. ___ was made to proceed with irinotecan to begin on 7.2 which will be adminstered q 2 weeks. CHRONIC ISSUES: #CAD s/p CABG: Continued ASA, isosorbide, losartan, HCTZ #HTN: Continued isosorbide, losartan, HCTZ #H/o chronic iron deficiency anemia: Hg 9.0 at admit, improving from baseline of ___. Remained at baseline upon discharge. #T2DM: Home metformin held while in-house and given HSSI. #Exercise Induced Asthma: Currently asymptomatic. Continued albuterol, fluticasone, prn #Gout: Continued allopurinol #Thrush: Continued nystatin Transitional Issues: -last day of Augmentin = ___ -full code -contact: ___ (wife/HCP) ___ -___ clinic visits to be called to the patient
summary: You were admitted for fevers, concerning for an infection. Your labs did not show evidence of a biliary source but given your history of biliary obstruction and stent placement, you underwent ERCP which showed biliary sludge, potential causing your fevers. CT of the abdomen/pelvis showed enlarging liver lesions, however no obvious abscesses. You were continued on antibiotics and will continue Augmentin until ___. After discussion with Dr. ___ decided to trial you on another chemotherapy (irinotecan). You will come back to clinic on ___ for chemotherapy and potential blood transfusion. You will need to take your Neulasta shot on ___, 24-hours after your chemotherapy infusion. This chemotherapy will be given every 2 weeks.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with Type 1 IDDM, poorly controlled, presented with right foot infection s/p surgical I&D and closure plus left breast cysts and left earlobe cyst.
