ACTIVE ISSUES: =============== # Right foot cellulitis/ulcer/Abcess: Podiatry performed bedside I&D (___) only expressing 1 cc of purulence, repeated bedside I&D ___. Surgical I&D conducted by podiatry on ___, removed more purulence and kept wound opened and packed. Pt went back to OR with podiatry on ___ for wound closure. For antibiotic coverage, Doxycycline and Unasyn were initially started (___), then changed to Vanc/Cipro/Flagyl (___) to cover anaerobes, then changed to Vanc/Zosyn (___) to add coverage pseudomonas. Antibiotics changed to Augmentin/Bactrim (___), to be continued until ___ for a total of 14 day course of antibiotics. After deep wound culture results (___) revealed rare growth of coag neg staph and gram negative rods, antibiotics were kept the same. Pain was controlled with tylenol, oxycodone, dilaudid PRN before dressing changes. # Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus: Pt has history of poorly controlled diabetes. ___ was consulted and pt was placed on an insulin sliding scale with Lantus and Humalog SS. Pt's blood sugars were well-controlled in hospital. She will f/u as scheduled as an outpatient. # Left nipple lesions: Chronic for months, have been expressing bloody purulence. Derm was consulted and sent wound culture of left breast ___, no growth. US and mammogram showed subcutaneous masses possibly representing infected sebaceous or epidermoid inclusion cysts or infected ___ glands. Pt seen at ___, recommended warm compresses and continue with antibiotics that were given for foot infection. # Cyst left ear base. Cyst appeared within days. Derm was consulted and felt this was an inflamed epidermal inclusion cyst, recommend pt follow up in derm surgery clinic as outpatient for possible cyst excision if cyst remains bothersome to patient. # Yeast infection: Pt had complained of itchiness. Yeast found in UA. Pt received 3-day course of Fluconazole ___ - ___. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # Asthma, Chronic Stable: Documented history of this issue; continued flovent and albuterol inhalers # Depression: Documented history of this issue; continued cymbalta, pt did not want SW consult. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ================= - F/u left ear cyst with derm surgery. - F/u foot care with podiatry, Dr. ___ - f/u with ___ as scheduled for management of DM
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of an infection in your right foot. The podiatrists saw ___ and drained the infection twice at your bedside, then they took ___ to the operating room to perform a further debridement of the infection since the infection had not been improving. The second surgery was done to close the incision from the first surgeryYou were started on antibiotics for the infection and will be continued until.. We also evaluated the nodules on your left breast. The dermatologists saw ___ and recommended further evaluation with the breast doctors. ___ had a mammogram and met with the breast center, and they said there is no malignant process and most likely they are inflammed occlusion cysts ___ glands. Regarding the cyst on your left ear, the dermatologists looked at this and recommended that ___ schedule a dermatology surgery appointment as outpatient for excision when the cyst gets smaller (see phone number below).

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with h/o gastroparesis and IBS, who presented her chronic abdominal pain, likely related to her gastroparesis/IBS. # Abdominal pain: Pt has had thorough workup in the past with numerous providers. Abdominal pain most likely secondary to her chronic gastroparesis. GI was consulted. The patient's pain was initially treated ___ IV dilaudid Q4H; per GI recommendations, this was later discontinued given that it worsens gastroparesis. She was prescribed 2.5mg Relgan to take with meals. While she initially agreed to try this, she later refused the medication as she was concerned she had an allergy to it. GI did not feel any further workup was warranted inhouse. She was also given phenergan for nausea, which provided some relief, and her omeprazole dose was changed from QD to BID to help with her epigastric pain. She tolerated breakfast and felt ready to go home, as her abdominal pain was chronic and the same as prior. Pt has close follow up with GI providers. # BRBPR: Patient reported BRBPR mixed with stool x2 the day prior to admission. She had no h/o straining/constipation and no hemorrhoids or fissues on rectal exam. NG lavage was negative for blood, Hct and BUN within normal limits, and guiac negative. #Hypovolemia: On admission, the patient had clinical and laboratory evidence of mild hypovolemia. She was treated with 3L IVF and given 40mEq potassium chloride for repletion. # Asthma: She was prescribed home fluticasone and an albuterol inhaler PRN. #Transitional Issues: - Patient is interested in getting a gastric pacemaker placed, she plans to explore hospitals in ___ and ___ who do this procedure. ___ is center that performs this procedure.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for your abdominal pain. Your symptoms are similar to prior. To treat your pain, you were briefly given medication through your veins called dilaudid; this was later stopped given that this medication worsens gastroparesis (see below). You also reported some blood in your stool. We performed a rectal exam which showed no blood. You also had an NG lavage which was negative for blood. Your blood counts were normal and stable during your hospitalization. You were given several liters of fluid to hydrate you. The GI doctors saw ___ and ___ that your pain was the same as prior. They recommended that you continue phenergan, start a small dose of reglan (2.5mg with meals) and increase your dose of omeprazole from once a day to twice a day. They also recommended that you stop taking the pain medication through your IV since this medicine worsens gastroparesis.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx of HTN, HLD, dCHF/HOCM,CAD s/p CABG, asthma, AF/Flutter s/p cardioversion ___ and ___ who presents after a syncopal episode this morning found to have subacute worsening anemia and gastrointestinal bleeding. #GI Bleed #Anemia: Patient presented with guiac positive melenic stools after an acute syncopal episode. Suspicion was for an upper GI bleed, likely ulcer, based on history. Her last colonoscopy was in ___ and showed gastritis and colonic diverticulosis. She was given 2u pRBCs, 40mg pantoprazole IV BID. Gastroenterology was consulted and proceeded with an EGD on ___ which showed bleeding angioectasias in the duodenum which were cauterized. Due to concern for further bleeding from other parts of the bowel, GI performed a capsule endoscopy on ___ which showed ______________________. A colonoscopy on ___ revealed melanotic stool but no source of bleeding in the colon. Her home rivaroxaban was held in the setting of an acute GI bleed. Would continue risk/benefit conversation in the outpatient setting. Asa was continued. #Left Chest Pain: Patient presented with a left-sided chest pain. EKG and troponins were unremarkable; this pain was thought to be musculoskeletal secondary to a fall. A CXR was performed which did not show any acute fracture. Patient was placed on standing APAP for pain. CHRONIC ISSUES: #Afib: Continued metoprolol fractionated at lower than home dose (home dose is 50/25, ___. Held anticoagulation. Continued discussion of risks/benefits should ensue when ensured of continued stability from GI bleeding. #CAD: Held home ASA 81mg initially and restarted. #dCHF: Held home Lasix during admission. #HLD: Continued home rosuvastatin. #DM: Diet controlled. #Asthma: Continued albuterol and fluticasone. CORE MEASURES: # Communication: ___ (Daughter, HCP) ___ # Code: Full
summary: You were admitted for evaluation of gastrointestinal bleeding. For this, you were given a blood transfusion. You had an endoscopy, capsule endoscopy, and colonoscopy. You were found to have gastritis and some small blood vessels in your small intestine that is likely the source of your bleeding. For this, you were given an acid suppressing medication to take twice a day. In addition, your blood thinner medication was discontinued due to bleeding. Please discuss with your primary care provider and cardiologist the risks and benefits of continuing this medication in the setting of gastrointestinal bleeding. Your aspirin was continued.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ male with past medical history significant for squamous cell carcinoma of the soft palate status post xray who presents with worsening facial swelling with CT imaging concerning for abscess along with osteonecrosis of mandible. ACUTE ISSUES: # Actinomyces osteomyelitis and abscesses: CT imaging on admission was concerning for abscess along with osteonecrosis of the mandible. Radiation to jaw most likely predisposed him to this infection. Patient initially received vancomycin/unasyn. He did have a fibroptic exam by ENT in ED that did not show any masses and had a widely patent airway. CRP was very elevated at 174.0. On ___, ___ took him to the OR and performed transcervical and intraoral incision with drainage of right submasseteric space. Cultures grew no specific organism and pathology was pending on discharge. He was treated empirically for actinomyces infection with 6 weeks of penicillin G (final pathology results upon writing this summary did confirm actinomyces from bone culture). He was discharged with home infusion and PICC line. Drains were left in place, to be removed by ___. # Mild hypoxia: Patient had several days of mild hypoxia (90-93% on room air). Most likely due to asthma (with wheeze on physical exam) vs atelectasis. There was low concern for PE given his Well's score of 1 and subcutaneous heparin prophylaxis. Chest xray did not show acute pneumonia. He was given duonebs PRN. Patient with normal O2 saturation on room air on discharge. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Hyperlipidemia: He continued with home statin. # GERD: He continued home omeprazole. # Asthma: He initially continued with home albuterol inhaler, however, then received nebulized albuterol-ipratropium treatments PRN. Pt discharged back on home albuterol inhaler. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Drains were left in place, to be removed by ___ on follow-up appointment. - Final pathology results pending on discharge - ANTIBIOTICS: Needs IV pencillin G until ___ - ASTHMA: Pt reports using rescue inhaler 3 times weekly on average; Consider maintenance medications - CXR showed "Probable chronic interstitial abnormality in the base, as well as right upper lobe bronchiectasis and scarring. If further characterization of these abnormalities is needed, a CT of the chest could be obtained." - Code: Full - Contact: ___ ___
summary: You were admitted due to persistent swelling and pain in your face. The oral surgeons took you to the operating room to drain any excess fluid and remove any unhealthy tissue. They left some drains in place, which they will remove at your next appointment. Cultures and other samples from the surgery showed no growth on the day of discharge, likely due to antibiotic therapy prior to the procedure. However, you will continue to be treated for a presumed infection called "Actinomyces." For this, we placed a special intravenous access line called a PICC and you will continue to receive the antibiotics at home for 6 weeks.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs. ___ is a ___ yo F with PMH of CAD, HTN, Afib, s/p pacemaker, severe AS who presents with abdominal pain, rapid Afib, and chest pain. . 1)Atrial fibrillation with RVR: She has history of atrial fibrillation, not previously treated with anticoagulation, controlled prior with amiodarone and amlodipine. She presented in rapid atrial fibrillation with associated tachypnea requiring transfer to the MICU. She was seen by the cardiology consult team and she was treated with diltiazem and continued on amiodarone. In addition, she was started on coumadin, with a heparin bridge, per cardiology recommendations. She was not cardioverted during this admission, but will follow up in one month as an outpatient to further address whether cardioversion is indicated. She did well and was converted to long acting diltiazem prior to discharge and continued on outpatient regimen of amiodarone 100mg every other day. She will follow up with Dr. ___ as an outpatient. . 2)Chest pain/CAD: she has h/o stable angina at home, on aspirin/statin/acei/ntg. Episode of increased chest pain w/ palpatations likely represents demand myocardial ischemia in the setting of RVR, cardiac enzymes and slight troponin leak also consistent with this. Her rate was controlled and her chest pain resolved. She was continued on statin and aspirin. . 3)Abdominal Pain: Initially there was concern for possible CBD stone and biliary obstruction based on patients symptoms and ultrasound. She was evaluated by the ERCP fellow, however no intervention was undertaken given patients acute cardiac issues. Her abdominal pain resolved and she had no LFT abnormalities or concerns for cholangitis. Given underlying medical issues, intervention was deferred given symptom resolution. She will follow up as an outpatient, as she did have gall bladder stone on ultrasound. . 3)Asthma -pt with ___ year h/o asthma, although no h/o smoking, living with a smoker or other exposures. She has been treated for acute infection and COPD flare since admission. She completed a course of azithromycin and ceftriaxone during her admission. She was also treated with prednisone taper of 30mgx3 days (___), 20mg ___, 10mg x3 ___ then stop. She was also continued on inhaled agents including fluticasone and salmeterol as well as prn atrovent nebulizers. Albuterol nebulizers were held because of her tachycardia. . 4)Aortic stenosis: severe AS (<0.8cm2) and moderate LVH on ECHO, h/o chronic dizziness but no syncopal episodes at home recently. . 5)Hypertension: normotensive currently on regimen of dilt and amio. Her lisinopril 10mg ___ was held during admission because of sbp <120. . 6)Dyslipidemia: continue lipitor . 7)Chronic renal failure: approximately baseline currently, with Cr 1. . 8)Hypothyroidism: continue synthroid (___ checked on admission, wnl) . 9)Glaucoma: continue timolol 10) FEN: nectar thick liquids, soft diet per speech and swallow evaluation. 11)Code status: No CPR; Intubation OK (confirmed with daughter ___ ___ 12)Communication: Daugther ___, home ___ cell ___ Neice ___ ___ ___ cell
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having belly pain, chest pain and palpitations. You were found to be in rapid atrial fibrillation, a fast irregular heart rate. You were treated with a medication called diltiazem and continued on your amiodarone. You were also started on a blood thinner, coumadin, to decrease your risk of complications from atrial fibrillation such as stroke. Your amlodipine was stopped. You will be taking diltiazem instead. You also had an ultrasound to evaluate your abdominal pain. You had a gall stone in your gallbladder and some dilation of your bile ducts. You did not require any intervention to remove the stones as your pain resolved and your blood work was normal.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year old female with a complicated PMH including type I DM, gastroparesis, asthma, chronic pancreatitis who presents to the ED with hyperglycemia and DKA in the setting of stopping her insulin. . Primary: 250.13 DIABETES TYPE I, KETOACIDOSIS Secondary: 250.03 DIABETES TYPE I, UNCONTROLLED, W/O COMPLICATIONS Secondary: 790.6 HYPERGLYCEMIA - Patient re-started Insulin upon admission with improvement in her blood sugars. She worked with the medical team to adjust her evening Lantus dose and her sliding scale coverage based on her oral intake and her tube feeds. She was followed by ___ during her stay. Prior to discharge, she agreed to continue taking Insulin as an outpatient and follow-up with her ___ ___. . Secondary: 493.90 ASTHMA, CHRONIC STABLE - Patient was continued on Advair and inhalers prn. . Secondary: 301.83 BORDERLINE PERSONALITY DISORDER - Patient was initially agitated and confrontational, but when symptoms of Tramadol withdrawal were addressed she calmed down considerably. She agreed to taper down her Seroquel (decreasing 25mg per day), taper off of Tramadol, stop using Trazodone, and limit Valium use. She continued to rely heavily on Benadryl to "keep calm and not anxious." In order for these steps in the right direction to continue, close outpatient psychiatry follow-up will be critical. She seroquel dose was tapered to 300 by discharge and her Tramadol was down to 150 -- both slow tapers should be continued as an outpatient. . Secondary: 577.1 PANCREATITIS, CHRONIC - Patient was continued on Ultram with a plan for a slow taper off. However, she states she receives no pain control from Tramadol; the slow taper is simply to avoid the symptoms of withdrawal. She received no narcotics while on the medical floor and did not complain of severe abdominal pain. Her G-tube was replaced and tube feeds were re-started. . VAGINAL BLEEDING: - Patient reports persistent, heavy vaginal bleeding, which is being addressed on an outpatient basis. She would like to have her IUD removed, but agrees than an alternative contraceptive plan will need to be agreed upon beforehand. .
summary: You were admitted with hyperglycemia, which was a result of stopping your Insulin. Your blood sugars improved when Insulin was re-started. You also pulled the cap off your G-tube, and had to have your G-tube replaced, which was done without complications. You began to taper down your Tramadol and Seroquel, which should be continued as an outpatient. You also found that you didn't require Trazodone at bedtime, and were able to use less Valium as well. These are all really important steps towards improving your overall health, and you've already made a lot of progress. Please continue working with Dr. ___ to continue these tapers. The updated doses of your medications are on the attached medication list. Dr ___ continue to work to find you a therapist; she will let Dr ___ ___ if this works out.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with h/o GERD and mild esophagitis/gastritis who presented with recurrent nausea, vomiting, and epigastric pain. # Vomiting/epigastric pain: Patient with h/o GERD and mild esophagitis/gastritis presented with transient, small-volume coffee-ground emesis following multiple episodes of non-bloody emesis, possibly ___ ___ tear. Her known esophagitis/mild gastritis likely contributed, as did GERD in the setting of heavy coffe and soda consumption and cigarette smoking. On a prior admission concluding ___, CXR and KUB were without e/o free air or obstruction, RUQ US was negative for acute cholelithiasis or cholecystitis, and EKG was negative for acute ischemic changes. Upper GI series on the current admission was completely unremarkable. She remained HD stable with stable Hct (37.8-39.7) and normal LFTs/lipase throughout admission. Home PPI, sucralfate, promethazine, and maalox/diphenhydramine/lidocaine were continued, and she was counseled on GERD risk factor reduction. She was tolerating a regular diet without recurrent emesis, hematemesis, or epigastric pain by the time of discharge. Gastric emptying study and close GI follow-up were planned post-discharge. # Nausea: Patient with chronic intermittent nausea with recent acute exacerbations on ___ and ___ admissions represented with severe nausea of uncertain etiology. Home promethazine was continued for symptomatic management. She was tolerating a regular diet without recurrent nause by the time of discharge. # Leukocytosis: Downtrending leukocytosis, absent by the time of discharge corresponding with symptomatic resolution, likely reflected stress response in the setting of nausea/vomiting. She remained afebrile/HD stable without other signs or symptoms of infection throughout admission. # Depression/Anxiety: She denied SI/HI, and home risperidone, sertraline, and trazodone were continued throughout admission. # Asthma: She remained asymptomatic, and home albuterol and fluticasone were continued throughout admission. # Lumbar radiculopathy: She remained asymptomatic, and acetaminophen was made available for pain control. #Transitional issues: -Nausea/vomiting/epigastric pain: Close GI follow-up has been arranged for continued monitoring, and gastric emptying study is scheduled. She remains on omeprazole, sucralfate, promethazine, and Maalox/diphenhydramine/lidocaine.
summary: As you know, you were admitted because you were experiencing nausea and vomiting. An x-ray study of your esophagus, stomach, and small intestine was normal without obstructions or masses. Please return to the hospital at 10am on ___ (please see below) for an additional study of your stomach motility. Your symptoms are likely partially due to irritation in the stomach and esophagus. We continued medication to help protect the lining of your stomach. Your symptoms are worsened by caffeine and cigarette smoking, and it is therefore recommended that you decrease your coffee and soda intake and consider smoking cessation.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ gentleman man with T1DM, cyclic vomiting syndrome, and asthma, admitted to the FICU with DKA and asthma exacerbation. # DKA: precipitant is likely N/V as well as poor compliance with home insulin. Abdomen is tender, but most likely in the setting of vomiting/DKA and also has chronic similar abdominal pain. He was started on an insulin gtt and fluids then transitioned to a subQ regimen. Per ___ Consult team: NPH 10U at breakfast while on prednisone, Glargine 22U at bedtime, Sliding scale with meals. While not eating well, sliding scale was changed to 4units BG 71-100 then +1:50>100, with 2:50>200 at bedtime. # Type 1 DM: diagnosed at age ___, not well-controlled. Last A1c 11.5 ___. See above for regimen adjustments. Hyperglycemia promoted by high dose corticosteroids for during hospitalization. Per ___ records, frequently misses endocrine appointments. ___ was consulted. Encouraged outpatient follow-up with endocrine # Asthma exacerbation: s/p 60 PO prednisone in the ED. He was started on a prednisone burst of 40 mg daily for 5 days (D1 = ___ and was continue on home flovent and albuterol inhaler PRN # Cyclic vomiting: protonix BID, Zofran. Has been followed by Dr. ___ at ___, as well as evaluation at ___. Gastric emptying study at ___ in ___ showed rapid gastric emptying. He was continued on Zofran PRN home protonix 40 BID TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Started on 5 days prednisone for asthma (to end ___ - Adjusted insulin while on prednisone: NPH 10U at breakfast while on prednisone, Glargine 22U at bedtime, Sliding scale with meals. While not eating well, sliding scale was changed to 4units BG 71-100 then +1:50>100, with 2:50>200 at bedtime. Communication: ___ Mother ___
summary: You were seen at ___ for DKA and an asthma exacerbation. You were given a medication for your asthma (prednisone) that can cause your blood sugar to be high. Please take the 10U NPH insulin for the remain three (3) days of your steroid course in addition to your Humalog sliding scale and your home-dose lantus. Please note that we have reduced your sliding scale while eating to: 4units for blood sugar 71-100 +1unit for every 50 over 100 blood sugar (eg 5U for 120, 6U for 170, etc) At bedtime: +2units for every 50 over 200 (eg 2U for 230, 4units for 270, etc) Once your meals return to normal, please restart your home sliding scale. We strongly recommend that you make an appointment with your endocrinologist within the next two weeks.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with metastatic NSCLC and h/o multiple venous thromboembolic events admitted with new LLE DVT and DVT while on anticoagulation. # DVT/PE: Pt has history of multiple thromboembolic events and was admitted with new left leg swelling and dyspnea and found to have LLE DVT and new R PE but overall decreased clot burden compared with ___. Recent decrease in dose of enoxaparin may have been inciting event for this new LLE thrombus although overall clot burden on CTA decreased. He was started on heparin drip in ED and admitted to the floor for management and consideration of IVC filter. We discussed options with patient and primary oncology team and decided to attribute clot to subtherapeutic lovenox dosing and increase lovenox to 80 SC BID. We decided not to place IVC filter since it would likely clot off and overall clot burden was decreased on CT. Final read of CTA should be followed up as an outpatient and he has an appointment on ___. # Pleuritic left CP: Patient reported pleuritic left sided chest pain likely from PE as well as lymphangitic spread of metastatic cancer. He did not have any signs or symptoms to suggest infectious process. Pain was controlled with Dilaudid PO (___). He was continued on Oxycontin. # Lung cancer: Patient has metastatic NSCLC with lymphangitic spread as above. Current management per primary oncologists, Dr. ___ Dr. ___ follow up with in 3 days. # Constipation/Abdominal pain: Patient complained of abdominal pain and constipation, likely secondary to narcotics. He had nonobstructive pattern on abd XR. Colace, Senna, and lactulose added while on dilaudid. # Asthma: Continued albuterol prn # Low grade fevers: Pt was having low grade temperatures to 100.3 at time of discharge which were attributed to DVT. He was instructed to return to ER or call oncologist if he began having fevers >100.7 as these were less likely to be from DVT
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with leg swelling and shortness of breath. These symptoms were likely from a blood clot in your leg which likely traveled to your lungs. You were started on a blood thinning medication called heparin and transitioned to an increased dose of Lovenox. We discussed this with your primary oncologists and decided not to pursue placement of an IVC filter (which prevents blood clots from traveling from the legs to the lungs). We made the following chnages to your medications 1. We increased your Lovenox to 80mg twice daily 2. We added Nystatin swish and swallow for thrush 3.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ FTM transgender (on testosterone) w/ PMHx depression, PTSD, question of fibromyalgia, reportedly Ehlers ___, and migraines, admitted with persistent headache, likely secondary to medication overuse. ACTIVE ISSUES ============= # Medication overuse headache: Pt presented with 3-week h/o of persistent headache. Neurologic examination was significant only for bilateral V1 distribution numbness. Pt was seen by neurology, who felt the headache was likely multifactorial secondary to medication overuse headache (increased Fiorcet and Naproxen use), with low suspicion for low pressure headache (possibly ___ CSF leak post LP). Per neurology, Fioricet and Naproxen were discontinued, Topamax was increased to 50mg BID. Pt also treated with Medrol dose pak. Pt discharged with close follow up scheduled with neurology and PCP and short course of tramadol for breakthrough pain. MRI head/spine were performed to r/o low pressure headache and was unremarkable except for incidental findings listed below. # Malnutrition Pt is underweight and severely malnourished in setting of anorexia and reported gastroparesis as evidenced by loss of 26% body weight in ___ year. Pt was seen by nutrition. Pt recommended to have supplements, and start multivitamin with minerals daily. Pt also received 100mg thiamine for three days for refeeding risk. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== # Fibromyalgia: continued home lyrica 125 mg TID # Asthma: continued home Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID # Depression/PTSD: continued home fluoxetine # GERD: Continue home ranitidine. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ============================= 1. Pt needs to complete Medrol taper. Pt to take Medrol 4mg on ___. 2. Pt may benefit from starting an albuterol inhaler in the future for treatment of exercise-induced asthma 3. Area of low signal is identified in the expected location of the gallbladder, suggestive of gallstone, correlation with abdominal ultrasound is recommended for further characterization. 4. Punctate area of enhancement identified in the left temporal lobe, with no evidence of mass effect or edema, follow-up MRI with and without contrast in two weeks, or as clinically warranted is recommended to demonstrate stability or any further change. # CODE: Full # CONTACT: ___ ("like a step mom to me") ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Docusate Sodium 200 mg PO BID 2. Fluoxetine 40 mg PO DAILY 3. Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID 4. Metoclopramide 10 mg PO TID 5. Omeprazole 40 mg PO BID 6. Pregabalin 125 mg PO TID 7. Ranitidine 150 mg PO BID 8. Tizanidine ___ mg PO BID:PRN muscle spasms 9. Topiramate (Topamax) 25 mg PO QHS
summary: You were admitted because of your worsening headaches. Your headaches are likely from medication overuse from too much Fioricet and Naproxen. Overuse of analgesic medications (like Fioricet and Naproxen) can cause medication overuse headaches. It is important to continue to hold these medications to prevent future medication overuse headaches. You were treated with steroids (Medrol dose pak), which you should continue. You will take Medrol 4mg on ___. You underwent an MRI of the head and MRI spine which ruled out a low pressure headache. There was an incidental finding of punctate lesion in the temporal lobe of the brain which is likely unrelated to your headaches.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo woman with HFpEF (40-50%), permanent afib (rate controlled on amiodarone), CKDIII, HTN, and hypothyroidism admitted for exertional dyspnea, elevated BNP (___), and pulmonary edema on CXR that was most consistent with acute on chronic heart failure exacerbation. Negative troponins, CBC wnl, INR 2.0, and negative UA. UCx with mixed flora c/w contamination. BCx pending on discharge. #Acute on Chronic Heart Faiulre: Precipitant of acute exacerbation felt to be acute elevation in blood pressure (145-162 systolic on admission). As the patient was not on any anti-hypertensives, we discharged her on 10 mg of Lisinopril that can be uptitrated with appropriate electrolyte monitoring as an outpatient. Patient did not endorse any medication non-adherence or dietary indiscretions. She did endorse a new mildly productive cough for 2 days prior to admission, but no fever and normal WBC. The patient improved symptomatically after IV Lasix 40 mg x 5 over 3 days with an ambulatory O2 sat 96. K/Mg were repleted as appropriate. She was net even on 40mg PO QD of Lasix before discharge. #Hypertension: As the patient had BP>140s systolic >2x on admission with report of variable BPs at home, we discharged her on Lisinopril 10 mg, which can be uptitrated as an outpatient. We felt this was a good first-line BP medication given concurrent CKD. We did not think this was white coat hypertension because her blood pressure tended to predictably increase to the 140s-50s in the ___ and early AM before her next dose of Lisinopril. Her Cr bump was within expected range after starting ACEi at 5 mg (1.4-->1.6) and then discharging her on 10 mg (Cr 1.4). #Permanent atrial fibrillation: Patient was monitored on tele and continued to be in rate-controlled atrial fibrillation on admission on amiodarone and apixaban. She has had many cardioversions in the past and underwent DCCV on ___ as an inpatient, which was successful and put her back in sinus rhythm. She has an appointment to follow-up with cardiologist Dr. ___. # CKD: Patient was around baseline Cr ~1.5 during admission and discharge Cr was 1.4 # Asthma: continue albuterol q6h PRN: shortness of breath # Hypothyroidism: continue levothyroxine 50 mcg 5x/week; 100 mcg 2x/week # Ventral hernia: Has been evaluated as outpatient; did not have signs of obstruction or incarceration during this admission. # GERD: continued home ranitidine 75 mg daily Transitional Issues: New Meds: Lisinopril 10mg daily Diuretic Regimen: Lasix 40 mg PO daily CODE: FULL CONTACT: Daughter, ___, ___ ___ weight: 83.7kg
summary: You were admitted for feeling short of breath when you were walking, which we attributed to a build up of fluid in your lungs due to your heart not pumping blood forward as well as it can. For this, we gave you an IV medication to take some fluid off your body called "Lasix" or "Furosemide". Your weight on admission was 189.1 lbs and your weight on discharge was 184. We discharged you on a home dose of Lasix of 40mg. Please take this daily to help prevent fluid building up again. There are many causes of the heart not working as well as it should. We suspect that your elevated blood pressures might be contributing, in part, to your heart not working as well as it should. Therefore, we started you on a blood pressure medication called Lisinopril, which also can help improve your kidney function in the long-term.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with new diagnosis of diffuse large B cell lymphoma here for her third cycle of R-EPOCH. No complications. ACTIVE Issues: # Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma: Received 5 days of R-EPOCH therapy through double lumen for lymphoma. No complications from therapy. Reported mild nausea well controlled with compazine #Port-site hematoma: Mild hematoma at port site. The IV nurse ___ evaluated and noted good return on needle aspiration, with no dermal extension. Decision made to continue chemotherapy, and monitor for extension. No extension noted. Believed to be ___ SQ lovenox #PICC associated DVT: H/o DVT of RUE in ___. RUE U/S found no thrombus, and SQ lovenox d/c'ed both at admission and discharge. Will need to follow-up with Dr. ___ additional treatment CHRONIC Issues: #Reactive Airway Disease: Pt has h/o asthma well controlled with albuterol nebs BID and rescue inhaler. Reported SOB for several nights while in-house that was relieved by albuterol inhaler. No cough, productive sputum, or physical exam findings consistent with pulmonary pathology. #Constipation: Pt has h/o severe constipation during last hospital stay. Treated with colace prophylactically, and senna/dulcolax when needed. BM normal at time of discharge TRANSITIONAL Issues: #Neupogen: Will need to start day after discharge and take until counts return. Pt has own supply from prior cycles #Lovenox: Decision of whether or not to restart to be made outpt physician ___ on ___: Preadmission medications listed are correct and complete. Information was obtained from webOMR. 1. Acyclovir 400 mg PO Q8H 2. Albuterol 0.083% Neb Soln 1 NEB IH BID:PRN shortness of breath 3. Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q6H 4. Enoxaparin Sodium 80 mg SC Q12H hold for plt < 50k 5. Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID 6. Lactulose 15 mL PO BID:PRN constipation 7. FIRST-Mouthwash BLM *NF* (___) 200-25-400-40 mg/30 mL Mucous Membrane TID 8. Nystatin Oral Suspension 5 mL PO QID 9. Ranitidine 150 mg PO DAILY 10. Sulfameth/Trimethoprim SS 1 TAB PO DAILY 11. Bisacodyl 10 mg PO DAILY:PRN constipation 12. Vitamin D Dose is Unknown PO DAILY 13. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO TID 14. Biotene Dry Mouth *NF* (lactoperoxi-gluc oxid-pot thio) Mucous Membrane BID 15. Milk of Magnesia 30 mL PO Q6H:PRN constipation
summary: You were admitted because you needed medication through your veins to help treat your cancer. You received 5 days of therapy. There were no complications from treatment. You will need to take Neupogen for several days after discharge because the treatment you received will lower your infection fighting cells. Please start taking this on the day after your discharge and finish the box that you have (8 days) unless otherwise instructed by your physician. Lovenox was stopped because your port site had a small area where blood was pooling. This was evaluated by the IV nurse, with the decision to continue your chemotherapy. Your port site was monitored daily and no enlargement of the blood collection was noted. We looked at your right arm veins with an ultrasound and found no sign of clot. Because there was no clot, we did not put you on lovenox at discharge.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ with a PMH of ___ syndrome and asthma who presented with cough, fever and chills with mild leukocytosis and CXR findings c/w pneumonia. Bronchospasm consistent with asthma was also evident on physical exam. The patient was treated with high dose steroids and antibiotics for asthma exacerbation and pneumonia and discharged with extensive follow-up. . This was a brief hospitalization lasting under 48 hours. The hospital course in full is summarized below: . PROBLEM #1: Shortness of Breath: The patient had prolonged expiratory wheezes on exam consistent with his known asthma. He additionally had CXR findings consistent with RLL PNA. Given accompanying fevers, chills, night sweats, leukocytosis, CXR and exam findings, we opted to treat for community aquired pneumonia and asthma. The patient recieved Azithromycin and Ceftriaxone while in-house. He was discharged on Azithromycin 250mg QD for 4 days and Cefpodoxime 200mg BID for 14 days. For his asthma, he was given nebulizers and his home inhalers; these were likewise continued at time of discharge. He recieved two days of pulse-dose Prednisone while in house and was given a prescription for one additional day for a total of three days high dose prednisone. After speaking with his outpatient doctors at the ___, it was determined that the patient should then return to his tapered, low-dose Prednisone regimen (5mg QOD). The patient requested an appointment with ___; an appointment was made with Dr. ___ for 10:30AM the following day. The patient was discharged without any shortness of breath but still with some wheezing. He was ambulating well with 02 sats approaching 100% on ambulation. . PROBLEM #2: ___ Syndrome: Mr. ___ is followed by the ___ Rheumatology and Pulmonology Groups (but expressed a strong desire to switch to ___ pulmonology as described above). Per discussion with his rheumatologist at the ___, the patient appeared at his baseline churg ___ burden. As described, he was discharged on his home low-dose taper of 5mg Prednisone QOD, to begin after his final dose of high dose Prednisone for asthma. Follow-up with his ___ rheumatologist (Dr. ___, ___ was arranged for ___ at 10AM. Follow-up with a new ___ pulmonologist waw arranged as described above. . PROBLEM #3: Hypertension: Patient was not hypertensive on admission. His home medications were continued. . PROBLEM #4: FEN: Patient recieved 3L NS. Electrolytes were monitored per usual. . PROBLEM #5: COMMUNICATION: with patient, unaccompanied, with whom the clinical diagnosis and prognosis was discussed and questions were answered to the best of my ability. . CODE: Remained full.
summary: You were admitted with pneumonia and asthma. You were given nebulizers, antibiotics, intravenous fluids, and high dose steroids. You recovered quickly. You will need to complete a course of antibiotics to ensure resolution of your infection. Likewise, you will need a very short course of high dose steroids before you resume your usual lower dose. Prescriptions are provided for these and your other medications in the attached pages. We have not made any other changes in your medications. You stated that you would like to have outpatient follow-up at our facility. We have set-up an appointment for you at our pulmonology clinic.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is an ___ gentleman with bladder cancer, dysphagia, and recent 40 lb unintentional weight loss, admitted for scheduled G-tube placement, s/p PEG on ___. ================ ACTIVE ISSUES: ================ #Unintentional weight loss #Failure to thrive Patient experienced a 40lb unintentional weight loss over the past year and a half. A significant medical work up included two endoscopies and swallow study, all of which were negative. Due to his continuing weight loss and failure to thrive, he was admitted to the ___ for his procedure, and additionally tube feed initiation with careful monitoring and inpatient colonoscopy. His PEG tube was placed by ___ on ___, without complications. He underwent colonoscopy during his inpatient stay to exclude additional primary malignancy at the request of his outpatient gastroenterologists and urologists. Colonoscopy on ___ which showed only polyp and diverticulosis. In the first 24 hours following PEG placement, his oral intake and appetite improved significantly. Nutrition was consulted for tube feeding recommendations, which werwe intiated ___. He tolerated goal rate without side effects. He was monitored for refeeding and labs were within normal limit at discharge. #Severe protein calorie malnutrition: #Dysphagia: GI and speech and swallow evaluations and work up were negative and our speech and swallow service determined diet of regular solids and thin liquids were safe. ___ have had a component of functional dysphagia per S+S eval ___, in addition to lack of appetite. Nutrition and speech and swallow recommendations were appreciated. Weight on discharge: 67.72kg. =============== CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #Bladder cancer: The patient has a history of recurrent bladder cancer s/p transurethral resection of bladder tumors in ___, ___, and ___ and BCG/IFN therapy He has mild hematuria at baseline which continued intermittently during his inpatient admission. His CBC was trended and his Hgb remained stable. #GERD: We continued the patient's home pantoprazole 40 mg daily. #Asthma: He was written for his home albuterol prn. ==================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [ ] Please check electrolytes by ___ [ ] PEG tube care and maintenance (was discussed with the patient but should be followed up) [ ] Follow weekly weights, titrate tube feeds as necessary Attending Addendum: I have seen and examined the patient on the day of discharge and agree with the resident's note above.
summary: Please see below for information on your time in the hospital. You were in the hospital because you needed a stomach feeding tube placed to help with your difficulty eating and recent weight loss. In the hospital, you had a feeding tube placed into your stomach. The interventional radiologists placed the stomach feeding tube on ___. We provided you instructions on how to care for the tube after you leave the hospital. We consulted the nutritionists and speech and swallow team to determine the plan for your tube feeds and to evaluate any medical causes of your difficulty swallowing and decreased food intake. Your swallowing was normal. You started to eat well. You will be going home with visiting nursing after your hospital stay.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with asthma, htn, morbid obesity s/p cholecystectomy and POD ___ s/p sacrospinous suspension, anterior repair, tension-free vaginal tape cystoscopy by Dr. ___ for urinary incontinence and recurrent UTIs who is admitted from urgent care with two days of epigastric abdominal pain and CT scan showing possible pancreatitis vs duodenitis. . # Epigastric Abdominal Pain: CT showed possible evidence of duodenitis and pancreatitis. Pancreatic enzymes were within normal limits. RUQ ultrasound did not show evidence of choledocholithiasis. She was evaluated by ob/gyn who felt that her pain was not related to her recent sacrospinous suspension repair. An upper endoscopy revealed gastritis which was felt likely to be the etiology of her pain. H.pylori antigen was negative. Biopsies were taken on EGD,with results to be followed up by her PCP Dr ___ ___ gastroenterologist Dr ___ at ___. Her pain was managed inhouse with narcotics, resolved following endoscopy, and she was discharged on a proton pump inhibitor. #Chest/shoulder pain: She developed left chest pain which subsequently moved to her left shoulder. Pain was reproducible on palpation. She had a negative workup for myocardial infarction. CXR showed findings felt to be consistent with atelectasis. Pain was felt to be due to a combination of reflux, musculoskeletal pain and anxiety and improved with treatment. #Anemia: She was noted to have a microcytic anemia with low iron. This was felt to be secondary to anemia of chronic disease or occult GI bleed. She did not note any gross evidence of GI bleeding. A screening colonoscopy by Dr ___ is recommended as an outpatient. . #Asthma: She continued her home albuterol and advair. . #Urinary incontinence: POD 8 sacrospinous suspension. She was healing appropriately and will follow up with gynecology as planned. She was DNR only, with intubation allowed, during this hospitalization.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having pain in your belly. You had an endoscopy which showed that you had areas of irritation called gastritis. Biopsies were taken from these areas. You were started on a new medication, pantoprazole, to decrease stomach acid, which can contribute to this kind of irritation. Please avoiding using non steroidal medications (for example, aleve, ibuprofen), which can worsen the irritation and aspirin which can predispose to bleeding. It is also recommended that you avoid fatty or spicy foods, and that you eat your last meal of the day at least three hours before going to bed. Sleep with the head of your bed at a 45 degree angle so that you do not lie flat in bed. You were also noted to have some anemia. It is recommended that you have a screening colonscopy as an outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old gentlemen with PMH of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, depression, BPH presenting with recurrent urinary retention requiring a Foley and two days of diarrhea. . # Urinary retention. Urology was consulted in the ED and recommended placement of a 2-way 22 ___ Foley catheter. The Foley did not clog up during the admission and was draining well on discharge. He has a history of BPH and also had a UTI on admission. Due to his frequent catheterization, the patient has been having clots that are obstructing Foley drainage. The patient will be discharged with his Foley in place and follow-up with his urologist, Dr. ___, as an outpatient for trial of void. He will be continued on cefpodoxime until his urologist appointment at which time it can be continued or stopped at the outpatient provider's discretion. . # UTI. Patient has had a UTI for approximately 2 months that has not responded to courses of Augmentin and Ciprofloxacin. This may be due to intermittent Foley placement over the past 2 months, colonization, or because of bacterial resistance to antibiotics. He will be treated with cefpodoxime until his outpatient follow-up appointment with urology. . # BPH. Continue home Flomax and Avodart. . # Diarrhea. Given his recent antibiotic use, a stool sample was checked for C. Diff toxin and was negative. The patient was encouraged to keep well hydrated at home. . # Depression. Continue home citalopram. . # Essential tremor. Continue home Sinemet. . # Hyperlipidemia. Continue home niacin. . # Asthma. Continue home Flovent and albuterol PRN. . # Rosacea. Continue home doxycycline. . # CODE: Of note, the patient expressed his wishes to be DNR/DNI.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for urinary retention secondary to a clogged Foley catheter, a urinary tract infection, and two days of diarrhea. Your Foley catheter was replaced with a 2-way 22 ___ catheter per urology recommendations. It has been flushing well and has not clogged during your admission. You will be sent home with this Foley in place and have a trial of void as an outpatient at Dr. ___ ___. Your UTI will be treated with cefpodoxime and your urine culture will be followed by your outpatient urologist. A stool sample was sent for infection and was negative. You were rehydrated with IV fluids. Please hydrate yourself at home by drinking plenty of fluids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o woman with Type 1 DM, Asthma/COPD, recurrant PNA admitted with new LUL and Lingular PNA and DKA, and due to BP 90/44 in ED was initially admitted to the ICU. . 1. LUL Pneumonia/recurrrent pneumonias/Asthma/COPD/sinusitis/rhinitis: She was initially placed on vancomycin, Levaquin, and ceftrriaxone. On hospital day 3 pt was afebrile, so ceftriaxone was discontinued. She remained afebrile, with WBC count trending down, so Vancomycin was stopped the following day. During the course of her admission, her respiratory status improved, and she was slowly weaned off oxygen. By discharge, 95% at rest, 88-90% with ambulation. Completed 10 day course levoquin in house, no further antibiotics prescribed. Maintained on advair, albuterol, atrovent added. Nasonex, allegra also maintained. Discharged on this regimen and with home oxygen given low ambulatory sat. Given the recurrent nature of her pneumonia (in different anatomical segments) as well as chronic asthma, sinsusitis, rhinitis etc, IgG levels were sent and were sent and were found to be mildly decreased (particularly IgG-2). Additionally, concern for ___. ESR 68, rheum factor negative, ANCA negative. IGE and asperigillosis ab sent--?ABPA. She will need outpatient follow up with her PCP and Dr. ___ pulmonologist, to further manage her care. BOth were updated with hospital course and concerns. . 2. Chest Pain Left sided pleuritic chest pain, most likely secondary to large L sided lobar pneumonia/pleuritis. She ruled out for MI by cardiac enzymes x3. CTA was negative for PE. Her pain was controlled by discharge.. . 3. DM Type 1 with DKA Metabolic acidosis (ABG shows pH 7.20) with AG up to 13 and urine with 150 ketones on admission. DKA likely triggered by underlying infection. Insulin gtt transitioned to glargine 25 units at night and humalog sliding scale. After transfer to the floor and more consistent food intake, fingerticks remained very elevated (in 300s-400s). Her Lantus dose was up-titrated and she was started on standing pre-meal insulin along with sliding-scale correction. These were repeatedly up-titrated. Lantus 50U by discharge. ___ follow up in place. . 4. Nausea/Vomiting Likely due to DKA, improved after transfer to the floor. . 5. Hyponatremia On admission. Likely hypovolemic hyponatremia. Improved following volume resuscitation with NS. . 6. HTN BP low on admission likely secondary to significant volume depletion. Initially, her home medications (HCTZ, amlodipine, and cozaar) were held. However, these were subsequently restarted after transfer to the floor since her pressures were elevated. . 7. Hyperlipidemia She was continued on lipitor. . 8) Lower Extremity Edema/Pulmonary Hypertension She had significant lower extremity edema after transfer to the floor. She was given Lasix 10mg IV daily after transfer to the floor. After 5 days, her lower extremity edema had disappeared. Given this edema and her significant respiratory complaints, a TTE was done and was as above, significant for a normal EF, but dilated RV with evidence of moderate pulmonary hypertension. Could be secondary to chronic pulmonary disease. Also, given her body habitus and report of significant snoring and daytime somnolence, she may also have undiagnosed obstructive sleep apnea. She would benefit from an outpatient sleep study to evalutae this. . . 9. Depression Continued on home celexa.
summary: You were admitted due to pneumonia and uncontrolled diabetes. You were treated with antibiotics for the pneumonia and now have completed a full regimen. For your diabetes, it is very important that you adhere to a diabetic diet and take your insulin as prescribed. You were found to have pulmonary hypertension on the ultrasound of your heart. This may be due to your asthma/COPD but sleep apnea could also be playing a role. You should talk to your primary care physician and pulmonologist about being set up with a sleep study to see if you do have sleep apnea, since this is a correctable condition that can have a great impact on your quality of life.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is an ___ yo woman with h/o CHF (EF ___, DM2, HTN, renal insufficiency, who was brought in by her daughter for progressive weakness, productive cough and SOB for the last ___ weeks. . #. Shortness of breath. Likely multiple factorial in this patient with h/o CHF with depressed EF, reactive airway disease, proof of infiltrative/infectious process on CXR LLL. Patient appears hypervolemic on exam: symmetric pitting edema, elevated JVD so likely contribution from vascular congestion. Wheezing: cardiac wheeze vs reactive airway disease. Patient received Lasix IV 40mg x1; home diuretic regimen of lasix and HCTZ restarted in house. Daily weights and I/Os recorded. Regarding reactive airway (flare likely triggered by PNA) disease patient maintained on home regimen with addition of standing and prn nebulizers. CAP treated with levofloxacin. At time of discharge patient respiratory status improved. . # Left lower lobe opacity: Opacity likely indicative of PNA which in patient with systemic sx of malaise, weakness. Patient treated with Levofloxacin 750mg QOD (renally dosed). Patient remained afebrile in house with WBC wnl at time of discharge. . # Elevated troponin on admission 0.06. After chart biopsy admission biomarkers consistent with baseline in patient with history of CKD. EKG unchanged from prior (Q waves in V1-V3) In house patient without complaints of chest pain/discomfort/palpations however h/o diabetes so silent MI was considered. Biomarkers trended in house; remained stable. . #. ? UTI. Patient asx. UA with few bacteria. ___ WBC. ___ epis. Patient will be double covered with levofloxacin though urine likely not source of infection with equivacol UA. Urine cultures with MIXED BACTERIAL FLORA ( >= 3 COLONY TYPES), CONSISTENT WITH SKIN AND/OR GENITAL CONTAMINATION. . # Chronic Kidney Insufficiency baseline creatinine 1.5-1.7. On admission 1.7. Daily chem 10 panel followed. Creatinine at time of discharge stable at 1.7. . # Diabetes. Oral therapy held in house. Patient monitored QID finger sticks, ISSS. . # HTN. Patient remained normotensive to slightly hypotensive in house. Outpatient home regimen of carvedilol, lasix, HCTZ, lisinopril initially continued as no evidence of ___. However on day of discharge patient noted to have asymptomatic hypotension with sbp in ___. Decision made to hold ACEI and HCTZ until patient seen in ___ clinic. . # Hyperlipidemia. Continued statin
summary: You presented to ___ due to gradual onset shortness of breath, productive cough and overall feelings of fatigue. Imaging demonstrated the presence of a pneumonia in your left lung. You were treated with antibiotics and your respiratory status was monitored. You also complained of left flank pain which could also be explained by the presence of pneumonia. While you were here your blood pressure was lower than normal. It will be important to continue to take in adequate fluids. In the interim please hold your lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide. Continue taking lasix and carvedilol as prescribed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: HOSPITAL COURSE: . Mr. ___ is a ___ y/o AAM w/ schizoaffective d/o, sleep apnea, asthma, obesity hypoventilation syndrome, on home o2 of 2L, bipap at night, w/ polycythemia likely ___ to hypoxemia, originally admitted for mania, transferred to micu for hypercapneic resp failure, transferred to general medicine wards for further management of pulm issues. . PROBLEMS: . # Hypercarbic Respiratory Failure: Per pulmonary, most likely secondary to either decreased resp drive from increasing patient supplemental oxygen to quickly suppressing his respiratory drive or secondary to lack of proper bipap settings. Patient had desaturated down to ___ while in inpatient psych facility. ACS was ruled out as a cause of resp failure. Patients baseline saturations on high ___ on 2L/NC on home o2. Patient was seen by the sleep consultants who felt that his bipap should be set as follows. Baseline sat ___ on RA per OMR. --Bipap used every night and w/ daytime naps, setting ___ w/ Oxygen 2L, titrated to sats above 85-90%. --Keep oxygen saturations between 85 and 90%. --During the day patient should be on n/c oxygen ___ titrate again sats btw 85-90%. --Avoid given supplement oxygen above 4L n/c, if needed while in house please reconsult pulmonary medicine and check ABG. --cont albuterol and ipratroprium standing nebulizers q6H. ___ switch back to inhalers as desired. --cont fluticasone as prescribed. . # Asthma( see above) . # Obstructive Sleep Apnea: (See above) Patient needs follow up with Dr. ___ in sleep clinic. He was evaluated by ENT for tonisellectomy. ENT felt tonsillectomy was not necessary at this time, but that pt may benefit from procedure at a later date. Sleep team felt that amphetamines or stimulants for daytime sleepiness were not indicated at this time. . # Secondary Polycythemia: Seen by heme consult service in ___ and again on ___. They felt polycythemia likely secondary to hypoxia, despite his epo being low in the past. He does not have the JAK2-V617F mutation, ,making a primary polycythemia less likely. No therapeutic indications for phlebotomy at this time. Heme did NOT think that hypoxia was secondary to sludging of RBCs in pulmonary vasculature. -Signs and symptoms to be aware of for sludging include headache, chest pain, and neurological or stroke like symptoms, numbness, weakness, sensory deficits, # Hypertension: Patient was normotensive during his hospital stay would continue. -continue lisinopril 10mg , HCTZ 25mg . # Hyperglycemia: No history of DM 2, however glucoses in 200s on the floor. ___ secondary to atypical antipsychotic. No evidence of DM HgA1C 6.9 -cont insulin sliding scale okay to d/c if sugars normalize . # Bipolar disorder: Defer to psychiatry team. While on the medicine floor he was responding to active internal stimuli. He was noted to have full conversation with himself while alone in his room. He stated that he did not want to discuss who he was talking to. He was followed by liason service while in house. He was discharged on the following medications. - geodon 80mg BID - valproic acid ___ BID . # Hypercholesterolemia: -continue simvastatin 20mg daily. . # Patient needs follow up with Hematology, repeat sleep study and then appointment with Dr. ___ Dr. ___ ___
summary: you were ___ to the ___ psychiatry facility to the ICU at ___, because of abnormalities in your breathing. You were found to have low oxygen levels in your blood and high CO2 levels in your blood. We believe this was because your bipap settings were not maximized. During your hospital stay you had a study of your heart done called an echocardiogram which was normal. You had a chest x-ray which was also normal. You were seen by the pulmonary doctors and sleep ___ who recommended that you continue using BiPap at night with a setting of ___ on ___ of oxygen titrating your pulse oximetry up to above 85%. During the day you should be on ___ of oxygen by nasal canula. This should be titrated to keep your oxygen sats above 85%. Please continue to use all of your nebulizers and inhalers.

text: Brief Hospital Course: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Right arm pain REASON FOR ADMISSION: ___ with recurrent MRSA cellulitis. Risk factors include pre-diabetes mellitus, PCOS, obesity, and family colonization with mrsa. She is afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and the abscess site appears grossly unchanged from yesterday with IV vanc (started ___ at 1g q12h). Her trough ___ was 3.3, however, so she was increased to 1.25g Q8h receiving first dose at midnight ___. ACTIVE ISSUES BY PROBLEM: # MRSA abscess and cellulitis: Her R upper arm abscess was incised and drained with wick packing ___, cultures of which subsequently grew MRSA. She was placed on IV vancomycin initially, then transitioned to PO bactrim on ___ with continued improvement. Her wound was repacked on a daily basis. Her other questionable infectious sites (groin, right nare) were observed closely. Her pain was controlled with ibuprofen and percocet. An infectious disease consult was obtained, who recommended decontamination of family members and house to prevent these recurrent MRSA infections. Please see transition of care issues below for steps on decontamination. Ms ___ will continue ___ DS two tabs BID for 8 more days through ___ and will follow up with her PCP that day to ensure resolution of wound/cellulitis and commence with decontamination for her and her family. # Intertiginous candidiasis: An erythematous rash was noted under both breasts, most consistent with an early candidal intertriginous process. Her intertriginous areas were kept dry with gauze and towels, and miconazole powder was used. The rash improved, and she was not given an rx for miconazole but was encouraged to see her PCP if this infection recurred. # Pre-Diabetes: Patient is obese with history of PCOS and gestational diabetes, as well as a family history of DM. She has pre-diabetes, as her HbA1c this admission was found to be 6.4. Given that several finger sticks were >200, she was placed on insulin sliding scale during hospitalization. She should have f/u with PCP with lifestyle interventions and possible anti-hyperglycemic medications. INACTIVE ISSUES BY PROBLEM: #Depression. Patient was continued on home citalopram 40mg daily #Asthma. Her lungs were clear on exam without wheezing throughout admission and she had subjective SOB or cough. She used her home albuterol and flovent. #Obesity. Undergoing ___ workup for gastric bypass TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -She will require completion of Bactrim course ending ___ -She and her family should undergo individual MRSA decontamination as well as household decontamination, as per infectious disease recommendations. -She has pre-diabetes and will require follow-up with PCP for lifestyle modifications and possible medications. DECONTAMINATION INSTRUCTIONS: Chlorhexadine 2-4% baths (cover whole body except groin/eyes) scrub for ___ minutes, rinse, for ___ day course. ___ cause irritation/drying -OR - ___ to ___ cup bleach ___ full bathtub, soak ___ minutes, rinse, ___. Additionally, should use mupirocin 2% to each nare BID for ___ days. MEDICATION CHANGES: START bactrim 2 pills twice daily until ___ START percocet as needed for severe pain START mupirocin nasal ointment to clear up MRSA colonization ___ the nose. Start this after finishing course of bactrim
summary: You were admitted with a right arm abscess. Your abscess was incised, drained, and packed. You were evaluated by our internal medicine and infectious disease teams and treated with an intravenous antibiotic called vancomycin, with improvement of your infection. You were then switched to an oral antibiotic (Bactrim), which you should continue until ___. It is recommended that you be decontaminated to MRSA, according to infectious disease suggestions. Given that your family has a history of MRSA skin infections, it is also recommended that you decontaminate your house and that individuals are decontaminated according to infectious disease recommendations. These are included ___ your discharge papers and can be addressed by your primary care doctor. While you are colonized, you should not be caring for sick or elderly persons. Your blood sugars were found to be high. Your HbA1c, a marker of your blood sugar, was 6.4. Your finger stick blood sugars were higher than 200 at times, so you were started on insulin injections while ___ the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old male with past history of CAD s/p stent at ___ E's ___, NIDDM, hypertension, hyperlipidemia who presents with several weeks of intermittent chest pain and a week of exertional dyspnea concerning for fluid overload and heart failure. # Coronaries: 100% chronic total occlusion of proximal total R PDA, s/p PCI to midLAD and Lcx, stent in midLAD, unknown type. (per ___ records) # Pump: normal EF # Rhythm: sinus #HFpEF exacerbation: Patient had progressive swelling of lower extremities with DOE and PND concerning for heart failure exacerbation. Trop neg x1. EKG had T wave flattening but no specific ST changes concerning for acute MI. Patient was started on IV Lasix for diuresis and transitioned to PO regimen of 40 mg Lasix. He was started on lisinopril 10 mg and carvedilol 12.5 mg PO BID. Echo records from outside hospital showed normal EF in ___. He also had records from ___ which showed chronic total occlusion of RCA and stent placed to mid-LAD. Stress echocardiogram was done which showed no inducible ischemia and stress ECG was without ischemic changes. He was discharged on atorvastatin 40mg, carvedilol 12.5mg BID, furosemide 40mg BID and lisinopril 20mg. He will follow up with an outpatient cardiologist, Dr. ___, for further management. Discharge weight: 130.8kg #Hypertensive urgency: Patient non-compliant with HTN meds at home (takes HCTZ intermittently) and found to have elevated SBP 180s on floor, asymptomatic. Home amlodipine, HCTZ, and verapamil were d/c'ed and patient was transitioned to lisinopril 20mg and carvedilol 12.5mg for both HTN and CAD/HF management. BP should be monitored and medications titrated as needed. #Low back pain - patient uses gabapentin, tramadol, and naproxen at home. Naproxen was discontinued given concern for ___ and cardiovascular risk. #Diabetes: Insulin sliding scale while inpatient. Restarted metformin on discharge. #Asthma: Controlled with Albuterol inhaler prn #OSA: non-compliant with CPAP. Encourage use as outpatient. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - patient to establish care with cardiologist for further outpatient f/u. Appointment schedule with Dr. ___. - continued titration of diuretic dose based on volume status and creatinine - monitor BP and titrate anti-hypertensives as needed - patient reports poor compliance with CPAP for OSA- please continue to encourage use Discharge weight: 130.8kg
summary: You were admitted to ___ due to lower extremity swelling and some new shortness of breath with walking. You were found to have signs of fluid overload and heart failure on exam and were treated with a medication called Lasix. You will need to continue this medication at home. You were also found to have very high blood pressure. You were started on carvedilol, a medication that helps treat your heart disease and high blood pressure. You were also started on Lisinopril for blood pressure control. You were also started on Lipitor for your heart disease. A stress echocardiogram was done to evaluate your heart for worsening heart disease and for heart pumping function. This showed no major abnormalities. We have set up an appointment for you with ___ cardiology (see below). You need to take these medications every day.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old female s/p fall down stairs, transferred from an outside hospital (OSH) with a CT torso showing right sided rib fractures ___ is comminuted)and an associated likely hemothorax for which a right chest tube was placed and put to suction. She was admitted to the Acute Care Surgery team and transferred to the floor for chest tube and respiratory monitoring and pain control. Because of her reported excessive alcohol consumption, she was administered IV fluids with thiamine and folate. Lorazapam was ordered and administered per CIWA scale. Mild tremors were reported, but she had no other acute episodes of delirium tremens. She was seen by the Social Worker for safety evaluation. While on the surgical floor, she was administered standing and as needed nebulizer treatments for wheezing which was consistent with her asthma history. She was placed on as needed oxygen via nasal cannula with oxygen saturations being in the low to high ___. On HD2, her chest tube was placed to water seal and her interval change CXR showed a small new right apical pneumothorax which was stable. Physical therapy was consulted and it was determined she may safely be discharged to home without services. On HD2, she reported concentrated urine for which a UA/UC was ordered and were both negative. On HD3, her chest tube was pulled and post-film showed the right apical pneumothorax was minimally decreased in size from the prior examination. She ambulated frequently and her oxygen saturation remained in the mid-high ___ at rest on room air. She remained hemodynamically stable, vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were encouraged throughout hospitalization. The patient received subcutaneous heparin and ___ dyne boots were used during this stay and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile and hemodynamically stable. The patient was tolerating a diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan. She was discharged to home with a 2 week follow-up appointment with ACS scheduled.
summary: You were transferred to ___ from an outside hospital for trauma evaluation after sustaining a fall. You were found to have multiple right sided rib fractures with an associated lung injury which required a chest tube placement. You were admitted to the Trauma service for further monitoring of your respiratory status and management of your chest tube. Your pain was managed with oral pain medication, you tolerated a regular diet, and ambulated safely. You were evaluated by Social Work as well as Physical Therapy who recommended your discharge to home. Your lung x-rays were stable and your chest tube was removed. You are now medically cleared to go home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr ___ is a ___, prior smoker, with CAD s/p CABG, HTN, DM, and chronic pancreatitis who presents with shortness of breath and cough. # Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure # Mild aspiration of thin liquids with pills # Possible pneumonia/aspiration pneumonia # Chronic cough associated with reactive airways, GERD, and post nasal drip # Shortness of breath, cough: He was admitted with diagnosis of pneumonia though a much broader workup was initiated given his history of relatively recent pneumonia s/p treatment as well as a history more consistent with subacute to chronic shortness of breath and cough. He underwent TTE, chest CT, SLP consultation with videoswallow, and pulmonary consultation. He was ultimately diagnosed with multifactorial cough related to (1) Hypervolemia/acute on chronic diastolic HF (based on elevated BNP, leg edema, mildly elevated JVP); (2) Pneumonia/aspiration is possible (based on possible pneumonia in LLL and documented aspiration with pills, especially given context of narcotic/benzodiazepine use; (3) Post-pneumonia reactive airways/cough variant asthma (given worsening/development of recalcitrant symptoms after his initial diagnosis of pneumonia); (4) cough related to post nasal drip (given his allergic rhinitis); and (5) cough related to GERD (given his GERD on PPI). He also had some mildly dilated bronchi bronchi in left lower lung which could be c/w bronchiectasis, and does have a long smoking history, so the pulmonary team felt that interval pulmonary followup with repeat chest CT was warranted to exclude an airway abnormality/neoplasm that could be causing symptoms. Notably he denied fevers, sweats, weight loss or hemoptysis that might be concerning for TB. His medications were adjusted as follows: - He was diuresed with IV lasix, and discharged on his home dose of 60mg daily - He was started on Losartan and Atorvastatin given dx of CHF and CAD - He was treated with CTX/Azithro inpatient, and discharged on cefpodox/azithro to complete a course of therapy for possible pneumonia/aspiration pneumonia - He was told to take all medications whole in applesauce or similar consistency to prevent aspiration events with pills - He was started on Symbicort for possible reactive airways/asthma/COPD - He was started on Flonase for allergic rhinitis - He was continued on BID PPI for GERD # Acute on chronic back pain: He had some worsening pain in the context of coughing and sitting in an uncomfortable hospital bed. Continued home regimen of fentanyl, oxycodone, pregabalin, tizanidine. He was given some standing tylenol while here. # Chronic panc # DM # HTN # CAD: Stable appearing. TTE did show segmental dysfunction c/w CAD. Continued home ASA, diltiazem. - Started Losartan and atorvastatin given CAD, DM, HTN. TRANSITIONAL: - IFN-gamma release assay pending at discharge for eval of TB exposure - Suggest monitoring BP and checking laboratory studies to follow Cr given start of Losartan - He needs interval cardiology followup for medical optimization of CAD and dCHF - He has pulmonary followup scheduled, but will need an interval chest CT performed just prior to this appointment. BILLING: >30 minutes spent coordinating his discharge.
summary: You were admitted to ___ with cough and shortness of breath. You had a detailed medical workup, including echocardiogram, chest CT scan, speech pathology consultation, and Pulmonary consultation. It was felt that your cough and dyspnea had several contributing causes: (1) Extra fluid retention from heart disease, (2) post-nasal drip from sinus congestion, (3) reflux, (4) post-infection airway reactivity and atelectasis, some aspiration of food particles. Your medications were adjusted to treat all of these, and you improved. As mentioned, you were seen by the speech pathology team and they did a videoswallow study that demonstrated some liquids going down the wrong pipe and into the lungs when you took medicines.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female with hypertension who presents with swollen right knee joint # Monoarticular arthritis, possibly due to calcium pyrophosphate cystals - Ms. ___ presented with a painful, swollen R-knee that was exquisitely tender to palpation. On presentation the range of motion in her knee was significantly diminshed due to pain, though she had full strength, proprioception and sensation in her R-foot. An xray of her R-knee showed a suprapatellar effusion, with degenerative changes consistent with calcium pyrophosphate deposition but no acute fracture. Arthrocentesis was performed and yielded 11K WBC and neg gram stain with no crystals. Rheumatology evaluated Ms. ___ on hospital day 2 and believed her presentation was consistent with a calcium pyrophosphate arthritis and recommended starting naproxen. Ms. ___ pain improved significantly on naproxen and she was able to ambulate with a walker. Physical therapy evaluated and recommended that she could go home with home ___. On day of discharge Ms. ___ was able to ambulate with a walker and had minimal pain. # Fever - Ms. ___ presented with fever of 100.9 with elevated CRP and ESR. However, she subsequently remained afebrile for the duration of her hospital course. CXR was negative for an acute process and cultures of urine, blood and synovial fluid were all negative as well. Her initial fever and elevated inflammatory markers were attributed to her pseudogout. # Asymptomatic Bactiuria: Ms. ___ urine culture performed on ___ grew Group A Strep. She complained of no dysuria or frequency and has had a normal mental status for the duration of her hospital stay. There seems to be no association with an infection precipitating a CPPD flair. We chose not to treat her for a UTI given she was asymptomatic. # Hypertension - Ms. ___ was normotensive throughout her hospital course. She was continued on her home atenolol. However, her home HCTZ was witheld for concern that it may have contributed to her synovitis. # Asthma- Ms. ___ was continued on her home albuterol and atrovent and had no respiratory complaints during her hospital course. # Hyperlipidemia- Ms. ___ was continued on her home atorvastatin.
summary: You were admitted to ___ with ___ painful swollen right knee. We evaluated your knee with an xray and joint fluid analysis. We believe the pain in your knee was caused by calcium pyrophosphate deposition, a condition in which minerals adhere to your joints and cause inflammation. We have treated you with naproxen, which is a strong anti inflammatory medication, as well as continued pain control with percocet. We do not believe your pain was caused by an infection, however, we are still waiting for culture results to effectively rule out that possibility. You were seen by physical therapy and they recommended that you continue to receive ___ at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with h/o asthma and hemorrhoids, blood in vaginal vault presents with c/o "BRBPR" but is likely on menstrual period. . ACUTE ISSUES: # BRBPR / clots in BMs: Pt came to ED describing several bloody BMs with clots in past day, with Hct at baseline and no symptoms of anemia. Differential included diverticulosis, hemorrhoid, rapid upper GI bleeding. Exam and CT abd/pelvis are not consistent with diverticuitis, diverticulosis, or colitis as a cause. Pt was hemodynamically stable with HCT stable during hospitalization, which does not support rapid upper GI bleed. It is more likely that the drops of blood were due to internal hemorrhoids or menstrual flow. Of note, patient was told in the ED that she would need a colonoscopy and was very upset when told that hemorrhoids are a likely explanation for her blood in her stool at this time and that colonoscopy may not be a helpful diagnostic or therapeutic tool. As there was no evidence of diverticulosis or acute blood loss with hemodynamic instability, there was no need for tagged RBC scan or CTA abdomen to assess for sources of lower GI bleeding. . Patient was also found to have blood in vaginal vault, but was also upset by the idea that the BRBPR could have been from vaginal bleeding. Pt was discharged with outpatient follow-up. Pt may require outpatient colonoscopy to evaluate her ___ yr weight loss and poor appetite, but the inpatient team did not feel that urgent colonoscopy was required. Pt also remained hemodynamically stable during hospitalization. NSAIDs were stopped upon discharge in case she was consuming frequent NSAIDs, but she denied melanotic stools. Pt was advised to use hydrocortisone suppository and hydrocortisone cream for 7 days for symptomatic hemorrhoid relief. And to increase fiber and PO water intake for further treatment/prevention of hemorrhoids. Pt tolerated POs well in the hospital and was discharged. . # Vaginal bleeding: Bleeding is noted from overnight to be clearly only from the vagina, not from anal canal. However, patient insisted that it was not time for her menstruation but then says that her last was the end of ___. HCTs have been stable at 34-36. Urine HCG was negative so miscarriage seemed less likely. Pt refused afternoon repeat HCT prior to discharge, however, as pt was not symptomatic and hemodynamically stable, she was discharged with advise to follow-up with outpatient provider. . # Hematuria: Likely contaminant from vaginal bleeding. No symptoms of urinary infection or stone. Pelvic exam in the ED showed blood in the vaginal vault and the UA was contaminated with 4 epis so possible that the hematuria is actually from vaginal or hemorrhoidal source. repeat UA with Epi 1 with significant RBCs likely from menstrual flow. . # Anxiety: Pt was given 0.5 mg ativan for extreme anxiety while in the hospital with several tearful discussions about her concerns over her health. . # migraines: Pt is very anxious about developing HA from not having coffee. Pt was allowed to tolerate PO coffee and was also continued on home topiramate and sumatriptan prn . Pt was also given fioricet while in the hospital for headache. CHRONIC ISSUES: # asthma: ___ was continued on home advair, montelukast, cetirizine, and albuterol . # disc bulge L3-4: pain control with home misoprostol.
