Diclofenac was DC-ed as patient was concerned for rectal bleeding and it is an NSAID, though there was no concern for upper GI bleeding during hospitalization. . TRANSITION ISSUES # Patient is extremely anxious about weight loss and poor PO intake, and was told to get colonoscopy per outpatient PCP. Recommend follow-up on PCP recs for colonoscopy
summary: You were admitted to ___ for bleeding from your rectum. We performed extensive testing including a vaginal ultrasound, and CT abdomen, which were thankfully all within normal limits. We also monitored your blood levels closely, and they were stable, which also was resassuring that you did not have significant bleeding to cause acute drop in your blood levels. Thus, we think that your rectal bleeding was likely from a hemorrhoid. It will be important for you to follow-up with your outpatient physician to further evaluate your recent weight loss, and follow throughout with your outpatient's doctors ___ regarding ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old female with a hx of end-stage sarcoidosis, COPD/asthma, dCHF, and pulmonary HTN who was initially admitted to the MICU with hypercarbic and hypoxemic respiratory failure, likely ___ to viral etiology vs. presumed apical pneumonia and COPD exacerbation in setting of profound lung disease. . #. Respiratory: She has significant pulmonary fibrosis ___s severe COPD and asthma, with yearly admissions for respiratory difficulties. With new apical infiltrates on CXR + wheezing with improvement on nebulizers, COPD exacerbation on a background of profoundly diseased lungs was considered to be the most likely cause. She was initially admitted to the MICU due to hypoxic/hypercarbic respiratory failure (pH 7.23, pCO2 74) and started on continuous nebulizer treatments, IV steroids, and broad antibiotic coverage. Once her neb treatments were spaced to every 4 hours, she was transferred to the floor, where her antibiotics were eventually narrowed to Levaquin alone for coverage of community acquired pneumonia for a total 7-day course. Pulmonary was consulted and recommended a 12-day steroid taper upon discharge, as well as her current home regimen of nebulized and inhaled bronchodilators and sildenafil/verapimil treatments for pulmonary hypertension. She will follow-up with Dr. ___ Dr. ___ weeks after discharge and will be sent home with ___ for continued monitoring of her respiratory status. . #. Metabolic alkalosis: Likely compensation of CO2 retention/respiratory acidosis. Her alkalosis continued to worsen up until discharge, but her clinical condition improved, satting well on 1L O2. She is normally on 0.5L O2 at home. . # Chronic diastolic CHF: She remained euvolemic on exam, no pulmonary edema on CXR, no JVD, and no ___ edema. She did require a small dose of Lasix over one evening for worsening shortness of breath. Otherwise, she was stable on her every-3-days regimen on furosemide.
summary: You were admitted because of your shortness of breath, cough, and your history of lung disease. You were initially admitted the intensive care unit so we could closely monitor your breathing and oxygen levels in your blood. We gave you nebulizer treatments, extra oxygen, antibiotics, and steroid treatments to help you breathe better. When you no longer required very frequent nebulizer treatments and high levels of oxygen, you were transferred to the regular medicine floor and we monitored you. oxygen, we felt comfortable sending you home. Upon your discharge, you will continue on antibiotics, oral steroids, and your home nebulizer and inhaler treatments.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with a past medical history of seronegative RA, depression/anxiety, who presented with hemoptysis in the setting of several months of fevers, night sweats, and cough. ACUTE/ACTIVE PROBLEMS: # Hemoptysis, FUO: ongoing ID/rheum ___ thus far negative (see OMR notes). Low suspicion for active TB given nature of symptoms; however given these FUO's and episodes of minor hemoptysis, she was admitted to rule out for active TB. Sputum AFB x 3 negative on concentrated smear. CT chest with mild bronchial inflammation since ___, which may be due to smoking or from allergy. Quantiferon gold returned as INDETERMINATE after discharge. # Asthma exacerbation: continued on home albuterol inhaler, added mucolytics for cough. Prednisone taper completes ___ with 5 mg. # RA: does not appear active, no synovitis symptoms currently. ESR and CRP normal. Home hydroxychloroquine restarted on discharge as TB ___ negative. CHRONIC/STABLE PROBLEMS: # Depression/anxiety: she no longer takes prazosin, hydroxyzine, or clonazepam; continued home duloxetine 100mg daily TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - AFB CULTURES pending on sputum samples - ongoing FUO ___ Greater than 30 minutes spent on discharge related activties
summary: You were admitted to assess for TB given your symptoms of cough, fevers, and blood in the sputum. Your sputum samples were negative for TB x 3. The blood test for TB (quantiferon gold) is still PENDING. Your blood work was otherwise normal here. Your CT of the chest showed mild inflammation of the airways consistent with bronchitis, and have been there since ___ - I recommend considering seeing an allergist or pulmonologist for this to understand why this inflammation has persisted. No other abnormalities were noted on the CT scan. You have one more day of the prednisone for the asthma exacerbation (tomorrow, 5 mg tablet).

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT: ___ is a ___ RH M with h/o HTN (reportedly well controlled OFF meds) and asthma who p/w occipital HA 4 days ago which today became severe and explosive, a/w vomiting and transient blurred vision. BP 112/83 on arrival to OSH, tox screen negative. On exam, he was awake and alert, generally appears well. Mental status fully intact. No dysarthria. +slight overshoot on mirror test bilaterally. No truncal ataxia. No upper motor neuron signs, toes downgoing. NCHCT showed an elongated midline cerebellar hemorrhage which was close to the tentorium. There was surrounding edema but patent ___ ventricle without hydrocephalus. CTA showed no e/o aneurysm or AVM. Etiology is unclear. Initially, given +FHx (aunt died of brain hemorrhage at ___), concern was for aneurysm or AVM/DVM. CTA showed some small vessels tracking up toward the tentorium on CTA concerning for possible vascular malformation but conventional angiogram failed to show any vascular anomaly. He has a history HTN, but was normotensive on arrival off meds. No h/o substance abuse. No Venous sinus thrombosis on imaging. At this point suspect transient HTN with resultant hemorrhage versus occult cavernoma with rupture. # NEURO: - MRI brain without contrast (no aneurysm, possible acute and subacute components to bleed) + MRV brain negative (no venous ___ thrombosis) - angiogram by NSG looking for aneurysm, no AVM etc - Goal SBP <140 with IV labetalol PRN - Hold SC heparin, NSAIDs etc - percocet PRN pain, Zofran PRN nausea - Small right groin hematoma developed s/p angio and resolved in 1 day with stable H/H. Pressure dressing per neurosrugery. - Repeat MRI and follow up with Dr. ___ in Neurology ___ at end of ___ - Repeat angio in 2 months and follow up with Neurosurgery Dr. ___ in ___. # CARDIOVASCULAR: - EKG NSR - Telemetry no events - Labetalol IV PRN SBP>140 # RESP: h/o asthma, uses albuterol daily - Albuterol PRN, hold if markedly hypertensive # RENAL: Cr 1.4 at OSH, improved to 1.2 after IVF. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Advised Mr. ___ to refrain form work until cleared by either Dr. ___ PCP, or by Dr. ___ he sees him in clinic in ___. - Strongly discouraged any work that involves lifting weights greater than 10 lbs. - We advised that he should not perform ANY work involving climbing ladders or heights that would put him at risk of falls or traumatic injury
summary: You were admitted on ___ with symptoms of acute onset occipital headache. A CT and MRI was done which showed that you had a new cerebellar stroke. We admitted you to the ICU for close monitoring and repeat imaging showed improvement in your bleed. We did additional test to look for a cause of your bleed. We did an MRI of your veins which showed no genetic abnormalities in your vessels. Also we sent you for a vascular procedure called an angiogram which was done by neurosurgery. This test is sensitive for vessel problems and also showed no source of your stroke. You had improvement in your symptoms and were able to walk unassisted with minimal headache.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of known bronchiectasis and broncholith, APLS c/b ischemic stroke in setting of PFO on anticoagulation who presented with submassive hemoptysis and underwent ___ embolization with resolution of symptoms. She was treated for CAP with 5 days of antibiotics. ACUTE ISSUES ============ #Hemoptysis #Bronchiectasis: S/p successful ___ embolization of bronchial artery on ___. Source of bleeding from known bronchiectasis. CBC and respiratory status stable at discharge. #Acute hypoxemic respiratory failure (Resolved): Likely ___ bronchiectasis and hemoptysis as above, with possible component of CAP. Now s/p antibiotics for 5 days (ceftriaxone and levaquin) for CAP coverage. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== #Lung nodules: Followed by Dr. ___ as outpatient. Lung findings c/w prior h/o granulomatous exposure. Admission CTA chest with stable LUL perifissural spiculated mass; also demonstrated RML nodule. #APLS c/b ischemic infarct: H/o APLS and PFO, on warfarin and ASA. Last dose of both on ___. Followed by Dr. ___ as outpatient. Patient placed on heparin drip after ___ embolization. Resumed warfarin on ___, 5mg daily. INR on discharge on ___ was 2.2. Simvastatin 20mg qd continued. She will discuss restarting ASA 81 mg daily with her outpatient hematologist in ___. #HTN - held home lisinopril 10mg qd in setting of low BPs (systolics in the low 100s) - Continued home metoprolol #Asthma - continued home fluticasone and albuterol TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== #CODE STATUS: Full (confirmed) #CONTACT: ___ (daughter), ___ [ ] Recommend repeat CBC on PCP ___ to ensure stability, Hgb 11.8 on ___ [ ] Patient previously on aspirin 81mg daily given h/o CVA- discuss need for restarting aspirin as an outpatient if amenable with outpatient hematologist [ ] Lisinopril was held during the hospitalization in the setting of bleeding and systolic blood pressures in the low 100s. Restart as needed as an outpatient.
summary: You came to the hospital because you were coughing up large amounts of blood a few days after being admitted to ___ ___ for the same symptoms. We first gave you medications to slow down the bleeding in your lungs. Then our interventional pulmonology team used a scope to look in your lungs. Ultimately, the interventional radiologists used a technique called embolization to stop the bleeding. Your bronchiectasis was likely the cause of your bleeding. You may continue to cough up phlegm with some dried blood, but this is to be expected. You were also treated for a possible pneumonia with antibiotics for 5 days.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of asthma presenting with nausea, vomiting and diarrhea since yesterday afternoon and two traumatic falls. CT head w/o contrast ruled out bleed. ============== Active issues: ============== #Gastroenteritis: Patient presented with acute onset nausea and vomiting followed by diarrhea which resolved within 24 hours. Symptoms improved dramatically within a few hours of admission to the hospital without N/V and with only minimal diarrhea. Treated conservatively with IVFs and antiemetics until she tolerated a regular diet. Leukocytosis downtrending from 21-->12.8 and patient remained afebrile during admit. No abdominal pain or distention. Gastroenteritis resolved with conservative measures supporting food born illness or viral pathogen. #Hypotension: Resolved during admission. Most likely was ___ to hypovolemia. # Acute Renal Failure: Resolved. Patient's creatinine was 2.8 on admission but down to 1.1 by morning of discharge. Baseline Cr is 0.7. Likely due to acute kidney injury in the setting of hypovolemia ___ N/V/D and poor kidney perfusion. Resolved with fluids and normal PO intake. #Head trauma: Patient reported falling twice while trying to use the bathroom. No loss of consciousness. Patient is not in any significant pain from traumatic falls. CT head performed which showed occipital scalp swelling only. No bleed. ================ Chronic Issues: ================ #Hypothyroidism: Levothyroxine Sodium 50 mcg PO/NG DAILY #Asthma: Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (100/50) 1 INH IH BID Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN asthma exacerbation #Eczema:Triamcinolone Acetonide 0.1% Cream #Allergic rhinitis/sinusitis: Fluticasone Propionate NASAL SPRY #Ppx: Aspirin 81mg daily =================== Transitional Issues =================== [ ] F/U with PCP ___ ___ days for repeat lab check (Rx given to patient for blood work) to ensure resolution of leukocytosis and ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of vomiting and diarrhea and after falling twice at home. We think your symptoms were most likely caused by a viral stomach infection or from food poisoning. While in the hospital you received IV fluids because your blood pressure was low from being dehydrated. You responded well to fluids. You should have your blood work checked in 1 week to ensure that everything continues to improve. You also received a Chest X-ray to make sure you did not have any infection in your lungs and it confirmed that you did not. Because you fell twice at home, we took a CT scan of your head and it did not show any bleeding in the brain. You improved and were able to eat liquids, and on your discharge day you were able to eat a regular diet and your labs improved.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH of extensive ischemic CM and CAD and goiter presents with progressive shortness of breath over the last week. PMH suggests that this may be a CHF exacerbation or worsening CAD, but lab data and HPI/CXR/PE suggest that perhaps an element of upper airway obstruction may be contributing to his symptoms. # CORONARIES: S/P CABG in ___ and multiple MI, last cath in ___ showed 3VD. - Worsening shortness of breath with exertion and partial relief by nitro suggestive of angina - EKG with no ST segment changes - Less concern for active coronary disease given neg biomarkers and no EKG changes - Deferred coronary imaging or stress at this time - Continued home plavix and atorvastatin . # PUMP: Last ECHO showed EF 25% with global diastolic dysfxn. History of worsening shortness of breath concerning for CHF exacerbation. - Pt with stable ___ edema, lungs without rales and only mild edema on CXR - Cont home BB, ___, nitrate - Restarted torsemide 20mg QD . # ___: Cr 1.7 on admission, decreased to 1.4 on DOD - Likely due to renal hypoperfusion in setting of volume overload and HF - Improvement to 1.4 in setting of negative fluid balance argues for this hypothesis . # Goiter - Pt has large goiter on exam and evidence of tracheal shift to the R with possible early narrowing on CXR from prior admission - This was thought to be another possible reason for his shortness of breath - CT neck and chest performed, appears that pt has a large substernal goiter that is not causing significant compression of the trachea - ENT consulted and they determined that the patient did not have significant airway obstruction above the vocal cords with fiberoptic laryngoscopy - Last TSH 0.85 on prior admission, had negative FNA in ___ - His goiter might be be functionally significant at this point in time from an endocrinologic standpoint, but may cause obstructive or mass effect problems in the future - Further workup will involve his PCP ___ . # HTN - BP within low 100/70's during hospital stay - Continued home ___ and Imdur at their usual doses . # Hyperlipidemia - Continued home statin . # Epilepsy - Continued home keppra . # GERD/PUD - Continued home Ranitidine . # DM2 - ___ normal 70/30 dose with ISS while in hospital, restarted home doses as outpatient . # Asthma - Continued home advair with PRN albuterol . # AICD - interrogated by the EP staff, no evidence of any tachyarrhythmic activity leading up to his hospitalization . # Nasal congestive symptoms - Flonase and astelin given, seemed to alleviate nasal congestive symptoms
summary: D@ ___, You were admitted to ___ for an evaluation of your shortness of breath. Our labs studies and EKG showed that you are not likely to be suffering from any further blockage of your coronary arteries. You may be more short of breath because you have not been able to take your home torsemide, which we restarted while you were here in the hospital. Also, we gave you some nasal inhalers to help with your congestion symptoms. The EP doctors checked your ___ and determined that you did not have any abnormal heart rhythms. We performed a CT scan to evaluate your goiter. It showed that your goiter is quite large and extends into your neck. We had an Ear Nose and Throat team evaluate you, and they think that although your goiter is very large it doesn't require any intervention at this point in time.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ pmh recent STEMI ___ - DES to LAD), asthma, GERD, HTN called the heart line with ___ chest pain, pleutric in nature that came on suddenly around 1pm on ___. In the ED initial vitals were stable at temp of 98.3, heart rate of 56, blood pressure of 116/71 with resp 20 and SaO2 of 99% RA. Labs/studies notable for elevated D-Dimer 697 and CTA showed no evidence of pulmonary embolism. His Trop-T was elevated to 0.17, but significantly downtrending from 18 during his STEMI on ___. MB 1. #Chest Pain: In the emergency department, he was treated with Aspirin 243mg, SL NTG 0.3mg, and IV Morphine 5mg which did not relieve the chest pain. Upon transfer to the floor, he reported 'manageable' left sided chest discomfort, worse with movement or deep inspiration, at a ___. He found no improvement with nitro or morphine in the ED. He remained in sinus bradycardia, afebrile and normotensive. Due to concern from complications of his recent MI, he was elevated for ventricular anyereum by TTE that showed LEVF of 45-50% and left ventricular cavity that is moderately dilated, mild regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with distal septal/apical septal hypokinesis and a mildly elevated pulmonary artery pressure with no major changes seen from ECHO on ___. #GERD: Continue rantidine 150 mg BID #Asthma: No evidence of exacerbation. On symbicort and albuterol/ipratropium inhalers at home. Inpatient he was started on advair (symbicort non-formulary) and - albueterol and ipratropium nebs as needed. On discharge, patient was continued on his home dose ipratropium, albuterol and symbicort. #Hypertension: While inpatient, his home dose of 75 mg metoprolol XL was fractionated to 25 mg metoprolol q8hr and continued his home dose lisinopril. Inpatient, he remained normotensive, in sinus rhythm with a heart rate of 60. He was discharged on home dose of 75 mg metoprolol XL and 5 mg lisinopril. =========================== Transitional Issues: ========================== - Follow-up scheduled with cardiology - CODE: Full
summary: You were admitted to ___ with chest pain on ___. In the emergency department, you underwent an ECG (electrocardiogram) to exam the electrical functioning of your heart, it showed changes related to your heart attack (myocardial infraction) that occurred on ___. Additionally, we test your blood for a marker of cardiac functioning (Trop-T, CKMB) which were high, but improved from when you had your heart attack on ___. We also performed an ECHO (echocardiogram) to look at the functioning of your heart and ensure there were not complications from your previous heart attack, it showed no major changes and no new complications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with HTN, asthma, h/o spinal stenosis s/p surgery ___, and multiple PEs on Coumadin who presented after a fall at home with a left acetabular fracture. #. Left acetabular fracture: She was evaluated by the orthopedic surgery team who felt she should be managed non-operatively at this time. She was seen by physical therapy who felt she would benefit from rehab. She should be TDWB on her LLE with posterior dislocation precautions. She was started on calcium and vitamin D. Her pain was reasonably controlled on Oxycontin bid, with oxycodone for breakthrough pain, and standing tylenol. Given her fall prior to admission, she had B12, TSH, and RPR levels that were normal. She was continued on her Coumadin during this admission. She did have a small drop in hematocrit two days after admission and was given 2 units of blood. She underwent a CT abdomen/pelvis that showed no significant hematoma or retroperitoneal bleed. #. ECG changes: She had T waves inversions in her precordial leads on admission that normalized the day after admission. #. HTN: Continued on home regimen of lisinopril, triamterene and HCTZ. #. History of multiple PEs: She was continued on Coumadin for her history of PE. She did have a subtherapeutic INR on the day of discharge and was started on SC heparin until her INR>2.0. Her goal INR is 2.0-3.0. #. Acute renal failure: Her creatinine increased from 1.0 to 1.3 after admission felt to likely be volume depletion and prerenal azotemia in the setting of a fracture. She was given IV fluids and her creatinine remained stable. #. Asthma: No current symptoms although has had flare recently. She was continued on home prednisone, albuterol prn. #. Hyperlipidemia: Continued on home simvastatin. #. Hypothyroidism: Continued on home levothyroxine. TSH within normal limits. #. Code Status: She was full code during this hospitalization.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after a fall with a left acetabular fracture. You were seen by the orthopedic surgery team who felt that your fracture should not be operated on. You will need a repeat CT scan in ___ weeks to re-evaluate your fracture and you have an appointment scheduled with orthopedics. Your INR was slightly low on discharge and therefore you are receiving subcutaneous heparin shots until your INR increases. Your goal INR is 2.0-3.0. You should have your INR checked after discharge every other day. In addition, you should have your hematocrit checked 2 days after discharge to ensure that it is stable.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with a past medical history of abnormal uterine bleeding, complex partial seizures s/p left temporal lobectomy, asthma, hypertension, and obesity, who presented with abdominal pain and cramping shortly after uterine artery embolization for treatment of known fibroids. # Post-embolization syndrome. Transferred to the medicine floor after experiencing significant pain, cramping, and nausea after UAE. She was treated with PCA IV Dilaudid and IV Zofran overnight. Her pain improved post op day 1 and we transitioned her to PO Oxycodone and Acetaminophen. That night, she required one dose of IV Ketorolac for severe pain. We continued her PO meds on post op day 2 and added Ibuprofen to her regimen. Her pain was well controlled without additional IV meds. # Iron deficiency anemia: ___ Hb of 10 around her baseline for the last year. Patient w/ chronic iron deficiency anemia i/s/o chronic menstrual bleeding. Continued home iron. #Leukocytosis: Initially 13.2, down to 12.6 on discharge. Thought likely stress response after embolization. #AUB: Patient taking norethindrone acetate at home. Continued here. # Thrombocytosis: Chronic thrombocytosis, ___ Platelets was 542. Iron deficiency can account for 8% of reactive thrombocytosis in 1 series. Recent surgery may exacerbate further. Low concern for infection and no history of splenectomy. CHRONIC ISSUES: ========================== # history of complex partial seizures : s/p right temporal lobectomy in ___. No significant seizure episodes since then. Fatigue and stress can cause blurry vision, diplopia, and word finding difficulties. Continued home lamotrigine, levetiracetam, lorazepam PRN. # Hypertension: continued home amlodipine, lisinopril. # Asthma: continued home albuterol, fluticasone # GERD: continued home omeprazole, ranitidine, sucralfate # Metabolic syndrome: Continued home metformin. # Iron deficiency anemia: continued home iron. # Allergies/upper airway symptoms: continued home loratidine, diphenhydramine TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== - Address renal ultrasound. Will need follow up in ___ years for left lower pole renal angiolypoma - Will need follow-up MRI in 3 months, to be arranged by ___ and ___ follow-up in 3 months - Please reassess need for norethindrone now that fibroids have been managed - Please reassess safety for driving given recent seizure activity
summary: You were admitted to ___ because of your recent surgery, the uterine artery embolization. You were experiencing abdominal pain, cramping, and nausea, which are common after this surgery. We treated your pain with intravenous opioid medications (dilaudid) and switched you to oral opioid medications (oxycodone) as your pain levels improved. You were also given oral acetaminophen and Ibuprofen to help with your pain. For your nausea, we gave you intravenous Ondansentron (Zofran). We also gave you medications to help with your constipation. At discharge, your pain and nausea were significantly improved.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT & PLAN: ___ year old female coming in acute asthma exacerbation and sinusitis # Asthma exacerbation/Sinusitis- The patient presented with several days of cough and dyspnea on admission. Exam was notable for frontal and maxillary sinus tenderness, bronchospam and cough on pulmonary exam. She was never hypoxic at rest or with ambulation maintaining sats in the high ___, with peak flows of 250 down from her baseline of 350. Her symptoms were felt to be consistent with acute asthma exacerbation. She was initally started on 40 mg pred, q6H duonebs, q2H prn albuterol nebs, and guafensin-codeine for cough. Augmentin was started on ___ for sinuitis with the addition of flonase. CXR did not show focal infiltrates or pulmonary edema. She also complained of pleurtic chest pain, and was tachycardic ( see below) with ambulation. She had a CTA which was negative for PE without evidence of pulmonary infiltrate. She had minimal improvment in her symptoms after 48 hrs, thus pulmonary was consulted for consideration of alternative diagnosis. They recommended sending total IgE, galactomanan, and ANACA as part of the work for alternative causes which are pending at discharge , but agreed that this was most likely an acute asthma exacerbation likely trigerred by acute sinusitis. They recommend the addition of singulair, increasing pred to 60 mg until symptoms improved with a 2 week taper. Neilmed sinus rinuses ( which the patient already has at home) were also recommended. Peak flows improved to 400 at the time of discharge. She has follow up with her outpatient pulmonolgoist Dr. ___ ___ in early ___. # tachycardia- The patient was was tachycardic with ambulation only with heart rates in the 120s. Her EKG showed NSR with rate of 90 at rest. She has a CTA to evaluate for PE in the setting of the tachycardia and pleuritic chest pain. CTA did not show PE. She also had an echo to evaluate for cardiac disease or evidence of pulmonary hypertension, which was also normal. Her tachycardia ultimately improved and may have been related to underlying anxiety. Chronic stable issues #Back pain ___ degenerative disk disease- the patient is on percocet 10 mg TID prn and ibuprofen at home. She was given tylenol and oxycodone while in house since that dose of percocet is non-formulary. She was discharged on her home regimen of percocet and ibuprofen # depression anxiety- c/w home ativan qhs and zoloft # HTN- cont home HCTZ # hyperlipidemia- cont simvastatin TRANSITONAL ISSUES -prednisone taper 60 mg x 3 days, 50 mg x 3 days, 40 mg x 3 days, 30 mg x 3 days 20 mg x 3 days and 10 mg x 3 days then stop ( end date ___ -follow up with Dr. ___ ___ -Augmentin end date ___ -IgE, ANCA, and galactomanan pending at discharge
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath and cough which is likely from an asthma exacerbation. You were also found to have a sinus infection which was likely the trigger for your asthma attack. We did a CT of your chest which did not show any infiltrates or evidence of a blood clot in your lungs as the cause of your symptoms. Your were seen by our pulmonary doctors ___ lung doctors) who recommended that we treat you with steroids which should be tapered over the course of two weeks. We also started you on antibiotics for your sinus infection which you will take to complete a 10 day course. It is also possible that your reflux symptoms are worsening your cough so we added rantidine to your regimen. Please continue to monitor your peak flows at home. If it is less than 300 call your pulmonologist to discuss your steroid dosing. You should also use Neilmed sinus rinses ( which you have at home) to help with your sinus congestion.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Summary ___ female with history of NASH cirrrhosis (c/b ascites, hepatic encephalopathy, grade 1 varices), poorly-controlled IDDM, hyperlipidemia, hypothyroidism, and asthma who presents with abdominal distension, anasarca and acute kidney injury. Acute issues # ___ # Ascites Initially given albumin for poor PO intake with improvement of Creatinine back to baseline. Subsequently underwent a large volume paracentesis which did not show signs of SBP. Ultrasound did not show signs of PVT and remainder of infectious workup was normal. Started on diuresis and patient's renal function remained stable while her weight and anasarca symptoms improved. She was noted to have a large left sided pleural effusion (previously found to be a hepatic hydrothorax). This area was not tapped given her concurrent ascites, but was planned to be intervened upon as an outpatient. She remained stable on a regimen of 60mg po Lasix and 150mg spironolactone. She was discharged home in good condition with ___ services. CHRONIC ISSUES # Esophageal Varices/Anemia: EGD ___ showed 2 cords of non-bleeding grade I varices. No evidence of active bleeding. Not on bb for grade I varices. # Hepatic Encephalopathy: Not currently confused. No asterixis. Continued lactulose and rifaximin. # Chronic Left-Sided Pleural Effusion: Previously tapped and found to be transudative non-complicated, non-infected effusion. No malignancy cells seen on cytology. Most likely consistent with hepatic hydrothorax. Currently respiratory status stable. # Ascites previously complicated by SBP: Previous episode of SBP. SBP ruled out in the ED. Therapeutic paracentesis as above. Continued prophylactic ciprofloxacin. # Cirrhosis: Secondary to NASH. Complicated by ascites, hepatic encephalopathy, grade 1 varices, and SBP. Labs on admission show MELD of 19. Not on transplant list. # Coagulopathy: No evidence of active bleeding. INR 1.3, Plt 77 on admission. # DMII: Last A1C ___ 7.1%. Recheck A1C 7.6% this admission. Continued Humalog ISS and trialed patient on detemir 6u QPM which she tolerated well. # Normocytic anemia: At baseline. Iron panel and B12 wnl on admission. Likely ___ liver disease and splenomegaly. # Hypothyroidism: Continued home levothyroxine TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Pt with large hepatic hydrothorax. Plan for ongoing diuresis after discharge with IP follow-up for possible thoracentesis. - Please draw lab work on ___ with results faxed to Dr ___ ___. - Patient notably with poorly controlled diabetes and only on high dose sliding scale. She reports an allergy to glargine (local irritation). For this reason, she was trialed on detemir insulin, which she well tolerated. Would consider starting her on a long acting regimen with detemir as an outpatient. - She continued to have some left shoulder pain during admission which is chronic. Previous imaging without fracture. She was given a short script of tramadol and should be re-evaluated by her PCP. - Patient may benefit from echocardiogram as an outpatient to ensure cardiac function is not contributing to some of her volume overload. - Discharge weight: 76.2kg - Discharge diuretics: 60mg furosemide / 150mg spironolactone daily - Pt has F/U appointments with PCP, ___, and IP. - Code: Full (confirmed) - Contact: ___ (daughter/HCP) ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN shortness of breath 2. Brimonidine Tartrate 0.15% Ophth. 1 DROP BOTH EYES BID 3. Ciprofloxacin HCl 250 mg PO Q24H 4. Dorzolamide 2% Ophth. Soln. 1 DROP BOTH EYES BID 5. Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID 6. Furosemide 40 mg PO DAILY 7. Lactulose 30 mL PO TID 8. Levothyroxine Sodium 200 mcg PO DAILY 9. Omeprazole 20 mg PO BID 10. Rifaximin 550 mg PO BID 11. Spironolactone 100 mg PO DAILY 12. Vitamin D ___ UNIT PO DAILY 13. Vitamin E 400 UNIT PO BID 14. Zinc Sulfate 50 mg PO DAILY 15. Insulin SC Sliding Scale Insulin SC Sliding Scale using HUM Insulin
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because your abdomen was very large and ___ had a lot of swelling in your legs. This was due to your cirrhosis and because ___ were eating too much salt in your diet. We determined that ___ did not have an infection or a blood clot as a cause of your symptoms. ___ underwent a paracentesis to drain the fluid from your belly, and then you were started on higher doses of diuretics to help remove the rest of your body. With a low salt diet, the new medications, and time, your symptoms improved. You will need to be very careful with your salt intake as if ___ aren't, the fluid and your symptoms will come right back. ___ also have a lot of fluid build up around your left lung.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ was admitted and treated for likely COPD exacerbation with bronchodilators, steroids,and antibiotics; hospitalization was complicated by persistent tachycardia, DVT/PE was ruled-out with negative D-Dimer; her symptoms slowly improved and she was able to ambulate on room air at discharge. ACTIVE ISSUES # Dyspnea/Tachypnea/Hypoxia History and exam are most consistent with COPD exacerbation. Although she has no known diagnosis of COPD, she is at risk given her tobacco use. She was treated with levalbuterol and ipratropiumn nebulizers, prednisone, and azithromycin. Although CXR did not show clear signs of pneumonia, she remained symptomatic despite the aforementioned treatment, and antibiotics were changed to levofloxacin to cover for CAP. There was a low probability for DT/PE, and was ruled-out with a low D-Dimer. Her hypoxia and dyspnea improved minimally. She was given a low dose of IV furosemide and reported significant improvement in symptoms. She does not have a diagnosis of CAD or CHF, but given improvement with diuretics, a basic workup for CHF was initiated. BMP added to admission labs was normal. ECG on admission had not shown signs of ischemia. Echo showed only a hyperdynamic left ventricle. She was weaned off of oxygen and able to ambulate with minimal symptoms. She remained afebrile throughout. She was discharged with levofloxacin to complete a ___ short prednisone taper, and an albuterol inhaler to use as needed. The clinical picture for her symptoms was presumed to be either an exacerbation of undiagnosed COPD or a viral illness causing significant reactive airway disease. She should have follow up with her PCP for further testing to establish a definitive diagnosis of COPD. # Sinus tachycardia She was tachycardic on admission, initially thought to be secondary to infection in the setting of COPD exacerbation or PNA. Did not improve with a fluid bolus, and she did not appear hypovolemic. Changing albuterol to levalbuterol did not help either. She had a low pretest likelihood of DVT/PE, and low D-Dimer ruled this out. Was likely a result of the subjective feeling of dyspnea. Sinus tachycardia was noted on a discharge summary from a psychiatric admission in ___. She may have a baseline elevated heart rate. She was discharged with HR of 100-108. This should be followed as an outpatient. # Leukocytosis WBC elevated to 19.5 with neutrophilic predominance on admission suggesting infection. She was noted to have a baseline elevated WBC from prior records of around ___. WBC decreased to 15.9 on discharge. # Tobacco Abuse Was started on a nicotine patch. She declined a prescription for more patches at discharge, but both she and her husband described methods of helping her quit, including throwing out ashtrays in the house and buying nicotine gum prior to discharge. CHRONIC ISSUES # Depression Stable, continued home venlafaxine, risperdone, mirtazapine, and benztropine. # GERD Stable, continued home ranitidine. # HLD Stable, continued home simvastatin. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Recommend PFT testing as an outpatient - It was not clear if she has underlying COPD; further workup as outpatient is suggested - Tobacco cessation support is highly suggested as she was motivated to remain off tobacco at discharge, though declined a prescription for NRT - Follow HR as outpatient as she had sustained sinus tachycardia in the 100s - Full code
summary: You were admitted to ___ for progressively worsening shortness of breath. You were breathing very fast, had a cough with some increased sputum, and your oxygen level was low requiring supplemental oxygen. Given your smoking history, we treated you for an exacerbation of COPD. You did not have significant improvement initially. There was no clear evidence of pneumonia on your chest xray, but given your symptoms, we changed your antibiotics to cover for a pneumonia. You had significant improvement in your symptoms with some furosemide (a medication to help you urinate out extra fluid), which made us somewhat suspicious for some underlying heart disease. However, your bloodwork and echo did not show evidence of heart failure. You were able to walk around without requiring oxygen. You will continue antibiotics at home to complete a 5-day course and also a taper of steroids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo woman PMH mechanical aortic valve, on coumadin, ESRD on home HD 5 days per week, who presented with GIB (melena). During her hospital course she required 5 units of pRBC over her hospital course. Coumadin was stopped on admission. She underwent EGD,capsule endoscopy, and tagged red blood cell study which did not reveal definitive source of bleed. While no source identified, bleeding may have been related to supratherapeutic INR (4.3 on admission). Bleeding resolved and coumadin was restarted at admission dose of 7.5 mg. On day of discharge INR was 2.9 (from 1.6 day prior) so coumadin was changed to 3 mg after discussion with pharmacy. Of note, goal INR is 2.5 to 3.5 for AVR. Her calcium was noted to be persistently low which was attributed to ESRD. She was started on Tums and calcitriol during this admission per Renal recommendations. # GIB: Ms. ___ presented with melanic stools and a drop in H/H. She underwent EGD intially and capsule study which did not reveal a source of bleed. She then underwent tagged red blood cell study that was unrevealing. She required 5 units pRBC over hospital course. Blood counts remained stable > 72 hours prior to discharge. # Mechanical Aortic Valve and supertherapeutic INR: Although Ms. ___ presented with active GIB and supertherapeutic INR, mechanical aortic valves have high risk of clot, therefore did not reverse INR. Goal INR 2.5- 3.5. She was placed on heparin drip and eventually restarted on coumadin. Initially started on 7.5 mg the down titrated to ___hange in INR. # ESRD: S/p failed transplant x 2. Recevied dialysis 5 days/ week. Her calcium was noted to be persistently low which was atrtributed to ESRD. She was started on Tums and calcitriol during this admission. - ESRD team followed - calcium acetate, Nephrocaps, lorazepam for nausea - prednisone for transplant # Labile blood pressure: Per last discharge summary, her BPs are very labile and run high. BP's decrease with HD. - continued labetolol with strict holding parameter: SBP < 120 and held before dialysis # Troponemia: Troponin was 0.09 on admission and downtrended to 0.08. Likely elevated secondary to her ESRD. Peaked at 0.08. Resolved. Chronic Issues: =============== # Chronic diastolic heart failure with EF 60% in ___. Dialysis as above. # PVD: s/p Right BKA and L toe amuptations. Had fall during hospitalization. Vascular surgery saw the patient and performed bedside debridement. - continued aspirin - Continued statin, gabapentin, acetaminophen for pain # HLD: Continued gemfibrozil, statin. # Asthma: albuterol # PVD: s/p multiple amputations. - wound care followed - acetaminophen Transitional Issues: - needs INR check on ___ please fax results to Dr. ___ at ___ - should continue Tums and calcitriol as outpt
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for bloody stools. We did many tests, including looking at your stomach with a camera (EGD) and your small bowel with a pill (capsule endoscopy), which did not show any evidence of bleed. We believe this may have been spontaneous due to your elevated INR on arrival. We held your coumadin while you were bleeding and restarted it after you stopped bleeding. After restarting your coumadin, your blood counts were stable without any evidence of additional bleeding for several days. We have decreased your home coumadin amount. Please have your INR checked on ___, and call Dr. ___ help with management. Lastly, we started 2 new medications on this admission (calcitriol and calcium carbonate). Please ensure that you continue them at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PNEUMONIA, ASTHMA EXACERBATION: Ms. ___ was admitted with a left lower lobe infiltrate and was started on ceftriaxone IV and azithromycin PO for a community acquired pneumonia. The morning after admission, she was changed to cefpodoxime for an oral regimen. Given her penicillin allergy of a rash (and other antibiotic allergies of erythema nodosum), she was monitored closely for signs of adverse reaction. She had no signs of rash upon discharge after greater than 72 hours of observation. She quickly defervesced and had symptomatic improvement on those antibiotics. She completed a five day course of azithromycin, and she was given a prescription to complete a fourteen day course of cefpodoxime. On physical exam on admission, she was very wheezy. She was placed on prednisone 40 mg QDay (home dose was 10 mg) and standing albuterol nebulizers with relief of symptoms. She was easily weaned of oxygen by the morning after admission. She was evaluated by ___ who felt she was safe to return home with ___ and home ___. Her ambulatory O2 sats were in the upper 90's on room air at discharge, and she was not sent home with home oxygen. HYPERTENSION: Per Ms. ___, she was not supposed to be taking diltiazem for her blood pressure. The medication was verified on the PCP's medication list (from Dr. ___, and she was kept on diltiazem SR 180 mg QDay with well-controlled BP while in-house. ANTICOAGULATION: Ms. ___ INR became supratherapeutic after the antibiotics were started. It was initially held until it beca,e less than 3.0 (INR was 2.1 on discharge). She was told to restart coumadin at 1 mg QHS and to have her INR checked on ___. This was communicated to the pheresis unit and her PCP, ___. ___, by email.
summary: You were admitted with a pneumonia and have been put on antibiotics for it. You should continue taking the antibiotic cefpodoxime for another ten days. For the next day, you should take your albuterol every six hours to help your breathing. After that, you can take it as you regularly do, if you feel you need it. Your steroids were increased to prednisone 40 mg each day. Please take 40 mg for three days after you go home. Then take 30 mg for the three days after that, and 20 mg for the three days after that. At the end of the your taper, you should take prednisone 10 mg each day as you were doing before you came into the hospital. Your coumadin blood levels have been slightly high in the hospital because of the antibiotics. You should only take 1 mg of coumadin tonight. Please get your blood checked at the pheresis unit on ___. They will tell you whether you need to increase your dose. You have been sent home with oxygen which you may need temporarily while your pneumonia and asthma exacerbation resolve. You may find you need the oxygen more when you are moving around than when you are at rest.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with a history of idiopathic chronic pancreatitis, ___'s thyroiditis, diverticulitis s/p colonic resection, s/p cholecystectomy, and asthma presents with abdominal pain and fever. # Fevers # Klebsiella bacteremia: ___ had several days of fever prior to admission. She spiked a fever to 101 during her hospital stay. Blood cultures here notable for Klebsiella bacteremia. Source unclear but presumed secondary to ERCP procedure at OSH prior to her admission here. She underwent CT scan to evaluate for evidence of intra-abdominal collection/abscess. CT scan was without evidence of abscess. Klebsiella was pansensitive. Given sulfa allergy, bactrim was avoided and she is discharged to complete a 2 week course of cipro. Given concurrent zofran use, QTc was checked and was 393. She will need repeat EKG for QTc evaluation at her next scheduled PCP ___. # Abdominal pain # Chronic pancreatitis: Patient presented with acute on chronic pancreatitis. No further intervention was pursued in house. She met with her outpatient gastroenterologist this admission to discuss potential nutritional options moving forward. The patient elected to hold off on tube feeds or PPN and to await a clinical trial to be available next month. She will follow up with Dr. ___ month after discharge. Symptoms ultimately resolved and she tolerated a regular diet with minimal difficulty. She was continued on her home chronic pain regimen. # HTN - Continued home lisinopril # Asthma - Continued home meds (Advair, Spiriva, Singulair) # Hypothyroidism - Continued home levothyroxine # HLD - Continued home atorvastatin, fenofibrate # Anxiety - On clonazepam and lamotrigine as outpatient, continued in-house Transitional: - Patient to continue ciprofloxacin for two weeks through ___ - Patient has follow up with her PCP ___ ___ at which time she should have an EKG for QTc monitoring
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with fevers and abdominal pain. The abdominal pain is likely related to your chronic pancreatitis. You met with your outpatient gastroenterologist to discuss alternative options like TPN or tube feeding. You decided that you will await the camostat trial. You were able to resume a regular diet and tolerated it quite well. You had a fever while you were admitted. Your blood cultures are growing a gram negative bacteria called "Klebsiella." This is bacteria that often originates in the GI tract. You had a CT scan to determine if you have an abscess in your abdomen and it did NOT reveal the presence of an abscess. This is good news.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo male w/ a pmh of PMHx of HIV (CD4 716, HIV VL undetectable in ___, HTN, Diabetes Mellitus type 2, HLD, as well as a recent abnormal stress echo presenting after an episode of chest tightness. # Coronary artery disease - unstable angina: The patient p/w non exertional chest pain a/w diaphoresis and lightheadedness, relieved by SLNTG. His EKG was unchanged and CE were negative. Given his recent abnormal stress test (reversible inferolateral ST depressions with symptoms). He underwent a cardiac catheterization which revealed 70-80% mid vessel stenosis in the LAD, LCx 70-80% stenosis in the distal OM, and mild disease in the RCA with a long 40% stenosis. Two DES were placed in the LCx, but the LAD stenosis was unable to crossed with the wire and no stents were placed. The patient has a history of GI upset from aspirin, but reported that he would be willing to take this medication for the stent. He later felt that even with stomach protective medications, he was unable to tolerate this medication at any dose. After long discussions with the patient and the team, the decision to transition from aspirin/clopidogrel to prasugrel was made. The patient understood that this regimen was felt to be less efficacious, but did not feel he could continue the aspirin. Also, the evening following his cath, he developed ___ left sided chest pain a/w SOB. It was relieved with SLNTG, but returned prompting the initiation of a nitro gtt. He was transitioned to isosorbide mononitrate with good relief of his symptoms. He was not started on betablockers due to h/o RAD and wheezing due to metoprolol, statins due to h/o myalgias. He was already on ___ # COPD/asthma: Paitent remained stable and was provided an albuterol inhaler prn. He reported shortness of breath during an episode of chest pain, but it did not appear to be c/w reactive airway disease. # HTN: The patient was intially continued on his home regimen on amlodipine, valsartan and terazosin. With the addition of the Imdur, the patient reported concern that his BP would be too low with his full home regimen. He was continued on his home valsartan. He was instructed to speak to his cardiologist regarding his need for amlodpine and terazosin, which were not continued upon discharge. He will likely continue to need these medications. # HIV: Most recent VL undetectable, CD4 count > 500. He was continued on his home Truvada and Raltegravir. # Diabetes Mellitus type 2: He was continued his home insulin regimen and his oral medications were held while in house. His blood sugars remained well controlled. # GERD: He was transitioned omeprazole to pantoprazole as he was c/o significant stomach upset with aspirin and was originally to be treated with clopidogrel. ========================================= TRANSITIONS OF CARE: -Pt was counseled extensively regarding the benefits of aspirin therapy in the setting on stent placement. He understood the risks/benefits and decided not to continue this medication. -BP meds: Imdur was added to help control his anginal symptoms. Although this medication is not typically a/w a decrease in BP, the patient expressed significant concern and his full home regimen was not continued. He is to continue to take valsartan, but his amlodpine and terasozin were held.
summary: You were admitted after you developed chest pain. You did not have a heart attack, but there was concern that this was due to coronary artery disease. You underwent a cardiac catheterization which revealed significant narrowing of some of the main coronary arteries. Two stents were placed in one artery, but they team was unable to place in the other narrow artery. You had some chest pain after the procedure and were started on a new medication to help control this. We do recommend you take at least a low dose "baby" aspirin daily to help both your heart disease and the keep the stents as open as possible. We understand that this is causing you significant GI distress. You have been transitioned to a medication regimen without aspirin to help avoid this, but we do not feel it is as efficacious as if you were able to continue with the aspirin. We hope you continue to feel well.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo W with PMH of Type ___ DM, MDS, B-thalassemia trait who is called out of the MICU after resolution of septic shock ___ pyelonephritis from obstructive nephrolithiasis, now s/p stent and nephrostomy tube placement. 1. septic shock secondary to obstructive nephrolithiasis: Pt was admitted with septic shock from urosepsis from obstructive pyelonephritis. Lactate up to 5 range, fevers to 104, leukocytosis to 14 with 8 bands. Now s/p 4 liters NS in ED. Antibiotics given in ED with IV Ceftriaxone and gentamicin. Patient had associated N/V, flank pain. She is now status post ___ nephrostomy placement which was complicated by worse shortness of breath prior to procedure so patient was intubated in ___ suite. Sepsis is now complicated by emerging DIC picture. Urology & ___ following closely. Patient s/p temporary stent in ___, then L percutaneous nephrostomy tube placed. Admitted to MICU where received aggresive fluid resuscitation, maintained initially on gent/zosyn, then just zosyn for antibiotic coverage. Urine grew out pan sensitive Ecoli and Blood from A-line grew out GPCs. Pt had RIJ removed due to concern for line infection and LIJ was placed under sterile conditions. After replacement of the CVL, pt was able to be weaned from pressors and started having increased UOP from nephrostomy and foley. Pt was gently diuresed, she was successfully extubated on ___. Pt was transitioned from zosyn to ampicillin and than to oral amoxicillin with plan for 14 day course (___). Patient will follow up with urology as an outpatient for definitive treatment. 2. Afib with RVR: likely related to acute illness & volume overload and pt was loaded with Amiodarone over wkd and went back into sinus. During SBT on ___, pt developed AF and after discussion with primary cardiologist, started on oral load of Amiodarone with plan for titration in 2wks, overall goal for rhythm control and avoid risks of anti-coag. Rate control with goal HR in 60- 80s achieved with metoprolol 25mg TID. TTE on ___ showed preserved LVEF, LA normal. Through the remainder of hospital stay, continued gentle diuresis with lasix 40mg IV prn to meet net fluid balance of - 1L/day. Remained in NSR on discharge. 3. Respiratory distress: Resolving. Pt developped respiratory distress while in the ___ suite when she became tachypneic and had decreased oxygen saturations which were likely due to fluid overload as patient has known ___ and had received 4L IVFs, 6 Units FFP. Pt self extubated on ___ and was able to be weaned off oxygen quickly. Despite meeting diuresis goals of -1 to -1.5 L/day, the patient remained subjectively dyspneic with signs of volume overload on exam. CXR on ___ showed new interstitial edema and small pleural effusions, so she was diuresed further on morning of discharge with improvement in symptoms. 4. ARF: Presented with acute renal failure secondary to hydronephrosis and severe septic shock. With reversal of pathophysiology, creatinine improved form peak 2.5 to 1.1 on discharge. 5. Transaminitis: Likely ___ shock. LFTs continue trending down but she will need further monitoring as an outpt. 6. Anemia / MDS: Pt with longstanding history of transfusion requiring MDS with anemia. She was transfused for goal hct>23. Diff remains abnormal at baseline. 7. Asthma: Pt with history of asthma who was intubated for tachypnea and volume overload. There is still some evidence of cardiac vs asthmatic wheeze. Continued on Albuterol nebs while diuresing. 8. Type ___ DM: Outpatient oral medications held on admission and patient maintained on basal insulin with sliding scale through duration of hospital stay.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with left sided back pain and were found to have a kidney stone blocking the outflow of urine and causing a severe infection. You were monitored in the intensive care unit, initially requiring medications to help maintain your blood pressure and a breathing tube. The urologists put a stent in one of your ureters and placed a tube to help drain urine from the right kidney. While you were sick, you developed a heart arrhythmia called atrial fibrillation and you were started on amiodarone to help the rhythm return to normal. You were treated with antibiotics, which you will need to take for a total of two weeks. You will eventually need to have the kidney stone surgical removed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with asthma, improving but persistent dyspnea and sputum production and cough, presenting for further expedited eval. # Dyspnea: PFTs improved from prior. CT chest was concerning for resolving interstitial or eosinophilic pna, atypical ABPA, atypical infxn, or other inflammatory process that is resolving (steroids likely accidentally treated). ANCA was neg. TTE and speech/swallow eval negative. Pulmonary recs regarding pulmonary toilet, bronchodilators, implemented (see below). Prednisone decreased to 15 mg daily, with Bactrim and vitamin D, until outpt pulmonary f/u. Continued home GERD and sinusitis regimens. # DM: Held home glipizide, continued home glargine with homolog SS. Restarted glipizide at discharge. # Chronic sinusitis: Continued home regimen. Pt should f/u ENT as an outpt. # Hypothyroid: Continued home levothyroxine # HLD: Continued home statin # Abd pain: Epigastric, mild, present ___ yr. Longstanding, no alarm signs, deferred w/u to outpt. LFTs were neg. Transition issues: - Please check K at f/u appointment as Bactrim and lisonipril can raise K - Consider further evaluation regarding ongoing intermittent abd discomfort - Consider referring to speech/swallow for voice therapy as outpt Please see detailed pulmonary recs below: Recommendations: - prednisone 15 mg PO daily, continue until pulmonary follow-up - start bactrim SS daily for PJP prophylaxis - pulmonary follow-up as scheduled, repeat spirometry with DLCO, serial thoracic imaging - further serological work-up as outpatient as needed - patient should obtain OSH records and have sent to ___ for review - if flares as prednisone coming down, need repeat thoracic imaging +/- BAL if infiltrates reappear or worsen - would consider repeat RAST, IgE, ANCA when off steroids completely # Asthma - continue current inhaler regimen including symbicort, can continue added flovent as per initial consult note - continue spiriva (started in house in lieu of acnidinium bromide prescribed by OSH provider) - optimization of GERD/sinus therapy as below - bronchopulmonary hygiene measures (mobilization, incentive spirometry, guaifenesin prn, etc) - consider occupational assessment +/- home evaluation given recent changes in her workplace with remodeling - consider home ___ rehab for deconditioning # Specter of heart failure/fluid overload - will defer to PCP but would be reasonable to stop lasix if no apparent clinical benefit as heart failure not suggested although could have fluid retention from steroid therapy # Sinus issues: - follow-up with MEEI # Possible GERD: reflux precautions, PPI on empty stomach ___ minutes prior to eating, further evaluation/management of presumed reflux disease, consider laryngology consult and/or further behavioral cough management with SLP # Other considerations - Needs outpatient evaluation for OSA given nocturnal symptoms and habitus - optimization of bone/mineral health including assessment of vitamin D status and DEXA in setting of prolonged corticosteroid therapy
summary: You were evaluated on the medicine service for the cause of your shortness of breath. A comprehensive evaluation revealed that you have resolving inflammation in your lungs that the steroids are treating. It is not clear exactly what caused the inflammation, but the pulmonologists will continue to work with you to figure this out. Your breathing regimen was adjusted to help your symptoms. Please use the incentive spirometer we have provided for you at home. The pulmonologists ___ discuss with you doing a home and work evaluation for triggers of your breathing difficulty. This can be discussed at followup. We also have started you on an antibiotic to prevent infections while on the steroids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Assessment/Plan: ___ year old woman with h/o EtOH/hepC cirrhosis, recently diagnosed cholangiocarcinoma not a surgical candidate due to polysubstance abuse who presents with abdominal pain and alcohol intoxication. . . # seizure disorder: Pt reportedly with seizure d/o since childhood for which she has been taking carbamazapine, and has reportedly been missing doses recently likely explaining her seizure in the setting of ETOH binge. No other obvious metabolic disturbance based on labs. She was continued on home carbamazapine 200mg BID during admission without further seizure activity. She was kept on ativan CIWA scale for alcohol withdrawal which was discontinued 3 days prior to discharge. . # cholangiocarcinoma: not currently a candidate for surgical or other therapies given polysubstance abuse and poor follow up. Pt with diffuse abdominal pain from this. She was maintained on oxycdone, initially ___ q4h, but this was decreased to ___ q4h given worsening of her encephalopathy. With regards to further treatment it appears that she has potentially curable disase were she to have a surgical resection, but would need to be abstinant from alcohol for 6 months prior to such an operation. She could have palliative chemo until that time, but would again need to demonstrate abstinace from alcohol to tolerate therapy. Were the tumor not to be treated she would likely have ___ months. Patinet was made aware of this situation and a family meeting was held to address need for alcohol abstinance and rehab. patient declined a direct admission, but was discharged to her daughter's care with a plan for her to enter to rehab soon after. She has follow up with dr. ___ primary oncologist. . # polysubstance abuse: h/o withdrawal seizures. With did have some evidence of minor withdrawal during admission with coarse tremor, diaphoresis, and tounge fasciulations which responded well to PRN PO ativan. She was maintained on folate, thiamine, B12, and Multivitamin. She was seen by social work who offered her an option for admission to an ___ facility which the patient expressed interest in but preferred to be discharged home prior to this. Unfortunately, her substance abuse has been a barrier to her treatment as described above. Patinet was discharged to follow up with rehab in the coming days. . # cirrhosis: due to EtOH and hepC. Pt with increased signs of encephalopathy with confusion and asterixis over the course of admission, so her oxycodone dose was decreased to ___ q6h, and lactulose was uptitrated for ___ BMs/day. Platelets, INR, and LFTs were trended over admission and stable . # chest pain: likely musculoskeletal. Atypical in nature for ACS. Reproducible on exam. Trop negative, and EKG stable from ___ . # gastritis: cont PPI . # hypertension: cont amlodipine . # asthma: cont flovent, albuterol . # depression: cont citalopram. Burproprion was held given propensity to lower seizure threshold. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for a seizure. This was likely from not taking your seizure medicines and from you alcohol intake. You also had some evidence of alcohol withdrawal in the hospital which we treated with ativan. You were also noted to have evidence of encephalopathy so your lactulose was increased. It is very important that you stop drinking as this is inhibiting your cancer treatment. At this time your cancer is potentially curable with surgery, but your drinking prevents you from having surgery. If you do not have this cancer treated it will kill you. You were offerred the opportunity to transition to an inpatient detox program but you preferred to go home and follow up with a detox program with the help of your daughters. We treated your abdominal pain with oxycodone. However this caused increased confusion from your liver disease so we reduced the dose.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with the past medical history of tobacco use, THC vaping, exercise induced asthma and DVT/PE (one in ___ and another in ___ post-surgery immobilization) who presents with worsening cough and SOB x 1 week. #Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangitis #Pneumonia #?Asthma Exacerbation The patient presented with worsening cough and shortness of breath. CT scan on admission showed multifocal pneumonia. The differential for the patient's presentation included EGPA vs AEP vs Vaping related lung disease, with less likely APBA or Coccidioides. The patient was also noted to have a significant peripheral eosinophilia. She was initially started on Ceftraixone/azithromycin for treatment of CAP. Which was transitioned to Vancomycin when sputum from BAL was +for MRSA- she was ultimately transitioned to clindamycin to complete a 5 day course of antibiotics. Following bronchoscopy, the patient was started on Prednisone 60mg daily, She had a CT sinus which did not show evidence of EGPA. She was also evaluated by dermatology who found no skin lesions to biopsy. Ultimately, the patient's ANCA (PR3 antibodies) returned positive. In addition she was found to have a significantly elevated IgE. The combination of ANCA positivity, eosinophilia, lung findings are consistent with EGPA. The patient was discharged on Prednisone 40mg daily to continue until close pulmonary follow up. She was continued on a PPI and started on atovaquone for PJP ppx. She was also started on Advair. ECG was without significant abnormalities. The patient will need an echocardiogram as an outpatient to asses for cardiac involvement of EGPA. The patient had significant improvement in her symptoms prior to discharge. #Hypertension The patient was noted to have significantly elevated blood pressures. She was started on HCTZ which was uptitrated to 50mg Daily and then amiodarone was added. Blood pressures not optimally controlled on discharge. Will likely require additional titration #Vulvovaginal candidiasis: - Patient was given Fluconazole x2 CHRONIC/STABLE PROBLEMS: #DVT/PE: on xarelto at home, no PE seen on CTA -continued home Xarelto 20 QD #Tobacco use: The patient was counseled on smoking cessation. She expressed interest in Chantix and was provided a prescription on discharge. #Mood disorder: The patient was continued on her home medications while hospitalized. She has follow up scheduled with her psychiatrist the week after discharge. Transitional issues: - Please arrange outpatient echocardiogram - Continue to monitor blood pressure, may require additional medications - Discharged on Chantix for smoking cessation- Would continue to encourage smoking cessation - Patient has follow up scheduled with pulmonary, can consider referral to rheumatology as outpatient Code: Full Patient seen and examined on day of discharge. >30 minutes on discharge activities.
summary: You were evaluated for shortness of breath. You were seen by the pulmonary team and had a CAT scan and a bronchoscopy in addition to a number of lab tests. You were treated for pneumonia with antibiotics while you were hospitalized. The results of your studies indicate you likely have Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangitis (EGPA). You were started on steroids which will treat this condition. You have also been given new inhalers to help your breathing. It is important that you stop smoking and vaping. You were started on Chantix to help with this. While you take steroids, you should take a medication to protect your stomach and calcium with vitamin D to protect your bones. You have also been started on an antibiotic to prevent an infection while you are on steroids. You were found to have high blood pressure during your hospitalization. You have been started on new blood pressure medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with h/o genital herpes who is called out from MICU after course with severe legionella PNA, never requiring intubation. Pt is currently sating well on 4L NC with metabolic alkalosis thought due to N/V. 1)Pneumonia/ respiratory distress- On admission the patient had a clear CXR. Had an asthma attack in the ED, and then another asthma attack on ___. Both responded immediately to albuterol. Since the second attack she has had persistent coughing productive of clear sputum. Found on CXR on ___ to have a large pneumonia and she was started on azithromycin and ceftriaxone. She continued to require increasing O2 to maintain her saturation. On ___ she went for CTA which was negative for PE. It is unclear if this PNA is related to her underlying presenting symptoms or if this is a superimposed process. On ___ her antibiotics were broadened to vanc, increased dose of ceftriaxone, azithro, and doxycycline. She continued to receive albuterol and atrovent nebs as needed. Pulmonary was consulted and a sputum culture was sent. Due to increasing respiratory distress and increasing oxygen requirements, patient was transferred to MICU. Patient was given oxygen via high flow mask and transiently required non invasive positive pressure ventilation. Patient did not require intubation however. Infectious workup revealed legionella pneumonia. Patient was treated initially with double coverage using Levofloxacin and Azithromycin, with subsequent switch to monotherapy using IV Azithromycin. Patient improved significantly and was able to be transferred out of the MICU with oxygen supplementation via nasal cannula. On the medicine floor, the patient's respiratory status remained improved on oxygen. She was continued on Azithromycin. Her oxygen requirement decreased during the remainder of her course. She remained afebrile while on the medicine floor. On discharge, she was off oxygen. She was afebrile and her rash had resolved. 2)Nausea, vomiting, metabolic alkalosis: Per the patient's report, nausea and vomiting did not begin until transfer to the MICU. Her nausea and vomiting continued on and off during her MICU stay, but was adequately controlled on Zofran. Arterial blood gas prior to transfer to the medicine floor revealed a metabolic alkalosis thought secondary to vomiting. After transfer to the floor, her nausea and vomiting remained well controlled on Zofran. On discharge, she denied nausea or vomiting. 3)Anemia - Patient has new anemia with Hct in the low 30's. Hemolysis labs (except for an elevated LDH) are not elevated. Her anemia may be due to the cause of her underlying process. 4)Transaminitis - Patient with a new transaminitis. Likely secondary to legionella. On discharge, transaminitis had resolved. 5)Hypotension - Patient developed significant autodiuresis after MICU stay. She had episodic hypotension which was occasionally symptomatic in the setting of 2L daily diuresis. Patient was instructed to increase po intake and salt intake and was ambulatory without symptoms at the time of discharge.
summary: You have been admitted with fever, rash, shortness of breath, cough, and joint pain and were found to be positive for legionella in your urine. You had a prolonged course, with hypoxia leading to a transfer to the medical ICU, but never required intubation. Your respiratory status improved and you were transferred to the medicine floor where you progressively improved. You were started on azithromycin for legionella, and you will need to continue the antibiotic course until ___. You also had episodes of low blood pressure. Please continue to drink fluids and increase your salt intake over the next few days.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ was admitted to the medicine ward for work up of her chest pain. Hospital course by problem: # Chest pain: Patient's symptoms initially concerning for ACS given pain associated with exertion, shortness of breath, diaphoresis and radiation to arms. ECG was unremarkable, cardiac enzymes were cycled and found to be negative. ECHO showed no evidence of wall motion abnormality nor effusion and she had a normal ejection fraction. She was placed on telemtry to rule out arrhythmia given history of palpitations and no events were noted. Pulmonary embolism was considered however she was not tachycardic, not tachypnic, had no leg swelling/pain, and had no desaturation events. Also, a negative CTA just weeks prior made this very unlikely. She was noted to have a somewhat elevated ddimer but this is difficult to interpret in the setting of her rheumatologic disease/ chronic inflammation. Anxiety also very high on ddx given history of significant depression/ anxiety however patient felt these were unrelated. Musculoskeletal causes of chest pain were considered however the pain was not reproducible on exam. A cardiology consult was obtained given this unclear presentation. It was felt that there was a very low risk of cardiac pathology. It was not felt that further workup during this hospitalization was warranted however they did suggest an outpatient dobutamine echocardiogram (unable to use persantine ___ history of asthma and risk of bronchospasm, unable to do exercise stress test given joint pain) given her family history of heart disease. A follow up appointment with cardiology was made the day following discharge. # Recent serotonin syndrome/ depression: Patient recently hospitalized x 2 for serotonin syndrome. On this admit, she had a few of the associated signs including jitteriness and hyperreflexia of the lower extremities however she had no evidence of autonomic instability. She had been taken off the majority of her antidepressant medications given serotonergic activity. In the interim she has been managed with only seroquel and librium however her symptoms of anxiety and depression were very poorly controlled. Her outpatient psychiatrist had her try one dose of wellbutrin as an outpatient however she did not tolerate this ___ palpitations and there was concern that she may develop serotonin syndrome once again (very low probability of serotonin syndrome however not impossible). During her admission we started wellbutrin and monitored her exam and vital signs closely for 48 hours to ensure that she did not develop signs or symptoms of serotonin syndrome. She tolerated this well and was discharged on wellbutrin 75mg daily. We were in close contact with her outpatient psychiatrist frequently during the admission and she was discharged with close followup. # JRA: No changes to outpatient management while in house. Continued on diclofenac BID and oxycodone PRN pain. # Graves disease: Not an active issue during this admission. Patients most recentR TSH 2.4, T4 5.4,and T3 83. Not currently on medication
summary: You were admitted with chest pain. You had an ECHO which showed mild mitral valve prolapse and regurgitation but overall very normal heart function. You had an ECG which was normal. You had labs which did not have any evidence of heart ischemia. You were seen by a cardiologist while here and they felt reassured that this chest pain is unlikely cardiac in nature. It is possible that some of your symptoms are related to your improving serotonin syndrome and your pain improved during this admission. While you were here, we started you on wellbutrin and monitored closely for signs/symptoms of serotonin syndrome. You tolerated the wellbutrin well and I will give you a prescription for this. Your librium dose was changed to 10mg twice daily with an extra 10mg if needed during the day. No other changes to your medications were made.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old male with history of Parksinson's disease, severe MR ___ MVR, asthma, and atrial flutter, and ischemic ileitis admitted with aspiration pneumonitis. Patient was observed with no evidence of fever and improvement of leukocytosis prior to discharge. Patient was evaluated by speech and swallow with recommendation to continue regular and think liquid diet with plan for outpatient video swallow test. Patient was noted to have subtherapeutic INR level of 1.5 during hospitalization. Lovenox BID started to bridge to therapeutic INR. INR to be checked daily and warfarin dose adjusted for goal INR ___. Lovenox to continue until INR therapeutic in ___ range. #leukocytosis with neutrophilic predominance: patient afebrile and HDS. Only localizing symptom is cough, but lungs and CXR clear and pt not hypoxic. No diarrhea, no abdominal pain, concerning neuro symptoms, or rash. WBC decreased dramatically on admission (28 -> 16), without antibiotics. ___ summary states that he was felt to be aspiration risk with speech and swallow eval, and rapid resolution does suggest possible aspiration pneumonitis. Leukocytoisis improved to 8.9 AM of ___, 9 on ___, 8.9 on ___ without intervention. Patient was placed on mechanical soft diet and evaluated by speech and swallow with recommendation to continue regular and thin liquid diet with plan for outpatient video swallow test # aflutter: Started Coumadin in the setting of aflutter/mesenteric ischemia at recent hospitalization. Patient received 4 + 2 mg Warfarin on ___ as INR low (1.5). INR ___. On telemetry irregular rhythm. Planned to bridge with Lovenox. Continued diltiazem 30 mg TID, recommended f/u for this medication with outpatient cardiologist. #cough: low suspicion for pna. ___ be related to aspiration pneumonitis +/- asthma vs worsening of ___. continued albuterol, fluticasone, montelukast, and guaifenesin. #CHF: currently appears euvolemic. Continued home lasix. ___ disease: continued home pramipexole, carbidopa-levodopa. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== -outpatient video swallow testing is recommended -please check INR on ___ at PCP follow up appointment, INR on discharge 1.5 -daily warfarin dose to be adjusted pending daily INR -lovenox 60 mg BID to continue until INR therapeutic above 2 -INR testing to continue daily until INR > 2 for goal INR of ___ (starting on ___ -Please send INR result to patient's PCP: ___ Address: ___, ___ Phone: ___ Fax: ___
summary: You came into the hospital because your Primary Care Doctor was concerned that about your elevated white blood cell count. We did tests of your blood and an X-ray of your chest which did not show any signs of an infection. Your white blood cell count improved, which made us think that this was probably related to irritation of the lungs from aspiration. We had our speech and swallowing team evaluate you and they found that your swallowing was fairly normal. They recommend an outpatient video speech and swallow test. While you were in the hospital, your INR was low. We decided to start a medication called lovenox for you to take twice daily until your INR is within the normal range of ___. We also had you work with our physical therapist who recommended home physical therapy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ yo female with history significant for systemic sarcoidosis, pulmonary hypertension on 2L home O2, and ESRD on HD (___) who presented with BRBPR and LLQ pain. . Active diagnoses: # BRBPR: Most likely a result of angioectasias or AVM's as previously noted throughout the GI tract. Ischemic colitis was initially a concern given her ESRD and HTN, but a CT on ___ did not reveal any signs of ischemia or other intraperitoneal processes. The differential also included diverticular bleed although no diverticuli noted on last colonoscopy. There was no prior NSAID use to suspect ulcerations. Attending performed a bimanual exam which was unrevealing. Because there was no further bleeding after admission, even though pain continued, it was thought that the pain and bleeding were not associated. The patient had increased cough recently and it was determined that the pain was likely musculoskeletal. The patient was started on tessalon perles and guaifenasin/codeine which improved the cough and eventually improved the pain. A colonoscopy was not performed, which was decided with GI input. She will followup with GI as an outpatient. The patient was continued on pantoprazole. . #Cirrhosis: CT revealed a nodular live with hypertrophy of the caudate and left lobe suggesting cirrhosis. Given these new findings, the patient was started on Ceftriaxone for prophylaxis and will followup with hepatologoy to review CT findings. . # ESRD on HD: Patient receives HD on ___, which was continued during the hospitalization.
