Continued sevelamer (switched to sevelamer carbonate while in-house because it is on formulary), nephrocaps, hydroxyzine, lorazepam . # Sarcoidosis: Patient has pulmonary, pancreatic and liver involvement. Spirometry from ___ notes no significant change in FEV1 & FVC since ___. Her prednisone taper and ursodiol were continued. # Reactive airway disease: Continued home albuterol and ipratropium nebs as necessary. # Hypertension: On admission, was normotensive in the setting of recent BRBPR. Held nifedipine, losartan on admisison. # Anemia: Patient receives Epogen with dialysis ___. # Secondary hyperparathyroidism: Her cinacalcet was continued. # H/o epilepsy: Last seizure ___. Her lamotrigine was continued. TRANSITIONS OF CARE: -Patient will follow-up with hepatology regarding new finding of cirrhosis on CT. -Patient will have a follow-up appointment with GI to determine if colonoscopy is necessary. -Patient will follow-up with PCP upon discharge and will continue HD.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having bleeding which we think was from your colon. Your blood counts slowly trended downwards, so we gave you a transfusion with one unit of blood before you left. Your bleeding stopped after the first day in the hospital which was reassuring. We did not feel that another colonoscopy would help fix the problem, so we just observed you in the hospital for a few days to make sure your bleeding stopped. Your left sided abdominal pain was probably from a pulled muscle in your abodminal wall. We gave you some medications to prevent coughing, so that the abdominal pain could improve. You did have a CT scan in the Emergency Room which showed that you might have cirrhosis of your liver. It might be helpful to have another liver biopsy done to see what stage of cirrhosis it is.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old woman with a mitochondrial disorder as well as PMH significant for esophageal dysmotility of unclear etiology and impact, as well as celiac disease and depression, who presents with abdominal pain. . #. ABDOMINAL PAIN Ms. ___ pain was hard to pin down on exam and history. It seemed possible that the barium swallow, and the retained contrast, ___ be contributing to the pain. The migratory nature of the pain suggested the possibility of migrating discomfort correlating with movement of material or air through the GI tract. Bowel regimen was given and she had significant loose bowel movements, though she did not report much subjective improvement with this. Pain medicines including morphine and acetaminophen were given. Her white blood cell count was stable and normal, and she was afebrile. She had no other signs of infection. Her abdominal exam remained consistently both diffusely positive by subjective measures, and unconcerning for peritoneal process by objective exam; she was distractible; tap tenderness was affirmed but without any guarding or flinching. Her UA was negative. PPI was continued. An abdominal ultrasound was performed as we could not perform a CT with contrast because of the barium. This was benign. We opted not to do a CT because after several days of observation she remained stable and non-toxic with a similar abdominal exam. We recommended close outpatient follow-up with GI and with her primary care clinic at ___. . #. MITOCHONDRIAL DISEASE Continued ascorbic acid, vitamin d3, folic acid, riboflavin, thiamine, vitamin E, coenzyme q10, lipoic acid, levocarnitine. Continued Keppra for seizure tx. Provigil and melatonin for arousal/sleep regulation. . #. DEPRESSION/ANXIETY Continued buspirone. . #. ASTHMA Has severe allergic rhinitis and sneezing in the past. No asthma symptoms in this admission. We continued fluticasone nasal spray; and continued montelukast, advair . #. DIZZINESS Not complaining of this currently. Chronic. Continued meclizine. . FEN: replete lytes prn . PPX: Sc heparin, bowel regimen . ACCESS: PIV's . CODE STATUS: full . DISPOSITION: pending resolution of above
summary: You were admitted with abdominal pain. We could not take a CT scan of your abdomen because you had barium from a previous study. Lab tests did not suggest an acute infection and did not point to other problems of the pancreas or liver or kidneys that can sometimes cause this kind of pain. We treated your pain and observed you over time. We did an abdominal ultrasound to further search for a possible cause of the pain. We found no abnormalities on your ultrasound. Ultimately we decided not to do a CT scan because your abdominal pain remained similar without signs of infection or necessity for surgical intervention.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old male with past medical history of sleep apnea, psoriasis, depression/anxiety, recent admission ___ for leukocytosis and hypoxia found to have a pneumonia status post antibiotic course, admitted ___ with acute onset chest pain, shortness of breath, leukocytosis, of unclear etiology, quickly resolving, declining additional workup and requesting discharge with plan for outpatient workup, provided with increased PPI dosing for possible esophagitis and albuterol for possible asthma, but recommended for additional workup to better characterize this recurrent intermittent process. Active Issues # Dyspnea and chest pain - patient with recent admission for pneumonia treated with levofloxacin who represented with acute onset chest pain, described as burning, feeling like a "bubble" in his chest. CXR showed a new patchy ill-defined opacity in the left lower lobe. CTA was peformed to rule out PE--it showed no evidence of PE, but did show bronchial wall thickening in the bilateral lower lobes with a small amount of mucus in the airways that could be a small amount of aspiration or bronchitis. Given history of onset on wakening, worse with laying flat, there was concern for nocturnal reflux and/or aspiration. His PPI was increased to BID and he was recommended not to take his medications immediately before bedtime (consider taking them at least an hour before laying supine). Given bronchial thickening, there was also concern for bronchitis vs aspiration. He was given an albuterol inhaler trial for use at home. He was recommended for additional workup for aspiration, including video swallow and consideration of endoscopy, but hepreferred to do so in the outpatient setting. Diagnosis of exclusion was also anxiety, which he was recommended to further discuss with his PCP. Chronic issues ================================== # Depression: Continued Lithium Carbonate CR (Eskalith) 225 mg PO DAILY and Sertraline 200 mg PO BID #GERD: Uptitrated omeprazole in case some of his symptoms represented worsening heart burn. Transitional issues =========================== - As above, patient recommended for additional testing and management as inpatient, but patient preferred to seek as outpatient via PCP and consideration of referral to pulmonologist vs gastroenterologist
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for chest pain and shortness of breath. We were initially concerned about acute, life threatening cardiac and pulmonary issues however these were ruled out by a series of tests. Your symptoms resolved quickly as well. It will be important to follow up with your outpatient providers to continue workup for what might have caused your symptoms in the first place. We have increased your omeprazole for potential gastric reflux. We also recommended that you get further evaluation for your trouble swallowing, and you said you would prefer to do this as an outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with recent ICU admission for severe allergic reaction to IV contrast dye presents with few week history of non-productive pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea on exertion and one day history of left lower extremity edema. . # Left lower extremity edema, DOE/pleuritic chest pain: Patient was admitted with concern for pulmonary embolism following prolonged stasis in the ICU. Left lower extremity duplex was negative for a DVT. However, the patient's D-dimer was elevated. Patient underwent a V/Q scan due to her severe contrast allergy and it was negative for pulmonary embolism. Her left lower extremity edema resolved without intervention. . #Cough/SOB: The patient has had a persistent non-productive cough/SOB since discharge from the ICU. Patient has a known history of asthma that has been more active since recent hospital discharge. Her shortness of breath was first thought to be related to pulmonary embolism. Workup for PE negative. Cough was more likely a manifestation of asthma. Patient was given additional education about the importance of mainetenance medication to prevent asthma flares. She was discharged home with new refills for Albuterol MVI and fluticasone MVI. . # Iron deficiency anemia: The patient's hematocrit remained at baseline. Continue home ferrous sulfate. . # Elevated liver enzymes: Continue to trend down from peak in 500s; ALT 82, AST 70. Negative, HepC ab, ___ and ceruloplasmin levels. Patient will follow up with Dr. ___ on ___. . # Asthma: Most likely cause of her non-productive cough, as cough improved with nebulizer treatments. Pt was started on fluticasone MVI at discharge for further control of asthma. . # Anxiety: Patient continued on fluoxetine 20 mg po daily. . # Migraines headaches: Patient prescribed propranolol 60 mg po qdaily. However, it was held during admission to monitor for tachycardia. Propranolol was restarted on discharge. . # Hypothyroidism: Levothyroxine 50 mcg continued daily throughout admission. . # Gastritis: Pantoprazole 40 mg po daily was continued throughout admission.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with symptoms of non-productive cough, chest pain with deep breathing, shortness of breath, and a swollen left leg. We examined your legs with a doppler to make sure that you did not have any blood clots in them. We also did a V/Q scan, and found that you do not have any blood clots in your lungs. While we were waiting for the results of the V/Q scan, you received 1 dose of anticoagulation in case we needed to treat you for a blood clot. Your leg swelling, pain, and shortness of breath resolved. Your cough may be related to your asthma. You were started on a new inhaler, Fluticasone, to help with your asthma.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman with metastatic breast CA on carboplatin, gemcitabine, and trastuzumab and intrathecal therapy for leptomeningeal disease re-admitted one day after hospital discharge for continued ataxia and normal-pressure hydrocephalus. The preveious admission was for headache, diplopia, loss of hearing, ataxia, and syncope and she was diagnosed with normal-pressure hydrocephalus. Neurosurgery placed a VP shunt ___ and her symptoms have been slowly improving since then. . # Headache, hearing loss, ataxia, syncope: Repeat MRI showed continued improvement of CNS lesions. New multiple foci may correlate to liposomal chemo distribution and not likely relevant. LP ___, opening pressure 32mmCSF, cultures NGTD, cytology negative. Drained 29cc CSF for high-volume relief of normal-pressure hydrocephalus. Post-LP, she reported improvement in vision, but ataxia was still severe. Neuro-oncology consulted. TSH normal. AM cortisol low, but on steroids. Telemetry unremarkable. "Code blue" was called ___ due to a syncopal episode while Ms. ___ was being moved onto a stretcher after MRI of the spine. Vitals were normal. She recovered consciousness and mentating normally after several minutes. EEG negative for seizure, but empiric levetiracetam for question of seizure as a cause of recent syncope was continued. VP shunt placed ___. Since placement of the VP shunt, she has made steady progress with resolution of headache, N/V, diplopia, and major improvements with ataxia. - Remove sutures ___, POD #10 and head CT in 4 weeks. - Taper dexamethasone: 2mg daily x3d, then 1mg daily x3d, then stop. - Tapering MSContin, 15mg BID to qHS. Continued oxycodone PO PRN for headache. . # UTI: Urine cx grew Corynebacterium urealyticum. Syncope/urinary retention possibly related to UTI. Since immunocompromised, treated with 5d-course vancomycin finishing ___. Urine at readmission malodorous. Recurrent UTI, culture has now grown E. coli resistant to ampicillin/sulbactam, ceftriaxone, and ceftaz, but sensitive to ciprofloxacin and TMP-SMX. Started course of ciprofloxacin. Foley removed again ___ for voiding trial. . # Metastatic breast CA: Responding to chemo by imaging, but Ms. ___ has expressed a desire to stop intrathecal treatment. Trastuzumab given ___. Anti-emetics PRN. . # Pancytopenia: Likely chemo induced. Given PLTs prior to OR. Thrombocytopenia and anemia stable. . # Constipation: KUB showed copious stool and ileus. Manually disempacted ___. Resolved. Continued docusate and senna scheduled. Polyethylene glycol PRN, lactulose PRN, magnesium hydroxide PRN, bisacodyl PRN. Enemas if needed. . # Hypertension: Metoprolol recently discontinued. Normotensive. . # Asthma: Continued outpatient albuterol PRN. . # Crohns: No treatment at this time, but on steroids. . # FEN: Regular diet. Repleted hypomagnesemia. . # Urinary retention: Voiding trial ___ unsuccessful; Foley replaced. Recurrent UTI; started ciprofloxacin. Failed voiding trial ___ Foley replaced and removed again ___ for voiding trial. Bladder scan ___ at 3:00PM had 517cc. However, Ms. ___ refused Foley replacement opting for more time to self-urinate. She understands that the Foley will need to be replaced by the end of the day if she cannot void. . # Pain (headache): Continue tapering MScontin, 15 mg BID to qHS. Oxycodone PRN. . # GI PPx: PPI and bowel regimen. . # DVT PPx: Heparin SC. . # IV access: Peripheral. . # Code status: FULL (confirmed with patient and sister) - until family can say goodbyes, she should be intubated.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for headache, double vision (diplopia), hearing loss, difficulty walking (ataxia), and fainting (syncope). MRI of the brain and spine showed stable/improved disease. EEG did not show seizure activity. Lumbar puncture (spinal tap) was negative for cancer cells, but revealed a very high pressure, consistent with a condition called normal-pressure hydrocephalus. The double vision improved after the spinal tap removed a high volume of spinal fluid. Neurosurgery then placed a VP shunt to continuously drain cerebrospinal fluid to decrease the pressure in the brain. You did have a fainting episode immediately after MRI of the spine, but you returned to baseline within a few minutes. The spine MRI was unchanged. You also required laxatives, enemas, and manual disempaction for severe constipation, likely due to narcotic pain medicine. A urinary catheter was placed because of urinary retention, possibly due to both the normal-pressure hydrocephalus and a recurrent urinary tract infection (UTI). You will need to finish a course of antibiotics (ciprofloxacin) for the most recent UTI. The urinary catheter was last removed ___ and you must have your bladder scanned to ensure minimal urinary retention. If urine is retained, the urinary catheter will need to be replaced promptly.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY STATEMENT FOR ADMISSION ========================================= Ms. ___ is a ___ female with AML s/p alloSCT in ___ complicated by pulmonary GVHD, secondary JAK2+ myeloproliferative disease/essential thrombocytosis on hydrea, thoracic aneurysm s/p stent graft ___, asthma, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on Xarelto, MI s/p CABG, adrenal insufficiency on prednisone, DMII, HFrEF (LVEF 42% ___, and vertebral fractures s/p kyphoplasty. She presented with weakness, fever, and worsening shortness of breath . Admitted for likely PNA vs. worsening GVHD. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== Discharge weight: 57.52 kg - 126.8 lbs Discharge Cr: 1.0 Discharge Hgb: 9.2 Discharge ANC: 2.28 Discharge platelets: 81 [ ] Follow-up with Dr. ___ ___ - will arrange sooner follow-up with NP [ ] Follow-up cell counts to consider need for repeat bone marrow biopsy [ ] Consider restarting hydroxyurea, which was held while inpatient in the setting of pancytopenia [ ] Consider restarting lisinopril which was held while inpatient due to soft blood pressure [ ] Recheck thyroid function tests given finding of slightly high TSH with normal free T4 ACUTE MEDICAL ISSUES ADDRESSED ================================= # Fever / SOB # Pneumonia # ? Worsening Pulmonary GVHD Patient presented with fever, weakness, worsening shortness of breath and cough. Unclear etiology given CXR, UA, and flu swab negative. She had documented fever to 101.4 in the ED. Her RVP was negative but CT chest reported possible pneumonia. Additional concern for worsening GVHD in setting of recent decreased dose of prednisone. Patient received full course of antibiotic for pneumonia (piperacillin/tazobactam). Additionally steroid dosing was increased temporarily in the setting of worsening SOB, fevers and known secondary adrenal insufficiency. Throughot admission shortness of breath and cough improved significantly and therefore we restarted steroid taper. # Secondary adrenal insufficiency Given patient's history of secondary adrenal insufficiency and fever as above with need for 3L NS for normalization of lactate in setting of recent decreased prednisone dose, we tripled the patient's prednisone dose to 30 mg daily. Prior to discharge we restarted steroid taper which was tolerated with no new symptoms. #Pancytopenia Pt with worsening pancytopenia on presentation which was different from her baseline. Plan was for bone marrow biopsy on ___ but counts remained stable and uptrended slightly throughout admission. Bone marrow biopsy was deferred but as a transitional issue patient needs to follow blood cell count trends to determine the need for repeat bone marrow biopsy. # JAK2-Positive Myeloproliferative Disorder # AML s/p Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplant complicated by Pulmonary GVHD Patient admitted with pancytopenia as above and therefore hydroxyurea was held. Prednisone dose was also increased as detailed above. She was continue on her home acyclovir, Advair, Montelukast. CHRONIC ISSUES PERTINENT TO ADMISSION ======================================= # CAD s/p CABG # Chronic HFrEF (LVEF 42% ___ # Mild MR - Patient continued on home ASA, Lipitor, metoprolol, and lisinopril # DMII: In setting of chronic steroid use. - Held home metformin and managed with ISS while inpatient # Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation - Patient continued on home metoprolol and rivaroxaban - One dose of rivaroxaban was held in anticipation of bone marrow biopsy but was restarted soon after decision to defer. # Hypothyroidism ___ checked and found to be slightly high with normal free T4 - Patient continued on home levothyroxine # Anxiety - Patient continued on home Ativan PRN # GERD - Patient continued on home dexlansoprazole as non-formulary
summary: You were admitted because you were having fevers and increased shortness of breath and cough We investigated the different possible causes for your fever. We believe the fever and shortness of breath were most likely caused by a pneumonia that initially was a viral infection and could have been superinfected by a bacteria. Also as you had history of pulmonary graft versus host disease, we increased your prednisone temporarily to cover the possibility of GVHD flare. Initially your blood counts were dropping and this initially made us consider that you could need a bone marrow biopsy. However, your blood counts recovered while getting the treatment for pneumonia and biopsy was not necessary.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yoM w/ HIV, hep C, ESRD, hx of afib, infected AV fistula graft x 2 episodes who now presents w/ erythema and tenderness at AV fistula site along w/ fever and chills x one day. . # Fever: Concern for infection at inactive left arm AV graft site given pain and expressible pus (reported per patient but not present on physical exam). Hx of infection of site x 2 over the past year, treated w/ only antibiotics per patient. Now undergoes dialysis from R tunneled IJ catheter. Small tender macular lesions on fingers and palm (single lesion) concerning for septic emboli. Differential diagnosis includes infected L AV fistula graft and ?endocarditis. Other etiolgies could also include pulmonary and urologic sources of infection. Blood cx were sent which were neg to date. Pt was started on Vancomycin, dose per HD empirically then discontinued since pt was afebrile and cultures were neg. Pt was taken to OR for exploration which did not show any evidence of infection. UA and urine cx unconcerning for infection. Pt has a surface ECHO that showed no concern for endocarditis. Pt remained afebrile and hemodynamically stable off abx and there was no leukocytosis as well, so pt was discharged with a neg infectious w/u. . # Chest pain. Atypical. No current symptoms. Negative troponin x 2, no new ECG changes. Denies past cardiac history. pt was monitored on tele. . # ESRD. On ___ dialysis. Last dialyzed on ___. Current access via R IJ tunneled HD catheter. Placed 3 months ago w/ no complications since that time. Pt underwent dialysis per schedule and continued on home renal meds. . # Dysuria. Only makes urine once per day. UA and urine cx were neg thus no tx was required. . # Penile lesion: r/o STD etiology. Denies any recent sexual contacts, unclear regarding distant sexual history. RPR was neg. G/C, HSV culture ordered as well, which were pending at time of discharge. . # Atrial fibrillation: In sinus rhythm currently. Pt is on Coumadin at home. INR became subtherapeutic to 1.8 so pt was started on a Heparin gtt. On day of discharge, INR was 3.2 so Heparin gtt was discontinued. Pt will need close INR monitoring after discharge. . # s/p MVR/TVR: On coumadin anticoagulation chronically (goal INR 2.5 to 3.5). was bridged with Heparin gtt as above. . # Prolonged QTc: Noted to have QTc 512 in ED. No torsades on tele. Electrolytes monitored and repleted as needed. Meds that can prolong QTc further were avoided. Home Prozac was discontinued for this reason. . # HIV/Hep C: Unclear current viral load, CD4 253 in ___. Pt was continued on home meds. . # Asthma: Pt was continued on home Albuterol. . # Hypertension: Pt was continued on home meds. BP were in good ranges. . # Depression: Home Prozac was discontinued as above. Dose was low so no need for taper. . # Pt was on renal diet. Pt on bowel regimen and home PPI. No need for DVT ppx as pt therapeutic on home Coumadin. Pt has PIV and HD catheter for access. Pt is full code. Pt is an inmate at ___. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - check INR and titrate Coumadin accordingly - penile lesion swab culture and blood cx are still pending - home Prozac was discontinued for prolonged QTc, thus monitor depression and consider starting another antidepressant if needed - continue HD per usual schedule
summary: We appreciated the opportunity to participate in your care at ___. You were admitted to our hospital due to a concern that your AV fistula graft was infected. Our transplant surgery team, who places these types of grafts, evaluated the possible infected area of your graft, but did not think this was the cause to your symptoms. They did not remove the graft from your arm as initially planned. We completed an evaluation for other possible sources of infection that could be causing your symptoms. This has included imaging of your chest, blood and urine cultures, and an echocardiogram of your heart which shows no signs of infection. At this point, it is unclear what the cause of your infection was, but regardless it has resolved. An EKG showed that your heart experiences an abnormality called long QT which may be caused by certain medications. Prozac is one of these medications, so it is advised that you stop taking it.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman with type 1 diabetes, asthma, and several psychiatric comorbidities who presents with nausea, vomiting and hyperglycemia found to be in mild DKA. Acute Issues 1) DKA: the patient presented to the ED with elevated glucose (307) and anion gap (27) which improved with administration of 3L IVF and 8u SQ insulin in the ED. The most likely cause of her DKA was medication non-compliance. On arrival to the floor, we started the patient on continuous IVF @100cc/hr, continued her home Lantus and gave her sliding scale insulin tailored to her glucose levels. We checked FSBG every 2hrs initially before spacing out to FSBG at meals and at night. We repleted her K 1x with 40 mEq PO. She was asymptomatic throughout her admission, with the exception of abdominal cramps which she attributes to menstrual cramps. Upon discharge, her glucose had decreased (161) and her anion gap was within normal range (9). We discussed with the patient the importance of continuing to take her diabetes medications. . 2) Psych (depression with passive SI, anxiety, bipolar disorder); the patient was admitted on ___ for "substantial risk of physical harm to the person himself/herself as manifested by evidence of threats of, or attempts at suicide or serious bodily harm." She had a 1:1 sitter at all hours. She has had several recent psychological stressors including recent diagnosis of bipolar disorder, recent abortion, and ending relationship without boyfriend of 18 months. Psychiatry came to see her on the last day of her admission and recommended a short stay in an inpatient psychiatric unit following discharge. She was ___ transferred to the psychiatry unit at ___. . Chronic Issues: 1) Asthma: stable. Patient was continued on albuterol and advair. . Transitional Issues: None
summary: You came into the hospital from your partial program at ___ because your mother was concerned that you were not taking your insulin. Your blood sugars were very high the evening prior to your admission and on the morning of admission, you had nausea and vomited once. You came here on a ___. In the emergency room, you were found to have glucose of 306, elevated anion gap (27) and ketones in your urine, suggesting that you were in diabetic ketoacidosis. You were given 3L of fluids and 8 units of insulin after which your glucose decreased to 122 and anion gap decreased to 15. When you came up to the floor, we started you on intravenous fluids, continued your home lantus, and gave you a sliding scale insulin tailored to your glucose levels. We also started you on a diabetc diet and repleted your potassium.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year-old right-handed man/woman with a past medical history including depression and ETOHism who presented to the ___ with complaint of nausea, emesis, L sided parasthesias and weakness. He was admitted to the stroke service from ___. . A CT of the head showed no evidence of hemorrhage, masses, or obvious signs of ischemia. There did not appear to be evidence of stenosis, dissection, or aneurysm on angiography. As the patient's symptoms were considered concerning for stroke, an MRI of the brain was performed. The MRI results did not reveal any infarcts (see full report above). In addition, patient had an MRA and MRV which were also essentially normal (see full reports above). Patient on repeat neuroexam had ___ strength, symmetric reflexes, CN2-12 intact (although he did report monocular blurry vision at times on the L), and no other focal neuro deficits. He had a normal gait. Of note on admission the patient had complained of SOB during the race. His EKG was reviewed and did not demonstrate any concerning changes. His Trop peaked at 0.07 on admission, but patient remanied asymptomatic and was monitored on tele throughout his stay. He had some associated renal insufficiency at that time with Cr up to 2.2, and also notable hypercalcemia up to 11.5 (asymptomatic). The patient received IVF hydration, after which his cardiac enzymes trended downwards, along with his Cr and Ca returning to normal. This likely represented a demand ischemia. . Patient was allowed to let his blood pressure autoregulate, although he did not have a history of HTN and did not require any supplemantary medications to control his BP during his admsision. . To evaluate modifiable risk factors for stroke, lipids and glycosylated hemoglobin were measured. Patient's LDL was at As the LDL was found to be 97. In the abscence of previous CV risk factors and CAD equivalents, we elected not to start any lipid therapy. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: 1) Follow-up with PCP ___ on ___: - Trazodone, 12.5 mg QHS - Albuterol inhaler, asthma - Lexapro, "lowest dose" QAM, 10 mg ?
summary: You were admitted due to some concerning signs and symptoms of having a stroke. We perforemd several imaging studies of your head, neck, and arteries and the results were reassuring. In addition we performed several studies on your blood, and initally we were concerned about your kidney function but this returned to normal after you received hydration through your IV. Also, your neurological exam was also reassuring. For this reason we think it is safe for you to continue your recovery at home. We recommend that you continue to keep up adequate hydration with several glasses of water a day. Also, please refrain from strenous physical activity for the next few days. We did not make any changes to your medciations.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with multiple medical issues most notable HTN, DM, COPD, ___ disease s/p colectomy complicated by intermittent SBO s/p G-tube, CAD s/p PCIs, anxiety, depression, and chronic pain on narcotics presents after accidentally cutting his G tube at home. #G-J tube replacement: Pt's tube was successfully replaced by ___ in the ED. Of note, pt did not know what his tube feeding regimen was supposed to be and had been ingesting Ensure through his feeding tube PRN. Pt was seen by nutrition during his hospitalization, and started on Nepro with a goal tube feed rate of 45cc/hr. #Chest tightness: Pt also complained of chest tightness and mild SOB in the ED. EKG/cardiac enzymes were not suggestive of cardiac etiology. Pt was noted to be wheezing, and he was started on nebulizers, with minimal benefit. Pt was felt to be having an asthma/reactive airway disease exacerbation, and he was started on a 5-day course of prednisone 40mg/Qday (d1 = ___. #Mixed metabolic/respiratory acidosis: Pt notably had a bicarbonate of 16 on admission, with a nadir of 12. ABG consistent with a mixed metabolic and respiratory acidosis, felt to be from his asthma exacerbation (see above) as well as diarrhea. Acidosis improved with several boluses of LR. Pt was also started on calcium carbonate for low calcium. Bicarb on discharge was 16. Pt was discharged with a plan to get a repeat VBG and Chem-7 on ___. ___: Creatinine on admission was 1.9, felt to be prerenal in the setting of diarrhea and inadequate tube feed intake. Pt's creatinine improved in the setting of IVF and initiation of tube feeds. Discharge Cr was 1.0. # ___ disease: Pt was not felt to be having a ___ flare during this hospitalization and he was notably not on any home medications for ___ disease. # Hypertension: Pt was continued on home labetalol. His lisinopril was held on admission because of ___, but it was not restarted at discharge because of borderline hyperkalemia. # Hypocalcemia: Pt notably with calcium of 6.4 on admission, and he was started on calcium carbonate this admission. Calcium at discharge was 7.8. # Diabetes w/ Neuropathy: Pt's home gabapentin was initially decreased in the setting of ___, but increased back to home dose with improvement in Cr. # Coronary Artery Disease: Pt was continued on home ASA 81 mg PO QDaily # Anxiety/Depression:Pt was continued on home Lorazepam and buproprion Transitional Issues: -Continue prednisone 40mg PO QDay for 3 additional days (d3 = ___ -Follow up with PCP. Will need to follow up fingerstick glucose after prednisone burst. Not on home insulin or hyperglycemic medications. -Continue tube feeds -Obtain repeat VBG and Chem-7 on ___ to ensure resolution of acidosis and stability of potassium -Consider restarting lisinopril when potassium is stable -Consider uptitrating gabapentin, patient complaining of persistent neuropathic pain
summary: You were admitted to ___ after you accidentally cut your feeding tube. Your tube was replaced by interventional radiology. You were also found to have some electrolyte abnormalities which was probably because you weren't getting enough nutrition and because of your diarrhea. We started you on tube feeds, and your electrolyte balance improved. We also found that you were wheezing, which was probably because of your asthma. You were started on a new medication called prednisone to help control your symptoms. After you leave, you should continue taking prednisone for the next 3 days to help your asthma. You should also continue your tube feeds to help with your nutrition, and we have set you up with services to help you manage these feeds.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Pan-colitis: Differential included cryptosporidium, c.diff, multiple bacterial/viral etiologies given CVID, and IBD. Broad spectrum antibiotics were intiated on admission including PO/IV vancomycin, cefepime and flagyl. The patient was started on IV fluids; leukocytosis and lactate were trended in the ICU. CT abdomen/pelvis showed diffuse severe pancolitis, small ileocolic intussiception wihtout evidence of obstruction. The surgical service was consulted but saw no acute indication for surgery and followed the patient with serial abdominal exams. The patient remained afebrile and hemodynamically stable in the ICU and was subsequently tranferred to the regular medical floor. The GI service was consulted and recommended a flexible sigmoidoscopy, stool cultures and labs to evaluate the etiology of her diarrhea. Thus far all stool labs for infectious causes are negative. The biopsy upon flexible sigmoidoscopy showed mild dysplasia and inflammation. It was recommended the patient continue her PO flagyl for a ___nd follow up with GI for a colonoscopy after discharge. . # Bacteremia: the patient was found to have S.pneumoniae on blood culture while on vancomycin. The Infectious Disease service was consulted. TTE and TEE were negative for endocarditis. Ceftriaxone was initiated and ___ placed for IV treatment for a 2 week course. The remainder of the blood cultures are negative to date. The patient remained afebrile during her admission. She has ID follow-up with ___ in several weeks. . # Chronic Hepatitis C: LFTs were elevated above baseline on admission. Initially cholestyramine, ursodiol and spironolactone were held. Her LFTs were trended and slowly returned back to baseline. After transfer to the medical service, given agressive fluid resuscitation in the ICU, the patient was fluid overloaded and required diuresis. Spironolactone was restarted and lasix 20mg po daily was added. An abdominal US showed a moderate amount of ascites which was tapped via ultrasound guidance. Approximately 500cc of fluid was removed, and labs were consistant with SBP, althought the patient was asymptomatic and already on ceftriaxone at that time. She will need follow-up for her ascites as an outpatient to ensure it does not reaccumulate. Her cholestyramine, ursodiol were restarted prior to discharge. A follow-up appointment was scheduled with ___ (hepatology). . # DM Type I: Patient reportedly hypoglycemic was hypoglycemic in the ICU, glargine was discontinued while the patient was NPO. Once her diet was advanced her home DM was restarted and fingersticks monitored. No changes were made to her regimen prior to discharge. . # Asthma: Home regimen of albuterol, pulmicort and tiotropium were continued. . # Coagulopathy: at baseline probably due to underlying liver disease. . # Follow-up: the patient has follow-up with the GI service, Infectious Disease, Hepatology and her PCP (which she will make on her own).
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for abdominal pain, diarrhea and fever. You were initially treated in the ICU for low blood pressure and infection with IV fluids and antibiotics. Your stool studies are negative for an infectious process. On flexible sigmoidoscopy you had a biopsy of the colon shows inflammation and mild dysplasia, which needs to be further evaluated by the ___ physicians. You were also found to have bacterial infection in your blood for which you need to be treated with IV antibiotics. A PICC line was placed to allow for a full 2 weeks of antibiotics (ceftriaxone). You will also need to complete the course of flagyl (antibiotic) for which you have a prescription. Your Alinia has been discontinued.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ year old female with a PMH significant for CAD s/p multiple stents, severe pulmonary disease who presented with pleuritic chest tightness that has since resolved and now with acute on chronic renal failure. . #. Acute on chronic renal failure: Patient with CKD stage III as baseline, initially presented with ___ with a creatinine over 3, her baseline renal disease attributed to her diabetes and hypertension. Initially appeared pre-renal, with improvement in her creatinine over the first night but then her renal function continued to worsen. She developed worsening hyponatremia as well due the inability of her kidneys to concentrate her urine, this improved with aggressive fluid restriction. However, as her renal function worsened she developed uremic symptoms, and a temporary HD line was placed on ___ for the initiation of HD. Over the next week she was dialyzed as needed and monitored for improvement in her renal function, but her creatinine continued to climb without HD and on ___ a tunneled HD line was placed. Hepatitis serologies were negative, a PPD was negative and she was discharged with outpatient HD set up at ___ Davtia Dialysis. . #. Chest pain/CAD: she initially presented with severe epigastric and low back pain, she ruled out for an MI, and the pain did not respond to nitro or morphine. A CT scan was done of her chest and abdomen, which showed fecaliztion of her small bowel loops, but no other significant findings. Her pain improved over the first few days in the hospital. She was continued on her home metoprolol, simvastatin, and aspirin 81mg. Also started on standing tylenol with lactulose and mirlax for regular bowel movements. . #. Chronic Systolic Heart Failure: EF 50-55%, repeat echo shows regional mild systolic dysfunction with moderate pulmonary hypertension, she did not appear volume overloaded on admission, and her outpatient torsemide was held. She was continued on her metoprolol, and discharged on 80mg of po lasix. Her lisinopril was held in the setting of her new renal failure. . #. Back pain/leg pain: Has degenerative disk disease of lower L spine, trochanteric bursitis, sciatica, recent x-rays show similar degnerative changes that were seen on prior MRI's. Her pain was controlled with standing tylenol. . #. A fib: patient remained in NSR during her stay, she was continued on her diltiazem, coumadin was held for her procedures, but restarted prior to discharge. . #. HTN: blood pressure remained well controlled on her metoprolol at 37.5mg BID . #. HL: continued home simvastatin . #. Poor pulm fx: Included moderate pulm HTN (as per recent echo), Asthma, COPD, OSA, and restrictive lung disease, continued home flovent and standing albuterol/atrovent nebs. Also used home CPAP in house. . #. DM: continued home glargine and humalog sliding scale . #. Crohn's disease: currently inactive . #. Depression: continued home paxil . #. Gout: patient with gout flare in her left wrist, responded well to prednisone, has not been on febuxostat due to prior authorization problems. . #. Hypothyroidism: continued home dose synthroid . #. GERD: continued home omeprazole . #. Restless Leg Syndrome: continued home neurontin
summary: You were initially admitted to ___ with chest and low back pain. At first we were concerned about your heart, but testing showed that you DID NOT have a heart attack. We did a CT scan of your chest and abdomen that showed you had some stool in your intestines, your pain improved over the next few days with an aggressive bowel regimen and pain control. However, your kidney function was above your baseline when you came in and continued to worsen during your stay. You started to become somewhat confused and more fatigued, so the kidney doctors decided to ___ dialysis. During your stay your kidney function did not recover so you needed to have a dialysis line placed and started on hemodialysis. You will be going to ___ ___, your first appointment will be on ___. Prior to your discharge you developed a small cellulitis (skin infection) around an IV site, which is being treated with vancomycin, you will receive this medication with hemodialysis.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Assessment and Plan: This is a ___ yo F with MMP including asthma, OSA not on BiPAP, depression, CKD (baseline Cr 1.2-1.4), HTN, HL, PMR on Prednisone, Afib on Coumadin and recently diagnosed seizure disorder not on seizure ppx currently, who presented with an unresponsive episode. . # Unresponsive episode: The patient was admitted to the hospital after an unresponsive episode. There was concern for subclinical seizure as she had recently stopped her anti-epileptic, Keppra, against medical advice. A head CT showed no evidence of acute intracranial process. B12, folate, and TSH were within normal limits. An infectious work-up including chest x-ray and urinalysis was negative. Blood cultures were pending at the time of discharge. ECG was without ischemic changes and troponins were negative. There were no abnormalities on telemetry. Neurology was consulted and felt that the episode could represent a subclinical seizure. A routine EEG was performed with results pending at the time of discharge. They recommended the the patient restart Keppra, which the patient's family declined. They then suggested starting Trileptal 200 mg bid which the family also declined. The family was given information regarding Trileptal which they will review and consider starting in the future. She will follow-up with her PCP and her cognitive neurologist. . # ? ___ Body dementia: The patient's exam and history are also concerning for a dementing illness, as she has memory decline (as per psychiatry), frequent auditory and visual hallucinations, and diffuse rigidity. Although the zyprexa could be repsonsible for some of the rigidity, there is a possibility that she is developing ___ Body dementia. We therfore stopped her zyprexa and switched to Seroquel at night for agitation and recommended starting Namenda at 5 mg daily. The patient's family declined to start Namenda. She will follow-up with her outpatient cognitive neurologist, Dr. ___. . # Acute on chronic renal failure: The patient's baseline Cr appears to be about 1.4. She presented with a Cr of 1.9. She had had poor po intake prior to admission and she was felt to be dry. Her lasix was held and she was given gentle IVF. FeUrea was 37.6%, closer to pre-renal picture. She was given a small bolus of IVF prior to discharge but her family was unwilling to wait for resoluton of her ___ or further labs prior to discharge. . # Anemia: The patient has baseline hct of 28 to 30. Her hct on admission was 25.9 and at discharge hct 24.5. B12 and folate were wnl. Her iron studies were consistent with anemia of chronic inflammation. She declined iron supplementation or transfusion prior to discharge. . # CT finding: The patient's CT of the head showed marrow hyperostosis frontalis with more diffuse marrow infiltrative appearance and focal areas of osteolysis that are all stable. She should follow-up as an outpatient with her PCP to consider ___ clinically for marrow infiltrative process such as multiple myeloma. . # Atrial Fibrillation: The patient was on coumadin and presented with a subtherapeutic INR. Her coumadin dose was increased on admission to 5 mg daily. Her INR was 1.5 at time of discharge, and she was discharged on her home dose of 4 mg coumadin daily. She will follow-up with ___ clinic. . # The patient's asthma, depression, hypertension, polymyalgia rheumatica, and bone health were stable. She was maintained on advair, albuterol, citalopram, zyprexa, venlafaxine, labetalol, prednisone, calcium, and vitamin D. . # Code Status: DNR/DNI
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after an unresponsive episode that may have been a seizure. You were seen by the neurology team. You had stopped taking your seizure medicine, and you refused to restart it or any other seizure medicine despite the knowledge that you will likely have more seizures in the future if not taking the medicine. The neurology was also concerned that you may have a condition called ___ Body Dementia which can be treated with a medicine called Namenda which you refused. It was noted that you were taking a medicine, Zyprexa, which may be worsening your condition. The neurologists recommend you stop taking Zyprexa and start taking a similar medicine called Seroquel. Finally, you were found to have very low blood levels, but you refused to stay in the hospital for further work-up and you refused iron supplementatin or blood transfusion to help increase your blood levels. We strongly recommend that you restart your seizure medication.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Primary Reason for Admission: This is a ___ year old woman with PMH significant for autoimmune hepatitis c/b variceal bleed and HE, DM2 and seizure disorder, who is presenting w/ concern for sacral decubitus wound infection. . Active Problems: . # Saccral Pressure Ulcer - Stage III decubitus ulcer with associated erythema. Surgery was consulted in ER and did not think there was a role for debridement. Upon admission, she was given IV Vancomycin and Unasyn. These were continued for 3 days; on re-evaluation of her pressure ulcer there was no e/o associated cellulitis, no purulence and no necrotic tissue and her antibiotics were stopped. Wound care was consulted and recommended nutritional optimiaztion and daily dressing changes; agrered the wound did not appear infected. A rectal tube was placed for fecal management given her sacral wound and q2h lactulose. . # Hepatic Encephalopathy: Her altered mental status on admission was felt to be related to acute HE. She was started on Lactulose q2h and her mental status improved dramatically. Rectal tube was placed per above and removed once her Lactulose was decreased to q6h. She was observed for 24h on q6h lactulose and d/c'ed home once her mental status was back at baseline, which, according to her daughter, is oriented to person and place, but not date. . # Cirrhosis/Autoimmune Hepatitis: Patient's cirrhosis is complicated by varices, gastropathy, portal HTN, encephalopathy, ascites. Her lactulose was titrated per above. Her rifaximin, nadolol, imuran and budesonide were continued throughout her admission. Her HCT remained stable, no e/o bleed. There was no concern for SBP and she did not have a paracentesis on this admission. Last EGD ___, varicies banded x2. She has GI f/u in ___. . # Leukoerythroblastic Smear: Concerning for myelofibrosis vs infiltrative BM process. She had a BM biopsy in ___ with increased megakaryocytes. We discussed the smear with the Heme/Onc consulte fellow and arranged for ___ clinic follow up. . Chronic Problems: . # Seizure: No seizures on this admission, all seizure meds continued . # Essential Tremor: Continued primidone . # Asthma: Continued on spiriva and albuterol . # Chronic Pain: Continue home MS ___ and Oxycodone. No issues with sedation on this admission. . # DM2: Continued home glargine, use HISS. Blood sugar was well controlled. . Transitional Issues: - CODE STATUS: Full (confirmed) - CONTACT: ___ (daughter) ___ - f/u in GI clinic - f/u in Heme/Onc for repeat BM biopsy
summary: You were admitted for an ulcer on your back. For this, you initially received antibiotics. The surgeons evaluated the wound and felt that there was no need for surgery. When we re-evaluated the wound, it did not appear infected and we stopped your antibiotics. The wound nurses helped us keep your wound clean and dry. This wound will take a long time to heal. It is important to have regular wound care and to spend as little time lying down in bed as possible. You were also confused, which we think is related to your liver. For this, we gave you lactulose, and your mental status improved. We also found that your blood cells are not normal. This may be related to a problem with the bone marrow. For this, we have arranged for you to see ___ in the department of Hematology. It is important that you follow up with Dr ___ ___ this problem.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year-old woman with PMH CKD s/p creation of AV fistula (starting dialysis in ___, HTN, morbid obesity and resolved sarcoidosis, admitted with dyspnea, fever and malaise. Symptoms of malaise, fever, dyspnea and cough/congestion prior to admission are all suggestive of viral respiratory infection. Interstitial abnormality seen on CXR may suggest viral pneumonia, atypical pneumonia or viral bronchitis, vs. chronic changes from sarcoidosis (no past CXR available in OMR). Given patient's history of asthma and continued smoking, it is possible that a viral bronchitis or influenza may have triggered a COPD/asthma flare. Given severity of symptoms necessitating hospitalization, wheezing on exam, and comorbidities, we initially treated with azithromycin for COPD flare, while in conjunction treating viral respiratory infection supportively. However, the azithromycin was stopped when the patient was found to have influenza. We did not treat with oseltamivir given the duration of symptoms. We continued to treat with prednisone given history of asthma and wheezing on exam. Patient's blood sugars were difficult to control. We adjusted the patient's insulin regimen with the help of the patient's ___ physician. Ambulatory O2 sat was 92% on the day of discharge. She will taper prednisone. Patient was strongly encouraged to stop smoking. Her BP, cholesterol, and kidney disease medications were continued during this admission. Her kidney function was stable. . Transitional Issues:: # Patient was full code during this admission. # Had interstitial abnormality of uncertain chronicity on chest X-ray, which could be due to sarcoid, although atypical infection could be excluded. Enlarged hila, likely from lymphadenopathy related to sarcoid. Consider correlation with prior imaging or a nonurgent CT of the chest for further evaluation. If these findings are new compared to prior chest X-rays (we did not have priors in our system), then follow up outpatient chest X-ray or CT chest is suggested.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with fever and shortness of breath. You were found to have influenza (i.e. the flu). You were treated with IV fluids, prednisone for your asthma, and nebulizers to open your airways. You were initially treated with antibiotics, but these were stopped when your flu test came back positive, as antibiotics only work on bacterial infections, not on viruses like the flu. You are strongly encouraged to stop smoking. Smoking will worsen your lung disease and can lead to cancer and death. Quitting smoke now will improve your health in the future. Your blood sugars have been very high due to the steroids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o lady with HIV/AIDS on HAART, ESRD on HD, and HTN who presents with 3 days of fevers and diarrhea. . # Fever/diarrhea: Patient was non-toxic appearing on arrival to the floor. She continued to have diarrhea and Tm overnight was 101. She complained of a dry cough, otherwise, her review of systems was negative and her exam was non-focal for infection. Etiology of diarrhea felt to include viral gastroenteritis vs. bacterial infection vs. opportunistic infection given HIV status, however CD4 on ___ was 264. UA in ED was within normal limits, no culture was sent. Blood cultures were sent and were NGTD at the time of discharge. CXR was negative for pnumonia. Given her high fevers influenza was on differential, however Pt. reported getting a flu shot and nasal aspirate was negative for viral resp. pathogens. Stool was sent off for comprehensive testing including c. diff, O&P, microsporidium, cyclospora, campylobacter, salmonella and cryptosporidium which were all negative. ID was consulted given her HIV status who recommended the above mentioned tests. Her diarrhea quickly resolved and the etiology was felt to be most likely viral gastroenteritis. She was able to be advanced to a regular diet which she tolerated. No antibiotics were given on the floor given high liklihood of viral etiology. . # Transient hypotension/tachycardia: Patient presented with history of decreased oral intake and significant diarrhea making volume depletion the suspected etiolgy of her hypotension. Patient also had HD session the day PTA. Her tachycardia was felt to be a physiologic response to her fevers and volume depletion and resolved with IVF. Her antihypertensives were held and she was given gentle IVF until she was able to take PO. Her BP stabilized. . # HIV: On ART as outpatient, followed by Dr. ___. Last CD4 264 and VL <48. ID followed during her hospital stay. She was continued on her outpatient ART. . # ESRD/HD: ___ HIV nephropathy, renal followed during hospital stay. She was continued on her regularly scheduled HD, ___ schedule. Her outpatient calcium acetate and nephrocaps were also continued. . # Asthma: Patient was mildly hypoxic on admission with scattered wheezes on exam. CXR was negative for infiltrate. She was placed on Albuterol/atrovent nebs prn and continued on ___ outpatient salmeterol INH. Her wheezing quickly resolved and her oxygenation improved.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with fevers and diarrhea. Your symptoms were felt to be due to a viral gastroenteritis. You had stool cultures to look for a bacterial cause for your symptoms and these were still pending at the time of discharge. You were tested for influenza and ruled out for this. Your diarrhea quickly improved and you were able to eat a regular diet without difficulty. In the hospital we held your blood pressure medications because your blood pressure was on the low side. You should restart your metoprolol but take half the dose for the next few days. You will have your blood pressure checked at dialysis and you can increase back to your normal dose once your blood pressure is stable. You can restart your amplodipine in 2 days. We have stopped your minoxidil. You should talk to your nephrologist about restarting this. We have increased your Phoslo dose.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ h/o diverticulitis s/p sigmoid resection in ___, being treated for clinically diagnosed recurrent diverticulitis for 5 days, now p/w abdominal, nausea, and mucus in stools. ACTIVE ISSUES ------------- # Abdominal pain. He was admitted for abdominal pain, nausea, and mucus in stools. Prior to admission, he received levofloxacin and metronidazole for 5 days to treat clinically diagnosed diverticulitis. His WBC was elevated at 16.9 on admission. Abdominal and pelvic CT was normal. Laboratory testing did not reveal a cause of his symptoms. C.diff PCR was negative, WBC trended down to 11.5, and antibiotics were discontinued. Gastroenterology evaluated him. A colonoscopy was recommended given no resolution of his left lower quadrant pain. The colonoscopy was not remarkable for an underlying cause of pain or diarrhea. However, the patient did have mild inflammation in the terminal ileum. Biopsies were taken and pending at the time of discharge. The patient was discharged when able to tolerate PO and pain control improved on tramadol. INACTIVE ISSUES --------------- # Mild intermittent asthma: Was written for albuterol PRN but did not require throughout admission. # Peptic ulcer disease: Continued home ranitidine 300 mg PO BID and pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q12H. # PTSD/anxiety: Continued home dosing of Clonazepam 1 mg PO BID PRN for anxiety. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ------------------- Follow up: Code: full code, confirmed Contact: mother ___ ___ - ___ biopsies pending at the time of discharge
summary: You were admitted for abdominal pain, nausea, and mucus in your stools. Antibiotics were started due to a concern for an infection in your intestines. Laboratory tests did not reveal a cause for your symptoms. Imaging of your abdomen was normal. Further results indicated that you did not have an active bacterial infection and antibiotics were stopped. Gastroenterology specialists evaluated you as well. Since your pain did not improve, a colonoscopy was done to further assess your colon (large intestine). It did not show a clear cause for your pain or diarrhea. While admitted, your symptoms eventually improved and you were able to tolarate drinking and eating. Your home medications were continued while you were hospitalized.
